example_name: Google Ireland v CNIL (Right to be Forgotten)
example_background: In 2014, the French data protection authority CNIL ordered Google to delist links from search results worldwide for individuals requesting removal under the right to be forgotten. Google applied delisting only within EU domains. CNIL imposed fines for non-compliance with the order to extend delisting globally. The case progressed through French courts and reached the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU), which examined the territorial scope of EU data protection rules and fundamental rights under the Charter.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2019 that Google must delist links within the EU but not globally, balancing fundamental rights with freedom of information. CNIL fined Google 100,000 euros in 2016, upheld by French Council of State in 2018.
source_rule: This Charter reaffirms, with due regard for the powers and tasks of the Union and for the principle of subsidiarity, the rights as they result, in particular, from the constitutional traditions and international obligations common to the Member States, the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the Social Charters adopted by the Union and by the Council of Europe and the case-law of the Court of Justice of the European Union and of the European Court of Human Rights. In this context the Charter will be interpreted by the courts of the Union and the Member States with due regard to the explanations prepared under the authority of the Praesidium of the Convention which drafted the Charter and updated under the responsibility of the Praesidium of the European Convention.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Schrems II Case
example_background: Max Schrems challenged Facebook's data transfers from EU to US servers, alleging inadequate protection against US government surveillance. Following Snowden revelations, concerns arose over bulk data access by US intelligence lacking equivalent safeguards to EU fundamental rights standards. The case revisited adequacy of Safe Harbor framework after Schrems I invalidated it.
process_and_outcome: CJEU invalidated EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and required stricter safeguards for data transfers. Facebook ceased transfers to US servers temporarily and implemented Standard Contractual Clauses with supplementary measures.

example_name: Digital Rights Ireland Case
example_background: The Data Retention Directive required telecom providers to retain metadata on communications for law enforcement access across EU member states. Irish and Austrian courts referred questions on compatibility with privacy and data protection rights, given indiscriminate retention without safeguards or objective criteria.
process_and_outcome: CJEU invalidated the entire Directive in 2014 as disproportionate interference with fundamental rights, requiring member states to adopt targeted national laws with strict safeguards.

example_name: Tele2 Sverige Case
example_background: Swedish telecom operator Tele2 retained traffic data under national laws implementing the invalidated Data Retention Directive. The referring court questioned if such retention complied with EU fundamental rights standards post-Digital Rights Ireland.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2016 that general and indiscriminate retention is incompatible with EU law. National laws must limit retention to serious crime threats with prior judicial review.

example_name: La Quadrature du Net Case (French Surveillance)
example_background: French NGOs challenged generalized data retention and real-time interception measures under French Intelligence Act for national security, arguing disproportionate impact on privacy without sufficient oversight.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2020 that EU law precludes generalized retention except in response to serious threats, requiring independent authorization and targeted access.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland GDPR Fine by Irish DPC
example_background: Irish Data Protection Commission investigated Facebook's processing of EU users' personal data on US servers post-Schrems II. Transfers continued without adequate supplementary measures to protect against US surveillance, affecting millions of users.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, DPC imposed 1.2 billion euro fine on Meta, the largest GDPR penalty. Meta appealed and suspended US data transfers pending resolution.

example_name: Schrems I Safe Harbor Invalidation
example_background: Max Schrems filed complaint against Facebook for transferring EU data to US under Safe Harbor, citing NSA PRISM program accessing data without adequate remedies for EU residents.
process_and_outcome: CJEU invalidated Safe Harbor decision in 2015, finding it failed to ensure equivalent protection to EU fundamental rights standards.

example_name: Luxembourg DPAs Approve WhatsApp Transfers
example_background: Following Schrems II, WhatsApp (Meta subsidiary) submitted supplementary measures including encryption, pseudonymization, and organizational safeguards for EU-US data transfers. CNPD reviewed compliance with CJEU requirements.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, CNPD approved WhatsApp's transfer mechanisms as providing essentially equivalent protection, allowing continued transfers.

example_name: European Commission's Data Privacy Framework Adoption
example_background: Post-Schrems II, EU and US negotiated adequacy decision for new Data Privacy Framework with executive data protection safeguards, limitations on signals intelligence, and redress mechanisms to align with EU fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: Commission adopted adequacy decision in 2023, enabling certified companies to transfer data without additional safeguards. Subject to ongoing review and EDPB opinion.

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Fine 2019
example_background: Google Ireland Limited processed personal data of European users for targeted advertising without a valid legal basis, transparency, and proper consent mechanisms. The company's practices involved combining data from various services like search, YouTube, and Android devices to create advertising profiles, affecting approximately 49.95 million EU citizens under French jurisdiction. The French data protection authority examined complaints from organizations such as None of Your Business (NOYB) and Quadrature du Net, focusing on the lack of clear information provided to users about data processing purposes and the pre-ticked consent boxes that did not allow granular control over data usage.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL imposed a 50 million euro fine on Google in January 2019, marking one of the first major GDPR enforcement actions. Google appealed the decision to the French Council of State, which largely upheld the fine in 2020 after referring questions to the CJEU, confirming the validity of the cross-border processing approach.

example_name: Facebook GDPR Fine by Irish DPC 2023
example_background: Facebook (Meta Platforms Ireland Limited) transferred personal data of EU users to the United States via the standard contractual clauses (SCCs) invalidated by the Schrems II judgment in 2020. The transfers occurred without adequate supplementary measures to protect data against access by US intelligence authorities, impacting millions of users' data flows between EU servers and US-based data centers for social networking services.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission issued a 1.2 billion euro fine in December 2023, along with orders to suspend future transfers and bring existing EU-US data transfers to compliance within five months. Meta announced plans to appeal the decision.

example_name: Amazon EU Data Protection Fine 2021
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. advertised products using an algorithm that relied on extensive personal data processing, including non-customers' data, to predict and influence purchases. The processing lacked transparency regarding the logic of the recommendation system and did not provide sufficient information on data retention periods, affecting user privacy across EU marketplaces.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD imposed a 746 million euro fine in July 2021 for GDPR violations related to advertising practices. Amazon contested the fine and initiated legal proceedings, with ongoing appeals.

example_background: Austrian privacy activist Max Schrems challenged Facebook's transfer of EU user data to the US under the Privacy Shield framework, arguing insufficient protection from US surveillance laws like Section 702 of FISA. The case highlighted systemic issues in transatlantic data flows essential for digital services operating across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in July 2020 and required stricter safeguards for SCC-based transfers, impacting thousands of companies relying on US cloud services.

example_background: British Airways experienced a cyber-attack in 2018 where hackers accessed payment card details of approximately 429,612 customers through a vulnerability in the website's chat functionality. The incident involved unauthorized access and processing of personal and financial data over several weeks, without timely detection or notification.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO proposed a 183 million pound fine in 2020 under GDPR, later reduced to 20 million pounds in 2021 after appeal, with orders for enhanced security measures.

example_background: Marriott acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016 and continued processing data of 339 million guest records, including 7.6 million EU citizens, without proper data protection impact assessments or notifications about the merger. A breach in 2014 exposed passport numbers, payment cards, and travel details stored in unsecured databases.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Marriott 18.4 million pounds in 2020; the Irish DPC issued a separate 81.5 million euro fine in 2022 for ongoing violations.

example_background: Following Schrems II, the European Commission and US government negotiated a new framework to enable secure data transfers, incorporating US executive order limitations on surveillance signals intelligence and establishment of a Data Protection Review Court. Adopted to support free movement of data as a service across Atlantic.
process_and_outcome: The Commission adopted adequacy decision in July 2023, allowing EU companies to transfer data to certified US entities without additional safeguards, with periodic reviews planned.

example_background: TikTok processed personal data of up to 1.4 million EU children under 16 without verifiable parental consent, exposed age gates, and default public account settings. The platform's practices involved profiling minors for personalized feeds and ads across member states.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok 345 million euros in September 2023 and ordered suspension of data transfers to China, addressing violations from July 2020 to December 2021.

example_name: Apple App Store Compliance with DMA 2024
example_background: Apple adjusted its App Store policies in the EU to comply with the Digital Markets Act, allowing developers to direct users to external payment options and reducing commissions on in-app purchases facilitated outside the store, promoting contestability and free movement of services in digital markets.
process_and_outcome: Implemented changes effective March 2024 following DMA designation as gatekeeper; European Commission monitored compliance with ongoing assessments.

example_name: Volkswagen Dieselgate Emissions Scandal
example_background: Volkswagen Group installed software in diesel engines of approximately 11 million vehicles worldwide that detected when the cars were undergoing emissions testing and altered engine performance to meet regulatory limits. During normal driving conditions, the vehicles emitted up to 40 times the permitted levels of nitrogen oxides, contributing to air pollution that affects public health and environmental quality. The software manipulation occurred across models sold in Europe, the United States, and other markets from 2009 to 2015. This practice allowed Volkswagen to market the vehicles as clean diesel options while actual emissions undermined air quality standards designed to protect human communities and future generations from harmful pollutants.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and California Air Resources Board initiated investigations after independent testing revealed discrepancies. Volkswagen admitted to the conduct, leading to a settlement exceeding $30 billion in the U.S. alone, including vehicle buybacks, fines, and mitigation measures. Criminal charges were filed against executives, with some receiving prison sentences. Globally, recalls and additional fines totaling over €30 billion were imposed.
source_rule: Enjoyment of these rights entails responsibilities and duties with regard to other persons, to the human community and to future generations.
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Article 37 - Environmental Protection)
Universal Declaration of Human Rights (Article 25 - Standard of Living)

example_name: Union Carbide Bhopal Gas Tragedy
example_background: In 1984, a pesticide plant owned by Union Carbide India Limited released approximately 40 tons of methyl isocyanate gas into the atmosphere over Bhopal, India, exposing over 500,000 residents. The incident resulted from a combination of equipment failure, water entering a storage tank, and inadequate safety systems. The gas cloud spread over densely populated areas, causing immediate deaths and long-term health issues such as respiratory diseases, neurological damage, and birth defects in subsequent generations. Groundwater contamination persisted for decades, affecting water supplies for communities and posing ongoing risks to human health and the local ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: Indian authorities investigated the disaster, leading to the arrest of the plant manager and charges against Union Carbide executives. Union Carbide settled with the Indian government for $470 million in 1989. Dow Chemical, which acquired Union Carbide in 2001, faced continued lawsuits. Cleanup efforts were ordered, but remediation remains incomplete, with ongoing legal actions for compensation.
source_rule: Enjoyment of these rights entails responsibilities and duties with regard to other persons, to the human community and to future generations.
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Article 35 - Health Care)
Universal Declaration of Human Rights (Article 25 - Health and Well-being)

example_name: Chevron Ecuador Oil Pollution Case
example_background: Texaco, acquired by Chevron in 2001, operated oil fields in the Ecuadorian Amazon from 1964 to 1992, during which time it discharged billions of gallons of toxic wastewater into rivers and unlined pits. This practice contaminated soil, water sources, and ecosystems, leading to health problems including cancer and skin conditions among indigenous communities and settlers. The pollution affected over 1,400 square kilometers, impacting drinking water for tens of thousands and biodiversity in the region, with effects persisting for future generations.
process_and_outcome: Ecuadorian courts awarded $9.5 billion in damages in 2011 after a 20-year lawsuit. Chevron refused payment, countersuing in U.S. courts alleging fraud. The case involved international arbitration under the U.S.-Ecuador Bilateral Investment Treaty, where Chevron won a ruling against enforcement. Remediation efforts by Petroecuador continued, but disputes persist.
source_rule: Enjoyment of these rights entails responsibilities and duties with regard to other persons, to the human community and to future generations.

Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Article 37 - Environmental Protection)

example_name: Unilever Sustainable Living Plan
example_background: Unilever launched its Sustainable Living Plan in 2010, committing to halve the environmental footprint of its products by 2020 while improving health and well-being for millions. The initiative included sourcing 100% of agricultural raw materials sustainably, reducing water usage in factories by 50%, and cutting greenhouse gas emissions. Programs focused on smallholder farmers for crops like tea and palm oil, providing training to enhance yields and livelihoods while preserving soil health and biodiversity for future use. Hygiene and nutrition campaigns reached over 1 billion people.
process_and_outcome: By 2020, Unilever achieved key targets, including 98% sustainable sourcing and a 35% reduction in emissions. Independent audits by bodies like the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil verified progress. The plan enhanced brand reputation and financial performance, with sustainable brands growing 69% faster than others.

example_name: IKEA IWAY Sustainability Program
example_background: IKEA developed the IWAY standard in 2000, requiring suppliers to meet strict environmental and social criteria, including no child labor, fair wages, and reduced chemical use in production. The program monitors over 1,000 factories globally, promoting renewable energy adoption and waste reduction. IKEA committed to using 100% renewable energy by 2020 and sourcing wood from more sustainable forests, with efforts to regenerate forests and support community development in sourcing regions.
process_and_outcome: Audits confirmed compliance levels above 90%, leading to supplier improvements and IKEA's achievement of 100% renewable electricity in operations by 2020. The company published annual progress reports, gaining recognition from sustainability indices.

example_name: BP Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill
example_background: In 2010, the Deepwater Horizon rig, operated by BP in the Gulf of Mexico, experienced a blowout that released over 4 million barrels of crude oil into the sea over 87 days. The spill contaminated 1,100 miles of coastline, killed marine life, and damaged fisheries and tourism economies. Long-term effects included persistent oil in sediments, health issues for cleanup workers and residents, and ecosystem disruption projected to last decades.
process_and_outcome: U.S. regulators investigated, resulting in BP paying over $65 billion in fines, settlements, and cleanup costs. BP pleaded guilty to felony charges, including manslaughter. A $20.8 billion settlement addressed economic and environmental restoration.

example_name: Patagonia Environmental Activism and Supply Chain
example_background: Patagonia committed to using 100% recycled or renewable materials by 2025 and donates 1% of sales to environmental causes. The company traces supply chains to ensure fair labor and low-impact production, sues the U.S. government over public lands, and invests in regenerative organic agriculture to restore soil health for future food security. Initiatives include repairing products to extend lifespans and reducing packaging waste.
process_and_outcome: Patagonia achieved milestones like 68% recycled materials by 2020 and influenced policy through litigation. Annual reports detail progress, earning top sustainability rankings.

example_name: Boeing 737 MAX Safety Failures
example_background: Boeing designed the 737 MAX with a Maneuvering Characteristics Augmentation System (MCAS) that relied on a single angle-of-attack sensor, leading to erroneous activations. Two crashes in 2018 and 2019 killed 346 people. The design prioritized cost savings over redundancy, and pilot training was minimized. This affected families, airlines, and public confidence in aviation safety.
process_and_outcome: FAA and global regulators grounded the fleet for 20 months. Boeing paid $2.5 billion in a U.S. DOJ settlement, including a $243.6 million fine. Software fixes were implemented, with recertification in 2020.
source_rule: Enjoyment of these rights entails responsibilities and duties with regard to other persons, to the human community and to future generations.

Chicago Convention on International Civil Aviation

example_name: Novo Nordisk Corporate Social Responsibility in Diabetes
example_background: Novo Nordisk provides affordable insulin access programs in low-income countries, invests in diabetes education, and reduces carbon emissions from production by 75% since 2005. The company supports patient communities through changing for diabetes initiative, reaching millions with awareness and advocacy, while committing to no net environmental impact by 2030.
process_and_outcome: Programs delivered insulin donations worth millions and trained healthcare workers. Sustainability reports show verified reductions, with recognition from CDP and UN Global Compact.
source_rule: Enjoyment of these rights entails responsibilities and duties with regard to other persons, to the human community and to future generations.

example_name: Google Spain SL v AEPD and Mario Costeja González
example_background: In 2010, Mario Costeja González discovered that a 1998 newspaper article about his home foreclosure auction appeared prominently in Google search results when searching his name. He requested the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) to require La Vanguardia newspaper and Google to remove the links. The AEPD ordered Google to remove the links but not the newspaper. Google challenged this decision, leading to a series of appeals through Spanish courts to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU). The case addressed the balance between the right to privacy and the right to information, particularly for outdated personal data. The CJEU examined whether search engine operators process personal data under EU law and if individuals have a right to request delisting of links from search results.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on May 13, 2014, that Google must remove links to personal data upon request if the information is inadequate, irrelevant, or no longer relevant, establishing the 'right to be forgotten.' Google was ordered to comply, and the practice became applicable across EU member states. This led to over 1 million delisting requests processed by Google since 2014.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
involved_parties: Google Spain SL and Google Inc.
Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)
Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Articles 7, 8, 11)

example_name: Schrems II - Facebook Data Transfers to US
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, filed complaints against Facebook Ireland in 2013, alleging unlawful transfer of EU users' personal data to servers in the United States. Following the 2015 Schrems I ruling invalidating the Safe Harbour framework, the case proceeded under the Privacy Shield arrangement. Concerns focused on US surveillance laws allowing bulk access to data by intelligence agencies, lacking equivalent protections to EU fundamental rights standards. The Irish Data Protection Commission referred the matter to the CJEU, highlighting systemic issues in transatlantic data flows affecting millions of users.
process_and_outcome: On July 16, 2020, the CJEU invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield and set strict requirements for standard contractual clauses, ruling they require supplementary measures if the recipient country does not ensure adequate protection. Facebook ceased data transfers relying on Privacy Shield, prompting widespread compliance adjustments by tech companies.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Articles 7, 8, 47)

example_name: Romanian Data Protection Authority v Microsoft (Windows 10 Update)
example_background: In 2016, the Romanian National Supervisory Authority for Personal Data Processing investigated Microsoft regarding the automatic upgrade to Windows 10 on users' computers without explicit consent. The process collected personal data on usage, location, and diagnostics, sending it to Microsoft servers. Users reported unexpected installations overriding their preferences, raising issues about consent, data minimization, and transparency in processing personal data across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: The authority imposed a fine of €6,000 on Microsoft Romania SRL in 2017 for processing personal data without a valid legal basis. Microsoft adjusted its update mechanisms to improve consent flows and notification processes in response.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
involved_parties: Romanian National Supervisory Authority for Personal Data Processing (ANSPDCP)
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Articles 7, 8)
Romanian Law No. 102/2005 on personal data processing

example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Breach Fine
example_background: In 2018, hackers accessed personal data of approximately 400,000 British Airways customers through a vulnerability in the website's payment processing system. Data included names, addresses, payment card details, and login credentials. The breach occurred over several weeks without detection, affecting passengers booking flights or holidays. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated the airline's security measures and compliance with data protection obligations.
process_and_outcome: In October 2019, the ICO proposed a £183.39 million fine, later reduced to £20 million in 2020 following an appeal. British Airways implemented enhanced cybersecurity measures and compensated affected customers.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Articles 7, 8)

example_name: Digital Rights Ireland Ltd v Minister for Communications
example_background: Ireland's Data Retention Directive (2006/24/EC) required telecom providers to retain traffic and location data of all users for up to two years for law enforcement access. Digital Rights Ireland and others challenged this as disproportionate interference with privacy, arguing blanket retention lacked safeguards and affected fundamental rights indiscriminately across the population.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on April 8, 2014, invalidating the entire Directive as invalid under EU law, citing serious interference with privacy and data protection rights without sufficient justification or limits. EU member states revised national retention laws accordingly.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
involved_parties: Minister for Communications, Marine and Natural Resources (Ireland)
Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Articles 7, 8, 11, 52)

example_name: La Quadrature du Net v Premier Ministre (French Surveillance Laws)
example_background: French laws from 2015 allowed intelligence services broad powers to intercept communications, install software on devices, and retain connection data without prior judicial authorization in many cases. NGOs challenged three laws enabling generalized surveillance, arguing they enabled mass data collection without adequate oversight, impacting privacy across the population.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on October 6, 2022, that certain provisions violated EU law by permitting disproportionate retention and access to electronic communications data. France amended its legislation to include stricter judicial controls and proportionality requirements.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Articles 7, 8, 11, 47, 52)

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB and Watson (Swedish Data Retention)
example_background: Following the invalidation of the EU Data Retention Directive, Sweden maintained national laws requiring telecom operators to retain traffic data for six months for crime prevention. Tele2 Sverige and a public prosecutor challenged this, as it applied general retention without targeted suspicion, mirroring issues in the Digital Rights Ireland case.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on December 21, 2016, that general and indiscriminate retention of data is incompatible with EU law except in serious crime scenarios with strict safeguards. Sweden's laws were declared invalid, leading to legislative reforms.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Articles 7, 8)

example_name: Marper v United Kingdom (ECtHR Biometric Data Retention)
example_background: In 2004, the UK began retaining DNA profiles and fingerprints of individuals arrested but not convicted of crimes indefinitely. Michael Marper and another challenged this policy at the European Court of Human Rights, arguing it constituted disproportionate interference with privacy rights through blanket, unlimited retention of sensitive biometric data.
process_and_outcome: On December 4, 2008, the ECtHR ruled the UK's policy violated Article 8 ECHR. The UK amended the Police and Criminal Evidence Act in 2012 to introduce time-limited retention rules based on offense gravity.
source_rule: CHARTER OF FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (Article 8)
UK Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984

example_name: Cambridge Analytica Data Scandal
example_background: Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, obtained personal data from millions of Facebook users without their explicit consent through a third-party app developed by Aleksandr Kogan. The data was harvested from users who interacted with the app and extended to their Facebook friends, totaling around 87 million profiles. This data was used for targeted political advertising during the 2016 US presidential election and the Brexit referendum in the UK. The incident raised concerns about data privacy, consent, and the potential for manipulation of democratic processes across Europe and beyond. Facebook's platform mechanisms allowed the data sharing, which occurred prior to stricter privacy regulations but highlighted systemic issues in data handling practices by tech giants operating in the European market.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated and fined Facebook £500,000 in 2018 for failing to safeguard user data. Cambridge Analytica ceased operations and filed for bankruptcy in 2018. Multiple investigations by US and EU authorities followed, leading to lawsuits, congressional hearings, and global scrutiny of data practices. Facebook implemented new data access restrictions and privacy tools.
source_rule: The peoples of Europe, in creating an ever closer union among them, are resolved to share a peaceful future based on common values.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (Article 8 - Protection of personal data)

example_name: Google GDPR Fine by CNIL
example_background: Google faced scrutiny over its handling of personal data for targeted advertising purposes in France. The company was found to process user data for personalized ads without providing a sufficient legal basis, adequate transparency, and valid consent from users. This involved combining data across Google services like Search, YouTube, and Android without clear user control. The practices affected millions of European users and were part of broader concerns about big tech's dominance in digital advertising markets, where data is central to business models. The case underscored tensions between commercial interests and individual rights in the digital single market.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority CNIL imposed a €50 million fine on Google in January 2019, one of the first major GDPR enforcement actions. Google appealed but lost subsequent court challenges. The decision prompted Google to revise its consent mechanisms and data processing policies across the EU.
source_rule: The peoples of Europe, in creating an ever closer union among them, are resolved to share a peaceful future based on common values.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Fine by Irish DPC
example_background: Meta Platforms, formerly Facebook, processed personal data of EU users for behavioral advertising based on US transfers following the Schrems II ruling, which invalidated the Privacy Shield framework. The Irish Data Protection Commission examined whether Meta's Standard Contractual Clauses provided adequate safeguards against US surveillance laws, affecting data of over 500 million users. This case arose from complaints by privacy activist Max Schrems and involved ongoing transfers essential to Meta's European operations.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion, the largest GDPR penalty to date, for unlawful data transfers. Meta appealed to EU courts. The company suspended US data transfers and developed new transfer tools compliant with EU requirements.

example_name: Apple App Store GDPR Compliance Measures
example_background: Apple responded to GDPR by implementing comprehensive privacy features in its iOS ecosystem, including App Tracking Transparency (ATT), which requires apps to obtain user consent before tracking across apps and websites. The company also introduced privacy nutrition labels for apps, detailed data usage reports, and on-device processing for features like Siri. These measures were rolled out to protect over 1 billion EU users' data, aligning with the EU's emphasis on user control and transparency in digital services.
process_and_outcome: Apple's initiatives received positive feedback from regulators and users, avoiding major fines and setting industry benchmarks. The ATT framework led to a reported 80% opt-out rate for tracking, demonstrating effective enforcement of consent requirements without regulatory penalties.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Fine by Dutch DPA
example_background: TikTok processed personal data of children under 16 without proper parental consent or age verification mechanisms. Default public account settings allowed children to share videos visible to all users, and data was used for algorithmic recommendations. This affected millions of young users in the Netherlands and across Europe, raising issues about child safety, privacy, and the responsibilities of social media platforms in the digital single market.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority fined TikTok €750,000 in 2023. TikTok adjusted its age gates and privacy settings. Broader EU investigations into TikTok continue under the Digital Services Act.

The company invested in cloud infrastructure ensuring data stays within the EU and provides customers with granular control over data subject requests. These efforts support thousands of European enterprises in meeting their regulatory obligations.

example_name: Amazon GDPR Fine by Luxembourg DPA
example_background: Amazon's advertising system processed personal data for targeted ads without a clear legal basis, relying on presumed consent rather than explicit user agreement. Data from purchases, searches, and device usage was combined to build profiles, affecting tens of millions of EU customers. The practices were integral to Amazon's e-commerce and AWS operations in Europe.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection (CNPD) fined Amazon €746 million in 2021, one of the largest GDPR penalties. Amazon appealed to EU courts, leading to ongoing proceedings. The company updated its consent banners and data processing.

example_name: Schrems I and II Privacy Advocacy Success
example_background: Privacy activist Max Schrems challenged data transfers from EU Facebook users to the US under the Safe Harbor framework and later Privacy Shield, arguing insufficient protection against US government surveillance programs like PRISM. These cases involved systemic data flows critical to transatlantic tech operations and affected hundreds of millions of users.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU invalidated Safe Harbor in 2015 (Schrems I) and Privacy Shield in 2020 (Schrems II), forcing companies to adopt new safeguards. This led to widespread adoption of Standard Contractual Clauses with supplementary measures.

example_background: In 2014, the Court of Justice of the European Union established the 'right to be forgotten' in the Google Spain case, requiring search engines to delist certain links upon request if they were deemed irrelevant or outdated. Following the introduction of the General Data Protection Regulation in 2018, Spain's data protection authority pursued enforcement against Google for delays in processing right-to-be-forgotten requests and other data processing issues. The case involved numerous complaints from individuals seeking removal of personal data from search results, reflecting broader societal shifts in digital privacy expectations amid evolving online information practices.
process_and_outcome: In September 2019, the Spanish Data Protection Agency imposed a 15 million euro fine on Google for obstructing data subjects' rights, including slow response times to delisting requests. Google implemented procedural changes to handle such requests more efficiently across the EU.

example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a data breach affecting 400,000 customers' payment card details and personal information due to a Magecart attack on its website. The incident involved malicious JavaScript injection exploiting vulnerabilities in third-party scripts, highlighting risks from supply chain dependencies in web technologies.

example_name: Schrems II Case - Facebook Data Transfers
example_background: In 2013, Austrian privacy advocate Max Schrems filed a complaint against Facebook Ireland for transferring personal data of European users to servers in the United States. The case questioned the safeguards under the EU-US Safe Harbor framework, which allowed data transfers between the EU and US. Following the invalidation of Safe Harbor by the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) in Schrems I, the investigation continued under the Privacy Shield arrangement. Concerns arose regarding US surveillance practices, including bulk data collection by intelligence agencies like the NSA, as revealed by Edward Snowden. Facebook processed vast amounts of user data, including sensitive information, and relied on standard contractual clauses for transfers. Schrems argued that these clauses did not sufficiently protect data from US government access, potentially undermining European data protection standards. The case involved detailed analysis of US laws such as FISA Section 702 and Executive Order 12333, highlighting discrepancies between EU fundamental rights and US practices.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on July 16, 2020, invalidating the EU-US Privacy Shield and requiring stricter scrutiny of standard contractual clauses. Facebook was ordered to suspend data transfers to the US, leading to operational changes including increased data localization in Europe. The decision prompted the EU Commission to negotiate new adequacy frameworks.

example_name: Cambridge Analytica Scandal - Facebook
example_background: In 2018, revelations emerged that Cambridge Analytica harvested personal data from up to 87 million Facebook users without explicit consent, primarily through a personality quiz app developed by Aleksandr Kogan. The data was used for targeted political advertising during the 2016 US elections and Brexit campaign. Facebook's platform allowed third-party apps extensive access to user data and friends' data under its legacy API policies. European regulators investigated Facebook's role in failing to protect user privacy, including inadequate oversight of data processors and insufficient breach notifications. The incident raised concerns about data protection, freedom of expression in political contexts, and effective remedies for affected individuals.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined Facebook £500,000 in 2019. Ireland's Data Protection Commission issued additional penalties and mandated audits. Facebook enhanced app review processes and data access restrictions globally.

example_background: Apple Inc. designed its European operations to align with stringent data protection requirements following GDPR implementation in 2018. The company localized iCloud data storage in the EU, using facilities in Denmark operated by third-party providers under strict contractual terms. Apple implemented differential privacy techniques for analytics, on-device processing for Siri and Face ID, and granular user controls for app tracking via App Tracking Transparency (ATT). These measures addressed privacy by design and default principles, minimizing data transfers outside the EU and providing transparency reports on government requests.
process_and_outcome: Apple faced no major GDPR enforcement actions in the EU and received recognition in regulatory audits for proactive compliance. The ATT framework led to industry-wide adoption and reduced unauthorized tracking.

A French employee challenged the transfer, arguing insufficient protection against US surveillance. The French data protection authority investigated the bank's data processing agreements and found gaps in supplementary measures to protect data subjects' rights, particularly regarding access and redress mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a €250,000 fine in 2019 and ordered suspension of transfers until compliance. BNP Paribas updated its transfer mechanisms to include additional safeguards.

example_name: Tele2 Sverige Surveillance Case
example_background: Swedish telecom provider Tele2 Sverige was required under national law to retain metadata on communications for law enforcement access. The practice involved storing location data, IP addresses, and call records for up to six months for all subscribers. Privacy advocates and Tele2 challenged the law, citing disproportionate interference with privacy and data protection rights. The case examined bulk retention practices across EU member states and their compatibility with targeted retention alternatives.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled in 2016 (Tele2 Sverige AB v Post- och telestyrelsen) that general and indiscriminate retention of metadata is incompatible with EU law, requiring targeted and limited retention. Sweden amended its legislation accordingly.

Launched post-Schrems II, the initiative uses hyper-scalar data centers in multiple EU countries, with commitments to avoid processing by non-EU subcontractors. Encryption keys are managed in the EU, and transparency reports detail compliance audits and government data requests handled under EU law.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved adequacy validations from EU regulators and maintained zero major data transfer fines. The model influenced enterprise cloud procurement standards in Europe.

example_name: Cambridge Analytica Data Harvesting
example_background: Cambridge Analytica, a British political consulting firm, obtained personal data from up to 87 million Facebook users without explicit consent through a personality quiz app developed by researcher Aleksandr Kogan in 2014. The app collected data from users who installed it and their Facebook friends, bypassing privacy settings. This data was used for targeted political advertising during the 2016 US presidential election and Brexit referendum. Facebook's platform allowed third-party apps to access friend data until 2014-2015 policy changes. The exposure by whistleblower Christopher Wylie in 2018 revealed how data influenced voter behavior through psychographic profiling. The incident raised concerns about individual autonomy and consent in digital environments, affecting democratic processes and user trust in social media platforms.
process_and_outcome: UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined Facebook £500,000; US Federal Trade Commission settled with Facebook for $5 billion. Cambridge Analytica ceased operations in 2018. Multiple investigations by FTC, ICO, and EU data protection authorities followed, leading to enhanced platform data policies.

example_name: Equifax Data Breach
example_background: Equifax, one of the largest US credit reporting agencies, experienced a data breach from May to July 2017, exposing sensitive personal information of 147 million people, including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and in some cases driver's license and credit card numbers. Hackers exploited a known vulnerability in the Apache Struts web application framework, which Equifax failed to patch despite a patch being available since March 2017. The company detected unusual activity but did not fully investigate initially. Post-breach, Equifax faced criticism for delayed public notification (announced September 2017) and inadequate consumer support. The incident underscored risks to personal identity and financial security, leading to widespread identity theft concerns and lawsuits from affected individuals.
process_and_outcome: US Federal Trade Commission, Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, and 50 states settled with Equifax for $700 million, including $425 million consumer restitution fund. Equifax paid additional penalties and invested $1.25 billion in security upgrades over years. Executives resigned, and criminal charges were filed against Chinese military personnel.

example_name: GDPR Compliance by Apple
example_background: Apple Inc. implemented comprehensive measures to align its European operations with the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) effective May 2018. This included enhancing user privacy controls in iOS, such as App Tracking Transparency, which requires explicit user consent for cross-app tracking. Apple established dedicated EU data protection teams, conducted Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing, appointed Data Protection Officers, and localized data storage in European data centers to minimize transatlantic transfers. The company also updated its privacy policy to clearly outline data practices, provided tools for data access and deletion requests, and integrated privacy-by-design in product development. These steps addressed concerns over user profiling and surveillance in mobile ecosystems, promoting informed consent and data minimization.
process_and_outcome: Apple faced no major GDPR fines post-2018 implementation and received positive assessments from EU regulators. In 2021, it successfully defended against complaints regarding tracking prompts, demonstrating effective compliance. Ongoing audits confirm adherence.

example_name: Schrems II EU-US Data Transfers Invalidated
example_background: In 2020, the Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU) invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield framework in the Schrems II case, following Max Schrems' challenge against Facebook's data transfers to the US. Concerns centered on US surveillance laws like Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and Executive Order 12333, which allow bulk collection of EU citizens' data without adequate safeguards or effective remedies. The court found Privacy Shield inadequate due to lack of equivalence with EU fundamental rights protections. Facebook's Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) were scrutinized but upheld with caveats for supplementary measures. The ruling affected thousands of companies relying on transatlantic data flows for cloud services, HR, and advertising.
process_and_outcome: CJEU annulled Privacy Shield decision; Irish Data Protection Commission enforced suspension of Facebook's EU-US transfers, leading to service reconfiguration. New EU-US Data Privacy Framework adopted in 2023 as successor.

Attackers injected malicious code into the booking process from August 21 to September 5, stealing names, addresses, card numbers, and CVVs. British Airways detected the breach on September 5 but delayed notification until September 28. The incident occurred during peak booking season, affecting travelers worldwide. The UK ICO investigated following complaints, focusing on security measures and breach response timelines.
process_and_outcome: ICO proposed £183 million fine in 2019 (later reduced to £20 million in 2020 due to COVID impacts). British Airways enhanced cybersecurity with multi-factor authentication and regular vulnerability scans.

This includes bias detection tools in SAP AI Core, human oversight requirements for high-risk applications like HR recruitment, and transparency reports for AI decision-making in ERP systems. SAP established an AI Ethics Board with diverse stakeholders and conducted impact assessments ensuring alignment with fundamental rights. Deployed in EU client projects, these practices prevent discriminatory outcomes and promote explainability, as seen in supply chain optimization tools that respect worker data dignity.

example_name: European Commission v. Hungary (Judicial Independence Case)
example_background: Hungary implemented legislative reforms in 2018 that restructured the administrative court system and modified the appointment process for judges and prosecutors. These changes included the government's ability to dissolve the existing administrative courts and create new ones under different oversight, along with alterations to the rules governing the National Judicial Council and the Prosecutorial Council. The reforms were part of broader judicial changes initiated since 2011, which affected the independence of the judiciary from executive influence. The measures raised concerns regarding the separation of powers and the ability of courts to operate without undue political interference, impacting the democratic framework within the EU member state.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings in 2018. The Court of Justice of the EU ruled in April 2020 that Hungary failed to fulfill its obligations under EU law. Hungary was ordered to pay a lump sum of €200,000 and a daily penalty of €1 million until compliance, totaling over €200 million in payments by 2022.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.

relevant_rules: Article 2 TEU (EU values including rule of law)

example_name: European Commission v. Poland (Judicial Reforms)
example_background: Poland enacted laws between 2015 and 2019 that reformed the judiciary, including lowering the retirement age for Supreme Court judges, allowing the Minister of Justice to dismiss and appoint judges, and restructuring the National Council of the Judiciary to include members appointed by parliament. Additional changes involved disciplinary proceedings against judges for their judicial decisions, creating a disciplinary chamber within the Supreme Court. These reforms were presented as necessary for efficiency but led to concerns about political control over the judiciary, affecting access to independent courts across the country.
process_and_outcome: Infringement proceedings started in 2017. The CJEU issued interim measures in 2018 and a final judgment in 2021 declaring the measures incompatible with EU law. Poland faced daily fines of €1 million from October 2021 and an additional €500,000 per day from 2023 for non-compliance with subsequent rulings.

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Fine by French CNIL
example_background: In 2018, Google Ireland Limited, responsible for advertising personalization in the European Economic Area, processed personal data of French users for targeted advertising without a valid legal basis. The company's consent mechanism bundled multiple purposes including personalization and storage, making it difficult for users to understand and control data processing. Data retention periods were indefinite, and settings did not allow granular control over purposes, affecting millions of users' right to data protection and privacy.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated following complaints. In January 2020, CNIL imposed a €50 million fine, the first major GDPR enforcement action. Google contested but the fine was upheld by the French Council of State in 2022.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.

example_name: Facebook GDPR Fine by Irish DPC
example_background: Following the 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal, Facebook Ireland Limited was found to have breached GDPR by allowing third-party apps unauthorized access to user data without proper consent. Approximately 87 million users globally, including 3 million in the EU, had their data harvested for political advertising. Facebook's transparency about data sharing was insufficient, and users were not adequately informed or given control over data transfers outside the EEA.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) coordinated a cross-border investigation. In November 2022, it imposed a €265 million fine on Facebook for transparency and consent violations related to page administrators' data sharing.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.

example_name: Luxembourg Rule of Law Procedure
example_background: Luxembourg faced scrutiny over the functioning of its prosecution service, where the Procureur général d'État could issue non-binding instructions to prosecutors in individual cases, potentially undermining judicial independence. The system allowed hierarchical oversight that might influence prosecutorial decisions, raising questions about the rule of law and separation of powers in criminal proceedings.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated an Article 7(1) TEU procedure in July 2022, conducting a preliminary assessment. Discussions continued into 2023, with Luxembourg committing to reforms to enhance independence.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.
involved_parties: Council of the EU (Potential Decision Maker)

example_name: European Court of Human Rights v. Bulgaria (Human Dignity Case)
example_background: In the case involving Romani applicants, Bulgarian authorities conducted forced evictions and tolerated private individuals damaging properties of a Roma community in 2013. The state failed to prevent or adequately investigate attacks, resulting in destruction of homes and displacement of families, which affected the applicants' right to respect for private and family life and home.
process_and_outcome: The ECtHR ruled in December 2020 that Bulgaria violated Articles 8 and P1-1 of the ECHR. Bulgaria was ordered to pay €20,000 in damages and costs to the applicants.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.
relevant_rules: Article 8 ECHR (Right to respect for private life)
EU Charter Article 7 (Respect for private life)

example_name: Microsoft Cloud EU Data Boundary Compliance
example_background: Microsoft enhanced its cloud services in 2023 by establishing a dedicated EU Data Boundary, ensuring that personal data of EU customers remains within the EU for processing. This included new datacenters in Sweden and Denmark, sovereign cloud controls, and transparency reports on data access requests, supporting EU individuals' data sovereignty and protection.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft committed investments exceeding €3.2 billion in EU cloud infrastructure by 2025. The initiative received positive feedback from EU regulators and was recognized as aligning with data protection standards without enforcement actions.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.

example_name: Apple App Store Privacy Compliance Program
example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in 2021, requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking across apps or websites. This framework, including privacy nutrition labels and on-device processing for features like Face ID, placed user privacy at the center, processing data locally to minimize exposure.
process_and_outcome: Implemented across iOS 14.5+, ATT reduced cross-app tracking by industry estimates of 80%. No major regulatory fines issued; praised in EU audits for enhancing user control.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.
relevant_rules: EU Charter Article 8 (Protection of personal data)

example_name: Danish DPA v. Nets Holding (Equality in Data Practices)
example_background: Nets Holding A/S, a payment services provider, profiled over 400,000 Danish citizens based on payment habits without consent, creating segments for marketing. The profiling lacked transparency and equal treatment, as users could not opt out or understand the basis for data use.
process_and_outcome: The Danish DPA investigated in 2021 and issued an administrative fine of DKK 59.5 million (€8 million) in 2023 for unlawful profiling.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.

example_name: Slovakia Rule of Law Investigation
example_background: Slovakia's 2020 criminal law amendments granted the Prosecutor General extensive powers to dismiss cases and remove prosecutors, alongside changes to the Special Prosecutor's Office handling high-profile corruption cases. These reforms potentially allowed executive influence over ongoing investigations, impacting judicial independence and public trust.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission launched an Article 7(1) TEU procedure in December 2023, assessing risks to rule of law standards. Ongoing dialogue with Slovakia for corrective measures.
source_rule: Conscious of its spiritual and moral heritage, the Union is founded on the indivisible, universal values of human dignity, freedom, equality and solidarity; it is based on the principles of democracy and the rule of law. It places the individual at the heart of its activities, by establishing the citizenship of the Union and by creating an area of freedom, security and justice.

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Data Processing Fine
example_background: In 2019, the French data protection authority examined Google's processing of personal data for personalized advertising purposes. The investigation focused on the transparency of data processing operations and the validity of consent obtained from users. Google had implemented a consent mechanism that bundled multiple data processing purposes under a single acceptance action, which did not provide users with granular control over their data. The processing involved location data, advertising identifiers, and other personal information collected through Android devices and web services across the European Economic Area. This affected millions of users who were presented with consent banners that did not clearly distinguish between necessary and optional data uses, leading to questions about whether users were adequately informed about the scope and purposes of data collection.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL imposed a fine of 50 million euros on Google Ireland Limited after finding insufficient transparency and invalid consent. Google appealed the decision, but the fine was upheld, prompting the company to revise its consent mechanisms globally.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)
relevant_rules: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Article 5(1)(a) - Lawfulness, fairness and transparency

example_name: British Airways GDPR Passenger Data Breach
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a cyber incident where attackers accessed customer payment card details and personal information of approximately 429,612 individuals through a vulnerability on the company's website. The breach lasted several weeks and involved the theft of names, addresses, payment card numbers, CVV codes, and login credentials. The incident stemmed from a supply chain compromise where a malicious script was injected into the website's checkout process, bypassing security measures. This event raised concerns about the adequacy of data security measures implemented by the airline to protect passenger information during online bookings.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) issued a monetary penalty notice of £20 million (reduced from an initial £183 million draft). British Airways implemented enhanced security protocols and faced class action lawsuits from affected customers.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.

example_name: Facebook GDPR Cambridge Analytica Scandal
example_background: In 2014-2015, an app developer accessed data from up to 87 million Facebook users without their explicit consent through a third-party application called 'This Is Your Digital Life.' The data was shared with Cambridge Analytica, which used it for political advertising purposes during elections. Facebook's platform allowed apps to harvest not only data from users who installed the app but also from their Facebook friends, without notifying or obtaining consent from those individuals. This practice continued until 2018 when it was publicly revealed, highlighting issues with data sharing policies and user privacy controls on the social media platform.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook Ireland Limited £500,000. The company suspended data access for questionable apps, overhauled its privacy settings, and faced multiple regulatory investigations and congressional hearings.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 5(1)(a) - Principles relating to processing of personal data
GDPR Article 25 - Data protection by design and by default

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Hotel Guest Data Breach
example_background: In 2014, hackers compromised the reservation system of Starwood Hotels and Resorts, which Marriott acquired in 2016. The breach was discovered in 2018 and affected up to 383 million guest records worldwide, including 7 million from EU residents. Stolen data included names, passport numbers, payment card details, travel dates, and email addresses. Marriott's integration of the Starwood system post-acquisition did not promptly identify the ongoing intrusion, allowing the attackers continued access for four years. The incident involved encryption key theft that enabled decryption of sensitive information.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Marriott International Inc. £18.4 million. The company enhanced cybersecurity measures, notified affected guests, and offered identity protection services.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 33 - Notification of a personal data breach

example_name: Apple GDPR App Store Transparency Compliance
example_background: Apple updated its App Store practices in response to GDPR requirements by introducing App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in iOS 14.5, launched in 2021. This feature requires apps to obtain explicit user permission before tracking them across apps and websites owned by other companies. Prior to this, apps could access the Identifier for Advertisers (IDFA) without user consent. Apple's implementation includes a system-wide prompt where users can opt out of tracking, applying across all apps. This change affects developers who rely on ad revenue and has been part of Apple's broader privacy framework, including on-device processing and privacy nutrition labels.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions were taken against Apple; the company received recognition from regulators and privacy advocates for proactive measures. ATT led to industry-wide shifts in data collection practices.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 25 - Data protection by design and by default

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children's Data Fine
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission investigated TikTok's processing of personal data of users under 16 years old. Practices included default public account settings, age verification methods that were easily bypassed, and processing of biometric data through facial recognition effects without adequate safeguards. The platform's algorithm-driven content recommendation relied on behavioral data from minors without parental consent mechanisms that met regulatory standards. This affected millions of young users in the EU who created accounts with minimal age gates.
process_and_outcome: In 2023 and 2024, the Irish DPC imposed fines totaling €530 million on TikTok Ireland Limited for data processing violations related to children. Additional orders required changes to age verification and default settings.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 8 - Conditions applicable to child's consent
GDPR Article 25 - Data protection by design

example_name: Amazon GDPR Advertising Personalization Fine
example_background: Amazon's operations involved processing personal data for targeted advertising based on inferred interests from purchase history, browsing behavior, and device information. The company used opaque methods to build user profiles without clear disclosure of the legal basis for processing or sufficient user control options. Consent banners and privacy notices were found not to meet transparency standards, affecting EU consumers who interacted with the platform for shopping and recommendations.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD fined Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. €746 million in 2021 for violations in personalized ads. The decision was appealed, with ongoing proceedings.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD, Luxembourg)
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 5(1)(a) - Lawfulness, fairness and transparency

example_name: SAP GDPR Data Transfers Compliance Program
example_background: SAP implemented a comprehensive GDPR compliance program including data protection impact assessments, binding corporate rules for intra-group transfers, and pseudonymization techniques for cloud services. The company established data protection officers across EU entities, conducted regular audits, and provided employee training on rights like access and erasure requests. This framework supports enterprise software processing vast amounts of customer data while ensuring transfers outside the EEA comply with adequacy decisions or standard contractual clauses.
process_and_outcome: SAP avoided major fines through proactive compliance, earning certifications like ISO 27701 and positive audit outcomes from supervisory authorities.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 44-50 - Transfers of personal data to third countries
GDPR Article 24 - Responsibility of the controller

example_name: Meta GDPR Facial Recognition Ban in EU
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland used facial recognition technology in its Facebook and Instagram services to suggest photo tags based on uploaded images. The system automatically analyzed facial features from billions of photos without explicit opt-in consent from all users whose images were processed, including non-users. This biometric data processing occurred at scale without sufficient legal basis or transparency notices, continuing post-GDPR implementation.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC ordered Meta to cease biometric data processing for facial recognition in the EU and imposed a €275 million fine in 2021 as part of the 'Facebook Ireland data series.' Meta complied by disabling the feature.
source_rule: The Union therefore recognises the rights, freedoms and principles set out hereafter.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 9 - Processing of special categories of personal data

example_name: European Court of Human Rights - Pretty v United Kingdom
example_background: Diane Pretty, who had motor neurone disease, sought assistance from her husband to end her life due to her deteriorating health condition. UK law prohibited assisting suicide, and she requested a declaration that the prohibition did not apply to her circumstances. The domestic courts dismissed her claim, leading to an application to the European Court of Human Rights. The Court examined whether the UK's refusal to permit assisted suicide interfered with her rights under Article 2, which protects the right to life, and related articles on privacy and discrimination. The case involved detailed medical evidence about her condition and arguments on the scope of state obligations to protect life.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights, in a judgment on 29 April 2002, held by 5 votes to 2 that there was no violation of Article 2, finding that the right to life does not include a right to die. The Court upheld the UK's margin of appreciation in protecting vulnerable individuals from abuse.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life)

example_name: Lautsi v Italy - Crucifixes in Schools
example_background: An Italian parent objected to the display of crucifixes in public school classrooms attended by her children, arguing it imposed religious symbols in a secular education setting. The case progressed through Italian administrative courts, which upheld the tradition of displaying crucifixes as part of national cultural heritage rather than indoctrination. It reached the European Court of Human Rights, where the Grand Chamber reviewed claims related to freedom of religion and education, indirectly touching on state duties to protect life and dignity in educational environments.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber, in a 15-2 decision on 18 March 2011, found no violation, recognizing the crucifix as a symbol of Italian identity compatible with secularism and not breaching rights protections.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life), Protocol 1 Article 2 (Right to education)

example_name: Turkish Military Intervention in Cyprus - Applications v Turkey
example_background: Following Turkey's 1974 military operation in Cyprus, numerous Greek Cypriot applicants alleged enforced disappearances, killings, and denial of access to missing persons' fates during the conflict. The European Court of Human Rights investigated patterns of conduct by Turkish forces, including failure to account for individuals taken into custody, impacting families' rights to know the circumstances surrounding their relatives' lives.
process_and_outcome: In the 10 January 2001 decision, the Court found continuing violations of Article 2 due to the lack of effective investigations into deaths and disappearances, ordering Turkey to pay compensation and provide information on missing persons.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life)

example_name: McCann and Others v United Kingdom
example_background: Three IRA members were shot dead by British special forces in Gibraltar in 1988 during a counter-terrorism operation. The soldiers believed the suspects posed an imminent bomb threat. The victims' families challenged the planning and execution of the operation, including prior surveillance, rules of engagement, and the failure to arrest rather than kill, raising questions on the use of lethal force.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights, in a 27 September 1995 judgment, found by 10-9 votes a violation of Article 2, criticizing the operation's planning for not minimizing lethal force recourse, though no intent to kill was found.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life)

example_name: European Union Action Plan on Human Rights and Democracy
example_background: The EU adopted a comprehensive action plan in 2020 to strengthen respect for human rights globally, including specific measures to protect the right to life through support for healthcare access, conflict prevention, and accountability for violations. This involved funding projects in partner countries, diplomatic advocacy, and integration into trade agreements, with annual implementation reports tracking progress.
process_and_outcome: The plan has been implemented through EU institutions, leading to funded initiatives and policy dialogues that enhanced protections in various regions, as detailed in progress reports.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights, Article 2 (Right to life)
EU Strategic Framework on Human Rights

example_name: Nachova and Others v Bulgaria
example_background: Two Roma men were shot dead by Bulgarian military police in 1996 while absent without leave. Initial investigations attributed the deaths to running away and falling, but autopsy evidence suggested shooting from behind. The applicants alleged racial discrimination in the use of force and inadequate investigation into the circumstances.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber, on 6 July 2005, found violations of Article 2 for failure to protect life and conduct effective investigations, plus a substantive violation linked to discrimination under Article 14.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life), Article 14 (Prohibition of discrimination)

example_name: Osman v United Kingdom
example_background: A schoolteacher faced death threats from a former pupil's father, reported to police multiple times. Despite warnings, no preventive measures were taken, and the teacher and his son were attacked, with the son killed. The family argued the police had a positive obligation under Article 2 to protect known vulnerable individuals.
process_and_outcome: On 28 October 1998, the Court found no violation, establishing the 'Osman test' for state operational duty to protect life, requiring real and immediate risk known to authorities.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life)

example_name: Giuliani and Gaggio v Italy - Genoa G8 Summit
example_background: During the 2001 G8 summit in Genoa, Carlo Giuliani was shot dead by a police officer during clashes with protesters. Another applicant alleged injuries from tear gas. Italian courts convicted the officer of manslaughter but suspended the sentence; investigations into broader policing were criticized for inadequacy.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber, on 24 March 2011, found violations of Article 2 for ineffective investigations into the planning and control of policing operations that led to the death.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life)

example_name: ECHR Case of Mastromatteo v Italy
example_background: A prisoner on day release committed murder and other crimes. The victim's family argued Italy's prison release system failed to adequately assess risks, breaching the positive obligation to protect the public from known dangerous individuals at large.
process_and_outcome: On 24 October 2002, the Court found no violation of Article 2, affirming states' wide margin of appreciation in penal policy and release decisions absent specific foreseeability.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to life.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights, Article 2 (Right to life)

example_name: European Court of Human Rights - Öcalan v. Turkey (2005)
example_background: Abdullah Öcalan, founder of the Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK), was arrested in Kenya in 1999 and extradited to Turkey where he was sentenced to death by the State Security Court in Ankara for treason and separatism based on activities conducted through the PKK. The sentence was issued following a trial that involved allegations of terrorist acts against the Turkish state. Turkey commuted the death sentence to life imprisonment in 2002 after signing Protocol No. 13 to the European Convention on Human Rights, which abolished the death penalty in all circumstances. Öcalan appealed to the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) claiming violations of fair trial rights and the prohibition on the death penalty under the Convention.
