process_and_outcome: The ECHR Grand Chamber ruled on 12 May 2005 that while the death sentence was commuted, initial trial procedures had shortcomings including lack of adequate time for defense preparation and restrictions on lawyer-client communications. Turkey was ordered to pay Öcalan €35,000 in costs and expenses. The commutation aligned with abolition requirements.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.
relevant_rules: Protocol No. 6 to the European Convention on Human Rights
Protocol No. 13 to the European Convention on Human Rights
Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Soering v. United Kingdom (1989)
example_background: Jens Soering, a German national, was charged in the United States with capital murder for the killing of his girlfriend's parents in Virginia in 1985. Facing extradition from the UK where he was arrested, Soering argued that extradition to face possible execution would expose him to inhuman or degrading treatment. The case raised questions about the compatibility of extradition to jurisdictions retaining the death penalty with European human rights standards, given the lengthy wait on death row known as 'death row phenomenon.' The UK government initially proceeded with extradition assurances from the US.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR ruled on 7 July 1989 that extradition without assurances against the death penalty would violate Article 3 of the European Convention. The UK sought and obtained assurances from the US prosecutor that the death penalty would not be sought or imposed, allowing extradition to proceed under those conditions.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.
relevant_rules: Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights
Protocol No. 6 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Turkey's Abolition of Death Penalty (2004)
example_background: Turkey had maintained the death penalty in its legal system post-World War II, with the last execution carried out in 1984. As part of its EU accession process initiated in 1999, Turkey undertook constitutional reforms to align with European standards. In 2002, Turkey signed Protocol No. 13 abolishing the death penalty in all circumstances. The Grand National Assembly approved amendments removing capital punishment provisions from the Constitution. No executions occurred after 1984, and all death sentences were commuted to aggravated life imprisonment.
process_and_outcome: On 3 August 2004, Turkey ratified Protocol No. 13, fully abolishing the death penalty. This fulfilled Copenhagen political criteria for EU membership and was verified by the Committee of Ministers of the Council of Europe.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.
relevant_rules: Protocol No. 13 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Germany's Post-WWII Abolition of Death Penalty
example_background: Following the Nazi era where the death penalty was extensively used, West Germany abolished capital punishment in 1949 through its Basic Law (Grundgesetz), Article 102 stating 'Capital punishment is abolished.' This was a foundational human rights commitment influenced by Allied occupation and the emerging European human rights framework. East Germany retained it until 1987. After reunification in 1990, the unified Germany enshrined the abolition. No death sentences have been imposed or carried out since 1949 in West Germany.
process_and_outcome: The Basic Law entered force on 23 May 1949, immediately abolishing the death penalty nationwide. Germany ratified Protocol No. 6 in 1983 and Protocol No. 13 in 2003, reinforcing the constitutional ban.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.
relevant_rules: Article 102 of the German Basic Law

example_name: European Union Accession Condition for Poland (1997-2004)
example_background: Poland retained the death penalty in its penal code after regaining independence in 1989, though no executions occurred since 1988. As part of EU enlargement negotiations starting in 1998, Poland was required to meet the Copenhagen criteria including full respect for human rights, specifically abolition of the death penalty. In 1998, Poland imposed a moratorium and passed constitutional amendments. The last death sentences were commuted.
process_and_outcome: Poland ratified Protocol No. 6 in 1994 and Protocol No. 13 on 8 July 2004, fully abolishing the death penalty. This was a prerequisite certified for EU membership on 1 May 2004.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.

example_name: ECHR - Gardner v. United Kingdom (2013)
example_background: Michael Gardner, a British national, was sentenced to death in the United States for murder in Florida in 1993. The UK refused his repeated appeals for consular access and assurances. Gardner challenged the UK's failure to request non-execution assurances upon his extradition in 1993, arguing it violated the European Convention. The case highlighted ongoing tensions between extradition treaties and ECHR obligations post-Soering.
process_and_outcome: On 23 May 2013, the ECHR found no violation as the UK was not obliged to seek assurances at that time under prevailing case law, though it noted evolving standards. Gardner remained on death row; Florida imposed a moratorium on executions.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.

example_name: Council of Europe Protocol No. 13 Ratifications (2002-2004)
example_background: Protocol No. 13 to the ECHR, opened for signature in 2002, prohibits the death penalty in all circumstances, extending Protocol No. 6 (peacetime abolition). All 46 Council of Europe member states except Russia (suspended) and Azerbaijan (pending) ratified it. Key holdouts like Italy and Spain had already abolished it domestically. The protocol entered into force upon three ratifications in 2003, with widespread adoption by 2004 ensuring continent-wide abolition.
process_and_outcome: By 2004, 43 states ratified, achieving universal abolition in Europe (excluding Belarus). The Committee of Ministers monitored compliance.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.
involved_parties: Council of Europe Member States (ratifying entities)
Committee of Ministers of the Council of Europe

example_name: France's Abolition of Death Penalty (1981)
example_background: France executed 36 people between 1945 and 1976 under guillotine for crimes including murder and treason. Public opinion shifted after high-profile cases like Christian Ranucci in 1976. President François Mitterrand, upon election in 1981, prioritized abolition. Justice Minister Robert Badinter presented the bill, drawing on France's Enlightenment heritage and emerging European norms.
process_and_outcome: The National Assembly passed Law No. 81-908 on 9 October 1981, abolishing the death penalty with 310 votes to 147. France ratified Protocol No. 6 in 1983.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.

example_name: ECHR - Al-Saadoon and Mufdhi v. United Kingdom (2010)
example_background: Two Iraqi nationals, captured by British forces in Basra in 2003, were transferred to Iraqi custody in 2009 facing possible death sentences for murder. They claimed the UK knowingly exposed them to execution, violating ECHR Article 3 despite occurring outside Europe. The UK argued no jurisdiction extraterritorially.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR ruled on 2 March 2010 a violation of Article 3 for transferring them without death penalty assurances. The UK paid £88,000 compensation. Iraq later sentenced them to death, commuted.
source_rule: 2.   No one shall be condemned to the death penalty, or executed.
involved_parties: Bassam Abdullah Muhammad Al-Saadoon and Omar Hussain Muhammad Al-Badri (applicants)

example_name: Guantanamo Bay Detention Practices
example_background: The United States military operated a detention facility at Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, where individuals captured in connection with counter-terrorism operations were held. Detainees underwent interrogations involving prolonged isolation, sleep deprivation, exposure to extreme temperatures, and other measures applied over extended periods. These practices were part of a broader detention and interrogation program implemented following the events of September 11, 2001, affecting numerous individuals from various nationalities. The facility housed detainees for years without formal charges in some instances, with conditions documented through leaked reports and detainee testimonies describing physical and psychological pressures.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the International Committee of the Red Cross, U.S. Senate Select Committee on Intelligence, and European Court of Human Rights led to reports confirming the nature of the treatment. Legal challenges resulted in court rulings declaring certain practices unlawful, though the facility continued operations with policy adjustments. No direct fines were imposed, but international condemnation and policy reviews followed.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
involved_parties: International Committee of the Red Cross
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4 (Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment)
European Convention on Human Rights Article 3

example_name: CIA Rendition and Black Sites Program
example_background: The U.S. Central Intelligence Agency conducted a program of extraordinary rendition, transferring individuals suspected of terrorism to secret detention sites in countries including Poland, Lithuania, and Romania between 2002 and 2007. Detainees were subjected to enhanced interrogation techniques such as waterboarding, confinement in small boxes, and prolonged stress positions. The program involved over 100 detainees, with operations coordinated across multiple European locations, as revealed through declassified documents and investigations.
process_and_outcome: European Parliament inquiries and Strasbourg court proceedings found member states complicit. Poland and Lithuania faced convictions, ordered to pay damages to detainees. The U.S. Senate Intelligence Committee released a detailed report in 2014 documenting the program's operations and ineffectiveness.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4
European Convention on Human Rights Article 3

example_name: Abu Ghraib Prison Scandal
example_background: U.S. military personnel stationed at Abu Ghraib prison in Iraq engaged in abusive treatment of detainees from 2003 to 2004. Practices included physical beatings, sexual humiliation, forced nudity, use of dogs to intimidate, and electrocution threats. Over 1,000 detainee abuse allegations surfaced, with photographs documenting incidents involving Iraqi prisoners held without trial. The abuses occurred amid the Iraq War occupation.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Army investigations like the Taguba Report confirmed widespread abuses. Eleven soldiers were convicted by court-martial, receiving sentences including prison terms. Civilian contractors faced charges, though outcomes varied. Policy reforms were implemented in military detention procedures.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
involved_parties: International Committee of the Red Cross
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4 (analogous application)

example_name: Ireland v. United Kingdom (IRA Detention Cases)
example_background: During the Troubles in Northern Ireland in 1971, British authorities implemented internment without trial, detaining over 1,900 individuals suspected of IRA involvement. Detainees were subjected to techniques including hooding, wall-standing, subjection to noise, sleep deprivation, and deprivation of food and drink for periods up to seven days. These measures were applied systematically to extract information.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission of Human Rights found the practices amounted to torture; the European Court of Human Rights ruled them inhuman and degrading treatment in 1978. The UK government discontinued the techniques and paid compensation to victims.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 3
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4

example_name: Greek Military Junta Torture Cases
example_background: The Greek military regime from 1967 to 1974 detained political opponents in facilities where systematic torture occurred, including falaka (beating soles of feet), electric shocks, beatings, and sexual assault. Thousands were affected, with methods applied during interrogations to suppress opposition.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission of Human Rights investigated, leading to a 1969 report confirming torture. Greece withdrew from the Council of Europe; post-regime trials convicted junta leaders. The European Court awarded compensation in related cases.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 3
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4

example_name: Selmouni v. France (Police Custody Abuse)
example_background: In 1991, French police detained Abdelfattah Selmouni, a Franco-Moroccan national, subjecting him to severe beatings, burns from cigarettes, forced ingestion of substances, and sexual assaults during custody related to drug trafficking suspicions. The incident involved multiple officers over several hours.
process_and_outcome: French courts initially convicted officers lightly; the European Court of Human Rights ruled in 1999 that the treatment constituted torture, awarding damages and ordering France to reassess domestic penalties.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 3
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4

example_name: Turkey Human Rights Report on Diyarbakir Prison
example_background: In the 1980s and 1990s, Diyarbakir Prison in Turkey held Kurdish political prisoners subjected to systematic beatings, sexual torture, electric shocks, and Palestinian hanging (suspension by arms). Thousands of complaints documented patterns of abuse amid the Kurdish conflict.
process_and_outcome: European Court of Human Rights issued over 100 judgments against Turkey, awarding compensation. The European Committee for the Prevention of Torture inspected and recommended reforms, leading to partial prison overhauls.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
involved_parties: European Committee for the Prevention of Torture
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 3
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4

example_name: EU Anti-Torture Regulation Implementation Monitoring
example_background: The European Union established mechanisms under its anti-torture regulation to monitor member states' compliance with prohibitions on torture and degrading treatment, including visits to detention centers and support for victims. Annual reports detail inspections and assistance programs across EU countries.
process_and_outcome: The European Committee for the Prevention of Torture conducts unannounced visits, issues recommendations adopted by states, and tracks improvements. No fines, but sustained compliance enhancements reported in sectors like prisons and police stations.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
involved_parties: European Committee for the Prevention of Torture
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4

example_name: Council of Europe Anti-Torture Committee Visits
example_background: The CPT conducts regular visits to places of detention in 47 Council of Europe member states, examining treatment of persons deprived of liberty. Reports cover police stations, prisons, and psychiatric facilities, identifying risks and good practices in preventing ill-treatment.
process_and_outcome: Over 400 visits since 1989 resulted in published reports with recommendations, most implemented by states. Examples include improved safeguards in France and Italy following specific findings.
source_rule: Right to the integrity of the person
relevant_rules: European Convention for the Prevention of Torture
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4

example_name: HeLa Cells Origin and Consent Issues
example_background: In 1951, cells were taken from Henrietta Lacks, a patient at Johns Hopkins Hospital, during treatment for cervical cancer without her knowledge or consent. These cells, known as HeLa cells, became the first immortalized human cell line and have been used extensively in medical research, including development of polio vaccine and cancer studies. The family was unaware of the cell line's existence and commercial use for decades, raising questions about patient rights and tissue ownership in biological research.
process_and_outcome: In 2013, the Lacks family reached an agreement with the National Institutes of Health for representation on oversight committees for HeLa genome data access. Johns Hopkins issued statements acknowledging the contributions and ethical concerns. No formal fines were imposed, but it led to policy changes on informed consent for biospecimens.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:

example_name: Cambridge Analytica and 23andMe Data Sharing
example_background: In 2018, reports emerged that 23andMe, a genetic testing company, had partnerships sharing anonymized genetic data with pharmaceutical firms and research entities. Separately, the Cambridge Analytica scandal involved harvesting Facebook data without explicit consent, though not directly biological; analogous issues arose in biotech with genetic data misuse concerns in Europe.
process_and_outcome: UK Information Commissioner's Office fined Facebook £500,000 for data protection failures. 23andMe faced FTC settlement in 2023 for privacy lapses in data sharing. Class action lawsuits ongoing for genetic data handling.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:
relevant_rules: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)

example_name: Pfizer GDPR Fine for Clinical Trial Data
example_background: In 2021, Pfizer's Irish subsidiary was investigated for processing personal data of clinical trial participants without fully compliant consent mechanisms under EU data protection rules. The case involved handling health data from thousands of participants in vaccine trials across Europe, where documentation of consent and data minimization was questioned.
process_and_outcome: Irish Data Protection Commission imposed a €17.7 million fine on Pfizer for GDPR violations related to clinical trial data processing. Pfizer implemented enhanced compliance measures and appealed aspects of the decision.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:

example_name: EMA COVID-19 Data Access Policy
example_background: The European Medicines Agency established a policy in 2021 for public access to clinical trial data during the COVID-19 pandemic, ensuring transparency while protecting patient rights through redaction of personal data and respecting foundational rights in biological research submissions.
process_and_outcome: EMA published redacted clinical study reports for authorized vaccines, balancing transparency with privacy. No enforcement actions; recognized as best practice for regulatory compliance in medicine.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 8)

example_name: Helsinki Declaration Violations in TGN1412 Trial
example_background: In 2006, a Phase I clinical trial of TGN1412 (CD28-SuperMAB) at Northwick Park Hospital in the UK resulted in severe adverse reactions in six healthy volunteers, who developed multi-organ failure. The trial proceeded despite preclinical concerns, with issues in risk assessment and informed consent processes for biological agents.
process_and_outcome: MHRA investigation led to suspension of the trial, regulatory reforms, and a £10 million compensation fund for participants. Company TeGenero filed for bankruptcy; stricter UK trial regulations implemented.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:

example_name: Novo Nordisk GDPR Clinical Data Fine
example_background: In 2021, Novo Nordisk was fined by the Danish Data Protection Agency for processing personal health data of over 800,000 individuals in diabetes studies without adequate legal basis or consent documentation, involving biological and medical data across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: Danish DPA imposed DKK 8.1 million (€1.1 million) fine. Novo Nordisk revised data handling practices and enhanced consent procedures for future research.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:

example_name: UK Biobank Ethical Framework
example_background: UK Biobank, established in 2006, collects biological samples and data from 500,000 UK participants for medical research, with comprehensive informed consent processes, data access controls, and ethics oversight to respect participant rights in large-scale biological studies.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing operations with ethical approvals from research ethics committees. Over 20,000 researchers accessed data compliantly; model for global biobanks with no major enforcement actions.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:
involved_parties: North West Multi-Centre Research Ethics Committee

example_name: Sanofi Pasteur MSD GDPR Fine
example_background: In 2022, the French data protection authority investigated Sanofi Pasteur MSD for storing health data of 300,000 patients from clinical trials without proper consent or anonymization, spanning biological research activities in France.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed €1.45 million fine. Sanofi implemented corrective measures including data deletion and process audits.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:
involved_parties: French CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Estonian Gene Bank Compliance Model
example_background: Estonian Biocentre Human Genome Project, launched in 2000, created a national gene bank with DNA samples from 200,000 participants, incorporating strict informed consent, opt-out options, and data protection aligned with EU foundational rights for biological research.
process_and_outcome: Successfully used in genomics research with ethical board oversight. Expanded partnerships while maintaining compliance; cited as best practice in EU reports.
source_rule: 2.   In the fields of medicine and biology, the following must be respected in particular:

example_name: Google GDPR Fine for Advertising Personalization
example_background: Google was found to have processed personal data of European users for personalized advertising without a valid legal basis or transparent consent. The French data protection authority examined complaints about Android users' settings where choices for ad personalization were not sufficiently clear or granular. Google's consent forms were pre-ticked or bundled, making it difficult for users to understand and control the processing of their location data, search history, and other identifiers. This affected approximately 49.5 million French users and involved complex layered consent mechanisms that did not meet transparency requirements.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL imposed a €50 million fine on Google in January 2019, the first major GDPR enforcement action. Google appealed but lost subsequent court challenges. The decision prompted Google to revise its consent mechanisms across the EU.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to respect for his or her physical and mental integrity.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
relevant_rules: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 6, 7, 12

example_name: British Airways GDPR Breach
example_background: In 2018, hackers accessed the personal data of around 400,000 British Airways customers through a vulnerability in the website's chat system. The breach exposed names, addresses, payment card details, and travel itineraries. The attack lasted several weeks undetected, during which data was siphoned off. British Airways' security measures were deemed insufficient, particularly in monitoring and responding to suspicious activity on their payment processing pages.
process_and_outcome: The UK's ICO proposed a £183 million fine in 2019, later reduced to £20 million in 2020 after appeal. British Airways implemented enhanced security protocols and compensated affected customers.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to respect for his or her physical and mental integrity.
relevant_rules: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 5, 32, 33

example_name: Marriott International Starwood Data Breach
example_background: Marriott acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016 but did not fully integrate or audit its IT systems until 2018, when a breach affecting 500 million guests was discovered. Attackers had been present in Starwood's reservation database since 2014, accessing passport numbers, payment card details, and travel histories. Encryption was partial, and monitoring systems failed to detect the intrusion for four years, compromising guests' sensitive personal information across multiple countries.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020 (reduced from £99 million). The company faced class-action lawsuits in the US, settling for $52 million, and notified affected individuals worldwide.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to respect for his or her physical and mental integrity.

example_name: Apple's App Tracking Transparency (ATT) Feature
example_background: Apple introduced the App Tracking Transparency framework in iOS 14.5 in 2021, requiring apps to obtain explicit user permission before tracking them across apps and websites via the Identifier for Advertisers (IDFA). This addressed concerns over pervasive tracking that collected behavioral data without clear consent, affecting mental privacy by preventing undisclosed profiling. Users gained control over their data through a system-wide prompt, significantly reducing cross-app tracking rates.
process_and_outcome: The feature led to a 70-80% opt-out rate from tracking, prompting industry-wide changes. No fines were imposed on Apple; instead, it set a compliance standard influencing competitors like Google.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to respect for his or her physical and mental integrity.
relevant_rules: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 4, 7, 21 (right to object)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Cookie Consent Fine
example_background: Meta's 'Like' button and Facebook Pixel on third-party websites used cookies to track users' browsing behavior across the internet without first obtaining consent. This 'forced tracking' occurred even for non-logged-in visitors, collecting data on preferences and movements to build detailed profiles, bypassing cookie banners and consent requirements.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL fined Meta €60 million in 2022. Similar fines were issued across EU authorities totaling over €200 million. Meta updated its tracking technologies to comply with consent rules.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to respect for his or her physical and mental integrity.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: H&M GDPR Employee Data Fine
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg store gathered extensive personal data from employees during onboarding and 'welcome meetings,' including health data, religious beliefs, pregnancies, and family issues. This data was stored in an HR Excel sheet accessible to managers and used to tailor performance discussions, without employees' explicit knowledge or consent, affecting their mental privacy and autonomy at work.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg's data protection authority fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2023. H&M discontinued the practice and enhanced data governance policies.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to respect for his or her physical and mental integrity.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information
relevant_rules: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 5, 9, 88

example_name: Microsoft Privacy Dashboard Compliance
example_background: Microsoft developed the Privacy Dashboard within its account settings, allowing users to view, manage, and delete their personal data such as search history, location data, and activity across Microsoft services. This tool provides granular controls, automated retention limits, and diagnostics for data processing, ensuring transparency and user autonomy over their digital footprint.
process_and_outcome: Implemented since 2018, it has been praised in GDPR audits with no major fines issued to Microsoft for related issues. It serves as a model for compliance in tech.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to respect for his or her physical and mental integrity.
relevant_rules: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 12, 15, 17, 21

example_name: Buck v. Bell Supreme Court Decision
example_background: In the 1920s, Virginia enacted laws authorizing the compulsory sterilization of individuals deemed feebleminded or otherwise unfit, as part of a broader eugenics movement in the United States. Carrie Buck, a young woman institutionalized at the Virginia Colony for Epileptics and Feeble Minded, was identified as suitable for sterilization under the state's 1924 Eugenical Sterilization Act. The case arose after Buck gave birth to a child out of wedlock and was committed to the institution, where she was classified as having hereditary mental defects. Her mother had also been institutionalized, and the state argued that sterilizing Buck would prevent the transmission of undesirable traits to future generations. The Supreme Court reviewed whether this procedure violated Buck's constitutional rights, amid a national trend where over 30 states had similar sterilization statutes, influenced by eugenics advocates promoting population improvement through selective reproduction control.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Supreme Court upheld the Virginia statute in a 8-1 decision in 1927, with Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes writing the majority opinion stating that the procedure served the public welfare. This ruling validated forced sterilizations across the U.S., leading to approximately 60,000 procedures nationwide until the practice waned post-World War II. Virginia continued sterilizations until 1974, and the state issued an apology and compensation in 2002.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;

example_name: Nazi Germany's Law for the Prevention of Hereditarily Diseased Offspring
example_background: Following the rise of the National Socialist regime in 1933, Germany implemented a systematic program of eugenics under the Gesetz zur Verhütung erbkranken Nachwuchses, which targeted individuals with conditions such as schizophrenia, epilepsy, Huntington's chorea, hereditary blindness, deafness, severe alcoholism, and feeblemindedness. Over 400 Hereditary Health Courts were established to review cases, often based on minimal medical evidence or denunciations. Between 1934 and 1945, approximately 400,000 people were sterilized, with procedures performed in state hospitals. This was part of a larger ideology promoting racial hygiene, drawing from earlier U.S. and European eugenics models but expanded coercively, affecting Jews, Roma, disabled persons, and others deemed genetically inferior.
process_and_outcome: The program resulted in over 400,000 forced sterilizations and influenced the subsequent euthanasia program (Aktion T4), which killed around 200,000 disabled individuals. Post-war Nuremberg Trials examined these actions as crimes against humanity, leading to convictions of key figures like Karl Brandt. The laws were repealed in 1945 by Allied authorities.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;
relevant_rules: Law for the Prevention of Hereditarily Diseased Offspring (1933)

example_name: Peru Forced Sterilization Program (1990s)
example_background: During Alberto Fujimori's presidency from 1990 to 2000, Peru's Ministry of Health conducted a family planning program that devolved into widespread coerced sterilizations targeting poor, rural indigenous women, particularly Quechua speakers. Officially aimed at reducing poverty through population control, the program sterilized over 300,000 individuals, with at least 200,000 women affected between 1995 and 1998. Many procedures were performed without informed consent, using misleading information, threats of benefit denial, or physical force in mobile sterilization campaigns. Victims reported complications including infections, hemorrhaging, and deaths, amid quotas pressuring health workers to meet targets.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the Peruvian Congress in 2002 documented abuses, leading to a Truth and Reconciliation Committee report. Fujimori was convicted in 2009 for crimes against humanity related to the program, receiving a 25-year sentence (later adjusted). In 2021, Peru's government pledged reparations to over 20,000 victims.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;
involved_parties: Congreso de la República del Perú
relevant_rules: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights

example_name: North Carolina Eugenics Sterilization Program
example_background: North Carolina's Eugenics Board, established in 1929 under the state's sterilization law, authorized procedures for individuals labeled as mentally retarded, mentally ill, blind, or promiscuous, with a focus on welfare recipients and minorities. From 1929 to 1974, over 7,600 people were sterilized, comprising about 25% of U.S. total eugenic sterilizations. The program intensified in the 1940s-1960s, using social worker recommendations and minimal due process. Cases included children as young as 9 and women sterilized post-childbirth without full consent, justified as preventing public burden from hereditary unfitness.
process_and_outcome: The program ended in 1974; a 2002 task force recommended compensation. In 2013, North Carolina enacted legislation providing $10 million for victim payments, with 220 eligible claimants receiving up to $50,000 each by 2015.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;
involved_parties: Office of Justice for Sterilization Victims

example_name: California State Sterilization Program
example_background: California's 1909 sterilization law, amended multiple times, empowered the State Board of Charities and Corrections and later institutions like Patton State Hospital to sterilize inmates deemed likely to procreate defective offspring. From 1909 to 1979, California performed 20,000 procedures, the highest in the U.S., targeting the poor, disabled, immigrants, and minorities. Influenced by eugenicists like David Starr Jordan, the program was funded partly by private foundations and aligned with Progressive Era social policies. Procedures often occurred without court orders or adequate consent, with records showing ethnic disproportionality.
process_and_outcome: The program continued until Governor Jerry Brown signed its repeal in 1979. A 2003 legislative apology acknowledged the abuses, and in 2021, California announced $125 million in compensation for living victims.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;

example_name: Swedish Sterilization Act of 1934
example_background: Sweden's Law on Preventive Measures Against Mental Deficiency and Other Conditions passed in 1934 allowed sterilization of individuals with intellectual disabilities, mental illness, or social maladjustment, without always requiring consent. Administered by the National Board of Health, approximately 63,000 people were sterilized between 1934 and 1976, many involuntarily, targeting the poor, Roma, and those deemed socially inadequate. The program was rooted in Nordic eugenics research and continued longer than in most countries, with parliamentary oversight but limited appeals.
process_and_outcome: The law was repealed in 1976. A 1999 government commission documented the extent, leading to a 2012 apology and payments of 175,000 SEK to surviving victims.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;

example_name: India Emergency Period Sterilization Campaign (1975-1977)
example_background: During the national Emergency declared by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi, a mass sterilization drive was launched under Sanjay Gandhi's leadership to curb population growth. Government officials set quotas for vasectomies, often coercing poor men through incentives like cash, food rations, or threats of job/benefit loss. Over 6 million sterilizations occurred in two years, with camps set up in rural areas lacking hygiene standards. Many procedures were performed on illiterate individuals without proper consent, leading to infections and deaths.
process_and_outcome: The Emergency ended in 1977 with Gandhi's electoral defeat, partly due to backlash. Subsequent inquiries confirmed excesses, though no direct prosecutions occurred; policy shifted to voluntary family planning.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;
relevant_rules: Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act implications

example_name: European Court of Human Rights - Case of Golder v. UK (Precedent Influence)
example_background: While not a direct eugenics case, the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (Article 3) explicitly prohibits eugenic practices for person selection, building on ECHR jurisprudence protecting dignity and bodily integrity. This framework has been applied in challenges to genetic selection technologies. For instance, in debates over preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD), countries like France banned certain uses to avoid eugenic selection.
process_and_outcome: France's 2011 bioethics law review upheld bans on embryo selection for non-medical traits, aligning with EU Foundation rights. No fines, but reinforced compliance through legislative adherence.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;
relevant_rules: Oviedo Convention on Human Rights and Biomedicine

example_name: Council of Europe - Prohibition of Cloning Embryos for Selection
example_background: The Council of Europe's 1997 Convention on Human Rights and Biomedicine (Oviedo Convention) prohibits practices aimed at selecting persons based on germline genetic modifications or embryo cloning, influencing EU policies. Ratifying states implemented bans on reproductive cloning and eugenic embryo selection to protect human dignity.
process_and_outcome: As of 2023, 29 countries ratified, with ongoing monitoring ensuring no violations; exemplary compliance in germline editing bans post-CRISPR advancements.
source_rule: (b) the prohibition of eugenic practices, in particular those aiming at the selection of persons;

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Consent Fines by Irish DPC
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated Meta Platforms Ireland Limited's practices for obtaining consent for processing personal data of EU Facebook users for behavioral advertising. The probe focused on the 'consent or pay' model introduced in 2022, where users had to either pay a monthly fee or consent to extensive data tracking and personalized ads. Additionally, prior mechanisms involved cookie banners with pre-selected options and insufficient information about data retention and sharing with third parties. The investigation covered data processing from 2018 onwards and found that consents were not freely given due to the economic disincentive of payment.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion for unlawful data transfers to the US but also addressed consent issues in separate proceedings, leading to orders for compliance changes. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued binding decisions in related cases. Meta appealed several decisions and modified its consent flows.
source_rule: (a) the free and informed consent of the person concerned, according to the procedures laid down by law;
relevant_rules: EU GDPR Article 7 (free and informed consent)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Fine by CNIL
example_background: TikTok faced scrutiny over its handling of children's personal data in France. The CNIL investigation revealed that the platform's age verification was inadequate, allowing children under 15 to create accounts without parental consent where required. Location data was collected by default without obtaining proper consent from minors or their parents. Cookie consent banners were non-compliant, lacking clear refusal options equivalent to acceptance. Data processing for advertising purposes did not provide sufficient information to young users, compromising the informed nature of consent.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €15 million fine in 2023 following complaints and its own investigations. TikTok was ordered to implement corrective measures, including improved age gates and parental controls. The company stated it would appeal certain aspects.
source_rule: (a) the free and informed consent of the person concerned, according to the procedures laid down by law;
relevant_rules: EU GDPR Article 7 and Article 8 (consent for children)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8 and Article 24 (rights of the child)

example_name: Amazon France Logistiques GDPR Fine
example_background: Amazon's French logistics subsidiary was investigated for its employee monitoring practices using data from badge swipes, network connections, and scanner usage. The CNIL found that workers were not adequately informed about the data processing, and consent was not obtained for certain tracking purposes. The system generated performance scores shared with managers without explicit agreement, affecting career progression. Transparency notices were buried in internal portals, making informed consent impractical.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, CNIL fined Amazon France Logistiques €32 million, one of the largest GDPR fines for workplace data issues. The decision followed worker complaints and union referrals. Amazon was required to revise its monitoring policies and enhance employee notifications.
source_rule: (a) the free and informed consent of the person concerned, according to the procedures laid down by law;
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Whisk Media GDPR Children's Consent Fine
example_background: Whisk Media Group operated kid-focused apps that collected personal data from children without obtaining verifiable parental consent. Eight apps targeted users under 13, using tracking technologies for analytics and ads. Age gates were easily bypassed by children entering random dates, and no mechanisms existed for parents to authorize data processing. Data was shared with third parties without disclosure, undermining any notion of informed consent.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Whisk £180,000 in 2020 after an investigation prompted by a complaint. The apps were removed from stores, and the company entered administration. This was the first fine under GDPR/PECR for children's privacy breaches.
source_rule: (a) the free and informed consent of the person concerned, according to the procedures laid down by law;
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 24 (child rights)
UK Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations (PECR)

example_name: ProtonMail End-to-End Encryption Consent Practices
example_background: ProtonMail, a privacy-focused email service based in Switzerland, implements end-to-end encryption by default and zero-access encryption for stored data. Users explicitly consent to data handling during onboarding with clear disclosures about what data is collected (minimal metadata) and encryption protections. Consent is granular, with options for two-factor authentication and recovery methods. The service complies with EU standards despite operating outside the EU, providing transparency reports and open-source clients.
process_and_outcome: Proton has faced no major fines and received positive audits from regulators and third parties. It successfully navigated GDPR adequacy decisions for Swiss data transfers and is often cited in privacy best practice guides.
source_rule: (a) the free and informed consent of the person concerned, according to the procedures laid down by law;
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8
Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP)

example_name: British Airways GDPR Fine by ICO
example_background: In 2018, hackers accessed personal data of 400,000 British Airways customers via a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack. The ICO investigated BA's consent and transparency practices, finding that customers were not adequately informed about data security measures or given clear consent for processing payment details. Cookie management and third-party scripts lacked proper consent banners, allowing unauthorized tracking.
process_and_outcome: ICO initially planned a £183 million fine in 2020, reduced to £20 million on appeal in 2021. BA implemented enhanced security and consent mechanisms. The case highlighted supply chain vulnerabilities.
source_rule: (a) the free and informed consent of the person concerned, according to the procedures laid down by law;
relevant_rules: EU GDPR Article 7 and Article 32 (consent and security)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Hecht v. Superior Court (California Body Parts Sale Case)
example_background: In 2005, California resident Michelle Hecht filed a lawsuit against a network of tissue banks and brokers after her deceased husband's body was harvested for corneas and bone tissue without her full informed consent. The defendants, including tissue procurement organizations and distributors, processed and sold the body parts to medical companies for implantation and research. Hecht alleged that the defendants obtained consent through misleading means and proceeded to extract and commercialize the tissues, generating revenue through sales exceeding $100,000 per body in some cases. The case highlighted practices where funeral homes collaborated with non-transplant anatomic donation organizations (NTADOs) to obtain bodies under the guise of donation, but then facilitated their segmentation into marketable components like skin, bones, and tendons for profit-driven distribution to pharmaceutical firms and tissue banks across the United States.
process_and_outcome: The Superior Court ruled that the commercialization of body parts constituted an unlawful interference with the right of sepulcher, awarding Hecht $1.795 million in damages including punitive awards. The decision set precedent limiting commercial traffic in human remains, leading to increased regulatory oversight on tissue handling practices in California.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;
relevant_rules: California Health & Safety Code § 7050 et seq.

example_name: Biomedical Tissue Services Scandal
example_background: Between 2002 and 2005, Biomedical Tissue Services (BTS) in New Jersey operated a black-market operation acquiring over 1,000 human bodies from unscrupulous funeral directors. Bodies were obtained using falsified consent forms from families who believed they were donating for science or education. BTS then dismembered the cadavers, processing skin, bones, tendons, and heart valves into salable tissues distributed to companies for transplant, research, and cosmetics production. Profits reached millions, with tissues sold at markups from $300 per square foot of skin to $10,000 per body. The scheme involved shipping parts nationwide and internationally, evading oversight by misrepresenting the bodies as unclaimed or donated.
process_and_outcome: FBI raids in 2005 uncovered the operation, leading to criminal charges against BTS owner Michael Mastromarino and associates for fraud and body stealing. Mastromarino was sentenced to 12 years in prison, the company was shut down, and civil settlements exceeded $10 million. The scandal prompted FDA and state investigations into tissue safety, resulting in recalls of contaminated implants.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;

example_name: EU Directive 2004/23/EC Enforcement Against Italian Tissue Bank
example_background: In 2012, Italian authorities investigated a tissue bank in northern Italy for engaging in unauthorized commercialization of human heart valves and skin grafts. The facility sourced tissues from deceased donors through hospital partnerships, processed them, and sold them to surgical centers and pharmaceutical firms at prices reflecting production costs plus profit margins. Operations involved over 500 units annually, with revenues undisclosed but estimated in the six figures. The bank operated under the guise of cost recovery but exceeded allowable reimbursements, distributing tissues across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Ministry of Health suspended the bank's license, imposed fines totaling €250,000, and mandated destruction of non-compliant inventory. The case led to stricter national implementation of EU tissue directives, with audits across similar facilities.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;
relevant_rules: EU Directive 2004/23/EC on human tissues and cells

example_name: Spanish Blood Products Commercialization Case (2015)
example_background: A private clinic network in Spain sourced plasma from paid donors, processed it into albumin and immunoglobulins, and marketed the products to hospitals and exporters for profit. Despite regulations prohibiting financial incentives for body products, the clinics offered compensation disguised as travel reimbursements, handling thousands of liters annually and generating revenues over €5 million. Products were sold commercially, with Spain becoming a net exporter of plasma derivatives amid shortages in other EU countries.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish Agency of Medicines and Medical Devices (AEMPS) conducted inspections, revoked licenses, and fined the operators €1.2 million. Criminal proceedings resulted in suspended sentences and clinic closures, prompting EU-wide reviews of plasma economics.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 3)

example_name: German Lindner Company Kidney Trafficking Investigation (2008)
example_background: Germany's Lindner company and associated clinics facilitated the procurement of kidneys from living donors in Eastern Europe, transporting them to recipients in Germany for transplantation. Payments were made to donors ranging from €5,000 to €10,000, disguised as compensation for medical expenses. Over 20 transplants occurred, with brokers earning fees per procedure. The operation blurred lines between altruistic donation and commerce, sourcing organs amid waiting lists.
process_and_outcome: Prosecutors charged executives with illegal organ trade; the company paid €500,000 fine and ceased operations. No prison sentences due to lack of direct coercion evidence, but the case influenced stricter EU organ laws.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;
relevant_rules: Additional Protocol to the Convention on Human Rights and Biomedicine

example_name: Eurotransplant Compliance Model
example_background: Eurotransplant, a nonprofit consortium managing organ allocation across eight EU countries including Germany, Austria, and the Netherlands, maintains a centralized system for kidneys, livers, hearts, and lungs from deceased donors. Allocation follows medical urgency and match criteria without payments or commercial elements. In 2022, it facilitated over 8,000 transplants, funded solely by member states and public grants, ensuring no financial gain from human materials. Strict auditing prevents any commodification.
process_and_outcome: Annual audits by national authorities confirm adherence, with zero violations recorded. The model serves as a benchmark, reducing wait times by 15% through efficient non-commercial exchange.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;

example_name: UK Human Tissue Authority (HTA) Oversight of NHS Blood and Transplant
example_background: NHS Blood and Transplant (NHSBT), under HTA licensing, manages tissue donation across the UK, processing corneas, skin, and bones from consented deceased donors. All activities recover only direct costs for procurement, processing, and transport, with no profit margins. In 2023, NHSBT provided 1,500 corneas and 20,000 skin grafts free to hospitals, supported by public funding and transparent pricing schedules published annually.
process_and_outcome: HTA inspections consistently rate NHSBT fully compliant, with public reports praising cost-recovery model. No enforcement actions in 20+ years of operation.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;

example_name: French Agence de la Biomédecine Plasma Collection Practices
example_background: France's Agence de la Biomédecine oversees public plasma collection centers where donors receive flat-fee reimbursements strictly limited to inconvenience and travel costs (around €30 per donation). Plasma is fractionated into medicines like immunoglobulins by licensed labs, with sales revenues reinvested into the public health system. No private profit occurs; 2022 collections yielded 8 million donations, supplying 70% of national needs without commercialization of the body itself.
process_and_outcome: Biennial audits by Ministry of Health affirm compliance, with model exported as EU best practice. Avoided penalties through rigorous cost controls.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;
involved_parties: French Ministry of Solidarity and Health
relevant_rules: Code de la Santé Publique (Articles L1221 et seq.)

example_name: ODT Italy National Transplant Center Operations
example_background: The Italian National Transplant Center (CNT) coordinates organ and tissue procurement through a public network of hospitals, allocating materials based on clinical priority without any financial transactions. Tissues like corneas and heart valves are provided cost-free to recipients, with logistics funded by regional health services. In 2022, CNT managed 3,700 transplants and thousands of tissue grafts, emphasizing donation as a gift of life.
process_and_outcome: ISO-certified processes pass annual reviews by AIFA and Ministry of Health, recognized by WHO as exemplary non-commercial model.
source_rule: (c) the prohibition on making the human body and its parts as such a source of financial gain;

example_name: He Jiankui CRISPR Babies Experiment
example_background: In 2018, Chinese biophysicist He Jiankui announced the birth of twin girls whose genomes had been edited using CRISPR-Cas9 technology to disable the CCR5 gene, with the stated goal of conferring resistance to HIV infection. The embryos were created through in vitro fertilization, and gene editing occurred at the single-cell embryo stage before implantation. A third child was later confirmed to have been part of the procedure. The work was conducted at a private lab affiliated with the Southern University of Science and Technology in Shenzhen, without prior ethics approval from institutional review boards or government authorities. Participants were recruited from HIV-positive fathers, and the procedure involved injecting Cas9 protein and guide RNA into embryos. The announcement was made via a YouTube video and press conference at the Second International Summit on Human Genome Editing in Hong Kong, sparking global debate on germline editing. While not reproductive cloning, the case involved heritable genetic modification of human embryos, raising concerns over safety, consent, and ethical boundaries in human reproductive technologies.
process_and_outcome: Chinese authorities launched an investigation immediately after the announcement. He Jiankui was detained, and in December 2019, a Guangzhou court sentenced him to three years in prison and a fine of 3 million yuan for illegal medical practice. Two collaborators received lesser sentences and fines. The Southern University of Science and Technology terminated his affiliation, and international scientific bodies issued moratoriums on germline editing. Follow-up studies in 2019 confirmed the edits were mosaic, with unintended off-target mutations detected.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
involved_parties: Southern University of Science and Technology
Chinese Ministry of Science and Technology
relevant_rules: Chinese Regulations on Human Assisted Reproductive Technology (2003)
Provisions for the Ethical Review of Biomedical Research Involving Humans (China)
UNESCO Universal Declaration on the Human Genome and Human Rights
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (Article 3 - Right to Integrity of the Person)

example_name: Huang et al. Human Embryo Cloning Attempt (2001)
example_background: In November 2001, researchers led by Huang Chong of Sun Yat-sen University in Guangzhou, China, claimed to have created the world's first cloned human embryo using somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT). They transferred the nucleus from a human cumulus cell into an enucleated oocyte, achieving development to the 4-6 cell stage. The experiment was described in a press conference and a submission to the journal Fertility and Sterility, though not peer-reviewed at the time. The stated purpose was to produce embryonic stem cells for therapeutic research, not implantation. The announcement drew widespread condemnation for bypassing ethical guidelines and risking the creation of cloned humans. Subsequent analysis questioned the validity of the claims due to lack of independent verification and methodological details. This occurred amid global debates following Dolly the sheep's cloning in 1996, with China lacking specific bans on human cloning at the time.
process_and_outcome: No formal legal action was taken due to absence of specific anti-cloning laws in China then, but the journal rejected the paper. Chinese authorities issued a temporary moratorium on human cloning research. Internationally, it prompted calls for global standards. The claims were later discredited as unverifiable by experts.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
relevant_rules: Council of Europe Convention on Human Rights and Biomedicine (Oviedo Convention)

example_name: Advanced Cell Technology Human Cloning Embryos (2001)
example_background: In November 2001, Advanced Cell Technology (ACT), a Massachusetts-based biotech company, reported creating human embryos through parthenogenesis and SCNT for stem cell research. They used SCNT by inserting human leg cell nuclei into enucleated bovine eggs, achieving hybrid embryos that developed to 12 cells before arrest. Separately, they stimulated human eggs to develop parthenogenetically to 4-6 cells. The work aimed at deriving patient-matched embryonic stem cells without destroying viable embryos. Funded privately, the experiments were disclosed in a press release and submitted to Science (later published in Cloning & Stem Cells). The company emphasized no intent for reproductive use and compliance with U.S. guidelines prohibiting federal funding for embryo creation or destruction.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions occurred as the embryos were not implanted or developed beyond early stages. The work contributed to debates leading to U.S. cloning bans. ACT continued therapeutic cloning research until shifting focus.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
relevant_rules: United Nations Declaration on Human Cloning (2005)

example_name: Briggs Human Embryo Cloning Claim (1998)
example_background: In 1998, Advanced Fertility Center director Richard Seed announced plans to clone humans using SCNT, inspired by Dolly. Separately, British researcher Alan Briggs claimed in a press release to have cloned a human embryo at his private lab in Ireland, using cumulus cell nuclei transferred to enucleated eggs, reaching the 8-cell stage. Briggs stated the goal was infertility treatment research, not birth. The claim lacked peer review or evidence, occurring amid post-Dolly hype. No institutional oversight was involved, and it was reported by tabloids before scientific scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: Claims were dismissed as unsubstantiated hoaxes by the scientific community. No legal proceedings ensued, but it heightened calls for regulation. Ireland later enacted cloning bans.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
relevant_rules: Additional Protocol to the Oviedo Convention on Cloning (1998)

example_name: EU Funding Ban on Human Cloning Research
example_background: The European Union has maintained strict prohibitions on funding research involving reproductive human cloning since the 1997 Council of Europe Protocol. In practice, the European Commission enforces this through Framework Programmes for Research (e.g., Horizon 2020, Horizon Europe). Applications proposing SCNT for reproductive purposes or germline modifications leading to cloning are rejected during ethical reviews by the European Commission’s Ethics Committee. For instance, in 2014, a proposed project on embryo models was denied funding for including cloning-like techniques. This ensures EU taxpayer funds do not support activities contravening foundational rights.
process_and_outcome: Ethical reviews routinely reject non-compliant proposals, upholding the ban without incidents of funding violations. Successful projects focus on non-reproductive alternatives like iPS cells.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
relevant_rules: Oviedo Convention Additional Protocol on Cloning

example_name: UK HFEA Ban on Reproductive Cloning
example_background: The UK's Human Fertilisation and Embryology Authority (HFEA) licenses all embryo research and IVF. Since the 1990 Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act, reproductive cloning has been prohibited, with licenses strictly limited to therapeutic cloning (e.g., SCNT for stem cells) under rigorous oversight. In 2004, the Act was amended to explicitly ban placing cloned embryos in a woman. HFEA monitors compliance through inspections and annual reporting. No licensed entity has attempted reproductive cloning; violations would revoke licenses.
process_and_outcome: HFEA has approved over 100 therapeutic cloning projects while rejecting any reproductive intent. Regular audits confirm adherence, with no enforcement cases.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
relevant_rules: Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 1990 (as amended)

example_name: Council of Europe Oviedo Protocol Enforcement
example_background: The 1998 Additional Protocol to the Convention on Human Rights and Biomedicine prohibits reproductive cloning across 30+ ratifying states. Compliance is monitored through national implementations and committee reports. For example, France's 2011 bioethics laws explicitly ban cloning, with penalties up to 15 years imprisonment. No violations have been prosecuted, but the framework has prevented research drift, as seen in halted private initiatives post-Dolly.
process_and_outcome: Periodic reports by the Council of Europe confirm uniform compliance. National courts uphold bans in related cases, deterring attempts.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
relevant_rules: Additional Protocol to Oviedo Convention on Prohibition of Cloning Human Beings

example_name: Clonaid Cloning Claims (2002)
example_background: Clonaid, a company founded by the Raëlian Movement, announced in December 2002 the birth of 'Eve,' the first cloned human baby, via SCNT. They claimed to have conducted the procedure in a secret lab, funded by believers, with additional clones promised. No evidence, DNA tests, or photos were provided despite offers of verification. The announcement followed years of promotion, including partnerships with fertility clinics, amid global bans.
process_and_outcome: FDA issued a cease-and-desist to Clonaid CEO Brigitte Boisselier. Investigations by U.S. and Bahamian authorities found no evidence. Claims deemed a hoax; company dissolved publicity.
source_rule: (d) the prohibition of the reproductive cloning of human beings.
relevant_rules: U.S. Food and Drug and Cosmetic Act (embryo research)

example_name: CIA Enhanced Interrogation Program
example_background: Following the September 11, 2001 attacks, the Central Intelligence Agency implemented a program of enhanced interrogation techniques at secret detention sites known as black sites. These techniques included waterboarding, stress positions, sleep deprivation exceeding 180 hours, confinement in small boxes, and use of insects in confinement spaces. The program was applied to at least 119 detainees, with 39 subjected to waterboarding multiple times. Medical personnel monitored sessions to prevent death, and legal memos from the Department of Justice's Office of Legal Counsel provided interpretations that these methods did not constitute torture under U.S. law. The practices were detailed in internal cables and reports, with operations spanning multiple countries including Thailand, Poland, Romania, and Lithuania. Detainees were held incommunicado without access to legal representation or family notification.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Senate Select Committee on Intelligence released a 6,700-page report in 2014, declassifying a 525-page executive summary, concluding the techniques were ineffective for intelligence gathering and involved significant inaccuracies in CIA representations to policymakers. No criminal prosecutions occurred for agency personnel, but the report led to executive orders banning the practices and influenced international criticism. Several European courts issued judgments against involved states.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment
involved_parties: U.S. Senate Select Committee on Intelligence

example_name: Guantanamo Bay Detention Camp Conditions
example_background: Established in 2002 at the U.S. naval base in Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, the facility held hundreds of detainees captured during counter-terrorism operations. Conditions included prolonged solitary confinement, exposure to extreme temperatures, limited medical care, force-feeding during hunger strikes involving nasogastric tubes without anesthesia, and interrogation methods such as prolonged shackling in stress positions. Detainees were often held without charges for years, with some experiencing sleep deprivation through constant light exposure and noise. The population peaked at nearly 800, with transfers to third countries occurring over time. Reports documented over 100 detainee deaths, many by suicide, amid allegations of psychological deterioration.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Supreme Court rulings in cases like Rasul v. Bush (2004) and Boumediene v. Bush (2008) affirmed habeas corpus rights for detainees. Multiple UN rapporteurs and human rights organizations issued reports calling for closure. President Obama ordered closure in 2009, though it remains operational with 30 detainees as of 2023. Force-feeding practices were challenged in federal courts without cessation.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment
relevant_rules: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (Article 7)

example_name: European Court of Human Rights - Ireland v. UK (1978)
example_background: During the Troubles in Northern Ireland, UK security forces applied the 'five techniques'—wall-standing, hooding, subjection to noise, sleep deprivation, and deprivation of food and drink—to 14 detainees in 1971 as part of interrogation to counter IRA activities. Detainees were held blindfolded against walls for hours in stress positions, exposed to continuous monotonous noise, and given minimal bread and water over seven days. This was part of Operation Demetrius, interning over 300 suspected paramilitaries without trial. Medical examinations post-release noted weight loss and psychological effects.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights ruled in 1978 that the techniques constituted inhuman and degrading treatment but not torture. The UK government discontinued the practices and paid compensation to victims. The judgment influenced reforms in British interrogation policies.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

example_name: ECtHR - El-Masri v. Macedonia (2012)
example_background: Khaled El-Masri, a German national, was detained by Macedonian authorities in 2003 at the border, held incommunicado for 23 days in a hotel room under surveillance, subjected to repeated strip searches, and then transferred to a CIA black site in Skopje. There, he endured secret detention with severe beatings, forced enemas, and stress positions while hooded and chained. After five months in Afghanistan's Salt Pit site involving further isolation and threats, he was released without charge. DNA evidence confirmed his mistreatment.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights awarded El-Masri €60,000 in damages, finding Macedonia responsible for torture and aiding extraordinary rendition. Macedonia was ordered to pay costs. The U.S. did not participate but faced related diplomatic fallout.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

example_name: ECtHR - Al Nashiri v. Poland (2014)
example_background: Abd al-Rahim al Nashiri, suspected in the USS Cole bombing, was held at a CIA black site in Stare Kiejkuty, Poland, from December 2002 to May 2003. He was subjected to waterboarding 95 times over two weeks, confinement in a small box with water up to his chin, walling (slamming against walls), and stress positions causing severe pain. Mock executions and threats of rape to family members were used. Poland provided the site and personnel support, with CIA flights documented via Eurocontrol data.
process_and_outcome: The Court ruled Poland aided torture and ill-treatment, awarding Nashiri €100,000. Poland was held liable for hosting the site without safeguards. Parallel U.S. military commission proceedings continue for Nashiri at Guantanamo.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

example_name: Council of Europe - Secret Detentions Report (2007)
example_background: The Council of Europe's Marty Report documented a network of secret CIA detention facilities across Europe post-9/11, involving at least 14 Council states. Practices included hooding, beating, sleep deprivation, enforced standing, reduced calorie intake, and waterboarding at sites in Poland, Romania, Lithuania, and others. Over 100 individuals were held secretly, transported via extraordinary rendition flights tracked by aviation data. Host states provided facilities and non-interference assurances.
process_and_outcome: Parliamentary Assembly debates led to resolutions condemning complicity. Subsequent ECtHR cases against Poland, Romania, and Lithuania resulted in findings of violations and compensation payments totaling over €200,000 per applicant. EU institutions reviewed member state cooperation policies.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

example_name: EU Fundamental Rights Agency - Detention Monitoring Guidelines (2010s)
example_background: The European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights (FRA) developed guidelines for monitoring places of detention, including police stations, prisons, and migration centers, to ensure conditions meet standards against inhuman or degrading treatment. These involve independent inspections assessing overcrowding, sanitation, medical access, and use of restraints. Implemented in member states like Greece and Italy for migrant facilities, the guidelines promote regular visits, detainee interviews, and reporting. Training for monitors emphasizes vulnerability identification, with pilot projects reducing incidents through recommendations.
process_and_outcome: Adoption by EU member states led to national action plans, with FRA reports showing improved conditions in monitored sites, such as reduced solitary confinement in Hungarian prisons. Annual reports track compliance, influencing EU funding allocations.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment
involved_parties: European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights (FRA)
relevant_rules: Optional Protocol to the UN Convention Against Torture (OPCAT)

example_name: Belgian Constitutional Court - Asylum Detention Conditions (2016)
example_background: The Constitutional Court examined conditions in closed asylum centers, finding prolonged detention in facilities with inadequate ventilation, lighting, and recreation space amounted to degrading treatment for vulnerable groups. Specific issues included 24-hour locked rooms, limited fresh air, and psychological stress from uncertainty. The ruling applied to centers holding thousands annually, prompting operational changes.
process_and_outcome: The Court annulled provisions allowing indefinite detention, mandating time limits and alternatives. Subsequent reforms reduced average detention lengths from months to weeks, with increased judicial oversight and vulnerability screenings.
source_rule: Prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

example_name: Guantánamo Bay Detention Camp Operations
example_background: The Guantánamo Bay detention facility was established in 2002 by the United States government to hold individuals captured during military operations in Afghanistan and elsewhere as part of the post-9/11 global counterterrorism efforts. Detainees were classified as 'unlawful enemy combatants' and held without formal charges or trials for extended periods. Reports documented conditions including prolonged solitary confinement, sleep deprivation through constant noise and light exposure, exposure to extreme temperatures, and use of stress positions. Interrogation techniques involved waterboarding, a method simulating drowning, applied to at least three individuals, alongside other practices such as walling, where detainees were slammed against walls, and confinement in small boxes. Medical personnel participated in monitoring these sessions to prevent death while allowing continuation. The facility housed hundreds of detainees from various nationalities, with many released years later without charges after prolonged detention averaging several years.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) in 2004 described the methods as tantamount to torture. The U.S. Senate Select Committee on Intelligence released a 2014 report confirming the CIA's enhanced interrogation program details. Legal challenges in U.S. courts, including Hamdan v. Rumsfeld (2006), led to Supreme Court rulings limiting military commissions. President Obama issued Executive Order 13491 in 2009 prohibiting such techniques and ordering closure attempts, though the facility remains operational. No U.S. personnel were criminally prosecuted for these actions.
source_rule: No one shall be subjected to torture or to inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment.
involved_parties: International Committee of the Red Cross (observer)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4
European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) Article 3

example_name: CIA Extraordinary Rendition Program
example_background: From 2001 to 2009, the CIA operated a program of extraordinary rendition involving the transfer of over 100 individuals suspected of terrorism links to foreign countries for interrogation. These transfers occurred without judicial oversight, often via aircraft to nations known for less restrictive interrogation practices. Detainees were subjected to methods including hooding, subjection to deafening noise, deprivation of food and sleep, suspension from ceilings by wrists, beatings, confinement in small boxes, and waterboarding. Specific cases included Abu Zubaydah, held in secret facilities in Thailand, Poland, and Romania, where he underwent 83 instances of waterboarding over 19 days, resulting in severe physical and psychological effects. Another case involved Khaled El-Masri, a German citizen rendered to Afghanistan and held for five months before release. European countries hosted black sites where such practices occurred under CIA arrangements.
process_and_outcome: European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) rulings in 2014 found Poland and Romania complicit, awarding damages to detainees. Council of Europe investigations, including the Marty Reports (2006-2007), detailed the program. U.S. Senate report in 2014 declassified program details. No convictions in U.S. courts, but civil settlements occurred. CIA acknowledged renditions but disputed effectiveness claims.
source_rule: No one shall be subjected to torture or to inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 4
European Convention on Human Rights Article 3

example_name: Council of Europe Al Nashiri v. Poland ECtHR Case
example_background: Abd al-Rahim al Nashiri, alleged USS Cole bombing organizer, was held in a CIA black site in Poland from December 2002 to May 2003. During this time, he was subjected to waterboarding twice, hooded, placed in a stress standing position for extended periods, confined in a small box with insects, and threatened with a handgun and power drill to his head. He was then rendered to Romania and other sites before Guantánamo.
