example_background: Facebook's platforms hosted political advertisements during EU parliamentary elections without adequate transparency labels, particularly for ads related to European issues funded by non-EU actors. This occurred in 2019, affecting the visibility of political messaging that could influence voter decisions on Union-level policies, with over 1,000 ads lacking required disclosures about sponsors.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) fined Facebook Ireland €12 million in 2021 following an investigation into compliance with transparency obligations for political ads.
source_rule: 2.   Political parties at Union level contribute to expressing the political will of the citizens of the Union.
relevant_rules: EU Regulation 2019/1150 on transparency of political advertising

example_name: Google EU Political Advertising Transparency Fine
example_background: Google was found to have insufficient mechanisms for disclosing political advertisers on its platforms during European elections, including YouTube and Google Ads. This impacted ads concerning EU policies and member state elections from 2019-2020, where users could not easily identify sponsors, potentially obscuring the expression of political will.
process_and_outcome: The French SAJE fined Google €150 million in 2021 for non-compliance with political ad transparency rules, prompting platform-wide remediation.
source_rule: 2.   Political parties at Union level contribute to expressing the political will of the citizens of the Union.
involved_parties: French SAJE (Service de l’action sur les élections)

example_name: ALDE Party Data Protection Best Practices
example_background: The Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe (ALDE), a Union-level political party, established a comprehensive data governance framework for the 2019 EU elections. This included audited consent processes, data minimization, and transparent reporting of political funding sources, enabling effective citizen engagement while respecting privacy rights across member states.
process_and_outcome: Independent audits by data protection experts confirmed full compliance, with no complaints upheld, positioning ALDE as a leader in ethical political data use.
source_rule: 2.   Political parties at Union level contribute to expressing the political will of the citizens of the Union.

example_name: Twitter (X) DSA Fine for Political Content Moderation
example_background: Following the EU's Digital Services Act implementation, Twitter (now X) was investigated for systemic failures in moderating illegal content and ensuring transparency in algorithmic amplification of political content during 2023 EU-related discussions, affecting visibility of Union-level political discourse.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission imposed a €10 million fine in 2023 under DSA preliminary findings, with ongoing proceedings for further penalties up to 6% of global turnover.
source_rule: 2.   Political parties at Union level contribute to expressing the political will of the citizens of the Union.

example_name: Greens/EFA Transparent Crowdfunding Compliance
example_background: The Greens/European Free Alliance (Greens/EFA), a European political party, utilized a public crowdfunding platform for EU election campaigns, fully disclosing all donors and expenditures in real-time. This practice ensured traceability of funds supporting political activities that express citizens' will on Union environmental and rights policies.
process_and_outcome: Certified compliant by EU electoral observers, with zero discrepancies in financial reporting, enhancing trust in the process.
source_rule: 2.   Political parties at Union level contribute to expressing the political will of the citizens of the Union.

example_name: TikTok DSA Political Ads Investigation
example_background: TikTok faced scrutiny for inadequate age verification and transparency in hosting political ads targeting EU users during 2023-2024, including content on Union migration policies. The platform's algorithms promoted such material without clear sponsor identification.
process_and_outcome: European Commission opened formal DSA proceedings in 2024, with potential fines pending; TikTok committed to interim improvements.
source_rule: 2.   Political parties at Union level contribute to expressing the political will of the citizens of the Union.

example_name: VioMe Workers' Self-Management Case
example_background: VioMe was a Greek building materials factory in Thessaloniki that ceased operations in 2011 amid Greece's economic crisis, leaving approximately 100 workers without employment. The workers occupied the factory and restarted production under worker self-management, producing cleaning products and other goods. They sought to exercise their rights to work and establish themselves by continuing operations without the original owner. The initiative drew attention for operating in a context of high unemployment and austerity measures affecting labor mobility within the EU, as Greek workers considered opportunities across member states but faced local barriers. Legal challenges arose over property rights and labor permissions, with courts initially ordering eviction, yet workers persisted through cooperatives, highlighting tensions between national property laws and EU foundational economic freedoms.
process_and_outcome: Greek courts issued multiple rulings, including eviction orders in 2013 and 2018, but enforcement was delayed due to appeals and public support. The case reached the European Court of Human Rights indirectly through property claims. Workers operated until 2022, when a new cooperative model was formalized, allowing continued production under legal recognition.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.
relevant_rules: Article 45 TFEU (freedom of movement for workers)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 15 (right to work)

example_name: European Commission vs Italy on Shop Opening Hours
example_background: Italy maintained national legislation restricting shop opening hours, particularly limiting operations on Sundays and holidays, which impacted retailers' ability to provide services uniformly across the EU. This created disparities for traders from other member states seeking to establish businesses or provide cross-border services in Italy. The rules were justified by Italian authorities on cultural and social protection grounds, but they affected the internal market by hindering free movement of services and establishment. The case emerged from complaints by foreign retailers facing inconsistent market access compared to Italian operators.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings in 2013, sending a reasoned opinion. Italy amended its laws in 2015 to liberalize opening hours, leading to the Commission's closure of the case without further escalation to the Court of Justice.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.

example_name: Laval un Partneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetareförbundet
example_background: Laval un Partneri, a Latvian construction company, sent workers to Sweden for a school construction project in 2004. Swedish trade unions demanded the company sign a collective agreement aligning with Swedish wages and conditions, which exceeded the posted workers' directive minimums. When Laval refused, citing cost implications that hindered its service provision from Latvia, the unions organized blockades and sympathy strikes, halting the project. This situation restricted the company's right to provide services across borders and the workers' freedom to work under Latvian terms in Sweden.
process_and_outcome: Swedish Labour Court ruled in favor of unions in 2007. The European Court of Justice in 2007 (C-341/05) found the actions disproportionate, violating EU law, leading Sweden to amend its labor laws and pay damages to Laval.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.

example_name: Commission v Germany (Pharmacists Case)
example_background: Germany's laws on pharmacy establishment required ownership by qualified pharmacists and limited corporate structures, preventing pharmacists from other EU states or companies from easily setting up pharmacies. This restricted the right of establishment for professionals and service providers from other member states, who faced nationality-based barriers despite mutual recognition of qualifications. The rules aimed to protect public health but were challenged for disproportionately limiting market access.
process_and_outcome: European Commission brought infringement action. The ECJ ruled against Germany in 2011 (C-405/09), finding violations, prompting Germany to adjust legislation for greater openness to EU professionals.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.

example_name: Viking Line ABP v Finnish Transport Workers' Union
example_background: Viking Line, a Finnish-Swedish ferry operator, planned to reflag its ship Rosella to Estonian registry to employ lower-cost Estonian crew, exercising its freedom to provide services and establish operations across EU states. Finnish unions threatened industrial action to prevent this, arguing it undermined local employment conditions. The dispute centered on balancing company operational freedoms with worker protections in a cross-border context.
process_and_outcome: Finnish courts referred to ECJ, which in 2007 (C-438/05) ruled union actions unjustified under EU law as they unduly restricted the undertaking's freedoms. No strikes occurred post-ruling; Viking proceeded with reflagging.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.

example_name: Free Movement of EU Citizens in Netherlands Housing Case
example_background: In 2013, the Dutch municipality of Rotterdam implemented a policy requiring four years of local residency for social housing priority, affecting EU citizens moving from other member states seeking employment and housing. This impacted workers' freedom to seek employment and establish residence, as newcomers from countries like Poland and Bulgaria faced delays in housing access crucial for job mobility within the EU.
process_and_outcome: European Commission investigated and initiated infringement proceedings in 2014. Rotterdam revised the policy in 2015 to comply, removing the residency requirement, with Commission closing the case.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.

example_name: EURES Job Mobility Portal Success
example_background: EURES, the European Employment Services network, facilitates job matching across EU member states, enabling workers to seek employment in any member state through online portals, job fairs, and advisor networks. Launched under EU coordination, it supports over 1.5 million job vacancies annually, assisting citizens from diverse backgrounds, including young professionals and long-term unemployed, to exercise their right to work abroad, with tools for recognition of qualifications and social security coordination.
process_and_outcome: Annual reports show sustained growth, with 1.3 million job placements in 2022. Recognized by EU institutions as a best practice, receiving ongoing funding and expansion.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.
involved_parties: National Public Employment Services (e.g., PES in 30+ countries)

example_name: Austria Taxi Licensing Restrictions
example_background: Austrian federal states imposed limits on taxi licenses based on population quotas, preventing new entrants, including service providers from other EU states, from obtaining licenses to operate. This affected cross-border providers seeking to establish or provide transport services, creating barriers despite harmonized EU road transport rules.
process_and_outcome: ECJ ruled in 2017 (C-532/15) that quotas violated freedom of establishment. Austria amended laws to introduce objective criteria, resolving the infringement.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.

example_name: Spain Professional Qualifications Recognition Delays
example_background: Spain's regional authorities delayed processing recognition of professional qualifications for regulated professions like architects and lawyers from other EU states, leading to barriers for workers seeking employment or establishment. Complaints highlighted waits exceeding three months, impacting service provision and job mobility.
process_and_outcome: European Commission launched infringement in 2018, issuing reasoned opinion. Spain reformed its system in 2020 via Royal Decree 967/2014 amendments, accelerating processes and closing the case.
source_rule: 2.   Every citizen of the Union has the freedom to seek employment, to work, to exercise the right of establishment and to provide services in any Member State.

example_name: Qatargate EU Parliament Corruption Scandal
example_background: In late 2022, investigations revealed that several Members of the European Parliament (MEPs) and staff received payments from Qatar to influence legislative decisions, including softening resolutions on human rights issues such as labor conditions for migrant workers building infrastructure for the 2022 FIFA World Cup. Qatar, as a non-EU state, engaged lobbyists and intermediaries to contact MEPs during a period when Qatar faced international scrutiny over its kafala sponsorship system, which ties migrant workers' legal status to their employers, often resulting in restrictions on changing jobs or leaving the country without permission. Evidence included bank transfers, text messages, and meetings documented in leaked chats, showing payments totaling hundreds of thousands of euros to MEPs from Belgium, Italy, and other nations. This occurred amid broader EU discussions on foundation rights and fair labor standards, with Qatar seeking to improve its image ahead of global events.
process_and_outcome: Belgian federal prosecutors launched Operation Qatargate, leading to arrests of MEP Eva Kaili, her partner Francesco Giorgi, and others in December 2022. Searches uncovered 1.5 million euros in cash hidden in luggage and suitcases. The European Parliament stripped immunities of involved MEPs, and ongoing judicial proceedings in Belgium and Italy resulted in pretrial detentions, asset freezes, and referrals to the EU Public Prosecutor's Office. No final convictions as of mid-2024, but reforms to parliamentary ethics rules were proposed.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 15 - Freedom to choose an occupation)

example_name: Germany's Hartz IV Reforms Labor Market Restrictions
example_background: Implemented in 2005 as part of Agenda 2010, Hartz IV combined unemployment benefits and social assistance into a single system while introducing strict conditions for recipients, including mandatory acceptance of any job offer deemed reasonable by job centers, even if it involved long commutes, lower qualifications, or temporary contracts. This affected millions of long-term unemployed individuals, particularly in eastern Germany, where structural unemployment persisted post-reunification. Job seekers faced sanctions such as benefit reductions of up to 30% for rejecting offers or failing to participate in activation programs. Critics noted that the system prioritized rapid re-employment over individual choice, leading to placements in low-wage sectors like cleaning or retail, often locking workers into precarious positions with limited mobility.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Constitutional Court ruled in 2010 and 2019 that certain sanction mechanisms violated human dignity and proportionality principles under the German Basic Law. Adjustments were made to reduce sanction severity and improve appeal processes. The system evolved into Bürgergeld in 2023, easing some restrictions on job choice while maintaining activation requirements. No direct fines, but ongoing litigation shaped policy changes.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.
relevant_rules: German Basic Law (Article 1 - Human dignity, Article 12 - Free choice of occupation)

example_name: Irish Direct Provision System for Asylum Seekers
example_background: From 2000 to 2023, Ireland's Direct Provision policy housed asylum seekers in state-funded centers while prohibiting them from working or accessing the labor market during claim processing, which often lasted years due to backlogs. Over 50 centers accommodated up to 4,000 residents at peak, providing basic meals and allowances of €38.80 weekly but restricting movement and employment. This affected tens of thousands from countries like Nigeria, Syria, and Afghanistan, preventing pursuit of chosen occupations and leading to dependency. A 2021 White Paper proposed reforms, but implementation lagged until court challenges.
process_and_outcome: High Court rulings in 2019 and 2021 declared the work ban unlawful under EU law, granting immediate work rights to some. The government ended Direct Provision in 2023, replacing it with accommodation recognition process allowing work after 90 days or sooner. Compensation schemes addressed past claims, with €5,000 interim payments to former residents.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.
involved_parties: Asylum seekers and advocacy groups like Nasc Ireland

example_name: EU Platform Work Directive Adoption
example_background: In 2024, the EU adopted the Directive on Improving Working Conditions in Platform Work to address misclassification of gig economy workers as independent contractors, granting them employee status with rights to fair wages, rest periods, and collective bargaining. This responded to cases where platforms like Uber and Deliveroo controlled work conditions via algorithms while denying labor protections. The directive mandates a rebuttable presumption of employment if platforms exert control, affecting 28 million workers across ride-hailing, delivery, and freelancing sectors in member states.
process_and_outcome: Negotiated by the European Parliament and Council in April 2024, member states must transpose by 2026. Early implementations in Spain and Belgium reclassified thousands, leading to back payments and union recognition without major fines initially.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.

example_name: Spain's Rider Law Enforcement Against Glovo
example_background: In 2021, Spain enacted Royal Decree-Law 9/2021 (Rider Law) classifying food delivery riders as employees rather than self-employed, entitling them to unemployment benefits, sick pay, and occupational choice protections. Glovo, a major delivery platform, initially resisted by structuring contracts as independent, leading to algorithmic management of shifts and routes that limited rider autonomy in selecting work.
process_and_outcome: Labor Ministry inspections in 2022-2023 fined Glovo €78 million for non-compliance. The company appealed but began reclassifying 10,000+ riders as employees, paying back social security contributions. Supreme Court upheld the law in 2024.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.

example_name: European Pillar of Social Rights Action Plan
example_background: Launched in 2021 by the European Commission, this plan sets targets for 2030 including 78% employment rate, 23 million fewer people at risk of poverty, and fair working conditions. It promotes active labor market policies allowing free choice of training and jobs, with €5.2 billion invested in 2021-2027 via ESF+. Member states report progress annually, focusing on upskilling for green and digital transitions.
process_and_outcome: Annual implementation reports show progress in Nordic countries exceeding targets through voluntary programs. No penalties, but peer reviews encourage best practices sharing.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.

example_name: Netherlands' Wet Arbeidsmarkt in Balans (LAB Act)
example_background: Enacted in 2020, the LAB Act reformed Dutch labor law to reduce differences between permanent and temporary contracts, making it easier to transition to open-ended deals and limiting successive fixed-term contracts to three years. It protects workers' ability to pursue stable occupations by curbing employer abuse of flex contracts, which previously allowed indefinite chaining without permanence rights.
process_and_outcome: Post-implementation data from 2021-2023 shows a 15% drop in flex contracts and increased permanent hiring. Courts upheld challenges from employers, with minimal fines for non-compliance.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.
involved_parties: Ministry of Social Affairs and Employment

example_name: CJEU Case C-232/09 Dita Danosa v LKB Lizings
example_background: A Latvian worker was dismissed from her board position after raising concerns about company irregularities. She argued this violated protections for workers' representatives. The case examined whether board members qualify for labor rights including free pursuit of occupation without retaliatory dismissal.
process_and_outcome: In 2013, the Court of Justice of the EU ruled that board members employed under labor contracts enjoy dismissal protections, reinforcing rights to occupational freedom. Latvia amended laws accordingly; no specific fine on the company.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Directive 2002/14/EC on informing and consulting employees

example_name: Uber BV Fined by Dutch Data Protection Authority
example_background: Uber's driver accounts in the Netherlands were automatically deactivated without notice based on algorithmic risk scores, preventing drivers from working and pursuing their chosen occupation in ride-hailing. Drivers had no access to decision criteria or appeal process, affecting hundreds.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, Dutch DPA fined Uber €10 million under GDPR for lack of transparency in automated decisions impacting employment. Uber updated systems for human review and explanations.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to engage in work and to pursue a freely chosen or accepted occupation.

example_name: European Court of Human Rights - Sidabras and Džiautas v. Lithuania
example_background: In 2003, the European Court of Human Rights examined cases involving two Lithuanian nationals who had previously worked for the KGB during the Soviet era. After Lithuania's independence, national security laws prohibited former KGB officers from holding certain public sector positions and roles in private security firms. The applicants applied for jobs in the police force and a private security company but were denied employment due to their past affiliations disclosed in mandatory security checks. They argued that these blanket restrictions prevented them from pursuing various occupations without individual assessment of current risk, affecting their ability to work in fields such as banking, insurance, auditing, and public administration. The restrictions applied broadly to around 6,000-8,000 former KGB agents, limiting their professional opportunities for an extended period post-independence.
process_and_outcome: The Court ruled that the restrictions constituted unjustified interference with the right to freely choose an occupation under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, as they were disproportionate and lacked individual evaluation. Lithuania was ordered to pay compensation to the applicants, and the judgment prompted revisions to national security vetting procedures to incorporate case-by-case assessments.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work
relevant_rules: Article 8 ECHR (right to private life, including choice of occupation)
Lithuanian Law on the Protection of State Secrets
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 15 (freedom to choose an occupation)

example_name: Thlimmenos v. Greece - ECHR Ruling on Professional Qualifications
example_background: In 2000, the European Court of Human Rights reviewed the case of Mr. Thlimmenos, a Jehovah's Witness convicted in 1972 for refusing military service on religious grounds, receiving a four-month suspended sentence. Under Greek law, individuals with criminal convictions exceeding three months were barred from becoming chartered accountants. Despite passing all required examinations, Mr. Thlimmenos was repeatedly denied registration by the Greek Institute of Chartered Accountants over 20 years. This exclusion prevented him from engaging in the accounting profession, forcing him to work in lower-skilled jobs, and highlighted systemic barriers to occupational access based on past convictions without regard to relevance or rehabilitation.
process_and_outcome: The Court found a violation of Article 14 (discrimination) in conjunction with Article 9 (freedom of religion), emphasizing indirect discrimination affecting occupational freedom. Greece was required to pay damages and adjust its certification processes to avoid automatic exclusions unrelated to professional fitness.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work
relevant_rules: EU Charter Article 15 (freedom to choose occupation)
Greek Presidential Decree 384/1970 on accountancy qualifications

example_name: EU Platform Work Directive Implementation - Uber Cases
example_background: Uber's platform model in various EU countries classified drivers as independent contractors, subjecting them to algorithmic management that determined work availability, pricing, and termination without appeal rights. Investigations in 2021-2023 revealed drivers faced deactivation without notice or recourse, limiting their ability to work on alternative platforms due to non-compete clauses and data access restrictions. In the Netherlands and Spain, drivers reported earnings instability and barriers to switching occupations, prompting collective actions. This reflected broader gig economy practices where platforms controlled access to work opportunities, echoing EU foundational rights concerns.
process_and_outcome: The EU Platform Work Directive (2024) was adopted mandating fair classification and transparency, with national courts (e.g., Dutch and Spanish) reclassifying drivers as employees in specific cases, awarding backpay. Uber adjusted policies in response, enabling better worker mobility.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work
involved_parties: National labor courts (e.g., Amsterdam District Court)
relevant_rules: EU Charter Article 15 (freedom to choose occupation)

example_name: Vander Elst Case - Free Movement of Workers
example_background: In 1994, the European Court of Justice addressed the situation of Mr. Vander Elst, a Belgian national working temporarily in France for a French company while residing in Belgium. Belgian authorities required a work permit for his short-term employment despite EU rules on free movement. This permit requirement delayed his ability to engage in cross-border work, illustrating administrative hurdles that restricted EU citizens' rights to provide services across member states without unnecessary national authorizations.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ ruled the permit requirement incompatible with EU law (Case C-43/93), affirming that self-employed EU nationals do not need work permits for temporary work in another member state under service provision rules. Belgium amended procedures accordingly.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)
relevant_rules: Regulation (EEC) No 1612/68 on freedom of movement for workers

example_name: Amazon Flex Worker Protections - EU Compliance
example_background: Amazon implemented changes to its Flex delivery driver program in response to EU scrutiny, providing drivers with access to training, insurance options, and the ability to work across multiple platforms without exclusivity clauses. By 2023, Amazon Flex in countries like Germany and France allowed couriers to set schedules freely and receive transparent algorithmic decisions, supporting occupational mobility in the gig economy while maintaining flexibility.
process_and_outcome: Following EU Commission inquiries, Amazon voluntarily enhanced worker rights, avoiding formal sanctions and aligning with emerging directive requirements, which improved driver retention and satisfaction as reported in internal audits.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work

example_name: Sørensen and Rasmussen v. Denmark - Pharmacist Licensing
example_background: Two Danish nationals holding pharmacy degrees from Danish universities were denied authorization to practice as pharmacists due to a national quota system limiting licenses to maintain market balance. The quotas prioritized existing pharmacies, preventing new entrants from establishing practices despite qualifications. This system operated from 1993, affecting graduates' ability to enter the profession independently.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR found no violation under Article 8 but highlighted limits on occupational freedom; however, subsequent ECJ influence led Denmark to phase out quotas by 2001, increasing license availability.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work

example_name: Bosman Ruling - Football Player Transfers
example_background: Jean-Marc Bosman, a Belgian footballer, challenged transfer rules imposed by football governing bodies that restricted players' ability to move clubs post-contract expiration, including transfer fees and quotas on foreign players. These rules limited professional athletes' freedom to negotiate employment across EU states, affecting career mobility.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ ruled in 1995 (Case C-415/93) that the rules violated EU free movement principles, abolishing transfer fees for out-of-contract players and nationality quotas, revolutionizing sports labor markets.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Airbnb Host Flexibility Program - EU Best Practice
example_background: Airbnb's platform in the EU enables individuals to list spare rooms or properties as hosts, providing supplemental income without full-time commitment. Hosts retain control over availability, pricing, and guest selection, with no non-compete requirements, allowing them to engage in hosting alongside primary occupations. EU-wide compliance includes transparent terms and data portability for multi-platform use.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators reviewed and approved Airbnb's model under digital services rules, with no major enforcement actions, recognizing it as enabling occupational choice in the sharing economy.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work

example_name: Volkswagen Forced Labor Audit Compliance
example_background: Volkswagen conducted extensive audits revealing historical use of forced labor from Eastern Europe during WWII and addressed modern supply chain risks in China. The company established a foundation for former forced laborers, provided compensation, and implemented supplier codes prohibiting restrictions on workers' occupational freedom, ensuring voluntary employment globally.
process_and_outcome: Independent audits confirmed remediation, with VW receiving recognition from human rights watchdogs for proactive measures, avoiding boycotts and legal claims.
source_rule: Freedom to choose an occupation and right to engage in work

example_name: German Construction Company Third-Country Worker Case
example_background: A construction firm in Germany employed workers from non-EU countries such as Serbia and Ukraine under posted worker arrangements for infrastructure projects. These workers were assigned tasks equivalent to those performed by German nationals but received lower hourly wages, reduced overtime compensation, and limited access to company-provided accommodation subsidies that were available to EU citizen employees. The disparity persisted over several months during a major highway expansion project involving over 200 workers, where payroll records indicated systematic differences in remuneration packages based on nationality. The company justified the differences by referencing lower labor costs in the workers' home countries, despite legal requirements for equal treatment in working conditions.
process_and_outcome: The German Federal Labour Court reviewed the case after complaints from affected workers and trade unions. The court ruled in favor of equal treatment, ordering the company to pay back wages amounting to €450,000 and implement corrective payroll practices. Additional monitoring was mandated for two years.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.

example_name: Netherlands Poultry Processor Wage Disparity Fine
example_background: A large poultry processing plant in the Netherlands hired nationals from Brazil and India on work permits for slaughterhouse and packaging roles identical to those held by Dutch and other EU workers. Documentation showed that third-country workers were paid 20% less per hour, denied shift bonuses routinely given to EU staff, and provided inferior health and safety training sessions. This occurred across multiple facilities employing over 1,500 workers during a period of high demand for poultry products in 2018-2020, with internal HR policies explicitly categorizing pay scales by nationality.
process_and_outcome: Dutch Labour Inspectorate conducted audits following whistleblower reports, resulting in a €1.2 million fine for unequal treatment. The company entered a settlement to equalize back pay for 800 workers and revise its compensation framework under supervision.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 15

example_name: Belgium Cleaning Sector Equal Pay Enforcement
example_background: In the Belgian cleaning industry, a service provider contracted for office maintenance employed Moroccan and Turkish nationals alongside Belgian and EU workers for the same cleaning duties in corporate buildings across Brussels. Records from 2019 indicated that third-country employees received lower base pay, no holiday allowances equivalent to those for EU staff, and shorter contract durations, affecting approximately 150 workers. The company maintained separate payroll ledgers for different nationality groups, leading to observable gaps in total annual earnings despite identical job descriptions and hours worked.
process_and_outcome: Following joint inspections by labor authorities and union representatives, the company was fined €300,000 and required to conduct a full audit of its workforce. Remediation included €200,000 in back payments and mandatory training on EU equal treatment rules.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.
relevant_rules: Treaty on the Functioning of the EU (TFEU) Article 45

example_name: Spain Hotel Chain Third-Country Worker Holiday Pay Case
example_background: A major hotel group in Spain's Balearic Islands staffed its resorts with workers from Latin America and North Africa who held valid work authorizations, performing housekeeping and kitchen roles indistinguishable from those of Spanish and EU nationals. Between 2017 and 2021, these third-country workers were excluded from pro-rated holiday pay calculations and seniority-based bonuses provided to EU employees, as per internal HR directives. This impacted over 400 seasonal workers, with payroll data showing average annual pay 15-25% lower due to these exclusions during peak tourist seasons.
process_and_outcome: Spanish National Labour Inspectorate investigated after union filings, imposing a €750,000 penalty. The company agreed to a collective settlement distributing €1.1 million in arrears and updated its policies for uniform benefits application.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.
involved_parties: Spanish Labour and Social Security Inspectorate

example_name: Ireland Meat Processing Equal Treatment Audit
example_background: An Irish meat processing facility employed Brazilian and other non-EU nationals on work permits for production line tasks matching those of Irish workers. A 2022 compliance audit revealed that third-country workers received reduced sick pay entitlements and no access to the same performance incentives as EU staff, despite working the same shifts and meeting identical productivity targets. The practice affected 300+ employees over two years, with company records documenting nationality-based eligibility criteria for benefits.
process_and_outcome: Workplace Relations Commission audit led to a €400,000 fine and an enforcement order for policy overhaul. The facility implemented a unified benefits system verified in follow-up inspections.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.

example_name: Unilever Netherlands Equal Conditions Program
example_background: Unilever's manufacturing sites in the Netherlands integrated third-country nationals from Asia and Africa into its workforce on equal footing with Dutch and EU employees for production and logistics roles. The company established a centralized HR system ensuring identical wage scales, overtime rates, health benefits, and training opportunities regardless of nationality. This approach was rolled out across facilities employing over 5,000 workers since 2018, with annual audits confirming parity in working conditions during supply chain expansions.
process_and_outcome: External audits by Dutch authorities and certifications from fair labor organizations validated full adherence, resulting in no penalties and recognition in sustainability reports. The program served as a model for industry peers.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.

example_name: France Logistics Firm Posted Worker Compliance
example_background: A logistics company operating warehouses in France employed Indian and Senegalese nationals authorized to work in the EU for forklift operation and inventory management tasks equivalent to those of French workers. From 2020 onward, the firm applied uniform pay grids, social security contributions, and leave policies across all staff, tracked via a digital compliance platform. This ensured no disparities in daily allowances or rest periods, covering 1,200 workers in cross-border operations.
process_and_outcome: Routine inspections by French labor authorities confirmed compliance, issuing a clean report and commending the firm's proactive monitoring. No corrective actions were required.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.
relevant_rules: Loi n° 2016-1088 (Equal Treatment for Posted Workers)

example_name: UK Pilkington Glass Equal Pay Settlement
example_background: Pilkington, a glass manufacturing company in the UK, faced claims from Portuguese and other non-UK EU workers (analogous to third-country nationals post-Brexit considerations) performing assembly line duties identical to British staff. Internal reviews from 2019-2022 showed initial gaps in bonus structures, which the company addressed by standardizing all compensation elements, including shift premiums and pension contributions for over 2,000 employees across sites.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary settlement with unions paid £2.5 million in equalizations, followed by court-approved uniform policies. Subsequent audits verified ongoing parity.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.

example_name: Poland Automotive Supplier Wage Parity Case
example_background: An automotive parts supplier in Poland hired Ukrainian nationals on work permits for welding and assembly roles matching those of Polish workers. Audits in 2021 revealed lower base rates and excluded accommodation reimbursements for non-EU staff, affecting 500 workers amid increased production for European carmakers. Company directives had segmented benefits by citizenship status.
process_and_outcome: Polish National Labour Inspectorate fined the firm PLN 1.5 million (€350,000) and ordered back payments of PLN 2 million. Policy revisions were enforced with quarterly reporting.
source_rule: 3.   Nationals of third countries who are authorised to work in the territories of the Member States are entitled to working conditions equivalent to those of citizens of the Union.

example_name: ECHR Case: Sporrong and Lönnroth v. Sweden
example_background: In the 1980s, two Swedish property owners in Stockholm faced long-term expropriation permits issued by the city government. These permits, intended for future public infrastructure projects, prevented the owners from selling, mortgaging, or developing their properties for over 20 years in one case and 23 years in the other. No actual expropriation occurred during this period, but the restrictions significantly diminished the properties' value and utility. The applicants argued that these measures constituted a de facto deprivation of their possessions without adequate compensation or clear public necessity, as the permits were precautionary and not tied to immediate projects.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found violations of Article 1 of Protocol No. 1. Sweden was ordered to pay compensation to the applicants for the interference with their property rights. The case established that prolonged restrictions on property use can amount to deprivation requiring fair balance and compensation.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to own, use, dispose of and bequeath his or her lawfully acquired possessions. No one may be deprived of his or her possessions, except in the public interest and in the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, subject to fair compensation being paid in good time for their loss. The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.
relevant_rules: Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: ECHR Case: James and Others v. United Kingdom
example_background: Under the UK Leasehold Reform Act 1967, tenants of certain long leaseholds in large buildings could compel the landlord to sell the freehold at a price determined by a statutory formula that excluded development value. Leaseholders in five blocks in London exercised this right, acquiring freeholds at reduced prices. The landlord argued this interfered with their property rights by forcing a sale below market value without fair compensation, as the formula did not account for potential future development gains.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights ruled that the interference pursued a legitimate aim of social justice and was proportionate in the public interest. No violation was found, as the UK government provided a fair balance through the legislative framework.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to own, use, dispose of and bequeath his or her lawfully acquired possessions. No one may be deprived of his or her possessions, except in the public interest and in the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, subject to fair compensation being paid in good time for their loss. The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.
relevant_rules: Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Belgian Language Laws Property Case
example_background: In the 1960s, Belgium enacted laws requiring property owners in certain linguistic regions to display real estate signs only in the local official language. Owners in bilingual areas who used both languages or the 'wrong' language faced fines and restrictions on property transactions. This affected sales and rentals, limiting owners' ability to freely dispose of their properties and reducing market value due to restricted advertising.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights determined a violation of property rights, as the measures went beyond necessary regulation for linguistic interests and lacked proportionality. Belgium was required to adjust the laws.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to own, use, dispose of and bequeath his or her lawfully acquired possessions. No one may be deprived of his or her possessions, except in the public interest and in the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, subject to fair compensation being paid in good time for their loss. The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.
relevant_rules: Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights
Belgian language laws on real estate signage

example_name: French Holiday Homes Regulation Case
example_background: French authorities imposed a special tax on second homes and holiday properties owned by non-residents, significantly higher than for primary residences. This applied across various regions and affected numerous foreign owners, reducing the economic value of their properties without prior notice or individualized assessment. The measure aimed to discourage speculation and promote local housing availability.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found the tax constituted a violation due to discriminatory application and lack of fair balance, ordering France to cease retroactive impositions and provide remedies.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to own, use, dispose of and bequeath his or her lawfully acquired possessions. No one may be deprived of his or her possessions, except in the public interest and in the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, subject to fair compensation being paid in good time for their loss. The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.
relevant_rules: Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: UK Agricultural Holdings Compensation
example_background: Under the UK's Agricultural Holdings Act, tenants of agricultural land received statutory rights to compensation upon termination of tenancy for improvements made to the land. Landlords were required to pay fair compensation based on the value added, even if not contractually agreed. This system balanced landlord and tenant interests, allowing tenants to bequeath or realize value from their investments while enabling public interest in productive land use.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights upheld the scheme as compliant, noting it provided fair compensation and pursued general interest in agricultural stability. No violation declared.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to own, use, dispose of and bequeath his or her lawfully acquired possessions. No one may be deprived of his or her possessions, except in the public interest and in the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, subject to fair compensation being paid in good time for their loss. The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.
relevant_rules: Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: ECHR Case: Hentrich v. France
example_background: A private seller in France offered property to a buyer at a certain price. The tax authorities exercised a pre-emption right, buying the property at that price to resell at a profit, suspecting undervaluation. The seller received the agreed price but lost the opportunity to freely dispose of the property and any potential higher value, with limited recourse to challenge the decision.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights ruled a violation, as the pre-emption lacked adequate safeguards and fair balance. France was directed to provide compensation and procedural reforms.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to own, use, dispose of and bequeath his or her lawfully acquired possessions. No one may be deprived of his or her possessions, except in the public interest and in the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, subject to fair compensation being paid in good time for their loss. The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.
relevant_rules: Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights
French pre-emption rights under tax law

example_name: Italian Building Permit Delays Case
example_background: Property owners in Italy applied for building permits that were delayed for years by local authorities without justification. During this time, owners could neither develop nor fully utilize or sell the land, leading to substantial financial losses from devaluation and missed opportunities. The delays were attributed to bureaucratic inefficiencies rather than specific public interest needs.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found a violation of property rights due to unjustified interference. Italy was ordered to pay compensation and streamline permit processes.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone has the right to own, use, dispose of and bequeath his or her lawfully acquired possessions. No one may be deprived of his or her possessions, except in the public interest and in the cases and under the conditions provided for by law, subject to fair compensation being paid in good time for their loss. The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.
relevant_rules: Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: German Rent Control Compliance
example_background: Germany's rent control laws (Mietspiegel) regulate maximum rents in certain areas to ensure affordable housing. Landlords must adhere to indexed rent ceilings but receive fair returns based on property costs and market adjustments. Periodic reviews and appeals allow adjustments for maintenance or improvements, balancing owner rights with public interest in housing access.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights affirmed the system's compliance, as it includes fair compensation mechanisms and proportionality. Multiple challenges dismissed.

The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.

example_name: ECHR Case: Gidarov v. Russia
example_background: Russian authorities seized vehicles from an individual without providing prior notice, legal basis, or compensation. The seizures occurred during traffic stops and were justified under anti-smuggling laws, but lacked procedural fairness, preventing the owner from using or disposing of the possessions for extended periods.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights declared a violation for deprivation without fair process and compensation. Russia ordered to pay damages and return property.

The use of property may be regulated by law in so far as is necessary for the general interest.

example_name: Uber GDPR Data Processing Fine
example_background: Uber Technologies Inc. operated its ride-sharing platform across Europe, collecting and processing personal data of drivers and passengers through its app. The company transferred driver data from Europe to the United States for centralized processing without implementing appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses or binding corporate rules at the time. This occurred prior to updates in data protection practices following the introduction of the General Data Protection Regulation. The Dutch Data Protection Authority examined Uber's data handling practices after receiving complaints regarding the transfer of sensitive personal information outside the European Economic Area without adequate legal basis or protections. Uber's operations involved millions of users and drivers, with data flows integral to matching riders, processing payments, and managing the fleet across multiple member states.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority conducted an investigation and issued a decision in 2018 imposing a fine of 600,000 euros on Uber for violations related to international data transfers. Uber appealed the decision, leading to further proceedings, but ultimately adjusted its data transfer mechanisms to include approved safeguards.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.

example_name: Google Android Competition Fine
example_background: Google LLC developed and distributed the Android operating system, which powers a significant portion of mobile devices worldwide. Through agreements with device manufacturers and mobile network operators in the European Economic Area, Google required the pre-installation of its Google Search app and Chrome browser as a condition for licensing the Google Play Store. Additionally, Google provided financial incentives tied to ensuring Google Search remained the default search engine on Android devices. These practices affected competition in general search services and mobile web browsers, influencing how over 90% of smart mobile devices in Europe ran Android. The European Commission initiated scrutiny following complaints from rivals alleging distortion of market competition.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission investigated from 2015 to 2018 and imposed a record fine of 4.34 billion euros in July 2018 for anti-competitive agreements. Google appealed to the General Court, which partially upheld the decision in 2022, reducing the fine slightly to 4.125 billion euros, and further appeals continued at the European Court of Justice.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.

example_name: Apple App Store Tax Practices Fine
example_background: Apple Inc. managed the App Store as the primary distribution platform for apps on iOS devices in the European Economic Area, charging developers a 30% commission on in-app purchases and subscriptions. The company restricted developers from directing users to alternative payment methods or external websites for purchases, and treated all app downloads through the platform uniformly regardless of developer location. Ireland, as Apple's EU headquarters, became the focus due to tax arrangements. The European Commission examined whether these practices and associated tax rulings granted selective advantages, distorting competition in the digital marketplace serving hundreds of millions of users.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission issued a 13 billion euro recovery order in 2016 for unlawful state aid through tax benefits. The General Court annulled the decision in 2020, but the European Court of Justice overturned that ruling in 2024, confirming the aid was unlawful and requiring repayment plus interest.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.

example_name: Facebook WhatsApp Merger Conditional Approval
example_background: Facebook Inc. sought to acquire WhatsApp Inc. in 2014, combining two major messaging and social networking platforms with hundreds of millions of users in the European Economic Area. The acquisition raised concerns about potential reduction in competition in consumer communications apps and data usage for advertising. Facebook committed to allowing users to maintain separate accounts and not merging user data without consent, while ensuring third-party access to WhatsApp data remained unchanged. The deal proceeded under these conditions to preserve market dynamics.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission conducted an in-depth merger review under the EU Merger Regulation and cleared the acquisition in 2014 subject to commitments. Subsequent investigations in 2018 addressed data use changes, leading to additional scrutiny but no block.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.

example_name: BNP Paribas Sanctions Violations
example_background: BNP Paribas SA, a major French bank, processed billions of dollars in transactions for entities in Sudan, Iran, and Cuba between 2002 and 2012 through its U.S. dollar clearing operations in New York. The bank deliberately structured payments to conceal the involvement of sanctioned countries, deleting references to sanctioned entities in wire transfer instructions and using indirect routing. This involved over 3,000 transactions totaling approximately 190 billion U.S. dollars, facilitating trade and financial services for prohibited regimes. U.S. regulators identified these activities during routine examinations and broader investigations into sanctions compliance.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice, OFAC, and other agencies investigated, leading to a global resolution in 2014 with BNP Paribas pleading guilty and paying a total penalty of 8.9 billion U.S. dollars, including forfeiture and a one-year suspension from certain U.S. dollar clearing activities.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services
relevant_rules: EU Common Foreign and Security Policy sanctions

example_name: UniCredit Sanctions Evasion Fine
example_background: UniCredit Bank AG, part of the UniCredit Group, handled U.S. dollar payments for Iranian clients from 2002 to 2011 via its New York branch. The bank processed transactions worth billions by removing or altering sanctions-related information in payment messages, using cover payments and intermediary banks to obscure origins. This supported Iran's nuclear and missile programs indirectly. German and U.S. authorities uncovered the practices through audits and intelligence.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, UniCredit agreed to pay over 1.3 billion U.S. dollars in penalties to U.S. authorities for sanctions violations, including criminal and civil fines, following investigations by the DOJ and OFAC.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.
involved_parties: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

example_name: ING AML Compliance Settlement
example_background: ING Bank N.V., a leading Dutch bank, managed corporate and retail accounts in the Netherlands from 2010 to 2016 with inadequate customer due diligence and transaction monitoring systems. The bank failed to perform timely customer risk classifications, monitor transactions for unusual patterns, and file suspicious activity reports, affecting over 2,100 high-risk accounts and transactions totaling 17 billion euros. Internal audits revealed systemic deficiencies in anti-money laundering controls.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Public Prosecution Service investigated and reached a settlement in 2018 with ING paying 775 million euros, including a criminal penalty of 700 million euros and confiscation of 75 million euros, with no admission of guilt but commitment to remediation.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.
relevant_rules: Dutch Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Prevention Act (Wwft)

example_name: ABN AMRO AML Deficiencies Fine
example_background: ABN AMRO Bank N.V. operated retail and private banking services in the Netherlands with shortcomings in its anti-money laundering framework between 2014 and 2018. The bank did not adequately monitor transactions, update customer risk profiles, or detect structured deposits indicative of money laundering, impacting numerous accounts. Regulators noted persistent issues despite prior warnings.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank imposed a penalty of 16.2 million euros in 2019 for AML control failures, following an investigation prompted by sector-wide reviews.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.

example_name: Schrems II Data Transfers Case
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, challenged Facebook Ireland Ltd.'s transfer of European user data to servers in the United States under the EU-US Privacy Shield framework. Facebook processed data of over 500 million EU users for advertising, relying on Privacy Shield self-certification. Concerns arose over U.S. government surveillance access lacking equivalent protections to EU law, affecting standard contractual clauses as a transfer mechanism.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU invalidated the Privacy Shield in July 2020 (Schrems II), requiring enhanced safeguards for data transfers, leading Facebook to suspend transatlantic data flows temporarily and implement new measures.
source_rule: The freedom to conduct a business in accordance with Union law and national laws and practices is recognised.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Data Transfers Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, formerly Facebook, engaged in transatlantic data transfers of European user data to the United States for advertising and analytics purposes. Following the invalidation of the EU-US Privacy Shield by the Schrems II judgment in 2020, Meta continued transferring vast amounts of personal data, including behavioral profiles from over 500 million users, without implementing fully effective supplementary measures. The company's business model relied heavily on processing this data in US data centers, where laws like FISA Section 702 allow government access. Irish regulators scrutinized Meta's use of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and additional protections, finding them inadequate against risks of unlawful surveillance. This practice persisted despite prior warnings and ongoing legal challenges from privacy advocates.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission issued a final decision in December 2022, fining Meta 1.2 billion euros, the largest GDPR penalty to date. The decision followed a multi-year investigation and coordination with other EU DPAs, prohibiting further transfers without compliant mechanisms. Meta challenged the ruling in courts.
source_rule: Freedom to conduct a business
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 16
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 7 and 8

example_name: Amazon EU Data Protection Fine
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. utilized personal data from its marketplace and advertising services in ways that involved opaque processing for targeted ads. The company's systems combined purchase history, browsing data, and third-party inputs to create detailed profiles shared across services. Luxembourg regulators probed Amazon's compliance with transparency and purpose limitation principles, finding that users were not adequately informed about data uses for business intelligence and advertising. This affected operations in multiple EU countries, with data processed in centralized EU and US facilities.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg CNPD issued a 746 million euro fine in 2021, one of the largest GDPR penalties. Amazon appealed to the Court of Justice, which partially annulled it in 2024 due to procedural issues, but core findings on data practices stood.
source_rule: Freedom to conduct a business
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD Luxembourg)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 16
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Schrems II Facebook Data Transfers Case
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, challenged Facebook Ireland Limited's practice of transferring personal data of European users to the US. Facebook processed data from over 1.5 million EU users daily, using it for social networking and advertising, with servers in the US subject to surveillance laws. After the Safe Harbor framework was invalidated in Schrems I, Facebook shifted to Privacy Shield, which Schrems contested for lacking adequate protections against US intelligence access. The case highlighted systemic issues in transatlantic data flows essential to tech companies' business models.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU ruled in July 2020 (Schrems II, Case C-311/18), invalidating the EU-US Privacy Shield and requiring stricter assessments for SCCs. This led to widespread compliance changes across the industry.
source_rule: Freedom to conduct a business
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 7, 8, 16, 47

example_name: Google Android GDPR Fine by French CNIL
example_background: Google LLC and Google Ireland Limited pre-installed apps on Android devices sold in Europe, collecting consent for personalized ads through a bundled interface. The consent banner combined multiple data processing purposes without granular options, affecting billions of data points from location, search history, and app usage. CNIL investigated user complaints about lack of free choice in data processing for Google's advertising business.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google 150 million euros in 2022 after a 2019 investigation. Google adjusted its consent mechanisms, and the decision influenced EU-wide practices.
source_rule: Freedom to conduct a business
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 16

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Data Breach
example_background: Marriott International Inc. acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database with personal data of up to 383 million guests, including passport numbers and payment details. A breach occurred from 2014 undetected until 2018, affecting guests who booked via Starwood properties across Europe. Marriott continued processing this data for loyalty programs and bookings without promptly addressing encryption and access control gaps.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Marriott 18.4 million pounds in 2020. Additional investigations by other EU DPAs followed, with Marriott enhancing data governance.
source_rule: Freedom to conduct a business
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 16

example_name: Apple App Store Compliance with DMA
example_background: Apple Inc. adjusted its App Store policies in response to the EU Digital Markets Act to allow developers more freedom in directing users to external payment options. Previously, Apple's ecosystem required in-app purchases through its system with commissions. Under DMA scrutiny, Apple enabled alternative terms while maintaining some oversight, demonstrating adaptation to new rules preserving business operations.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission accepted Apple's commitments in 2024, avoiding fines and setting a precedent for gatekeeper compliance. Ongoing monitoring ensures adherence.
source_rule: Freedom to conduct a business
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 16
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 17 (Right to Property)

example_name: EU Commission Proceedings Against Hungary on Asylum Procedures
example_background: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings against Hungary due to systemic issues in its asylum system that occurred after 2015. Hungary enacted legislation accelerating asylum procedures, restricting access to the territory for asylum seekers, and limiting judicial review. The procedures placed asylum seekers in transit zones at the Serbian border with limited time to register claims. Access to legal aid and interpreters was restricted, and appeals had no suspensive effect, leading to rapid returns. This setup resulted in over 130,000 asylum applications processed in 2015-2016, with most rejected summarily. The government justified measures as responses to large migrant inflows from Syria, Afghanistan, and Iraq, maintaining only 14 asylum grants in 2016. Border fences were constructed, and pushbacks were reported by NGOs. The situation persisted, affecting third-country nationals seeking protection under international standards.
process_and_outcome: In December 2017, the Commission referred Hungary to the Court of Justice of the EU. In April 2020, the CJEU ruled Hungary failed to comply with EU asylum law requirements for effective access, fair procedures, and suspensive appeals. Hungary was ordered to pay daily penalties of €1 million from 2021 until compliance. Proceedings continue with ongoing assessments.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (Adjudicator)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 18
Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union

example_name: CJEU M.S.S. v Belgium and Greece Case
example_background: An Afghan national applied for asylum in Belgium after transiting through Greece. Belgium transferred him back to Greece under the Dublin Regulation. In Greece, the applicant faced overcrowded facilities, lack of procedural safeguards, homelessness, and destitution without access to asylum procedures. Reception conditions were inadequate, with reports of 6,000-8,000 asylum seekers in Athens airport area in 2009, limited registration, and no effective appeals. Systemic deficiencies included delays in examining claims and insufficient support, impacting rights under refugee instruments.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber of the CJEU ruled in January 2011 that Belgium violated EU law by transferring the applicant to Greece knowing of systemic flaws. Greece also infringed by not providing adequate reception and procedures. The transfer was annulled, and Greece reformed its system with EU funding.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 4 and 18

example_name: Germany's Asylum Processing Model During 2015-2016 Crisis
example_background: Germany received over 890,000 asylum applications in 2015 amid large arrivals from Syria, Afghanistan, and Iraq. The government suspended Dublin transfers for Syrians, allowing direct substantive examinations. Federal agencies processed claims efficiently using digital systems, providing accommodation, integration courses, and work rights post-recognition. Reception centers handled high volumes with multilingual support, medical care, and legal aid, aligning with EU standards during peak pressure.
process_and_outcome: Germany granted protection to over 445,000 individuals by 2016, with recognition rates around 50%. The approach was praised in EU reports, leading to no infringement actions and influencing EU resettlement efforts.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
involved_parties: Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 18
Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union

example_name: EU Infringement Against Poland on Refugee Reception Conditions
example_background: Poland faced scrutiny for conditions in asylum reception centers, particularly overcrowding and inadequate healthcare. In 2016-2018, facilities hosted Chechen and other applicants with reports of 150% occupancy, limited psychological support, and poor sanitation. Procedures for family unity and vulnerability assessments were delayed, affecting compliance with standards during increased arrivals from Belarus borders.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission opened infringement proceedings in 2019, leading to a 2021 Letter of Formal Notice. Poland improved facilities with EU funds, resolving some issues by 2022.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 18

example_name: CJEU Tarakhel v Switzerland Case
example_background: A family of six Afghan nationals, including young children, sought asylum in Switzerland after arriving via Italy. Switzerland planned Dublin transfer to Italy, where reception conditions included reports of homelessness among minors, lack of family shelters, and overwhelmed services. The family argued risks of inhuman treatment due to inadequate safeguards for vulnerable groups.
process_and_outcome: In February 2014, the CJEU ruled that transfers require individual guarantees from Italy for accommodation and care. Switzerland obtained assurances and proceeded with transfer.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 4 and 18

example_name: Greece Reception Conditions Infringement Proceedings
example_background: Post-2015 crisis, Greece's asylum reception centers on islands like Lesbos and Samos were overcrowded, with capacities exceeded by 200-300%. Conditions included poor sanitation, limited medical access, and delays in asylum processing for Syrians, Afghans, and others. EU-Turkey deal in 2016 aimed to manage flows but led to hotspot congestion.
process_and_outcome: European Commission launched infringement in 2018 for violations of reception directive. Greece received €509 million EU funding for improvements, reducing occupancy by 2020.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 18

example_name: Sweden's Compliance with EU Asylum Resettlement
example_background: Sweden participated in EU voluntary resettlement schemes, admitting over 2,500 refugees from Turkey and Jordan in 2015-2017. The process involved thorough status determination pre-arrival, family tracing, health checks, and integration planning, ensuring adherence to non-refoulement and procedural rights.
process_and_outcome: Successful resettlement with high integration rates; Sweden received EU recognition and funding without compliance challenges.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 18

example_name: Italy's Implementation of EU-Turkey Statement
example_background: Following the 2016 EU-Turkey deal, Italy managed Mediterranean arrivals by processing asylum claims in hotspots on Sicily and mainland. NGO reports noted initial delays but improvements in registration, legal aid, and relocations to other EU states for eligible persons from Eritrea, Nigeria, and Syria.
process_and_outcome: Italy relocated over 10,000 asylum seekers by 2019, complying after early infringement risks, supported by EU hotspots funding.
source_rule: The right to asylum shall be guaranteed with due respect for the rules of the Geneva Convention of 28 July 1951 and the Protocol of 31 January 1967 relating to the status of refugees and in accordance with the Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Treaties’).
