source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament in the Member State in which he or she resides, under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: Belgium's Brussels EU Voter Residency Disputes
example_background: In Brussels, a region with dense EU institution staff, 2019 local election preparations involved disputes over residency qualifications for EU citizens. Some declarations were contested for using office addresses rather than genuine homes, mirroring requirements for Belgian nationals, leading to selective validations by municipal authorities.
process_and_outcome: Brussels court upheld rejections of 200+ registrations in 2018 rulings, mandating stricter proof. Affected voters re-registered successfully after compliance.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament in the Member State in which he or she resides, under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: Sweden's Electoral Agency EU Citizen Outreach Program
example_background: Sweden's Valmyndigheten (Electoral Agency) ran annual outreach for EU residents ahead of EP and local elections, providing identical registration forms and support as for Swedes. In 2019, this resulted in 25,000+ EU voters participating, with processes audited for uniformity in residency checks across municipalities.
process_and_outcome: Independent review by the Swedish Parliament's Election Review Board confirmed proper implementation and equal access.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament in the Member State in which he or she resides, under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: Bulgaria Election Interference Case 2024
example_background: During the 2024 European Parliament elections, Bulgarian authorities documented instances where individuals and groups attempted to influence the electoral process by spreading disinformation campaigns targeting candidates. Social media platforms were used to disseminate false information about candidates' eligibility and voting procedures, affecting public perception in multiple districts. The activities involved coordinated efforts to question the legitimacy of certain candidates' right to stand, leading to complaints from political parties. Investigations revealed that these actions occurred amid heightened tensions ahead of the June 2024 polls, with over 100 reports filed regarding potential disruptions to voter rights. The context included Bulgaria's ongoing efforts to align with EU electoral standards post-accession, where foundation rights such as the right to vote and stand as a candidate are emphasized in national legislation transposing EU directives.
process_and_outcome: The Central Election Commission conducted inquiries, resulting in administrative sanctions against several individuals for violating electoral codes. Criminal proceedings were initiated against key actors, with preliminary court decisions imposing fines totaling approximately €50,000. Platforms were required to remove content, and monitoring mechanisms were enhanced for future elections.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: EU Directive 2019/1156 on transparency of political advertising
Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: Romania Constitutional Court Ruling on Candidate Eligibility 2024
example_background: In preparation for the 2024 European Parliament elections, the Romanian Constitutional Court reviewed challenges to candidate registrations from diaspora communities. Several candidates faced objections based on residency requirements, prompting a detailed examination of their rights to stand for election. The case highlighted procedural discrepancies in how electoral authorities verified documentation for candidates residing outside Romania, affecting approximately 20 individuals. This occurred against the backdrop of Romania's large expatriate voter base, which has historically influenced EP election outcomes. The court process involved analysis of national laws implementing EU electoral rights, ensuring consistency with foundational principles.
process_and_outcome: The Court upheld the rights of 15 candidates to stand, striking down restrictive interpretations by electoral bodies. One case was remanded for further verification, leading to updated guidelines for future validations. No fines were imposed, but procedural reforms were recommended.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Act on the Election of European Parliament Members (Law No. 33/2007)
Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: UK Electoral Commission Action Against Voter Suppression 2019
example_background: Ahead of the 2019 European Parliament elections (pre-Brexit extension), the UK Electoral Commission identified campaigns that discouraged certain groups from voting through misleading mailings and online ads. These efforts targeted ethnic minorities and younger voters, falsely claiming registration issues or ineligibility. Over 1,000 complaints were received, with activities linked to third-party groups not officially registered as campaigners. The incident unfolded during a politically charged period with high voter turnout concerns, emphasizing the need for unimpeded exercise of voting rights as per EU-derived standards still applicable at the time.
process_and_outcome: Investigations led to fines of £35,000 against two organizations and referrals to police for potential criminal offenses. Reporting requirements were strengthened, and public awareness campaigns were launched.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: Germany Federal Returning Officer Guidelines Implementation 2024
example_background: For the 2024 European Parliament elections, Germany's Federal Returning Officer issued comprehensive guidelines ensuring equal access for candidates from all member states residing in Germany. This included streamlined nomination processes and multilingual support for voter registration. The measures addressed past disparities noted in 2019 elections, where non-German EU citizens faced documentation hurdles. Implementation involved training for over 400 local electoral offices and digital platforms for candidate verification, supporting over 5,000 candidates nationwide.
process_and_outcome: Elections proceeded without major disputes, with turnout reaching 60.9%. Post-election audits confirmed adherence, serving as a model for other member states.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: France CNCCEP Decision on Candidate Disqualification 2019
example_background: The Constitutional Council of France (via Conseil National des Candidats aux Elections Professionnelles) reviewed a challenge to a candidate's eligibility for the 2019 EP elections due to undeclared dual nationality issues. The candidate, a French-Polish national, had not properly disclosed status during nomination, leading to objections from competitors. The case involved scrutiny of over 80 nominations in France, amid debates on transparency in cross-border candidacies.
process_and_outcome: The Council disqualified the candidate after finding procedural non-compliance, imposing no additional fines but ordering re-nominations in the district. The decision was upheld on appeal.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: Italy Data Protection Authority Fine for Voter Data Misuse 2024
example_background: In the lead-up to 2024 EP elections, Italy's data protection authority investigated a political party's use of personal data to target voters via unsolicited messages questioning their voting rights. The campaign reached 500,000 individuals, sourced from leaked public registries. This raised concerns over privacy intersecting with electoral rights, especially for vulnerable groups like recent immigrants eligible to vote.
process_and_outcome: A €1.2 million fine was issued, with orders to cease processing and delete data. The party appealed, but the decision was confirmed in administrative court.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: Spain Best Practice in Remote Voting for EP Elections 2024
example_background: Spain introduced secure remote voting options for the 2024 EP elections, enabling EU citizens abroad to exercise their right to vote without physical presence. The system used blockchain-verified digital IDs, piloted successfully in regional votes. Over 100,000 expatriates participated, with extensive awareness campaigns in 20 languages to ensure accessibility for standing candidates as well.
process_and_outcome: Implementation achieved 85% satisfaction in post-election surveys, with zero reported integrity issues. It was praised in EU reports as innovative adherence.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Organic Law 5/1985 on General Electoral Regime
Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: Poland Electoral Commission Handling of Candidate Challenges 2019
example_background: During the 2019 EP elections, Poland's National Electoral Commission processed over 50 challenges to candidate nominations, primarily related to signature collection validity for independents. Issues arose from discrepancies in verification processes across voivodeships, affecting candidates' ability to stand in multiple constituencies.
process_and_outcome: Most challenges were dismissed, allowing candidates to proceed; three were upheld with fines of PLN 20,000 each. Procedural handbook was revised post-elections.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: EU-Wide OSCE/ODIHR Election Observation Report 2024
example_background: The OSCE Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights observed the 2024 EP elections across 27 member states, noting varied practices in upholding voting and candidacy rights. Reports highlighted successful implementations in Nordic countries contrasting with administrative delays in Southern Europe, based on monitoring 10,000 polling stations and interviews with 2,000 stakeholders.
process_and_outcome: Recommendations were issued for 12 states to improve candidate verification; no enforcement actions, but influenced national reforms.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at elections to the European Parliament
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 39)

example_name: Case C-116/18 P Christos Kalaitzakis v Greece
example_background: A Greek national of Greek origin residing in Germany sought to register on the electoral roll to participate in municipal elections in his municipality of origin in Greece. He submitted an application to the relevant municipal authority, which rejected it on the grounds that he did not meet the condition of having his permanent residence in that municipality. The applicant then brought an action before the Athens Administrative Court of Appeal, which dismissed his action, leading to an appeal before the Council of State. The case raised questions about the interpretation of EU law regarding the right to vote and stand as a candidate in municipal elections for Union citizens residing in another Member State. The Court of Justice of the EU was asked to clarify whether national legislation requiring permanent residence in the specific municipality for electoral registration was compatible with EU provisions on foundation rights.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice ruled on 26 February 2019 that Article 8(1) of Directive 94/80/EC precludes a Member State from refusing to register on the electoral roll a Union citizen who is not a national of that Member State solely because that citizen does not have his permanent residence in the municipality concerned. The Court emphasized that the directive requires registration based on residence in the Member State and proof of residence, without additional municipal-level requirements. Greece was required to adjust its practices accordingly.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (adjudicator)
relevant_rules: Directive 94/80/EC on the right of non-national EU citizens to vote and stand as candidates in municipal elections

example_name: Case C-313/01 Morgenbesser v Italy
example_background: A French national residing in Italy applied to register as a student at a private university in Italy but was refused enrollment because she lacked an equivalency certificate for her French baccalauréat diploma. She pursued studies anyway and later sought recognition of her qualifications. The case extended to broader residence rights, including political rights under EU foundation rights. The Italian authorities required additional documentation not mandated by EU law, impacting her ability to fully exercise residence-based rights, which intersect with electoral participation rights for municipal elections in her place of residence.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice ruled on 11 September 2003 that Italy must recognize the equivalence of the French diploma under Directive 89/48/EEC. The decision reinforced residence rights for EU citizens, indirectly supporting electoral rights by ensuring lawful residence documentation for voting in municipal elections. Italy implemented the ruling by granting recognition.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union
relevant_rules: Directive 89/48/EEC on mutual recognition of diplomas

example_name: Spain's Electoral Registry Implementation for EU Citizens
example_background: Spain maintains a specific section in its electoral census, the Census Electoral de Residentes Temporales (CERE), for non-Spanish EU citizens residing in Spain who wish to exercise their right to vote in municipal elections. EU citizens must apply for inclusion in this census by submitting proof of residence, such as empadronamiento registration, and a declaration of intent to vote in Spanish municipal elections rather than their home state. This process has been operational since the transposition of Directive 94/80/EC into Spanish law via Organic Law 5/1985 and subsequent amendments. Thousands of EU citizens, particularly from neighboring France, Portugal, Germany, and Italy, register annually, enabling participation in local elections across municipalities like Barcelona, Madrid, and Valencia.
process_and_outcome: The system processes applications efficiently through local town halls and the Instituto Nacional de Estadística (INE), with over 300,000 EU citizens registered in recent elections. No major disputes have arisen, and the European Commission has not initiated infringement proceedings against Spain on this matter, indicating effective implementation.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections
relevant_rules: Organic Law 5/1985 on the General Electoral Regime

example_name: Case C-108/09 Gerda Müller v Austria
example_background: An Italian national residing in Austria applied to vote in municipal elections in the municipality where she lived. Austrian authorities refused her registration because national legislation required EU citizens to apply within a three-month period before elections and provide additional proof of residence beyond standard requirements. The refusal led to a legal challenge, highlighting discrepancies in how Austria implemented EU directives on political rights for mobile Union citizens.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice ruled that Austria's time limit and additional requirements were not provided for in Directive 94/80/EC and thus precluded. Austria amended its electoral laws post-ruling to align with EU standards.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: Denmark's EU Citizen Municipal Voting System
example_background: Denmark transposed Directive 94/80/EC through the European Parliament Election Act and municipal election regulations, allowing EU citizens residing in Denmark to vote and stand as candidates in local council elections after registering with the local authority and declaring intent within a specified timeframe. The system is managed by the Ministry of Ecclesiastical Affairs and local municipalities, with automatic inclusion in the electoral roll upon empadronamiento-like registration. This has enabled participation by citizens from Sweden, Germany, and other EU states in elections in Copenhagen, Aarhus, and other areas.
process_and_outcome: The framework has facilitated smooth participation, with EU citizens holding seats on local councils. Compliance verified by the European Commission without infringement actions.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections

example_name: Case C-145/04 Spain v United Kingdom (Gibraltar Electoral Rights)
example_background: The case involved the enfranchisement of non-resident British citizens in Gibraltar for European Parliament elections, but it intersected with municipal rights discussions. Spain challenged the UK's extension of voting rights in Gibraltar elections to non-residents, arguing it diluted residence-based rights under EU law. The Court examined the framework for municipal and European elections, clarifying residence requirements for foundation rights.
process_and_outcome: The Court ruled on 12 September 2006 that Gibraltar's system for European elections was incompatible with EU law due to non-resident voting, but upheld residence-based municipal rights. UK adjusted Gibraltar's electoral system.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections

example_name: Netherlands' Basisregistratie Personen (BRP) Electoral Integration
example_background: The Netherlands integrates EU citizens' registration in the Personal Records Database (BRP) at municipal level, automatically notifying them of eligibility to vote in municipal elections. EU residents receive a stemgerechtigheid (voting right) declaration upon proof of residence, allowing participation and candidacy without additional hurdles. This system supports elections in Amsterdam, Rotterdam, and other cities, with high registration rates among EU migrants.
process_and_outcome: Effective implementation leads to robust participation; no EU infringement proceedings. Regular audits confirm alignment with directives.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections
involved_parties: Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations

example_name: European Commission Infringement against Luxembourg (2004)
example_background: Luxembourg's initial implementation of Directive 94/80/EC imposed a six-week pre-election registration deadline and required a certificate from the home Member State confirming no deprivation of electoral rights, which exceeded EU requirements and created barriers for EU citizens wishing to vote in municipal elections.
process_and_outcome: The Commission initiated infringement proceedings in 2004, leading to a reasoned opinion. Luxembourg amended its Electoral Code in 2005 to remove the excessive requirements, bringing it into conformity.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections

example_name: Ireland's Local Government Reform for EU Citizens
example_background: Ireland updated its Electoral Act following Directive 94/80/EC, allowing EU citizens on the electoral register based on residential qualification to vote in local elections. Registration occurs via local authorities, with proof of address and identity, enabling participation in Dublin, Cork, and other councils.
process_and_outcome: Successful transposition with EU citizens serving on local councils; compliance affirmed by Commission reports.
source_rule: Right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections

example_name: Spain's Organic Law on Suffrage Violation Case (2007)
example_background: In Spain, a non-national EU citizen residing in the country for several years sought to register to vote in municipal elections but was denied based on administrative requirements that effectively applied stricter conditions than those for Spanish nationals. The individual had fulfilled residency duration but faced additional documentation hurdles not imposed on nationals, leading to exclusion from the electoral roll. This occurred amid broader debates on integrating EU citizens' voting rights post the 1992 Treaty on European Union which enshrined municipal voting rights for EU residents. The case highlighted discrepancies in local implementation across municipalities where registration processes varied, sometimes requiring proof of long-term intent to reside or other nationality-specific validations.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Justice ruled that Spain's practices constituted unequal treatment. The court ordered alignment of procedures and Spain amended administrative guidelines to standardize registration for EU citizens equivalent to nationals. No direct fine was imposed but the ruling set precedent for uniform application.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.
relevant_rules: Article 8b(1) of the Treaty establishing the European Community (now Article 20 TFEU)
Directive 94/80/EC on electoral arrangements for EU citizens in municipal elections
Spanish Organic Law 5/1985 on the Electoral Regime

example_name: Commission v Belgium Municipal Voting Case (1995)
example_background: Belgium implemented municipal electoral laws that required EU citizen residents to obtain special authorization from their home Member State to vote and stand as candidates, a process not required for Belgian nationals. This created additional bureaucratic layers including declarations from home country authorities confirming no deprivation of voting rights. The system affected thousands of EU citizens living in Belgium, particularly in Brussels where cross-border residency is common, and delayed or prevented participation in local elections despite meeting residency criteria.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Justice found the requirement disproportionate and infringing equal treatment. Belgium was required to repeal the provisions and simplify procedures. The case resulted in legislative changes by 1996 to allow direct registration upon proof of residency.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: Commission v Luxembourg (2006)
example_background: Luxembourg's electoral laws excluded EU citizens from standing as candidates in municipal elections unless they had resided there for at least five years prior, a condition not applied to Luxembourg nationals who could stand immediately upon citizenship. This affected professionals and workers from other EU states residing in Luxembourg, limiting their political engagement in local governance despite equal voting rights being granted. The policy stemmed from concerns over integration but created de facto discrimination in candidacy access.
process_and_outcome: ECJ ruled the residency requirement for candidacy incompatible with EU law. Luxembourg amended its electoral code in 2008 to remove the distinction, allowing EU residents to stand under same conditions as nationals after standard residency proof.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: Denmark's Implementation of EU Municipal Voting Rights (Post-1994)
example_background: Following Directive 94/80/EC, Denmark established a streamlined registration system for EU citizens residing in municipalities to vote and stand in local elections. EU residents automatically receive voter cards upon municipal registration if they declare intent to vote, with conditions identical to Danish nationals: proof of residence and age. This system has been operational since 1994, enabling participation in elections without additional nationality-based hurdles, and includes provisions for candidacy upon meeting residency thresholds applied equally.
process_and_outcome: No infringement proceedings; the European Commission recognized Denmark's full compliance in implementation reports. EU citizens have regularly participated, with turnout data tracked in national statistics.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.
involved_parties: Ministry of Ecclesiastical Affairs (now Interior Ministry)
relevant_rules: Danish Local Elections Act (Bekendtgørelse af lov om kommunalbestyrelsesvalg)

example_name: Netherlands Equal Access for EU Voters Model
example_background: The Netherlands integrated EU municipal voting rights into its national electoral framework, allowing all EU citizens registered in a municipality to vote and stand for local councils under identical procedures as Dutch nationals. Registration occurs via the Personal Records Database (BRP), with automatic inclusion on electoral rolls upon residency declaration. This applies to elections for municipal councils where EU residents can nominate and be elected without extra conditions, facilitating integration in diverse urban areas like Amsterdam and Rotterdam.
process_and_outcome: Sustained compliance affirmed in EU Commission reports; no adverse rulings. Regular elections see EU citizen participation, with guidelines updated for clarity.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.
involved_parties: Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations

example_name: Ireland's Local Elections for EU Citizens
example_background: Ireland permits EU citizens residing in local electoral areas to vote and be nominated as candidates in local elections under the same timelines and qualifications as Irish nationals. Registration involves applying to the local authority with proof of residence and EU citizenship, mirroring national processes. This has enabled participation since 1997, with EU residents serving on councils in areas with high expat populations like Dublin.
process_and_outcome: Compliance maintained without challenges; Electoral Commission oversees equal application, with public data on registrations.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: Commission v United Kingdom (Gibraltar Municipal Elections 2004)
example_background: In Gibraltar, part of the UK, electoral laws for local government restricted voting and candidacy to British citizens, excluding other EU nationals resident there despite Gibraltar's EU membership at the time. This affected Spanish and other EU workers commuting or residing, preventing their involvement in local polls under conditions differing from locals.
process_and_outcome: ECJ declared the rules incompatible, requiring extension of rights. UK amended Gibraltar's election laws post-ruling to include EU residents equally.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: Portugal's EU Citizen Municipal Voting Framework
example_background: Portugal allows EU citizens with residence registration to enroll on municipal electoral rolls for voting and candidacy under procedures matching those for Portuguese nationals. Declaration of intent is submitted to parishes or town halls, ensuring equal access in local elections across the country, including in regions with significant EU migrant communities.
process_and_outcome: Full alignment confirmed in EU audits; smooth implementation with voter guides in multiple languages.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.
relevant_rules: Portuguese Electoral Law for Local Authorities (Lei Eleitoral Autárquica)

example_name: Greece Infringement on Candidate Rights (2010s)
example_background: Greece initially imposed a 10-year residency requirement for EU citizens to stand as municipal candidates, longer than for Greek nationals, affecting long-term EU residents in Athens and islands seeking local office. The rule was part of broader electoral codes not fully transposed.
process_and_outcome: European Commission infringement proceedings led to 2018 law changes removing the barrier, standardizing conditions.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union has the right to vote and to stand as a candidate at municipal elections in the Member State in which he or she resides under the same conditions as nationals of that State.

example_name: European Commission v. Greece - Excessive Delays in Tax Procedures
example_background: Greece faced ongoing issues with prolonged processing times in its tax administration system, where taxpayers experienced significant delays in handling appeals, refunds, and assessments. This stemmed from understaffing, outdated IT systems, and high caseloads following the financial crisis, affecting thousands of individuals and businesses seeking resolution of their tax affairs. The delays often exceeded several years, leading to complaints filed with the European Court of Human Rights and infringement proceedings by the EU Commission. The situation was part of broader administrative inefficiencies in public services during Greece's economic adjustment programs.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings in 2018, sending a reasoned opinion. Greece undertook reforms including hiring additional staff and digitalizing processes, resulting in the closure of the case in 2021 after demonstrated improvements in average processing times.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
relevant_rules: Article 41 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights
Article 47 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: CJEU Case C-619/18 - Commission v. Italy on Judicial Delays
example_background: Italy's court system was characterized by extended durations for civil and administrative proceedings, with average times surpassing three years in first-instance courts and even longer on appeal. This impacted citizens' access to timely justice in matters involving public administration, such as contract disputes and administrative decisions. Contributing factors included insufficient judicial resources, complex procedural rules, and a backlog of over 3 million cases, prompting multiple EU infringement actions over the years.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU ruled against Italy in 2020, finding systemic failures. Italy was ordered to implement structural reforms, leading to legislative changes like mandatory mediation and increased magistrate recruitment, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union
relevant_rules: Article 47 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: European Ombudsman Decision 1909-2015/MHZ on EFSA Delays
example_background: A complainant experienced a 15-month delay by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) in handling a freedom of information request related to pesticide assessments. The delay arose from internal resource constraints and prioritization of other tasks, despite EFSA's commitment to process such requests within 15 working days as per EU transparency rules. This case highlighted broader challenges in EU agencies balancing workloads with timely responses to public inquiries.
process_and_outcome: The European Ombudsman found maladministration due to the unreasonable delay, recommending EFSA adopt stricter timelines and tracking systems. EFSA implemented changes, including better resource allocation, and closed the case without further escalation.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights
Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001 on public access to documents

example_name: EBA Decision on ECB Supervisory Delays (2019)
example_background: The European Banking Authority reviewed complaints against the European Central Bank regarding delays in processing authorization applications for fintech firms under its prudential supervision. Processing times averaged over 12 months, exceeding the 6-month legal deadline, due to complex assessments and high application volumes post-Brexit. This affected multiple institutions seeking to operate in the Single Supervisory Mechanism.
process_and_outcome: The EBA issued a decision in 2019 upholding some complaints, requiring the ECB to improve timelines. The ECB responded by streamlining procedures and increasing staff, reducing average times to under 9 months by 2021.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: EU Digital Public Administration Best Practice - Estonia e-Government
example_background: Estonia developed a fully digital public administration system through its e-Estonia initiative, enabling over 99% of government services to be provided online. Citizens can handle tax filings, business registrations, and social benefits applications within hours or days via secure digital IDs. This system was built on interoperability standards and blockchain technology, ensuring efficient, transparent processing across ministries and local governments.
process_and_outcome: The model has been recognized by the EU as a benchmark, with Estonia consistently ranking first in EU digital public services indices. No major complaints on delays, contributing to high citizen satisfaction scores.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: European Commission Infringement 2014/2142 - Belgium Social Security Delays
example_background: Belgium's social security administration encountered prolonged processing times for disability and unemployment benefit claims, often lasting 6-12 months due to manual verifications, fragmented regional systems, and staffing shortages. This affected thousands of applicants, many of whom faced financial hardship during waits, leading to widespread complaints and EU scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: The Commission launched infringement proceedings in 2014, culminating in a 2017 reasoned opinion. Belgium digitized processes and centralized data, reducing times to under 3 months by 2020, resolving the case.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Court of Justice Case C-416/17 - Commission v. Cyprus Asylum Delays
example_background: Cyprus asylum authorities processed applications with average durations exceeding 18 months, far beyond the 6-month EU deadline, due to overwhelmed reception facilities, insufficient decision-makers, and backlogs from migration influxes. This impacted thousands of applicants' rights to timely status determination and support.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled in 2018 that Cyprus failed obligations. Cyprus hired more staff and accelerated procedures, bringing compliance in line with EU timelines by 2022.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Finnish Tax Administration Digital Service Model
example_background: Finland's Vero Skat (Tax Administration) implemented a comprehensive digital platform, MyTax, allowing real-time filing, pre-filled returns, and instant feedback on queries. Over 90% of taxpayers use it, with processing times for refunds under 2 weeks and appeals resolved in months, supported by AI-driven case management and 24/7 access.
process_and_outcome: Recognized in EU DESI reports as a leader in digital public services, with low complaint rates and high efficiency metrics, serving as a model for other member states.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: European Ombudsman Case 2630/2017/EA on EMA Medicine Authorization Delays
example_background: The European Medicines Agency delayed centralized marketing authorization for orphan drugs by up to 8 months beyond the 210-day standard, attributed to scientific committee workloads and additional data requests. This affected pharmaceutical companies and patients awaiting treatments.
process_and_outcome: Ombudsman found the delays unjustified in 2018, prompting EMA to revise timelines and prioritization protocols, achieving consistent adherence thereafter.
source_rule: 1.   Every person has the right to have his or her affairs handled impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time by the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union.
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: European Ombudsman Decision on Glyphosate Renewal Transparency
example_background: The European Commission renewed the approval of glyphosate, a widely used herbicide, in 2017. A complaint was filed alleging that the Commission did not adequately inform stakeholders about the process, limited access to scientific studies, and excluded public input during the risk assessment phase. The renewal process involved multiple scientific committees and member states, but the complainant argued that the Commission failed to provide clear timelines, reasoning for decisions, and opportunities for interested parties to submit evidence or participate effectively. This case highlighted concerns over transparency in executive decision-making at the EU level, particularly in handling controversial substances where public health and environmental impacts were at stake. The Ombudsman's inquiry examined whether the Commission conducted its administrative procedures with sufficient openness and impartiality.
process_and_outcome: The European Ombudsman found maladministration due to lack of transparency and ordered the Commission to explain its handling of conflicting scientific opinions and improve stakeholder involvement. The Commission responded by publishing more documents and clarifying procedures, though no financial penalties were imposed.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41
Regulation (EC) No 1107/2009 on plant protection products

example_name: Ombudsman Ruling on EFSA Glyphosate Assessment Access
example_background: The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) conducted a peer review of glyphosate's risks in 2015 ahead of the EU renewal decision. A member of the European Parliament requested access to the scientific studies used in the assessment, but EFSA denied full access citing confidentiality of industry-submitted data. The requestor contended that this refusal prevented effective scrutiny of the safety evaluation process, which involved over 100 studies, and limited parliamentary oversight. EFSA provided summaries and redacted versions but maintained that raw data protection was necessary to encourage industry submissions. The case raised issues about balancing commercial confidentiality with public access to information in regulatory science.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman determined maladministration for insufficient justification of confidentiality claims and recommended EFSA reconsider access. EFSA later released more documents and updated its transparency policy.
source_rule: Right to good administration
involved_parties: Member of the European Parliament (Bart Staes)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41
Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001 on public access to documents

example_name: Irish Data Protection Commission GDPR Complaint Handling
example_background: An individual lodged a complaint with the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) regarding Meta Platforms Ireland's processing of personal data under GDPR. The complainant raised concerns about data transfers to the US and the lawfulness of processing for behavioral advertising. The DPC's investigation involved coordination with other EU supervisory authorities through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) mechanism. The process included multiple draft decisions, requests for additional information from Meta, and input from concerned authorities across the EU. Delays occurred due to the complexity of cross-border data flows and differing national interpretations.
process_and_outcome: The DPC issued a final decision in 2023 fining Meta €1.2 billion for unlawful data transfers, ordering the cessation of transfers using the invalidated Privacy Shield, and requiring implementation of new safeguards. Meta appealed the decision.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41

example_name: European Commission Handling of COVID-19 Vaccine Contracts
example_background: During the COVID-19 pandemic, the European Commission negotiated advance purchase agreements with multiple pharmaceutical companies for vaccine procurement on behalf of EU member states. A complaint alleged lack of transparency in contract negotiations, redactions in published agreements, and insufficient disclosure of pricing and delivery terms. The Commission justified redactions on commercial confidentiality grounds but released redacted versions after public pressure. The process involved joint negotiation committees and member state representatives, with total contracts valued at over €70 billion.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman found no maladministration but recommended further transparency improvements. The Commission published additional contract details and amended its transparency policy for future procurements.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41

example_name: Luxembourg Tax Ruling Investigation by Commission
example_background: The European Commission investigated selective tax rulings issued by Luxembourg authorities to Amazon, allowing the company to channel profits through European operations with reduced tax liability. The probe examined whether the rulings constituted state aid by providing selective advantages not available to other companies. Amazon's structure involved an IP holding company in Luxembourg that licensed rights to affiliates, generating royalty payments. The Commission analyzed over 200,000 pages of documents and consulted economic experts on transfer pricing practices.
process_and_outcome: In 2017, the Commission ordered Luxembourg to recover €250 million in unpaid taxes from Amazon. The decision was partially annulled by the General Court in 2021, finding insufficient evidence of selective advantage.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41

example_name: Ombudsman Decision on EU-US Privacy Shield Adequacy
example_background: Following the Schrems I ruling invalidating Safe Harbour, the European Commission adopted an adequacy decision for the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2016. Privacy advocates challenged the decision, arguing inadequate protection against US surveillance. The Commission conducted consultations, public hearings, and dialogues with US authorities but faced criticism for insufficient scrutiny of US laws like FISA 702 and Executive Order 12333. The administrative process included draft decisions and stakeholder input from civil society, industry, and data protection authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU invalidated Privacy Shield in Schrems II (2020), citing deficiencies in US legal protections. The Commission revised adequacy assessment procedures post-ruling.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41
GDPR Chapter V on international transfers

example_name: Finnish Border Guard Visa Processing Delays
example_background: A Schengen visa applicant experienced significant delays in processing by the Finnish Border Guard, exceeding statutory timelines without adequate communication or justification. The applicant requested updates multiple times but received generic responses. The process involved document verification, consultations with other Schengen states, and security checks, but the authority failed to provide reasoned explanations for extensions or interim decisions.
process_and_outcome: The European Ombudsman found maladministration and recommended the Border Guard improve communication and decision timelines. The authority adopted new guidelines for handling visa applications.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41

example_name: EUIPO Trade Mark Appeal Procedures Reform
example_background: Following Ombudsman recommendations, the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) reformed its appeal procedures to enhance impartiality and transparency. Previously, the same boards handled appeals without sufficient separation from initial decisions. The improvements included establishing independent appeal boards, clearer reasoning in decisions, and better access to files for parties. The changes applied to thousands of annual trade mark and design cases.
process_and_outcome: EUIPO implemented the reforms, leading to higher satisfaction rates in user surveys and fewer complaints about procedural fairness.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41

example_name: EBA Consumer Complaint Handling Investigation
example_background: The European Banking Authority (EBA) reviewed how EU banks handled consumer complaints related to payment services and loans. The review found variations in response times, acknowledgment processes, and resolution rates across institutions. Some banks provided detailed updates and reasoned decisions, while others offered minimal information.
process_and_outcome: EBA issued guidelines in 2021 mandating standardized complaint handling, including timelines and transparency requirements, which banks adopted to align practices.
source_rule: Right to good administration
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 41

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Right to Erasure Fine
example_background: In December 2019, the French data protection authority investigated Google Ireland Limited regarding its handling of user requests to remove personal data from search results under the right to be forgotten. The inquiry stemmed from complaints where Google applied delisting only to EU domain versions of its search engine rather than globally, and failed to extend the removal to all relevant search results. Google processed vast amounts of personal data through its search engine, serving over 90% of searches in France, and the case highlighted challenges in balancing this right with public interest and freedom of information. The authority noted that Google's policies did not fully align with requirements for effective implementation across its services.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a €50 million fine to Google Ireland, marking one of the first major penalties under GDPR for right to erasure issues. Google was ordered to comply within two months and implement necessary measures globally where appropriate. The company appealed but later adjusted its processes.
source_rule: 2.   This right includes:

example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Subject Rights Fine
example_background: British Airways faced scrutiny after a cyber-attack in 2018 exposed personal data of approximately 400,000 customers, including payment card details and travel information. Beyond the breach, the UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) examined the airline's response to data subject rights requests, such as access and rectification, amid delays and incomplete responses during the incident aftermath. The company operates extensive passenger data systems for booking, loyalty programs, and customer management across global operations.
process_and_outcome: ICO imposed a £20 million fine in October 2020 for failures including inadequate handling of data subject rights. British Airways implemented enhanced security and rights processing procedures as part of remediation efforts.
source_rule: 2.   This right includes:

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Right to Access Fine
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database with personal data of up to 383 million guests, including names, addresses, phone numbers, emails, and payment information. A breach was discovered in 2018, but investigations revealed prior unauthorized access dating back to 2014. The ICO also found that Marriott failed to inform users about data transfers post-acquisition and did not properly handle data subject access requests, delaying responses and lacking transparency in data processing activities spanning loyalty programs and reservations worldwide.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million for multiple GDPR infringements, including rights handling deficiencies. Marriott cooperated and enhanced its data protection framework.
source_rule: 2.   This right includes:

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Right to Object Fine
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited, part of Meta Platforms, was investigated for its 'pay or consent' model introduced in late 2023 for EU users, which conditioned access to personalized advertising services on consent. The model raised concerns about processing special categories of data for behavioral advertising without sufficient user control over objections. With over 400 million EU users, the platform collects extensive data via likes, shares, and tracking across its ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: In January 2025, the Irish Data Protection Commission fined Meta €200 million for violations related to inadequate handling of rights to object and withdraw consent. Meta announced plans to appeal and modify its model.
source_rule: 2.   This right includes:

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children's Rights Fine
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited processed personal data of up to 1.4 million EU children under 16 without proper parental consent mechanisms, exposing them to harmful content and inadequate age verification. The platform's algorithmic feeds and data practices were scrutinized for failing to honor parental rights to access, rectify, or delete children's data promptly.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the Irish DPC imposed a €345 million fine, one of the largest for children's data rights issues. TikTok agreed to overhaul its child safety and rights fulfillment processes.
source_rule: 2.   This right includes:

example_name: Apple GDPR Transparency and Rights Compliance
example_background: Apple Inc. developed its Privacy Nutrition Labels and App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework in iOS 14.5 (2021), allowing users to control cross-app tracking and providing detailed disclosures on data collection practices. This enabled straightforward exercise of rights to object to processing for advertising, with over 80% of EU users opting out initially. Apple's ecosystem processes data for millions via App Store, iCloud, and device features.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators acknowledged the features as exceeding GDPR minimums, avoiding enforcement actions. Apple received commendations for enhancing user rights implementation.

example_name: H&M GDPR Employee Data Rights Fine
example_background: H&M employed a 'welcome-back interview' practice where returning employees' private conversations about health, family, and stress were noted by managers and stored in HR systems without consent. This data was accessed by over 50 colleagues across Germany and used for performance decisions, affecting thousands of workers over years.
process_and_outcome: In October 2023, Hamburg DPA fined H&M Justice CV €35.25 million. The company discontinued the practice and improved data governance.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Scraping Rights Violation
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent, building a database sold to law enforcement for facial recognition. EU residents' data was included, and the company ignored rights requests to delete images, claiming public availability justified processing.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs, coordinated via EDPB, fined Clearview €30.5 million in 2022 (Dutch DPA lead). Binding decisions required cessation of EU data processing; Clearview restricted services but faced ongoing enforcement.

example_name: Microsoft GDPR Data Subject Services Success
example_background: Microsoft launched its GDPR Data Subject Request (DSR) portal in 2018, allowing EU users to exercise rights like access, portability, and deletion across services including Azure, Office 365, and Xbox. The system handles millions of requests annually with automated fulfillment where possible and human review for complex cases, achieving response times under 30 days.
process_and_outcome: No major fines issued; Microsoft earned ISO 27701 privacy certification and positive EDPB feedback for scalable rights tools.

example_name: Ryanair Passenger Compensation Delays
example_background: Ryanair, a low-cost airline operating across Europe, faced multiple complaints regarding delays in processing compensation payments to passengers affected by flight cancellations and delays. This occurred during periods of high operational disruptions, such as staff strikes and the COVID-19 pandemic aftermath, where thousands of passengers sought reimbursements for expenses and standardized compensation under EU air passenger rights. The airline's automated systems and customer service processes were reported to have bottlenecks, leading to extended waiting times for refunds that impacted passengers' access to timely financial redress for essential travel-related services. National enforcement bodies noted patterns of non-compliance in handling claims efficiently, affecting social cohesion by disproportionately burdening lower-income travelers reliant on affordable air travel.
process_and_outcome: The UK Civil Aviation Authority investigated complaints and secured commitments from Ryanair to improve processes, resulting in accelerated payments totaling millions of euros. In Italy, the transport authority fined Ryanair €4.5 million in 2019 for unfair practices including compensation delays. Similar actions in other member states led to operational changes and public settlements.
source_rule: The Union recognises and respects access to services of general economic interest as provided for in national laws and practices, in accordance with the Treaties, in order to promote the social and territorial cohesion of the Union.

example_name: EDF French Public Electricity Service Obligations
example_background: Électricité de France (EDF), the state-majority-owned utility, maintains universal service obligations for electricity provision across France, ensuring continuous supply to all households including remote and vulnerable areas. This includes tariff protections for low-income customers via the 'tarif de première nécessité' and investments in grid maintenance to prevent outages in rural regions. Amid energy market liberalization, EDF balanced commercial operations with public service mandates, funding social programs and territorial grid expansions to uphold access standards amid rising demand and renewable transitions.
process_and_outcome: EDF received regulatory approval for public service compensation mechanisms from the European Commission in 2020, confirming compliance and state aid adjustments totaling €3.4 billion annually. Performance audits by French energy regulator CRE affirmed fulfillment of service quality targets with minimal disruptions.

example_name: Enel Universal Electricity Service in Italy
example_background: Enel, Italy's primary electricity distributor, operates the universal service for non-interconnected zones, providing power to remote islands like Sicily and Sardinia, as well as mainland vulnerable consumers. This involves dedicated infrastructure investments and subsidized tariffs to ensure equitable access, supporting territorial cohesion in geographically diverse regions prone to isolation. During economic downturns, Enel expanded vulnerability assessments to maintain supply continuity.

example_background: Deutsche Post DHL, holder of Germany's universal postal service license, was examined for practices in its reserved letter mail segment that potentially extended beyond legal monopoly bounds into competitive parcel markets. This involved pricing structures and cross-subsidization allegations amid digital shifts reducing mail volumes, affecting competitors' access to downstream markets and overall service affordability for consumers across urban and rural Germany.
process_and_outcome: Bundeskartellamt imposed a €24.6 million fine in 2019 for abuse of dominance. The company appealed but implemented pricing adjustments and structural separations as mandated.

example_name: E.ON Sweden District Heating Obligations
example_background: E.ON, a major energy provider in Sweden, manages district heating networks classified as services of general economic interest, ensuring heat supply to residential and public buildings in urban and peri-urban areas during harsh winters. This includes capacity expansions and efficiency upgrades to maintain affordability and reliability.

example_name: Equifax Data Breach 2017
example_background: Equifax, one of the largest credit reporting agencies in the United States, experienced a massive data breach between May and July 2017, affecting approximately 147 million people. Hackers exploited a vulnerability in the Apache Struts web application framework that Equifax failed to patch despite a patch being available for two months. The breach exposed highly sensitive personal information including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and in some cases driver's license numbers and credit card details. Consumers were unable to access accurate information about the breach status or their own data exposure for weeks, and Equifax's notification processes were delayed and inadequate, preventing timely access to their personal files and credit reports. The incident raised significant concerns about consumer rights to access and understand their own data held by credit bureaus.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC), Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), and multiple state attorneys general investigated. Equifax agreed to a settlement exceeding $700 million, including up to $425 million for consumer compensation, $275 million in penalties to the CFPB, and $100 million to the FTC. The company also committed to enhanced data security and free credit monitoring for affected individuals.
source_rule: (b) the right of every person to have access to his or her file, while respecting the legitimate interests of confidentiality and of professional and business secrecy;

example_name: Cambridge Analytica Facebook Scandal
example_background: In 2018, it was revealed that Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, harvested personal data from millions of Facebook users without their explicit consent through a third-party app called 'thisisyourdigitallife.' The app collected data not only from users who installed it but also from their Facebook friends, totaling around 87 million profiles. This data was used for targeted political advertising during the 2016 U.S. presidential election and Brexit referendum. Facebook users were not informed about the data sharing, denied access to how their data was used or transferred, and had no straightforward way to review or retrieve their full data files from the platform, undermining transparency and access rights.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) and U.S. FTC investigated. Facebook was fined $5 billion by the FTC in 2019, the largest privacy penalty ever at the time. Cambridge Analytica ceased operations. Facebook implemented changes to its data-sharing policies and provided tools for users to download their data.
source_rule: (b) the right of every person to have access to his or her file, while respecting the legitimate interests of confidentiality and of professional and business secrecy;

EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: GDPR Enforcement Against Google (CNIL 2019)
example_background: France's data protection authority, CNIL, investigated Google's GDPR compliance following complaints about lack of transparency in personalized advertising. Google processed vast amounts of personal data for ad targeting without providing users with sufficiently clear information on data processing purposes, legal bases, and user rights. Users faced difficulties accessing comprehensive details about their data files, including what data was collected, retention periods, and recipients, due to complex privacy policies and opaque settings buried deep in the interface.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google €50 million in 2019, the first major GDPR fine. Google appealed but lost subsequent court challenges. The company revised its privacy notices and consent mechanisms to improve transparency.

Additionally, through iOS settings, users can download a copy of all their data stored by Apple services, including iCloud, App Store history, and Siri interactions. These tools provide granular access to personal files while maintaining Apple's business confidentiality through aggregated, non-identifiable insights shared with developers.
process_and_outcome: These practices have been praised in regulatory audits and user trust surveys. Apple avoided major GDPR or CCPA fines related to access rights and received positive feedback from the European Data Protection Board.

example_background: Marriott disclosed in 2018 that a breach affecting its Starwood reservation system, acquired earlier that year, had been ongoing since 2014. The incident compromised passport numbers, payment card details, and other personal data of up to 500 million guests. Affected individuals were not promptly notified, and Marriott did not provide easy mechanisms for guests to access full details of their compromised data files or verify the extent of exposure, delaying transparency.
process_and_outcome: The UK's ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million (reduced from £99 million on appeal) under GDPR. The U.S. FTC settled for $23.8 million in 2023. Marriott enhanced its data access portals and security measures.

Stolen data included names, addresses, payment card details, CVV numbers, and login credentials. Customers were not informed promptly, and the airline initially provided limited access to details about their specific data exposure, complicating efforts to review personal files.
process_and_outcome: The UK's ICO fined British Airways £20 million (reduced from £183 million on appeal). The airline improved its data subject access request processes and notification timelines.

example_name: Tjebbes v Minister voor Immigratie Case
example_background: A Dutch national residing in the Netherlands had her name removed from the Municipal Personal Records Database upon her death, which triggered the removal of her adult child's name from the register due to Dutch nationality laws. The adult child challenged this administrative decision, arguing that the authorities did not provide adequate reasons for the removal. The case progressed through Dutch courts and was referred to the Court of Justice of the EU for a preliminary ruling on the interpretation of EU law regarding the right to good administration. The proceedings examined the balance between data protection and the obligation to justify administrative decisions affecting individual rights. The Dutch Council of State initially upheld the decision but the matter was escalated to assess compliance with fundamental rights standards.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU ruled in 2019 that EU law requires Member States' administrations to provide reasons for decisions that adversely affect individuals, particularly when personal data processing is involved. The case was returned to national courts for further consideration, reinforcing the need for transparent reasoning in administrative actions.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.
involved_parties: Minister voor Immigratie, Integratie en Asiel (Dutch authority)
Raad van State (Dutch Council of State)
Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Article 41 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Menesheva v Russia ECtHR Judgment
example_background: The applicant was detained by Russian police following a complaint by her neighbor. She alleged excessive use of force during the arrest and subsequent detention conditions. The domestic authorities conducted an investigation but decided not to prosecute the police officers involved. The applicant complained that the authorities failed to provide sufficient reasons for closing the criminal investigation and for decisions related to her pre-trial detention. The case reached the European Court of Human Rights, where the adequacy of reasoning in administrative and judicial decisions was scrutinized under the European Convention on Human Rights framework.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the ECtHR found violations of Articles 3 and 5 of the ECHR, noting that the lack of detailed reasons for investigative decisions prevented effective remedies. Russia was ordered to pay compensation, and the judgment emphasized the duty to justify decisions impacting rights.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (analogous)

example_name: European Commission v Kingdom of Spain Infringement
example_background: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings against Spain for systemic failures in Spanish courts to provide adequate reasons for judgments, particularly in social security and employment disputes. Numerous cases demonstrated that court decisions lacked sufficient motivation, hindering the right to effective judicial protection. The Commission gathered evidence from preliminary references and complaints, highlighting patterns where national courts omitted explanations for rejecting appeals or applying EU law.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Court of Justice ruled that Spain failed to fulfill obligations under EU law, imposing a lump sum fine of €10 million and a daily penalty until compliance. Spain was required to implement training and reforms to ensure reasoned decisions.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Article 41 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: UK Home Office Visa Refusal Case (R (on the application of A) v Secretary of State)
example_background: An applicant for a UK visa had their application refused by the Home Office without detailed reasons, only generic templates stating non-satisfaction of requirements. The refusal letter did not explain how the specific evidence submitted failed to meet criteria, leading to a judicial review challenge. The case underscored issues in immigration administration where standardized refusals lacked individual justification.
process_and_outcome: The High Court quashed the decision in 2017, ruling it unlawful for failing to provide adequate reasons. The Home Office was ordered to reconsider with proper motivation, setting precedent for detailed reasoning in visa decisions.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.
relevant_rules: Common law duty to give reasons

example_name: Dutch Data Protection Authority GDPR Fine on Dutch Healthcare Insurer
example_background: A Dutch healthcare insurer processed sensitive personal data for risk equalization without providing clear reasons to data subjects for decisions affecting their premiums. When individuals queried automated decisions, the insurer's responses lacked transparency on the logic involved, prompting complaints to the Dutch DPA. The incident involved thousands of policyholders whose data was used in algorithmic models without sufficient explanation.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the Dutch DPA imposed a €2.1 million fine for violations of GDPR transparency obligations, requiring the insurer to enhance explanation processes for administrative decisions.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.

example_name: CNIL v Google GDPR Decision Transparency
example_background: The French data protection authority investigated Google's processing of personal data for advertising purposes. Data subjects challenged the lack of clear information and reasons provided for data handling decisions. Google had implemented notice and choice mechanisms, but the CNIL found initial shortcomings in explaining the purposes and legal basis for processing.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, CNIL imposed a €50 million fine but noted subsequent improvements by Google in providing reasoned transparency notices, demonstrating steps toward compliance after enforcement.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (later reference)

example_name: European Ombudsman Decision on Commission Refusal
example_background: A complainant requested access to documents from the European Commission regarding a legislative proposal. The Commission refused partial disclosure citing exceptions but provided a detailed statement of reasons, explaining the specific risks to protected interests and balancing tests applied. The Ombudsman reviewed whether the reasoning met standards of good administration.
process_and_outcome: In 2018, the Ombudsman found the Commission's decision well-reasoned and compliant, closing the case without further action and praising the transparency demonstrated.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on Automated Decision-Making
example_background: A company used automated algorithms for credit scoring, notifying applicants of refusals with explanations of the decision logic, factors considered, and right to human review. The Irish Data Protection Commission investigated following queries and confirmed the process included clear reasons for each outcome.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the DPC closed the investigation without sanctions, affirming the company's adherence to transparency requirements in administrative decisions.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.

example_name: 意大利 Autorità Garante Fine on ENI S.p.A.
example_background: ENI, an Italian energy company, processed employee data for internal mobility decisions without providing individualized reasons to affected workers. Employees challenged opaque internal transfers, leading to a DPA probe into justification practices.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Italian DPA fined ENI €1 million and mandated provision of reasoned decisions, with implementation monitored.
source_rule: (c) the obligation of the administration to give reasons for its decisions.
involved_parties: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italy DPA)

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica Scandal
example_background: In 2014-2015, the app 'thisisyourdigitallife' collected data from 87 million Facebook users worldwide without their explicit knowledge or consent through a personality quiz given to 270,000 users, exploiting Facebook's platform policies. The data was shared with Cambridge Analytica, which used it for political advertising targeting US voters in 2016 elections. Users were not informed that their Facebook friends' data was harvested, nor given an opportunity to object before their information was processed and shared externally. Facebook learned of the improper data use in 2015 but did not notify affected users until 2018.
process_and_outcome: FTC, ICO, and Irish DPC investigated. Facebook agreed to a 5 billion USD FTC settlement in 2019, including privacy program overhaul. ICO fined 500,000 pounds. Multiple class actions settled, and Facebook implemented new data access controls and user notifications.
source_rule: (a) the right of every person to be heard, before any individual measure which would affect him or her adversely is taken;
relevant_rules: EU GDPR Article 5 (principles relating to processing)
EU GDPR Article 14 (information where data not obtained from data subject)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 7 (respect for private life)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8 (protection of personal data)

example_name: Marital Status Discrimination Case (C-267/06)
example_background: Redwood Pharmacies dismissed an employee shortly after learning she had married, citing restructuring needs. The employee, who had informed her employer of her marriage, argued the termination was motivated by her new marital status. The company claimed operational reasons but provided inconsistent explanations during proceedings. This EU Court of Justice case arose from national proceedings in the UK where the employee sought protection under equal treatment directives, highlighting issues where personal status changes led to adverse employment actions without prior hearing or justification.
process_and_outcome: ECJ ruled in 2008 that marital status discrimination falls under EU sex discrimination law, requiring member states to protect against dismissal based on marriage. The case was referred back to UK courts, resulting in compensation for the employee.
source_rule: (a) the right of every person to be heard, before any individual measure which would affect him or her adversely is taken;
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 21 (non-discrimination)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47 (right to effective remedy)

example_name: GDPR Right to Be Forgotten Success - Mario Costeja González
example_background: In 1998, a Spanish newspaper published notices about Mario Costeja González's forced property auction for social security debts. By 2010, the debts were resolved, but the links appeared prominently in searches for his name. Costeja requested Google to delist the links under Spanish data protection law, arguing the information was outdated and irrelevant, impacting his professional reputation. Google initially refused, leading to AEPD referral to CJEU.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2014 that search engines must delist irrelevant, outdated personal data links upon request under certain conditions. Google delisted the links, establishing the 'right to be forgotten' framework applied millions of times since.
source_rule: (a) the right of every person to be heard, before any individual measure which would affect him or her adversely is taken;
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8 (personal data protection)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47 (effective remedy)

example_name: Apple's App Tracking Transparency Framework
example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in iOS 14.5 (2021), requiring apps to obtain explicit user permission before tracking them across apps and websites via the Advertising Identifier (IDFA). This addressed prior practices where apps tracked users without clear notice or choice, affecting privacy for over 1 billion iPhone users. Users now receive a prompt to 'Allow' or 'Ask App Not to Track,' with one-tap opt-out.
process_and_outcome: Implemented voluntarily ahead of full GDPR enforcement. Led to significant reduction in cross-app tracking rates (over 80% opt-out). Faced antitrust scrutiny but upheld in courts as privacy protection.
source_rule: (a) the right of every person to be heard, before any individual measure which would affect him or her adversely is taken;
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 7 (private life)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8 (data protection)

example_name: Uber GDPR Data Breach Investigation 2018
example_background: In 2016, hackers accessed data of 57 million Uber users and drivers globally, including names, emails, phone numbers, and driver license images.
