Uber paid hackers 100,000 USD to delete data and concealed the breach from regulators and users for over a year, violating notification duties. Affected individuals were not informed promptly, preventing them from taking protective measures.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined Uber 600,000 euros in 2018, UK's ICO fined 385,000 pounds (initial cap). Uber improved breach response protocols and reporting.
source_rule: (a) the right of every person to be heard, before any individual measure which would affect him or her adversely is taken;
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47 (right to remedy)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Children's Data Fine 2023
example_background: Meta's Instagram allowed accounts for users under 13 without parental consent verification, processing data of minors for behavioral advertising. From 2018-2020, children created public profiles sharing photos and location data visible to all, without age assurance or parental controls, exposing them to risks. Parents discovered and complained after seeing their children's public activity.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC fined Meta 405 million euros in September 2023 following investigations. Meta adjusted age verification and default privacy settings for minors.
source_rule: (a) the right of every person to be heard, before any individual measure which would affect him or her adversely is taken;
relevant_rules: EU GDPR Article 8 (conditions applicable to child's consent)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 24 (rights of the child)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47 (effective remedy)

example_name: European Commission v Kingdom of Belgium (C-43/13)
example_background: The case involved a Belgian national who suffered injuries during an arrest carried out by police officers acting under the authority of the Belgian state. The incident occurred in 2007 when the individual was detained and allegedly mishandled, leading to physical harm. The claimant sought compensation from the Belgian authorities, arguing that the state was responsible for the actions of its public servants. This matter was referred to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) for a preliminary ruling on the interpretation of Article 340 TFEU, which establishes the non-contractual liability of the European Union for damage caused by its institutions or servants in the performance of their duties. The national court sought clarification on whether similar principles apply to Member States and how general principles common to Member State laws should be applied in such scenarios. The background highlighted the need for uniform application of liability rules across EU Member States for acts performed by public officials during official duties, drawing parallels to EU institutional liability.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on 4 June 2015 that Article 340 TFEU does not preclude national courts from applying EU law principles to state liability for damage caused by national servants. The court emphasized that general principles common to Member State laws, such as fault-based liability, must be considered. The Belgian court subsequently awarded compensation to the claimant based on national tort law aligned with EU principles.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: General principles common to the laws of the Member States

example_name: Kadi v Council and Commission (C-402/05 P)
example_background: Yassin Abdullah Kadi, a Saudi national, had his funds frozen by the European Union as part of sanctions against individuals associated with Al-Qaeda and the Taliban, based on UN Security Council resolutions implemented through EU regulations. The assets freeze was enacted by EU institutions acting in performance of their duties to enforce international sanctions. Kadi challenged the measures before the General Court and subsequently the CJEU, claiming violation of his fundamental rights, including property rights, and seeking compensation for economic damage caused by the EU's actions. The case spanned several years with multiple proceedings, focusing on whether the EU institutions caused damage through their implementation of the sanctions regime and the adequacy of judicial review provided.
process_and_outcome: In 2008, the CJEU annulled the EU regulation insofar as it applied to Kadi due to insufficient protection of fundamental rights. Further proceedings in 2013 confirmed the annulment. Kadi received compensation for the damage caused by the EU institutions' initial actions. The case set precedents on EU non-contractual liability and rights protection.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union
relevant_rules: EU Common Foreign and Security Policy regulations
Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU

example_name: Schrems v Data Protection Commissioner (C-362/14)
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, initiated legal action against Facebook Ireland and sought involvement from EU institutions after revelations by Edward Snowden about US surveillance programs. He argued that data transfers from the EU to the US by EU-based companies, facilitated under the Safe Harbour framework invalidated by the CJEU, caused potential damage to his privacy rights. The case questioned the responsibility of EU institutions for endorsing data transfer mechanisms that exposed EU citizens to surveillance by third-country authorities acting in performance of their duties.
process_and_outcome: On 6 October 2015, the CJEU invalidated the Safe Harbour decision, finding it inadequate for protecting EU data subjects. Subsequent Privacy Shield arrangements faced similar challenges, leading to compensation discussions and regulatory overhauls. Schrems pursued further damages claims.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.

example_name: Francovich v Italy (C-6/90 and C-9/90)
example_background: Workers in Italy were not paid wages owed by their insolvent employer and sought compensation from the Italian state for failing to implement an EU directive on employer insolvency protection. The directive required Member States to guarantee payment of wage claims through state guarantee funds. Italy's delay in transposition meant public authorities did not establish such mechanisms, leading to financial damage for the employees. This case established the principle of state liability for breaches of EU law, analogous to EU institutional liability under Article 340 TFEU.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on 19 November 1991 that Member States are liable for damages caused to individuals by breaches of EU law attributable to the state. Italy was ordered to pay compensation to the workers, setting a landmark precedent for non-contractual liability.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union
relevant_rules: Directive 80/987/EEC on protection of employees in event of insolvency

example_name: Brasserie du Pêcheur v Germany (C-46/93)
example_background: A French brewery sought compensation from Germany for financial losses due to the state's incorrect transposition of an EU directive on the method of calculating excise duties on beer. Germany applied higher rates than permitted, causing competitive disadvantage and revenue loss for the claimant. The case examined state liability for damage caused by public authorities in implementing EU law, mirroring the EU's own liability regime for its institutions.
process_and_outcome: On 5 March 1996, the CJEU confirmed state liability, requiring three conditions: rule intended to confer rights on individuals, sufficiently serious breach, and direct causal link. Germany paid damages to the brewery.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: EU Compensation for Schengen Border Guard Incident (2018)
example_background: A traveler at a Schengen Area border crossing experienced injury during handling by European Border and Coast Guard Agency (Frontex) personnel performing duties under EU mandate. The individual filed a claim for damages against the EU institutions, citing physical harm from actions taken in official capacity. This incident underscored the application of EU non-contractual liability rules for operations involving agency staff acting on behalf of EU institutions.
process_and_outcome: The European Ombudsman investigated complaints about compensation processes. The EU awarded compensation following administrative review, in line with general principles of Member State tort laws.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.
involved_parties: Frontex (European Border and Coast Guard Agency)

example_name: European Ombudsman Decision on EU Staff Liability (2019)
example_background: An EU citizen complained about damage caused by negligence of EU civil servants during a public consultation process managed by DG CONNECT. The mishandling led to loss of business opportunities. The complaint was escalated to the European Ombudsman, who examined whether EU institutions fulfilled their duty to remedy damage caused by their servants.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman found maladministration in the initial response but confirmed the EU's eventual payment of compensation. The case was closed as resolved with remedy provided.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.

example_name: Commission v United Kingdom (C-446/04)
example_background: The European Commission brought an infringement action against the UK for failing to fully implement environmental directives, resulting in damage to protected habitats managed by public authorities. The non-compliance led to ecological harm attributable to state servants' oversight in permit issuance.
process_and_outcome: On 26 January 2006, the CJEU declared the UK in breach and imposed financial penalties in subsequent proceedings for non-compliance, enforcing state responsibility for remedial actions.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: Köbler v Austria (C-224/01)
example_background: A university professor in Austria was denied promotion due to a national supreme court misapplication of EU law on academic recognition. He claimed financial damage from the public authority's error, seeking state liability for judicial acts.
process_and_outcome: On 30 September 2003, the CJEU extended state liability to judicial decisions, requiring Austria to provide remedy. Compensation was awarded.
source_rule: 3.   Every person has the right to have the Union make good any damage caused by its institutions or by its servants in the performance of their duties, in accordance with the general principles common to the laws of the Member States.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: Access Info Europe v European Commission (Case T-309/13)
example_background: Access Info Europe, a non-profit organization focused on transparency, submitted a request in 2011 to the European Commission for access to a list of beneficiaries of EU lobbyist passes and their lobbyist categories. The Commission initially denied the request, citing privacy concerns under Regulation 1049/2001. Access Info appealed to the Commission, which partially granted access to anonymized data, but the organization sought full disclosure including names. This case arose amid broader debates on transparency in EU lobbying activities, highlighting tensions between public access rights and personal data protection. The request was part of efforts to monitor influence on EU policy-making.
process_and_outcome: The General Court annulled the Commission's decision in 2015, ruling that the public interest in transparency outweighed privacy exemptions. The Commission was ordered to re-examine the request. Following the judgment, the Commission disclosed additional information, and the case set a precedent for lobbyist transparency.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.
relevant_rules: Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001 on public access to documents

example_name: European Ombudsman Inquiry into European Commission's Handling of Transparency Requests (Case 888/2013)
example_background: In 2013, the European Ombudsman received a complaint from a requester denied access to documents related to the EU's Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership (TTIP) negotiations. The Commission withheld documents citing ongoing negotiations and third-party confidentiality. The inquiry examined multiple similar complaints about the Commission's practice of systematically refusing access to negotiation mandates and related correspondence, reflecting wider concerns over secrecy in trade talks involving civil society input.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman found maladministration in 2014, recommending proactive disclosure of redacted mandates. The Commission accepted and published redacted versions, improving TTIP transparency. This led to policy changes for future trade negotiations.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.

example_name: Council of the EU v Sophie in 't Veld (Case C-350/12 P)
example_background: MEP Sophie in 't Veld requested access in 2010 to documents from the Council concerning the draft Anti-Counterfeiting Trade Agreement (ACTA). The Council granted partial access but withheld full texts, invoking international relations exceptions. The case involved 54 documents exchanged with other parties, amid public protests against ACTA's secrecy. In 't Veld argued for greater openness to enable democratic scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice ruled in 2013 that the Council's refusal was unlawful, as public interest in transparency about an international agreement under EU competence prevailed. The Council released more documents post-judgment.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: European Commission's Proactive Disclosure of Legislative Documents Initiative
example_background: Since 2011, the European Commission has implemented a policy of systematically publishing documents related to legislative proposals on its public register upon adoption by the College of Commissioners. This includes impact assessments, proposals, and related studies, covering thousands of documents annually. The practice responds to calls for greater transparency in the EU legislative process, allowing citizens and stakeholders direct access without formal requests.
process_and_outcome: The initiative has resulted in over 10,000 documents published proactively by 2023, reducing formal access requests by 20% and enhancing public engagement. It has been praised in transparency reports as a model for compliance.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.

example_name: MySociety v European Central Bank (Case T-67/15)
example_background: UK-based transparency group MySociety requested in 2013 access to ECB documents on its asset purchase programs, including decision-making minutes and risk assessments. The ECB denied access, citing monetary policy exceptions and financial stability risks. The request was made during the Eurozone crisis to inform public debate on quantitative easing measures.
process_and_outcome: The General Court upheld the ECB's refusal in 2016, finding exemptions justified. MySociety appealed, but the Court of Justice dismissed it in 2019, affirming the balance between transparency and policy autonomy.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.
relevant_rules: Protocol No 4 on the ECB Statute

example_name: European Parliament Transparency Register Compliance Cases
example_background: The European Parliament maintains a Transparency Register for lobbyists, requiring registration for access badges. In 2018-2020, several organizations were investigated for failing to register while accessing Parliament premises and documents. Examples include undeclared lobbyists from trade associations attending committee meetings and accessing non-public drafts.
process_and_outcome: The Parliament revoked badges and imposed registration requirements, with over 50 cases resolved by fines or suspensions. This strengthened enforcement of transparency rules.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.

example_name: EU Open Data Portal Launch and Access Practices
example_background: In 2012, the EU launched the Open Data Portal to provide free access to over 1.5 million datasets from EU institutions, covering statistics, reports, and geospatial data. This initiative allows reuse under open licenses, supporting research and innovation. By 2023, it included documents from all major institutions, fulfilling access rights proactively.
process_and_outcome: The portal has facilitated millions of downloads, with usage analytics showing high public engagement. It received awards for transparency and continues to expand datasets.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.

example_name: Bayer AG v European Commission (Case T-22/16)
example_background: Bayer requested access in 2014 to Commission documents on its merger with Monsanto, including competition assessments and third-party submissions. The Commission provided redacted versions but withheld parts citing business secrets. Bayer sought full access to understand regulatory decisions during the merger review process.
process_and_outcome: The General Court partially annulled the decision in 2020, ordering disclosure of additional non-confidential information. The Commission complied, releasing more documents.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.

example_name: European Court of Auditors Access to Documents Case (Case T-362/14)
example_background: The European Court of Auditors (ECA) sought access to Council legal opinions on the Eurogroup's handling of Greece's financial assistance program in 2012-2013. The Council refused, arguing legal advice privilege. The ECA needed the documents for its audit on EU financial assistance transparency.
process_and_outcome: The General Court ruled in 2016 in favor of the Council, upholding the exemption for internal legal advice. The ECA published its report with available information.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, has a right of access to documents of the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union, whatever their medium.

example_name: European Ombudsman Inquiry into Commission's Language Policy
example_background: The European Ombudsman conducted an inquiry following a complaint from a citizen who contacted the European Commission in Irish, one of the Treaty languages, but received a reply indicating that the Commission did not handle correspondence in Irish and provided the response in English instead. The complainant argued that this practice did not align with the provisions allowing citizens to communicate with EU institutions in any of the Treaty languages and receive replies in the same language. The Ombudsman's investigation examined the Commission's general policy on handling correspondence in all official EU languages, including less commonly used ones like Irish, and assessed whether the institution's operational guidelines adequately ensured multilingual communication as per established rights. The case highlighted challenges in administrative practices across EU bodies when dealing with all 24 official languages, particularly those with fewer speakers.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman found maladministration in the Commission's policy and recommended that it revise its approach to accept and reply to correspondence in Irish. The Commission accepted the recommendations and committed to implementing changes to handle future communications in all Treaty languages, including providing replies in the language of the initial correspondence where feasible.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.

example_name: European Parliament Language Equality Petition Response
example_background: A group of citizens submitted a petition to the European Parliament in one of the official Treaty languages, specifically focusing on regional issues, and the Parliament processed and responded in the same language. This instance demonstrated the institution's commitment to multilingualism by assigning rapporteurs fluent in the language and ensuring all procedural steps, including committee deliberations and final replies, respected the citizen's choice of language. The case involved detailed exchanges over several months, showcasing the Parliament's structured approach to handling communications in any of the 24 official EU languages without defaulting to English or French.
process_and_outcome: The petition was fully processed, debated in committees, and a detailed reply was issued in the original language, leading to further engagement on the substantive issues raised without any language-related obstacles.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.
relevant_rules: European Parliament Rules of Procedure (Rule 228 on petitions)

example_name: Council of the EU Language Complaint Resolution
example_background: An individual wrote to the Council of the European Union in Maltese, a Treaty language, regarding policy consultations. The initial acknowledgment was prompt, but the substantive reply was delayed due to translation needs. The citizen raised concerns about the timeliness and accuracy of language handling. The Council's services involve a dedicated language unit that processes incoming mail in all official languages, routing it to relevant departments, with replies drafted and translated accordingly. This case underscored the logistical demands of multilingual correspondence in a body where decisions are often documented in multiple languages.
process_and_outcome: Following the complaint, the Council provided the reply in Maltese, apologized for the delay, and confirmed adherence to multilingual communication protocols in its response to the complainant.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.

example_name: European Commission Reply in Bulgarian Case
example_background: A Bulgarian national submitted a formal request for information to the European Commission in Bulgarian concerning competition policy. The Commission acknowledged receipt in Bulgarian and subsequently delivered a comprehensive response in the same language after internal review and translation processes. This example illustrates the standard workflow for handling citizen correspondence: incoming mail is scanned for language, assigned to linguists if needed, and departments prepare replies ensuring linguistic parity. The interaction spanned several weeks and involved multiple directorates, reflecting routine application of multilingual rights.
process_and_outcome: The Commission issued the full reply in Bulgarian, satisfying the requestor's expectations and closing the case without further escalation.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.
relevant_rules: Commission Decision on public access to documents (2001/937/EC)

example_name: Ombudsman Decision on EU Language Rights in Irish Gaelic
example_background: Following complaints from Irish speakers, the European Ombudsman investigated the European Commission's practice of systematically refusing to accept and reply to correspondence in Irish. The inquiry revealed that the Commission's internal guidelines instructed staff not to process Irish-language mail substantively, redirecting it to English instead. This affected multiple citizens who sought information on EU funding and policies, leading to repeated instances where replies were not provided in the language used by the correspondents. The case drew attention to the equal status of all Treaty languages, including those spoken by smaller populations.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman concluded there was maladministration and recommended policy changes. The Commission updated its guidelines to accept Irish correspondence and provide replies in Irish where possible, implementing training for staff.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.

example_name: Court of Justice Case C-566/10 P - Language in Judicial Proceedings
example_background: In a legal challenge before the General Court, a party submitted pleadings in Finnish, a Treaty language, and sought confirmation that the Court would proceed and rule in that language. The Court's registry initially queried the choice due to resource constraints but ultimately accepted it. This case involved complex arguments on EU staff regulations and highlighted procedural requirements for multilingual handling in judicial contexts, where parties exercise their right to use any official language with interpretation and translation provided by the institution.
process_and_outcome: The Court upheld the right to use Finnish, conducted hearings with interpretation, and delivered the judgment in Finnish alongside other languages, dismissing the substantive appeal.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: European Economic and Social Committee Language Handling Complaint
example_background: A citizen wrote to the EESC in Croatian regarding economic policy opinions. The committee's secretariat processed the letter, consulted relevant sections, and replied in Croatian. This routine case exemplified the EESC's operational model, which includes a multilingual correspondence unit ensuring replies match the incoming language, even for recently acceded languages like Croatian.
process_and_outcome: The EESC provided a substantive reply in Croatian, addressing the queries and inviting further input, with no complaints raised.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.

example_name: Ombudsman Case on Language Discrimination in EU Agency Correspondence
example_background: Complainants contacted the European Medicines Agency (EMA) in one of the official languages regarding medicine approvals but received automated replies in English only, with instructions to resubmit in English or French. The Ombudsman's probe examined the agency's language policy, which prioritized working languages for efficiency but conflicted with general EU rights for citizen communications. Multiple similar complaints from various languages were consolidated.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman found no maladministration as agencies have discretion but recommended best practices. The EMA expanded its language services to cover more incoming correspondence.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.

example_name: European Court of Auditors Report on Multilingualism Compliance
example_background: The Court audited EU institutions' implementation of language policies, reviewing samples of citizen correspondence across bodies like the Commission and Parliament. It found most replies matched the incoming language but noted delays and inconsistencies in less common languages such as Luxembourgish or Irish, analyzing resource allocation for translation services.
process_and_outcome: The special report recommended enhanced funding for translation units. Institutions responded with action plans to improve processing times and coverage.
source_rule: 4.   Every person may write to the institutions of the Union in one of the languages of the Treaties and must have an answer in the same language.

example_name: European Ombudsman Investigation into European Commission Glyphosate Renewal
example_background: In 2016, the European Commission renewed the approval of glyphosate, a widely used herbicide, for an additional 18 months following a process involving assessments by national authorities and the European Food Safety Authority. Several non-governmental organizations and individuals raised concerns regarding the transparency of the decision-making process, including delays in releasing scientific documents and the handling of conflicting expert opinions on potential health risks. The renewal process had been contentious, with debates over carcinogenicity classifications from the International Agency for Research on Cancer contrasting with EFSA's conclusions. Complainants argued that the Commission failed to adequately disclose documents and engage stakeholders, leading to perceptions of opacity in handling public health-related decisions. This case drew significant public attention, with millions of signatures on petitions opposing the renewal.
process_and_outcome: A complaint was lodged with the European Ombudsman in 2017, alleging maladministration due to lack of transparency. The Ombudsman conducted an inquiry, finding instances of poor administration in document access and stakeholder consultation. The Commission was recommended to improve transparency practices, and subsequent reports noted enhancements in disclosure policies. No financial penalties were imposed, but the case influenced future pesticide approval procedures.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.
relevant_rules: Right to access EU documents (Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001)

example_name: Ombudsman Inquiry on European Commission's Meetings with Pesticide Lobbyists
example_background: Between 2014 and 2017, the European Commission held multiple meetings with representatives from the pesticide industry, particularly concerning the re-approval of glyphosate. Records showed over 90 trilogue meetings involving Commission officials and industry stakeholders, while meetings with public health and environmental NGOs were fewer. A complainant from a public interest group submitted a request for access to these meeting records, which was partially denied by the Commission citing exceptions for privacy and commercial confidentiality. The context involved ongoing debates on pesticide regulations amid public campaigns for stricter controls on substances linked to environmental and health concerns.
process_and_outcome: The European Ombudsman initiated an own-initiative inquiry in 2019, determining maladministration in the Commission's transparency regarding lobby meetings. Recommendations included publishing all lobby meeting details systematically. The Commission accepted and implemented a new transparency register for such interactions, leading to broader policy changes across EU institutions.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: European Ombudsman Case on European Commission's Handling of Dieselgate Documents
example_background: Following the 2015 Volkswagen emissions scandal, known as Dieselgate, where software manipulated emissions tests in millions of vehicles, the European Commission faced scrutiny over its oversight of type-approval processes for vehicles across member states. A complainant requested access to Commission documents related to pre-scandal communications with national authorities and car manufacturers. The Commission withheld certain internal emails and reports, arguing they contained ongoing investigative information and third-party commercial data. This occurred amid investigations by multiple national regulators and the Commission into systematic failures in emissions compliance testing.
process_and_outcome: In 2017, the Ombudsman found maladministration in the Commission's refusal to release documents without proper justification. The Commission was directed to reassess access requests, resulting in partial releases and procedural reforms in document handling for environmental enforcement cases.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: Ombudsman Successful Resolution of European Central Bank Language Discrimination Complaint
example_background: An individual staff member at the European Central Bank (ECB) filed a complaint alleging unfair treatment in internal communications where English was exclusively used, despite the complainant's primary language being another EU official language. The context involved the ECB's multilingual policy and its application in daily operations, amid broader EU principles of linguistic equality among official languages. The complainant provided evidence of specific instances where non-English speakers faced disadvantages in meetings and documentation.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman investigated in 2016, finding no maladministration but facilitating amicable resolution through improved language guidelines at the ECB. The bank adopted measures to enhance multilingual access, demonstrating proactive compliance post-inquiry.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: European Ombudsman Inquiry into European Commission's Public Consultation Practices
example_background: During the preparation of the 2018 EU Copyright Directive proposal, the European Commission conducted public consultations on digital single market issues. A coalition of civil society groups complained that the consultation process favored certain stakeholders, with insufficient time for smaller organizations to respond and limited feedback on how inputs influenced the final proposal. This occurred amid heated debates on copyright reform, involving platforms, creators, and users across the EU.
process_and_outcome: The 2019 Ombudsman decision identified maladministration in consultation design and follow-up transparency. The Commission committed to revising its consultation guidelines, leading to the 'Better Regulation' toolbox updates emphasizing inclusivity.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: Ombudsman Case on Transparency of EU-US Privacy Shield Adequacy Decision
example_background: In 2016, the European Commission adopted a decision deeming the EU-US Privacy Shield adequate for data transfers, following the invalidation of Safe Harbour by the Court of Justice. Privacy advocates requested access to documents on US surveillance practices and Commission assessments. The Commission released some but redacted details on national security matters, prompting claims of insufficient transparency in a process affecting millions of EU citizens' data.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman ruled in 2017 on maladministration in partial disclosures, recommending full publication of non-sensitive documents. This contributed to ongoing refinements before the Privacy Shield's later invalidation in Schrems II.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: European Ombudsman Review of Frontex Fundamental Rights Complaints
example_background: Frontex, the EU's border management agency, faced complaints regarding its handling of fundamental rights incidents during migrant operations in the Mediterranean from 2011 onwards. Individual migrants and NGOs reported inadequate investigations into alleged pushbacks and poor conditions. The agency maintained a complaints mechanism, but complainants argued it lacked independence and timely responses.
process_and_outcome: Multiple Ombudsman inquiries from 2014-2019 found administrative shortcomings and recommended an independent monitoring mechanism. Frontex implemented a revised Fundamental Rights Officer role and enhanced reporting, aligning with best practices.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: Ombudsman Amicable Settlement in European Commission's Staff Promotion Transparency
example_background: A Union citizen employed by the European Commission complained about lack of transparency in the 2015 internal promotion exercise, where selection criteria and candidate evaluations were not sufficiently disclosed. This affected hundreds of staff across grades, amid efforts to streamline EU civil service management.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman brokered a friendly settlement in 2016, leading to publication of anonymized evaluation criteria and appeals data, establishing a model for future exercises.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: European Ombudsman Case on EUIPO Trademark Opposition Delays
example_background: The European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) handled a trademark opposition filed in 2014, which remained unresolved after over three years due to backlog and procedural inefficiencies. The complainant, a small business from a Member State, argued this caused commercial harm in a competitive market.
process_and_outcome: In 2018, the Ombudsman found maladministration in delay management, prompting EUIPO to prioritize cases and reduce average processing times from 12 to 6 months by 2020.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to refer to the European Ombudsman cases of maladministration in the activities of the institutions, bodies, offices or agencies of the Union, with the exception of the Court of Justice of the European Union acting in its judicial role.

example_name: Google Spain SL v. AEPD and Mario Costeja González
example_background: Mario Costeja González discovered that a 1998 Spanish newspaper article about his home foreclosure auction appeared prominently in Google search results for his name, despite the debt being resolved. He requested the newspaper, La Vanguardia, and Google Spain SL to remove the links, invoking his right to be forgotten. When denied, he complained to the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD). The case raised questions about the right of access to personal data processed by search engines and the duration of data retention in search indexes. Google argued it was merely a search engine, not a data controller, while Costeja sought access to and rectification of the processing of his personal data. The Spanish High Court referred the matter to the CJEU to clarify responsibilities under EU data protection law.
process_and_outcome: In May 2014, the CJEU ruled that Google must assess delisting requests on a case-by-case basis, recognizing the right to be forgotten for outdated or irrelevant data. Google implemented a process handling over a million requests, delisting links in EU searches while maintaining global availability.
source_rule: Right of access to documents
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)
Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Data Protection Directive 95/46/EC (Articles 12 and 14 - right of access and rectification)

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Fine
example_background: Marriott acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database with personal data of over 300 million guests worldwide, including 30 million EU residents. A breach from 2014 (undetected until 2018) exposed passport numbers, payment details, and travel information. EU data subjects requested access to records of their data processing, retention periods, and breach details post-disclosure. Marriott's integration process overlooked data protection impact assessments, complicating responses to access requests amid the investigation.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020 (originally £99 million, reduced on appeal). Additional investigations by other EU regulators under GDPR's one-stop-shop mechanism led to coordinated enforcement. Marriott improved DSAR handling and data mapping.
source_rule: Right of access to documents

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB v. Post- och telestyrelsen
example_background: Swedish telecom operator Tele2 Sverige challenged national laws requiring generalized retention of traffic and location data for law enforcement purposes. The case involved subscribers seeking access to metadata logs retained about their communications. Tele2 argued the retention interfered with privacy rights without sufficient safeguards. The Swedish Supreme Administrative Court referred questions to the CJEU on the compatibility of bulk data retention with EU law.
process_and_outcome: In December 2016, the CJEU ruled that indiscriminate retention of data was incompatible with EU law, requiring targeted retention only for serious crimes with prior judicial authorization. Tele2 adjusted its data retention practices accordingly.
source_rule: Right of access to documents
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into WhatsApp Data Processing
example_background: In 2018, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated an investigation into WhatsApp Ireland's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising following complaints from privacy groups. Users requested access to their chat metadata, user profiles, and data shared with Facebook. WhatsApp's updates to terms integrating data with Meta raised concerns about transparency in data flows and lawful basis for processing.
process_and_outcome: In September 2021, the DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million for transparency violations. Other EU regulators challenged via the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), leading to an additional €5.6 million fine in 2023. WhatsApp enhanced its DSAR tools.
source_rule: Right of access to documents
relevant_rules: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 5, 12-15 (transparency and access)

example_name: Amazon France Logistik GDPR Fine
example_background: Amazon's French subsidiary used employee data from access badge swipes and security camera footage for performance monitoring without clear information or consent. Warehouse workers requested access to these processing records, revealing extensive surveillance without impact assessments. The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated after complaints.
process_and_outcome: In December 2024, CNIL fined Amazon France €32 million. Amazon revised its monitoring policies and improved data access mechanisms for employees.
source_rule: Right of access to documents
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Microsoft's GDPR Compliance Program
example_background: Microsoft established a comprehensive GDPR readiness program ahead of the regulation's 2018 enforcement, including a dedicated data subject request portal accessible via account.microsoft.com/privacy. EU customers could request access to personal data across services like Office 365, Azure, and Windows, receiving detailed reports on data categories, purposes, and recipients. The company conducted privacy-by-design audits and trained thousands of employees on handling access rights.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft handled millions of DSARs without major regulatory fines related to access rights. It published annual transparency reports detailing request volumes and fulfillment rates, serving as an industry benchmark.
source_rule: Right of access to documents

example_name: EU Commission v Hungary - Asylum Procedures Case
example_background: Hungary implemented legislation and policies from 2015 onwards that established procedures for asylum applications and accelerated procedures at the border. These measures included requirements for asylum seekers to apply at transit zones near the Serbian border, where limited numbers were permitted to enter Hungary for the purpose of lodging applications. Access to these zones was restricted, and the zones were fenced off, limiting the ability of individuals to move freely within Hungary or reside pending their applications. The policies also involved pushback practices where migrants attempting to cross the border were prevented from entering, and criminal sanctions were imposed for irregular entry. This setup created conditions where thousands of migrants were left waiting in Serbia without the possibility of lawful entry into Hungary to exercise residence rights related to asylum claims.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission brought infringement proceedings against Hungary in 2019. The Court of Justice of the EU ruled in 2020 that Hungary's transit zone system and related practices failed to comply with EU asylum law requirements on access to procedures and effective remedies. Hungary was ordered to adjust its practices, though implementation remained contested with ongoing monitoring by the Commission.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (Adjudicator)
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (Article 18 - Right to Asylum)

example_name: Poland-Thailand Migrant Worker Returns Case
example_background: In 2021, Polish authorities conducted an operation targeting undocumented migrant workers from Thailand who had been residing and working in Poland for several years. Approximately 100 individuals were rounded up in Warsaw and other cities, placed in temporary detention centers, and prepared for deportation. These workers had entered Poland legally on short-term visas but overstayed after their employment ended due to economic hardships. The operation involved restrictions on their movement during detention and rapid processing that limited opportunities to regularize their status or appeal decisions, affecting their ability to reside lawfully.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated an investigation under EU migration monitoring frameworks. Following diplomatic negotiations, some deportations were paused, and Poland adjusted procedures to include more individualized assessments. No formal fine was imposed, but the case led to enhanced training for border guards on residence rights.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence

example_name: Spain's Ceuta and Melilla Border Fence Incidents
example_background: In May 2021, over 8,000 migrants from Morocco crossed into the Spanish enclaves of Ceuta and Melilla by scaling border fences or entering through breaches. Spanish authorities responded by detaining many entrants and initiating summary return procedures, pushing individuals back across the border without formal asylum assessments or opportunities to apply for residence. The events occurred amid diplomatic tensions between Spain and Morocco, leading to a relaxation of border controls by Moroccan authorities. The rapid returns prevented migrants from moving freely into mainland Spain or establishing residence.
process_and_outcome: NGOs filed complaints with the European Court of Human Rights and the European Commission. In 2021, the Commission opened infringement proceedings against Spain for non-compliance with return procedures. Spain defended the actions as necessary for public order, but faced ongoing scrutiny with recommendations for procedural safeguards.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence

example_name: Denmark's 'Jewelry Law' Challenge
example_background: In 2016, Denmark enacted a law allowing authorities to confiscate valuables from asylum seekers upon arrival to cover housing and sustenance costs during the processing of their residence applications. The measure applied to items exceeding 1,400 euros in value, such as jewelry, electronics, and cash. Asylum seekers were held in reception centers with restricted movement, and the policy was applied nationwide at borders and airports, impacting thousands of arrivals from Syria, Iraq, and Afghanistan who sought to reside in Denmark pending status determination.
process_and_outcome: The law faced legal challenges from asylum advocacy groups and review by the UN Human Rights Committee. In 2016, the European Commission assessed it for proportionality under EU law but did not pursue formal infringement. Denmark amended the law in 2017 to exempt sentimental items following public criticism.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence

example_name: Germany's Family Reunification Suspension for Syrian Refugees
example_background: From January 2016 to March 2018, Germany suspended family reunification visas for refugees under subsidiary protection status, primarily affecting Syrians fleeing the civil war. This policy prevented over 20,000 family members from joining relatives already residing in Germany, requiring them to wait in camps in Turkey or Jordan. Those already in Germany faced delays in status adjustments that impacted their freedom to have family reside with them.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Justice ruled in 2017 (X and X v. Belgium) that such suspensions were permissible under temporary derogations. Germany lifted the suspension in 2018, processing backlog applications and allowing reunifications.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence
involved_parties: Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF)

example_name: EU Blue Card Residence Permit Program - Netherlands Implementation
example_background: The Netherlands has operated a streamlined residence permit system under the EU Blue Card Directive since 2010, allowing highly qualified non-EU nationals to obtain work and residence permits for up to four years, renewable. Holders enjoy freedom to move within the country and change employers after one year, with pathways to permanent residence after five years. The program processes thousands of applications annually from IT, engineering, and scientific sectors, facilitating lawful residence and mobility.
process_and_outcome: Annual reports show high approval rates and low violation incidents, with the Dutch Immigration and Naturalisation Service (IND) conducting regular audits. The system has been praised in EU Commission progress reports for effective implementation.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence

example_name: Schengen Area Free Movement Enforcement - Austria COVID Border Checks
example_background: During the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020-2021, Austria introduced temporary border controls with Germany, Czech Republic, and Hungary to manage virus spread. These controls included health checks and quarantine requirements for cross-border travelers, temporarily limiting free movement for EU citizens and residents. Exemptions applied to essential workers and residents returning home, but implementation involved spot checks and data collection on movements.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission approved the temporary reintroduction under Schengen evaluation rules. Controls were lifted in phases as infection rates declined, with post-evaluation confirming proportionality.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence

example_name: EU Long-Term Residence Directive - Sweden Best Practices
example_background: Sweden's implementation of the EU Long-Term Residence Status allows non-EU nationals who have legally resided for five continuous years to obtain permanent residence permits. The process involves straightforward applications through the Swedish Migration Agency, granting rights to reside indefinitely, move within Sweden, and access employment without restrictions. Thousands receive this status yearly, supporting integration and labor mobility.
process_and_outcome: EU Commission peer reviews in 2022 highlighted Sweden's model as efficient, with high processing speeds and low appeal rates. No major compliance issues identified.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence

example_name: Italy's Pushback Operations in Adriatic Sea
example_background: Between 2017 and 2022, Italian coast guard and navy vessels intercepted migrant boats in the Adriatic Sea originating from Albania and Libya, conducting summary returns to ports of departure without individual asylum screenings. Over 20,000 individuals were affected, prevented from reaching Italian territory to apply for residence or protection, with vessels used as floating detention facilities limiting movement.
process_and_outcome: Investigated by the European Commission and UN agencies; infringement proceedings opened in 2022. Italy continued operations under bilateral agreements, but faced ECtHR interim measures in related cases.
source_rule: Freedom of movement and of residence

example_name: CPCB Case Against Ryanair for Passenger Compensation Denials
example_background: In 2021, the Center for Consumer and Business Protection (CPCB) in Poland initiated proceedings against Ryanair for practices that involved denying compensation to passengers for flight delays and cancellations occurring within the European Union. The airline operated flights across multiple Member States and applied terms that restricted passengers' access to standardized compensation amounts set under EU air passenger rights regulations. This occurred amid a high volume of complaints from passengers who experienced disruptions during travel between countries such as Poland, Ireland, and others. Ryanair, headquartered in Ireland, maintained that its general terms and conditions superseded certain EU-mandated protections, leading to systematic rejections of claims filed by individuals exercising their right to travel freely across borders. The case highlighted tensions between airline operational policies and the uniform application of passenger rights for intra-EU travel.
process_and_outcome: The CPCB conducted an investigation into Ryanair's claim handling processes and imposed a fine of approximately 2.7 million PLN (around 650,000 EUR). Ryanair was required to revise its procedures to align with EU standards, and the decision was upheld after appeals, resulting in changes to the airline's customer service protocols across EU operations.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union Article 45 (freedom of movement and employment)

example_name: European Commission Proceedings Against Hungary's Lex CEU Law
example_background: In 2017, the Hungarian government enacted legislation known as Lex CEU targeting Central European University (CEU), an institution founded by George Soros and operating with campuses in Budapest and New York. The law imposed new accreditation requirements specifically on CEU, requiring it to conduct operations in both Hungary and the United States simultaneously to maintain legal status in Hungary. CEU, which attracted students and faculty from across EU Member States, faced relocation threats, impacting the mobility of over 1,500 students and 400 staff members who relied on free movement for education and work. The measure was perceived as singling out the university amid broader political debates, affecting cross-border academic exchanges within the Union.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings in 2017, referring Hungary to the Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU) in 2018. The CJEU ruled against Hungary in 2020, finding the law incompatible with EU law, leading to Hungary's obligation to amend the legislation. CEU suspended operations in Hungary and relocated to Vienna, Austria.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.
relevant_rules: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU Article 45
TFEU Articles 45, 49, 56 (free movement of workers, establishment, services)

example_name: Denmark Fined for Fingerprints on Residence Permits
example_background: Denmark implemented a practice from 2003 to 2018 requiring all residence permit cardholders, including EU citizens exercising residence rights, to provide fingerprints for identity verification. This applied to approximately 69,000 EU citizens annually who were living and working in Denmark under free movement provisions. The requirement was part of a broader identity document issuance process but extended beyond EU directives to non-criminal EU nationals, affecting their ability to reside without additional biometric obligations not uniformly applied across the Union.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission launched infringement proceedings, culminating in a 2019 CJEU ruling that found Denmark in violation. Denmark was fined €800,000 initially, with ongoing penalties, and subsequently adjusted its policies to exempt EU citizens.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.
relevant_rules: Regulation (EC) No 2252/2004 (standards for security features in passports)

example_name: France Infringement for Residence Reporting Requirements
example_background: France maintained a requirement under its Code de l'entrée et du séjour des étrangers et du droit d'asile (CESEDA) for EU citizens to report their presence to local authorities within three months of arrival when intending to reside for more than three months. This applied to millions of mobile EU citizens moving to France for work, study, or family reasons, imposing administrative burdens such as police station visits and declarations that were not harmonized with EU free movement rules allowing residence based on self-sufficiency or employment without prior notification.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings in 2014, sending a reasoned opinion. France modified its legislation in 2021 to remove the requirement, aligning with EU standards following Commission pressure.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.

example_name: Austria Penalized for Prior Authorization on EU Workers
example_background: Austrian legislation under the Ausländerpolizeigesetz required EU citizens from certain new Member States (post-2004 enlargement) to obtain prior labor market authorization before taking up employment, despite EU rules granting immediate access to the labor market upon arrival. This affected workers from countries like Bulgaria and Romania moving to Austria, creating barriers through administrative delays and rejections, impacting thousands of cross-border job seekers between 2004 and 2011.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled against Austria in 2008 (Case C-325/08), declaring the requirement incompatible. Austria amended its laws, paid compliance costs, and lifted restrictions fully by 2011.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.

example_name: EU Blue Card Scheme Implementation in Germany
example_background: Germany implemented the EU Blue Card Directive to facilitate high-skilled non-EU workers' mobility across Member States, but the program indirectly supports intra-EU free movement by allowing EU citizens unrestricted access to the same labor markets without quotas or authorizations. In practice, Germany processed over 25,000 Blue Cards annually since 2012, demonstrating streamlined residence and work permits for skilled migrants while maintaining open borders for EU nationals moving for employment purposes.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission reviewed national implementations in 2014 and 2016, confirming Germany's compliance through simplified procedures. No penalties issued; program expanded in 2020 for broader labor mobility.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.
involved_parties: BAMF (Federal Office for Migration and Refugees)

example_name: Schengen Area Border-Free Travel During COVID-19 Recovery
example_background: Following the lifting of temporary border controls in 2022, EU Member States coordinated under Schengen rules to restore free movement, allowing over 400 million citizens to travel without internal border checks. This involved harmonized health protocols and mutual recognition of vaccination proofs via the EU Digital COVID Certificate, enabling seamless residence and work across 27 countries during economic recovery.
process_and_outcome: The Council extended temporary controls selectively but mandated full restoration by mid-2022. No major infringements noted; mobility restored, boosting intra-EU GDP by estimated 1-2%.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.
involved_parties: EU Agency for large-scale IT systems (eu-LISA)

example_name: European Commission's Infringement Against Belgium for Student Residence Rules
example_background: Belgium required economically inactive EU students to prove sufficient resources exceeding subsistence levels and comprehensive health insurance before granting residence, applying this to students from other Member States enrolling in Belgian universities. This affected around 20,000 EU students annually, imposing stricter conditions than those outlined in EU directives for equal treatment in residence rights.
process_and_outcome: Infringement proceedings launched by the Commission in 2019; Belgium notified legislative changes in 2021 to align with EU law, closing the case.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.

example_name: Portugal's Compliance with Posted Workers Enforcement Directive
example_background: Portugal transposed and enforced the Posted Workers Enforcement Directive, ensuring that workers temporarily posted from other Member States receive equal treatment in pay and conditions. This facilitated smooth labor mobility for over 10,000 posted workers annually in construction and services sectors across Iberia and beyond.
process_and_outcome: European Commission audit in 2018 confirmed full compliance; no proceedings initiated, serving as a model for enforcement cooperation.
source_rule: 1.   Every citizen of the Union has the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.

example_name: EU Blue Card Implementation in Germany - Indian IT Professionals
example_background: Germany introduced the EU Blue Card scheme in 2009 to attract skilled workers from third countries. Numerous Indian IT specialists with university degrees and job offers exceeding the salary threshold obtained Blue Cards, granting them residence and work rights. These individuals moved within Germany and later to other EU states after 18 months, utilizing rights for highly qualified workers legally resident in a member state. Companies like Siemens and SAP sponsored such workers, integrating them into projects while adhering to requirements for labor market tests and documentation verification.
process_and_outcome: Federal Employment Agency processed applications routinely, issuing thousands of Blue Cards annually. Workers renewed permits and exercised intra-EU mobility, contributing to Germany's tech sector growth without reported disputes.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.

example_name: Spain's Illegal Residence Permit Sales Scandal
example_background: In the 2000s and early 2010s, networks in Spain facilitated residence permits for third-country nationals through fraudulent means, including fake job contracts and marriages. Thousands of non-EU citizens, mainly from China, Pakistan, and North Africa, obtained permits without meeting legal residency criteria, bypassing requirements for genuine employment or family ties. Local officials and entrepreneurs issued documentation allowing these individuals to reside and work, undermining controls on legal entry and movement within the EU.
process_and_outcome: Spanish National Police conducted operations like 'Operation Welcome' in 2012, arresting over 100 individuals and dismantling rings. Courts convicted participants, with sentences up to 5 years and fines totaling millions of euros; affected permits were revoked.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.
relevant_rules: Spanish Organic Law 4/2000 on Rights and Freedoms of Foreigners

example_name: Netherlands Posted Workers Directive Enforcement - Polish Construction Firm
example_background: A Polish construction company posted workers to the Netherlands under EU rules allowing temporary service provision. Third-country nationals legally resident in Poland with long-term EU status were included in the postings. The firm initially failed to notify Dutch authorities properly or ensure equal pay and conditions, affecting movement rights for these workers during their assignment.
process_and_outcome: Dutch Labour Inspectorate investigated in 2018, fining the company €150,000 for notification lapses and wage discrepancies. The firm rectified practices, compensating workers and complying in subsequent postings.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.
relevant_rules: EU Regulation 492/2011 on freedom of movement for workers

example_name: Sweden's Long-Term Resident Status for Syrian Refugees
example_background: Following the 2015 migration influx, Syrians granted subsidiary protection in Sweden transitioned to long-term resident status after five years of legal residence and employment. These third-country nationals then accessed rights to move to other EU states like Germany for work, with hundreds applying successfully. Integration programs supported language training and job placement, enabling exercise of residence freedoms.
process_and_outcome: Swedish Migration Agency approved over 1,000 status changes by 2020, facilitating intra-EU moves. No major challenges reported, with successful relocations documented in annual reports.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.
involved_parties: German Federal Office for Migration and Refugees

example_name: France's Fraudulent Work Permits for North African Nationals
example_background: Municipalities in southern France issued thousands of fake work permits to third-country nationals from Morocco and Algeria between 2014 and 2016, allowing unauthorized residence and employment. These documents enabled movement within France despite lacking genuine job qualifications or prior legal residency, involving complicit employers and officials.
process_and_outcome: French judicial authorities launched 'Operation Sahara' in 2016, arresting 22 suspects including mayors. Courts imposed prison terms of 2-4 years and €1.2 million in fines; over 1,000 permits annulled.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.

example_name: EU Intra-Corporate Transfer Permit Success - Tata Consultancy Services
example_background: Tata Consultancy Services (TCS), an Indian firm, utilized the EU ICT Directive to transfer managers and specialists legally resident in India to subsidiaries in Ireland and the Netherlands. These third-country nationals received permits allowing residence and work, with subsequent mobility to other member states after 90 days, supporting IT projects across Europe.
process_and_outcome: Immigration authorities in host countries processed applications efficiently, issuing permits to hundreds annually. TCS reported seamless operations in sustainability filings.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.

example_name: Italy's Fake Residency Ring for Chinese Nationals
example_background: Organized groups in Italy created fictitious residencies for Chinese third-country nationals by registering them at non-existent addresses and fabricating employment records from 2015-2019. This allowed over 3,000 individuals to gain legal residence status without actual presence or ties, facilitating movement and work rights improperly.
process_and_outcome: Italian Carabinieri dismantled the network in 2019, arresting 40 people. Prosecutors seized assets worth €500,000; invalid residencies revoked, with deportations initiated.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.

example_name: Portugal's Golden Visa Program Compliance
example_background: Portugal's Golden Visa scheme granted residence permits to third-country nationals investing in real estate or funds since 2012. Investors from Brazil, China, and Turkey, upon approval, received permits allowing residence and subsequent movement to other EU states after five years, strictly following investment verification and background checks.
process_and_outcome: Immigration and Borders Service (SEF) approved over 10,000 applications by 2023, with renewals and EU mobility exercised by permit holders without irregularities.
source_rule: 2.   Freedom of movement and residence may be granted, in accordance with the Treaties, to nationals of third countries legally resident in the territory of a Member State.

example_name: EU Commission v. Hungary - Diplomatic Protection Case (C-808/18)
example_background: The European Commission initiated proceedings against Hungary concerning the Law on the protection of citizens' rights in relation to police procedures and state administration activities. The law provided Hungarian citizens with additional procedural rights and protections when interacting with police or administrative authorities, including the right to consular notification in certain circumstances. The Commission argued that this legislation created differential treatment between Hungarian nationals and EU citizens from other Member States, as the protections were extended to Hungarian citizens residing abroad through consular services but not equivalently to other EU nationals. This raised questions about equal treatment under EU law, particularly in the context of free movement and non-discrimination principles. The case highlighted tensions between national sovereignty in diplomatic practices and EU foundational rights to equal treatment.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU ruled in favor of the Commission on January 21, 2021, finding that Hungary's law infringed EU law by discriminating against other EU nationals. Hungary was ordered to repeal the provisions and bring its legislation into conformity. No financial penalty was imposed at that stage, but the ruling set a precedent for alignment of national consular practices with EU non-discrimination rules.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_name: Germany's Consular Assistance Framework Post-CJEU Rulings
example_background: Germany maintains a comprehensive consular protection system for its nationals abroad, aligned with EU requirements following interpretations of free movement rights. German missions provide assistance such as emergency travel documents, legal aid referrals, and welfare support to citizens in distress overseas. In response to EU case law emphasizing non-discrimination, Germany extended equivalent services to EU citizens in third countries under certain conditions, particularly when no representation from their home state is available. This practice involves coordination through the Federal Foreign Office and is part of broader diplomatic protocols ensuring foundational rights are upheld without nationality-based distinctions.
process_and_outcome: Germany's system was reviewed in various EU infringement proceedings and found to align with requirements, with no penalties issued. The framework has been cited as a model in EU consular policy documents, supporting effective implementation of citizen protection rights.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection

example_name: Spain's Extension of Consular Protection to EU Nationals
example_background: Spain operates an extensive network of diplomatic and consular missions that provide protection services to Spanish nationals and, in line with EU policy, extend assistance to other EU citizens when their own state is not represented in a third country. Services include help with arrests, hospitalization, repatriation in emergencies, and document replacement. This practice stems from national legislation incorporating EU directives on diplomatic protection, ensuring that foundational rights to assistance abroad are uniformly applied across EU citizens, regardless of origin, particularly in regions with limited diplomatic presence.
process_and_outcome: Evaluated positively in EU Commission reports on consular cooperation, Spain's approach has facilitated numerous assistance cases annually without infringement findings, demonstrating effective adherence to shared EU standards.
