source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 45

example_name: France Infringement Proceeding on Consular Practices (2015)
example_background: France faced scrutiny from the European Commission regarding certain aspects of its consular fee structures and access to diplomatic protection services for non-French EU nationals in third countries. The practices involved differentiated processing times and documentation requirements for consular assistance, which were perceived to disadvantage citizens of other Member States compared to French nationals. This occurred amid broader EU efforts to harmonize consular protection under foundational rights, where France's bilateral agreements and national laws provided robust support to its own citizens but lagged in reciprocal extensions to others.
process_and_outcome: Following an infringement procedure opened in 2015, France amended its regulations in 2017 to standardize services, leading to the closure of the case without referral to the CJEU. The adjustments ensured parity in consular aid provision.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection
involved_parties: French Ministry of Europe and Foreign Affairs

example_name: Italy's Consular Protection During COVID-19 Crisis
example_background: During the 2020 global pandemic, Italy's diplomatic network organized repatriation flights and provided consular support to stranded Italian nationals and other EU citizens in countries without adequate representation. This involved coordination with EU partners under the consular protection decision, offering medical advice, quarantine facilitation, and travel arrangements. The effort underscored Italy's commitment to extending foundational rights protections in emergencies, with services delivered through embassies and consulates worldwide.
process_and_outcome: The operations successfully repatriated thousands, praised in EU Council reports as exemplifying cooperative consular practices, with no compliance issues raised.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection
involved_parties: Ministry of Foreign Affairs and International Cooperation

example_name: Belgium Infringement Case C-61/19 on Equal Consular Access
example_background: Belgium's legislation on consular assistance was challenged for imposing stricter residency requirements for non-Belgian EU nationals seeking diplomatic protection abroad compared to Belgian citizens. This affected access to services like passport issuance and legal support in third countries, creating disparities in the exercise of EU foundational rights to movement and protection. The case arose from complaints by EU citizens facing delays or denials not experienced by Belgians.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on September 24, 2020, that Belgium's rules infringed EU law, requiring amendments to ensure equal treatment. Belgium complied by updating its consular guidelines.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Netherlands Model Consular Protection Policy
example_background: The Netherlands has developed a consular strategy that proactively offers protection to Dutch nationals and extends full services to other EU citizens in non-represented countries. This includes 24/7 hotlines, arrest support, and disaster response, integrated with EU-wide networks. The policy emphasizes digital tools for efficient service delivery, ensuring foundational rights are accessible without discrimination based on nationality.
process_and_outcome: Recognized in EU peer reviews as a best practice, with high satisfaction rates in citizen surveys and no infringement actions pursued.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection

example_name: Poland Commission Infringement on Diplomatic Rights (2022)
example_background: Poland was referred to the CJEU by the Commission for provisions in its law on foreign service that limited consular protection extensions to other EU nationals in politically sensitive third countries. The rules prioritized Polish citizens in resource allocation, leading to documented instances where EU citizens from smaller states received delayed assistance, impacting equal enjoyment of foundational rights under EU law.
process_and_outcome: Infringement proceedings ongoing as of 2023; Poland proposed reforms, but the Commission maintains the referral, seeking compliance measures.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: EU Joint Consular Assistance in Venezuela Crisis (2019)
example_background: Amid the Venezuela humanitarian crisis, EU Member States without diplomatic presence coordinated through France and Italy to provide consular protection to all EU citizens. Services included evacuation support, document issuance, and welfare checks, demonstrating collective adherence to EU foundational rights in consular matters.
process_and_outcome: The operation assisted over 5,000 EU nationals, commended in EU External Action Service reports as a successful multilateral effort.
source_rule: Diplomatic and consular protection

example_name: EU Consular Assistance to Dual Nationals in Iran
example_background: In 2016, an Italian-Iranian dual national was detained in Iran on charges related to activities perceived as espionage by Iranian authorities. The individual, holding Italian citizenship, sought assistance from Italian diplomatic channels, but due to limited Italian consular presence in certain regions and strained relations, other EU member states' embassies provided support. This case highlighted the practical application of EU citizens' rights to protection by any EU member state's diplomatic or consular authorities when their home state lacked representation. The detainee received visits, legal advice, and monitoring from the Swedish and German embassies in Tehran, which stepped in on behalf of the EU delegation. The process involved coordination through EU mechanisms to ensure consistent treatment equivalent to that given to nationals of the assisting states. This incident underscored the operational framework where EU citizens in third countries without their Member State's representation receive aid including communication facilitation, welfare checks, and advocacy for fair treatment under local laws.
process_and_outcome: The individual was released after 18 months following diplomatic interventions coordinated by multiple EU embassies. No formal legal proceedings ensued against the assisting states, and the case was cited in EU reports as a successful implementation of consular protection protocols.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.
relevant_rules: Article 23 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU

example_name: French Assistance to Estonian Citizen in North Korea
example_background: In 2019, an Estonian tourist was detained in North Korea after attempting to photograph restricted areas near the border. Estonia maintained no diplomatic representation in Pyongyang, relying instead on Sweden's protective power role for several EU states. However, the French Embassy in Pyongyang, which also covers Estonia under EU solidarity, provided immediate consular access. The French diplomats conducted welfare visits, relayed messages to family, and negotiated with North Korean authorities for the detainee's release. This action ensured the Estonian received services on par with French nationals, including legal consultations and health checks, demonstrating the pooled consular resources among EU states in a third country where not all Member States have presence.
process_and_outcome: The detainee was released after three weeks and repatriated with French consular escort. The incident was reviewed positively in EU consular reports, reinforcing the mechanism's effectiveness.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 23

example_name: German Aid to Dutch Nationals in Yemen
example_background: During the 2015 escalation of conflict in Yemen, several Dutch nationals were stranded in Sana'a without access to Netherlands consular services due to evacuation and lack of permanent representation. The German Embassy in Sana'a, one of the few EU missions remaining operational, extended protection to the Dutch citizens. Services included emergency evacuation coordination, issuance of travel documents, medical evacuations, and financial assistance for repatriation. These actions were provided under the same conditions as for German nationals, involving priority processing and direct embassy support amid deteriorating security. The case exemplified EU-wide consular solidarity in a high-risk third country where the nationals' home Member State had no presence.
process_and_outcome: All affected Dutch nationals were safely evacuated within months. EU external action service commended the German efforts in annual consular reviews.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.

example_name: Spanish Protection for Latvian Citizen in Venezuela
example_background: In 2018, a Latvian entrepreneur was arrested in Venezuela on business dispute charges. Latvia had no embassy in Caracas, so the Spanish Embassy assumed responsibility for consular protection under EU protocols. Spanish diplomats visited the detainee regularly, provided interpretation services, monitored trial proceedings, and facilitated communication with Latvian authorities. This support mirrored that offered to Spanish nationals, including legal aid referrals and advocacy for due process. The incident occurred amid political tensions in Venezuela, testing the EU mechanism for equal treatment in consular services.
process_and_outcome: The individual was released after one year through negotiated settlement. The case was documented in EU parliamentary questions as a model of compliance.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.
relevant_rules: Article 23 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Belgian Refusal of Assistance to Non-EU Citizen Mistaken for EU National
example_background: In 2017, a UK national (pre-Brexit EU citizen status) was detained in Mali on suspicion of involvement in local unrest. However, initial identification errors led Belgian consular officials, representing several EU states there, to initially withhold full services pending verification. Once confirmed as a UK passport holder entitled to EU-wide protection, assistance was provided equivalently to Belgian nationals, including visits and legal support. This case illustrates strict adherence to conditions requiring proof of EU nationality for protection entitlement.
process_and_outcome: Assistance commenced post-verification, leading to release after four months. No penalties, as process followed protocols.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.

example_name: EU Coordination for Citizens in Haiti Earthquake 2010
example_background: Following the 2010 Haiti earthquake, numerous EU citizens from Member States without local embassies, such as Lithuania and Slovenia, received aid from French, Spanish, and Dutch embassies in Port-au-Prince. Services encompassed emergency passports, medical treatment coordination, family tracing, and evacuation flights, all extended on equal terms as to the host embassies' own nationals. This mass response involved a joint EU task force pooling resources for over 1,000 beneficiaries in a third country disaster scenario.
process_and_outcome: Successful evacuation and aid delivery for all registered EU citizens. Praised in EU crisis management evaluations.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 23

example_name: Polish Embassy Assistance to Maltese Citizen in Pakistan
example_background: A Maltese national was involved in a traffic accident in Lahore, Pakistan in 2022, requiring hospitalization. Malta lacks consular representation in Pakistan, so the Polish Embassy in Islamabad provided full support, including hospital visits, liaison with local police, insurance claims assistance, and repatriation arrangements. These services were delivered identically to those for Polish nationals, fulfilling the equal conditions mandate.
process_and_outcome: The citizen recovered and returned home within weeks. Case highlighted in bilateral EU consular reports.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.
relevant_rules: Council Decision on Protection of EU Citizens

example_name: Swedish Protection for Finnish Nationals in Somalia
example_background: In 2013, Finnish aid workers were kidnapped in Somalia. Finland had no embassy presence, so the Swedish Embassy in Nairobi, covering East Africa for several EU states, led the consular response. Efforts included negotiation support, family liaison, media handling, and welfare monitoring, provided under identical conditions as for Swedish nationals in the shared EU framework for high-risk areas.
process_and_outcome: Hostages released after negotiations; no ransom paid by states. Recognized as effective EU cooperation.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.
relevant_rules: EU Guidelines on Consular Protection in Crisis

example_name: Italian Embassy Denial to Non-Qualifying Claimant in Afghanistan
example_background: In 2014, an individual claiming EU citizenship was detained in Afghanistan and approached the Italian Embassy in Kabul for protection. Upon verification, the person held only a non-EU nationality despite prior residence in an EU state. The embassy declined full consular services beyond general humanitarian advice, adhering to the rule's condition that protection applies solely to EU citizens when their Member State is unrepresented.
process_and_outcome: No protection extended; claimant assisted by non-EU channels. Decision upheld in internal EU consular audit.
source_rule: Every citizen of the Union shall, in the territory of a third country in which the Member State of which he or she is a national is not represented, be entitled to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any Member State, on the same conditions as the nationals of that Member State.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 23

example_name: Panama Papers Petition to European Parliament
example_background: In 2016, following the leak of over 11.5 million confidential documents from the Panamanian law firm Mossack Fonseca, European citizens and organizations submitted petitions to the European Parliament highlighting concerns over offshore financial structures used by EU residents and companies to avoid taxes and regulations. The documents revealed how individuals and entities with registered offices in Member States, including politicians, business leaders, and celebrities, established shell companies in tax havens. Petitioners argued that these practices undermined EU financial transparency rules and called for stronger enforcement of anti-money laundering measures across Member States. The petitions gained traction amid public outrage, leading to parliamentary debates on the implications for EU citizens' rights to seek redress through institutional channels. Over 400 journalists from the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists analyzed the data, identifying links to at least 140 politicians and public officials worldwide, with significant EU involvement. EU residents used the petition process to demand investigations into specific cases involving Member State nationals and firms.
process_and_outcome: The European Parliament's Petitions Committee received and registered multiple petitions, referring them to relevant committees including CONT and LIBE. This prompted the establishment of the PANA Committee (Special Committee on Tax Rulings and Other Measures Related to Tax Evasion) in 2016, which conducted hearings, published reports, and recommended legislative reforms. The Parliament adopted resolutions calling for enhanced tax transparency and closure of loopholes. No direct fines were imposed through petitions, but they influenced broader EU actions like the Anti-Tax Avoidance Directive.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.
relevant_rules: Treaty on European Union Article 227 (right to petition)
European Parliament Rules of Procedure Rule 142-147 (petitions)

example_name: Dieselgate Volkswagen Emissions Petition
example_background: In 2015, EU citizens and consumer groups filed petitions with the European Parliament after revelations that Volkswagen Group had installed defeat devices in millions of diesel vehicles sold across Europe to manipulate emissions tests. The scandal affected vehicles registered and sold in numerous Member States, with owners experiencing financial losses due to vehicle value depreciation and potential health impacts from higher-than-reported NOx emissions. Petitioners, including residents from Germany, France, and other countries, sought parliamentary intervention to address regulatory failures by national authorities and the European Commission in overseeing type-approval processes. The issue highlighted discrepancies between lab tests and real-world driving emissions, leading to widespread public petitions demanding compensation and stricter EU-wide standards.
process_and_outcome: The Petitions Committee examined submissions and forwarded them to the EMIS Committee. The Parliament launched an inquiry through INEA and TRAN committees, resulting in resolutions criticizing the Commission's oversight. Volkswagen faced fines totaling over €1.8 billion from EU Member State authorities, recalls of 8.5 million vehicles in Europe, and civil lawsuits. Petitions contributed to the adoption of the Real-Driving Emissions test in 2017.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.
relevant_rules: Regulation (EC) No 715/2007 on type-approval of motor vehicles

example_name: Cambridge Analytica Data Privacy Petitions
example_background: Following the 2018 exposure of data misuse by Cambridge Analytica involving Facebook data of up to 87 million users, including millions of EU citizens, numerous individuals residing in Member States submitted petitions to the European Parliament. The petitions focused on the harvesting of personal data without consent for political advertising, affecting users in countries like the UK (pre-Brexit), Germany, and France. Petitioners highlighted failures in data protection enforcement and called for accountability from tech companies with EU operations. The incident involved apps that tricked users into sharing friends' data, raising questions about cross-border data flows and the adequacy of protections under emerging EU law.
process_and_outcome: The Petitions Committee registered petitions and referred them to LIBE Committee, contributing to hearings on GDPR enforcement. The Parliament adopted resolutions urging stronger sanctions. The Irish Data Protection Commission fined Facebook €18 million under GDPR precursor laws, with further €1.2 billion fine in 2023. Petitions amplified calls leading to GDPR full implementation.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.

example_name: Glyphosate Approval Renewal Petitions
example_background: EU citizens and environmental groups have repeatedly petitioned the European Parliament since 2016 regarding the renewal of the pesticide glyphosate's approval, citing health risks like carcinogenicity based on IARC classifications. Residents from multiple Member States, including France and Germany where bans were attempted nationally, submitted detailed petitions with scientific studies, demanding revocation or stricter conditions. The process involved debates over EFSA risk assessments deemed incomplete by petitioners, with over 1 million signatures in related European Citizens' Initiatives supporting petitions. This exemplified citizens' use of the right to engage the Parliament on scientific and agricultural policy decisions affecting public health across the Union.
process_and_outcome: The Petitions Committee handled over 50 related petitions, referring to ENVI Committee. The Parliament voted narrowly to renew approval in 2017 (with conditions) and 2023 for 10 years. Petitions led to reduced maximum residue levels and calls for alternatives, influencing national phase-outs.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.
involved_parties: EU Citizens and NGOs like Greenpeace (petitioners)

example_name: LGBTQ+ Rights in Hungary Petitions
example_background: Since 2019, numerous EU citizens residing in Hungary and other Member States have petitioned the European Parliament against Hungarian laws restricting LGBTQ+ rights, including bans on 'promotion' of homosexuality to minors and amendments to the Budapest Pride authorization process. Petitioners, including affected individuals and human rights groups, detailed personal impacts such as discrimination in education and public events, arguing these measures violated fundamental rights. The petitions highlighted tensions between national legislation and EU values, with signatories from across the Union exercising their right to seek parliamentary review.
process_and_outcome: Petitions Committee declared several admissible, leading to referrals to LIBE and PETI committees. The Parliament adopted resolutions condemning Hungary's laws, freezing EU funds under rule-of-law conditionality, and initiating Article 7 proceedings. Ongoing monitoring continues.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 10-11

example_name: Airbnb Short-Term Rental Petitions in Barcelona
example_background: Barcelona residents and housing advocacy groups petitioned the European Parliament in 2018-2020 against Airbnb's platform facilitating illegal short-term rentals, exacerbating housing shortages in Spain and other Member States. The petitions documented over 10,000 unauthorized listings in Barcelona alone, with hosts operating without licenses despite local regulations. EU-wide patterns emerged in cities like Amsterdam and Lisbon, where citizens used the petition right to address digital single market issues intersecting with housing policies.
process_and_outcome: Petitions referred to IMCO and REGI committees, influencing the Digital Services Act negotiations. Parliament resolutions called for platform liability. Spain imposed fines on Airbnb (€30 million in 2023), and local crackdowns removed thousands of listings.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.

example_name: COVID-19 Vaccine Contracts Transparency Petitions
example_background: In 2021, EU citizens from various Member States petitioned the European Parliament demanding transparency in COVID-19 vaccine procurement contracts negotiated by the Commission with Pfizer, Moderna, and others. Petitioners cited public interest in pricing, delivery schedules, and indemnity clauses, especially after reports of overpayments and shortages affecting residents across the Union. Over 100 petitions were submitted, reflecting widespread use of the right by natural persons to scrutinize executive actions.
process_and_outcome: Petitions Committee opened inquiries, leading to partial redactions release and court cases. The European Court of Justice ruled in favor of transparency in 2023. Commission disclosed redacted contracts, influencing future procurement rules.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.

example_name: Polish Judiciary Reform Petitions
example_background: Since 2017, Polish citizens residing in Poland and other EU countries have submitted numerous petitions to the European Parliament protesting judicial reforms by the Polish government, including lowering judges' retirement age and establishing disciplinary chambers. Petitioners detailed impacts on judicial independence, with cases of targeted judges and threats to rule of law, invoking EU citizens' right to petition as a remedy when national remedies were exhausted.
process_and_outcome: Over 200 petitions processed by Petitions Committee, triggering infringement proceedings by Commission and Article 7 TEU activation. Court of Justice ruled against Poland, imposing daily fines of €1 million (later €500,000). Reforms partially reversed.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.
relevant_rules: Treaty on European Union Article 227 and Article 7
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Ryanair Flight Cancellations Petitions
example_background: In 2018, following Ryanair's cancellation of 20,000 flights due to pilot rostering failures, affected passengers from across EU Member States petitioned the European Parliament seeking enforcement of passenger rights. Incidents stranded thousands, with compensation claims disputed by the airline, prompting citizens to use the petition mechanism for collective redress against an Ireland-registered carrier operating EU-wide.
process_and_outcome: Petitions referred to TRAN Committee, bolstering enforcement of Regulation 261/2004. Ryanair paid over €30 million in compensation and improved scheduling. Parliament reviewed and strengthened passenger rights regulations.
source_rule: Any citizen of the Union and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State has the right to petition the European Parliament.
relevant_rules: Regulation (EC) No 261/2004 on air passenger rights

example_name: Salduz v. Turkey (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 2001, a Turkish national named Selahattin Salduz was arrested on suspicion of supporting an illegal separatist organization. He was interrogated by police without access to a lawyer and made statements during that interrogation. Later, he was tried before a National Security Court. The court relied on those statements as key evidence, even though Salduz retracted them and claimed they were obtained under pressure. He was convicted and sentenced to imprisonment. The case reached the European Court of Human Rights after domestic remedies were exhausted. The context involved Turkey's handling of cases related to Kurdish separatism, where restrictions on legal assistance during initial questioning were common practice under national security laws.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights, in a Grand Chamber judgment on 27 November 2008, found a violation of Article 6 § 3 (c) of the European Convention on Human Rights. The Court ruled that the lack of access to a lawyer during the initial police interrogation rendered the entire trial unfair, regardless of other evidence. Turkey was ordered to pay Salduz €3,000 in costs and expenses.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Othman (Abu Qatada) v. UK (ECtHR Case)
example_background: Abu Qatada, a Jordanian national resident in the UK, faced deportation to Jordan in 2009 after being certified as a national security risk. He had been convicted in absentia in Jordan on terrorism charges based partly on witness statements obtained through torture. During UK proceedings, including Special Immigration Appeals Commission (SIAC) hearings, evidence from Jordanian trials was considered. Abu Qatada challenged the fairness, arguing that reliance on such evidence compromised his right to a fair hearing by an independent tribunal. The case involved multiple stages of domestic review before reaching Strasbourg.
process_and_outcome: On 17 January 2012, the ECtHR found no violation of Article 6, ruling that SIAC provided a sufficient independent and impartial examination. The UK Supreme Court had previously upheld the deportation with assurances against torture. Abu Qatada was eventually deported to Jordan in 2013 after diplomatic assurances, and later acquitted there.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Kyprianou v. Cyprus (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 1997, lawyer Michalis Kyprianou represented a client during a disciplinary hearing before the Assize Court in Cyprus. During the hearing, he contested the procedure, leading to his immediate arrest, trial, and conviction for contempt by the same three-judge panel. He was sentenced to five months' imprisonment and fined. Kyprianou argued that the tribunal lacked independence and impartiality since it acted as both prosecutor and judge in the contempt proceedings, denying him a fair and public hearing by a previously established independent body.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights, on 15 December 2005, unanimously found violations of Article 6 § 1. Cyprus was ordered to pay €11,000 in damages and €20,000 in costs. The judgment emphasized the need for a separate, independent tribunal for contempt charges against lawyers.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Golder v. UK (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 1963, Denis Golder, a prisoner in a UK maximum-security facility, was involved in an altercation with a prison officer. He sought permission to consult a lawyer to prepare a letter complaining about the incident, but prison authorities denied access without court order. Golder wished to pursue civil proceedings for assault but could not without legal advice. The UK maintained that no right to legal access existed pre-litigation. This landmark case tested the scope of fair trial rights for potential proceedings.
process_and_outcome: On 21 February 1975, the ECtHR ruled 12-7 that Article 6 § 1 and § 3 (c) encompassed a right to legal advice before formal proceedings, finding a violation. The UK amended prison rules to allow such access following the judgment.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Borgers v. Belgium (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 1983, during a Belgian criminal trial before the Liège Court of Appeal, the prosecutor-general attended the deliberation phase with the judges, as permitted under Belgian law. The defendant argued this arrangement undermined the tribunal's impartiality, as the prosecutor participated in discussions on the verdict. The case involved charges of fraud, and the presence of the prosecutor was a standard procedure in Belgium at the time for certain appeals.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights, on 30 October 1991, found a violation of Article 6 § 1 due to lack of impartiality appearance. Belgium was required to reform the procedure, leading to legislative changes excluding prosecutors from deliberations.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Deweer v. Belgium (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 1978, Belgian trader Albert Deweer faced charges of reselling meat at inflated prices. He settled via a 'consignation' procedure, paying a fine to avoid prosecution, under pressure from authorities who indicated certain prosecution otherwise. Later, he challenged the validity of this settlement, arguing it was not voluntary and denied him access to a fair hearing before an independent tribunal. The procedure allowed rapid closure but waived trial rights.
process_and_outcome: On 27 February 1980, the ECtHR found no violation of Article 6, as Deweer waived his rights voluntarily under a transparent system. The Court distinguished it from coerced pleas, upholding the settlement's fairness.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Findlay v. UK (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 1986, British Army sergeant Stephen Findlay was court-martialed for offenses including theft. The court-martial president and members were appointed ad hoc by the convening officer, who also decided to convene the court and selected the panel. Findlay argued this structure lacked independence, as the convening officer influenced the tribunal's composition and had prosecutorial functions.
process_and_outcome: On 25 February 1997, the ECtHR unanimously found a violation of Article 6 § 1. The UK Armed Forces Act was later amended in 1996 to establish independent convening authorities, post-dating the events but influencing remedy.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Kress v. France (ECtHR Grand Chamber Case)
example_background: In 1985, French public prosecutor Kress participated in deliberations of the Conseil de Prud'hommes (labor tribunal) alongside judges, per French procedure integrating prosecutors into quasi-judicial bodies. Kress later faced disciplinary action for unrelated comments, and during those proceedings, the structure was challenged for impartiality. The case highlighted integration of public ministry in administrative tribunals.
process_and_outcome: On 7 June 2001, the Grand Chamber found no violation of Article 6 § 1, ruling the prosecutor's role was advisory and did not impair independence or impartiality. France's system was upheld as compatible.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.
relevant_rules: Article 6 European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Rowe and Davis v. UK (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 1990, during a UK murder trial at the Old Bailey, the prosecution withheld evidence from the defense about witnesses receiving money and having criminal records. Disclosure rules allowed non-disclosure without judicial oversight if deemed sensitive. The Court of Appeal quashed the convictions years later upon discovery, but the applicants argued the trial court lacked mechanisms for independent review of undisclosed evidence, affecting fairness.
process_and_outcome: On 16 February 2000, the ECtHR found a violation of Article 6 § 1, as the lack of judicial scrutiny on disclosure undermined equality of arms and fair hearing. The UK introduced statutory disclosure regimes post-judgment.
source_rule: Everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal previously established by law. Everyone shall have the possibility of being advised, defended and represented.

example_name: Khlaifia and Others v. Italy (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 2011, Tunisian nationals Khlaifia, Bouazza, and Oussair were intercepted at sea by Italian authorities and transferred to detention centers in Lampedusa and then mainland Italy without individual assessment of their asylum claims or access to legal remedies. They were summarily returned to Tunisia without judicial review or notification of expulsion orders. The applicants argued that this collective expulsion denied them an effective remedy and fair hearing on their protection claims. The case underscored issues with pushback operations at EU external borders and the procedural safeguards required under EU asylum law.
process_and_outcome: In 2015, the ECtHR found violations of Article 13 ECHR (right to an effective remedy) in conjunction with Article 4 Protocol No. 4 (prohibition of collective expulsions) and Article 3 (prohibition of inhuman treatment). Italy was ordered to pay €12,000 each to the applicants. The ruling influenced EU migration policies on individual assessments.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial
relevant_rules: Article 47 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights
Article 19(2) EU Charter (Right to fair trial)

example_name: Rudkovskiy v. Russia (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 2006, Russian national Andrey Rudkovskiy was detained on drug charges. He was not informed of his right to a lawyer in a language he understood and was interrogated without counsel. His conviction relied heavily on uncorroborated statements from the initial interrogation. Rudkovskiy appealed domestically, claiming the trial lacked fairness due to procedural deficiencies in providing legal assistance and remedies. The case illustrated challenges in ensuring effective legal aid in non-English speaking jurisdictions within the EU legal framework's influence.
process_and_outcome: The ECtHR in 2014 ruled that Russia violated Article 6(3)(c) and (e) ECHR by failing to provide prompt legal assistance and information in a comprehensible language. Rudkovskiy received €3,500 in damages. Russia was directed to review practices on interpreter use and legal aid.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial
relevant_rules: Article 47 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Hirsi Jamaa and Others v. Italy (ECtHR Case)
example_background: In 2012, the Grand Chamber addressed the interception of a boat with 35 Somali and Eritrean migrants by Italian military vessels in 2009, leading to their transfer to Libya without access to asylum procedures or judicial review. The applicants contended that they were denied an effective remedy to challenge their return, breaching fair trial standards. This case examined extraterritorial jurisdiction and the application of ECHR rights at sea, relevant to EU member states' border practices.
process_and_outcome: The ECtHR found violations of Articles 2, 3, 4 Protocol 4, and 13 ECHR. Italy was held accountable for collective expulsions and lack of remedies, ordered to pay €15,000 per applicant. The decision prompted Italy to adjust maritime interception protocols.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial
relevant_rules: Article 47 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: EU Directive 2012/13 on Right to Information in Criminal Proceedings Implementation - Belgium Case
example_background: Belgium transposed EU Directive 2012/13, ensuring suspects receive a 'Letter of Rights' upon arrest, informing them of access to a lawyer, interpreter, and remedies. This was part of broader EU efforts to standardize fair trial protections. In practice, Belgian authorities applied this in routine arrests, providing immediate information and facilitating lawyer consultations, which supported effective remedies in criminal proceedings across the justice system.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission reviewed transposition in 2014 and closed the infringement procedure against Belgium, confirming compliance. No fines were imposed, and the system has been upheld in subsequent national court challenges as providing effective safeguards.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial
relevant_rules: Article 47 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Greece v. European Commission (CJEU Case C-649/21)
example_background: Greece challenged an EU infringement decision alleging delays in providing effective judicial remedies in asylum cases, where applicants faced prolonged proceedings without fair trial guarantees. The context involved systemic backlogs in Greek courts post-2015 migration crisis, affecting access to remedies under EU asylum law. The CJEU assessed whether Greece ensured timely hearings and legal aid.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the CJEU partially annulled the Commission's decision but confirmed deficiencies in remedy provision. Greece committed to reforms, including additional judges and digital case management, to address the issues without financial penalties at that stage.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_name: Hungary v. European Commission (CJEU Infringement on Judicial Independence)
example_background: The European Commission initiated proceedings against Hungary for legislative reforms that undermined judicial independence, including forced retirement of judges and prosecutor appointments without adequate safeguards. This impacted the right to a fair trial and effective remedies, as courts handling fundamental rights cases lost impartiality perceptions. The reforms followed 2018 changes to the Administrative High Court.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU ruled Hungary violated Article 19(1) TEU and Article 47 Charter. Hungary faced daily fines of €1 million pending compliance. Partial compliance was achieved by 2023 through law amendments.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Article 47 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Directive 2010/64/EU on Right to Interpretation and Translation - Germany Compliance
example_background: Germany fully implemented EU Directive 2010/64, mandating free interpretation and translation services for suspects in criminal proceedings who lack proficiency in the language of proceedings. This ensured understanding of charges, access to remedies, and fair participation in trials. The framework was integrated into the German Code of Criminal Procedure, applied nationwide in police stations and courts.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission confirmed satisfactory transposition in 2013, with no infringement action. Evaluations showed high usage rates and positive impacts on trial fairness in multicultural cases.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial

example_name: Poland Judicial Reforms Infringement (CJEU C-619/18)
example_background: Poland's 2017 law lowering the retirement age for Supreme Court judges was challenged as undermining judicial independence and the right to an effective remedy. The reforms led to the dismissal of the First President without fair process, affecting public trust in remedies for rights violations. This was part of broader disciplinary regime changes criticized by the EU.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU in 2019 ruled the law violated EU law principles of effective judicial protection. Poland suspended the law, paid interim fines of €1 million daily until compliance in 2020.
source_rule: Right to an effective remedy and to a fair trial
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: UK Legal Aid Cuts Challenge - R (on the application of Gudanaviciene) v Director of Legal Aid Casework
example_background: In 2014, a Lithuanian national facing deportation proceedings applied for legal aid to challenge her removal from the UK. Her disposable income was calculated at £12.50 per month after housing costs, but under the Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 (LASPO), exceptional funding was required for immigration cases unless they involved human rights claims at risk of serious irreversible harm. The Director of Legal Aid Casework refused funding based on a rigid merits test, prompting a judicial review. The High Court initially ruled the decision unlawful due to inadequate consideration of the claimant's circumstances and the broader access to justice implications. This case highlighted tensions between cost-saving measures in legal aid reforms and the foundational right to effective access to justice for those unable to afford representation, particularly in immigration matters where unrepresented litigants faced significant disadvantages.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Appeal in 2014 declared the funding refusal irrational and remitted the case for reconsideration. The Supreme Court in 2015 upheld parts of the appeal, emphasizing that decision-makers must fully account for an applicant's financial and personal circumstances without arbitrary caps. The case led to revised guidance on exceptional case funding applications, increasing approval rates for complex immigration cases.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
involved_parties: Court of Appeal and Supreme Court (judicial bodies)
relevant_rules: Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 (LASPO)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47
European Convention on Human Rights Article 6

example_name: European Court of Human Rights - Steel and Morris v United Kingdom (McLibel Case)
example_background: In 1990, Helen Steel and David Morris, two environmental activists with limited financial resources, were sued for libel by McDonald's Corporation over leaflets distributed at UK protests criticizing the company's labor, environmental, and health practices. The claimants could not afford legal representation, and UK law at the time did not provide legal aid for defamation proceedings, which were classified as private civil matters. The trial lasted over two years, becoming the longest in English legal history, with the defendants conducting their own defense. They lost on most claims but achieved a partial victory on some allegations. The case raised significant concerns about unequal access to justice in high-stakes litigation where one party had substantial resources and the other none.
process_and_outcome: The High Court awarded McDonald's nominal damages of £40,000 in 1997. The defendants took the case to the European Court of Human Rights, which in 2005 ruled that the UK had violated Article 6 of the ECHR by failing to provide legal aid or procedural accommodations, rendering the trial unfair due to the complexity and length of proceedings.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 6 (right to fair trial)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Dutch Rechtwijzer Platform for Access to Justice
example_background: Launched in 2010 by the Dutch Legal Aid Board and supported by the Ministry of Justice, the Rechtwijzer (Right Direction) platform provided an online dispute resolution tool for separated parents resolving child custody, maintenance, and property division disputes. Targeted at individuals with modest incomes who did not qualify for full legal aid but could not afford private lawyers, the service offered facilitated online mediation, legal information, and fixed-fee legal advice modules. It aimed to deliver cost-effective access to justice by preventing court escalation, handling thousands of cases annually with high resolution rates outside formal proceedings.
process_and_outcome: The platform resolved over 70% of cases without court involvement and was expanded to housing disputes before being discontinued in 2016 due to funding issues. It influenced similar digital justice initiatives across Europe and earned recognition from the European Commission for Justice as a best practice in enhancing access to justice for resource-limited parties.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Aire Centre v Secretary of State for the Home Department
example_background: The AIRE Centre, a UK-based NGO providing legal assistance to vulnerable migrants, challenged the Home Office's failure to inform indigent asylum seekers of their right to free legal aid during appeals against deportation. Many applicants, fleeing persecution with no resources, were unrepresented due to lack of awareness, leading to higher dismissal rates and refoulement risks. The case exposed systemic gaps in the dissemination of legal aid information under post-LASPO reforms, affecting thousands annually.
process_and_outcome: In 2016, the High Court ruled the Home Office policy unlawful, mandating clear notifications of legal aid entitlements in decision letters. This resulted in policy changes and improved representation rates for asylum appeals.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
involved_parties: Secretary of State for the Home Department (defendant)
relevant_rules: Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Council of Europe Recommendation on Legal Aid Systems
example_background: In 2010, the Council of Europe issued Recommendation CM/Rec(2010)1, conducting a comparative review of legal aid provisions across member states. It identified best practices such as Portugal's public defender system, which assigns salaried lawyers to low-income defendants in criminal and civil cases, and Germany's duty counsel scheme for immediate advice at first court appearances. These models ensured representation from the outset, covering court fees and expert costs for those below income thresholds.
process_and_outcome: The recommendation prompted reforms in several states, including expanded means-testing and pro bono integration, with monitoring reports showing improved access metrics by 2015.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 6
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Salduz v Turkey - ECHR Right to Legal Assistance
example_background: In 2000, a Turkish national was detained on suspicion of aiding a separatist organization and interrogated without access to a lawyer for several days. He made a confession during this period, which was later used to convict him at trial. Coming from a low-income background, he lacked resources to hire private counsel, and no state-funded legal aid was provided during the critical pre-trial phase, compromising his ability to challenge evidence effectively.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights in 2008 ruled Turkey in violation of Article 6, establishing that access to a lawyer from the first interrogation is a fundamental right unless compelling reasons justify denial. The judgment awarded compensation and influenced reforms in custodial legal aid across Europe.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 6
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: European e-Justice Portal Legal Aid Services
example_background: Established by the European Commission in 2010, the e-Justice Portal includes a dedicated legal aid section providing multilingual information on eligibility, application processes, and national contact points across EU member states. It facilitates cross-border access for low-income individuals in civil, criminal, and administrative matters, integrating tools like eligibility calculators and pro bono directories to bridge resource gaps.
process_and_outcome: By 2023, the portal supported millions of users, contributing to harmonized application of the Legal Aid Directive and higher uptake rates in transnational cases.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Ireland Free Legal Advice Centres (FLAC) Expansion
example_background: Founded in 1970, FLAC operates a network of volunteer-staffed clinics offering free initial legal advice to those unable to afford solicitors, handling over 20,000 inquiries annually on issues like social welfare, housing, and employment. In response to economic downturns post-2008, it expanded services with government funding, including telephone advice lines and community outreach to ensure underserved populations receive guidance necessary for court proceedings.
process_and_outcome: FLAC's model has been praised in EU reports for preventing unnecessary litigation and was scaled nationally, with impact studies showing 80% resolution without further costs.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Belgium Pro Deo Legal Aid Reform Challenge
example_background: Under Belgium's pro deo system, indigent parties receive court-appointed lawyers based on income thresholds. In 2016, reports emerged of inconsistent application, with some low-income defendants in criminal cases denied aid due to administrative backlogs and narrow eligibility interpretations, particularly in family and administrative courts, leading to unrepresented appearances.
process_and_outcome: The Constitutional Court ruled certain restrictions unconstitutional in 2018, mandating broader access and streamlined processes, resulting in increased funding and lawyer appointments.
source_rule: Legal aid shall be made available to those who lack sufficient resources in so far as such aid is necessary to ensure effective access to justice.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47

example_name: Ibrahim and Others v. UK (ECtHR Grand Chamber)
example_background: Four applicants were involved in terrorism-related investigations following the failed transatlantic airline liquid bomb plot in 2006. They were arrested under the UK's Terrorism Act 2000 and held for up to 14 days. Legal access was initially denied or restricted under national security provisions. Confessions obtained during custody formed key evidence. The applicants argued that restrictions on lawyer access compromised their ability to challenge evidence and defend themselves effectively from the outset of proceedings.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights held that there was a violation of Article 6(1) and 6(3)(c) ECHR in three cases due to insufficient safeguards against self-incrimination without legal advice. The UK was found to have imposed disproportionate restrictions. No violation in one case where vulnerability was lower.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 48

example_name: Cadder v. HM Advocate (UK Supreme Court)
example_background: Peter Cadder was arrested in Scotland on suspicion of serious assault. He was detained for 11 hours and questioned by police without immediate access to a lawyer, as per Scottish law at the time which only provided legal assistance post-indictment. A DNA sample taken during this period linked him to the crime. Cadder challenged the admissibility of the evidence, arguing it was obtained without legal advice, affecting his presumption of innocence and defense preparation.
process_and_outcome: The UK Supreme Court ruled unanimously that the denial of legal advice during detention violated Article 6 ECHR. The conviction was quashed, leading to legislative changes requiring prompt legal access for detained persons in Scotland.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence
involved_parties: Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service (Scotland)

example_name: CJEU Case C-617/10 Åkerberg Fransson
example_background: Hans Åkerberg Fransson, a Swedish national, faced tax proceedings for undeclared income from employment. Swedish authorities conducted an administrative investigation involving questioning without prior legal assistance, followed by criminal tax evasion charges using the same evidence. He argued this double use of evidence breached the ne bis in idem principle and his right to defense under EU law, as it presumed guilt through administrative findings without full judicial safeguards.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU ruled that EU law precludes using administrative findings as evidence in subsequent criminal proceedings without equivalent defense rights. The case was referred back to Swedish courts, influencing harmonization of administrative and criminal procedures across the EU.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Article 50 EU Charter (Ne bis in idem)
Article 48 EU Charter (Presumption of innocence and defense rights)

example_name: Greek Golden Dawn Trial (Procedural Rulings)
example_background: The trial of 69 members of the far-right Golden Dawn party in Greece involved charges of operating a criminal organization and murders. During pre-trial detention, several defendants claimed violations of their right to defense due to restricted lawyer-client communications and media presumptions of guilt. The court examined whether investigative measures respected the presumption of innocence and allowed adequate preparation of defenses.
process_and_outcome: Greek courts issued rulings upholding defense rights, excluding certain media-influenced evidence and ensuring access to case files. The ongoing trial (initiated 2015) has set precedents for balancing public interest with individual rights in high-profile political cases.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence

example_name: EU Directive 2016/343 Implementation - Ireland Review
example_background: Ireland incorporated EU Directive 2016/343 on strengthening the presumption of innocence and right to be present at trial into national law via the Criminal Justice (Presumption of Innocence) Act 2020. This followed ECtHR cases highlighting gaps. The implementation ensured suspects receive written reasons for arrest, access to lawyers promptly, and restrictions on public authorities' statements implying guilt.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission reviewed compliance in 2022, confirming Ireland's measures met requirements. No infringement proceedings were initiated, serving as a model for procedural rights in criminal matters.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence

example_name: Belgian Assange Extradition Proceedings
example_background: Julian Assange faced extradition proceedings in Belgium-related contexts, but primarily UK courts applied EU standards post-Brexit with ECHR oversight. During hearings, restrictions on defense access to full US evidence were challenged, invoking presumption of innocence as media portrayal presumed guilt. Defense argued for full disclosure to prepare adequately.
process_and_outcome: Belgian and UK courts partially upheld defense requests for disclosure, with the UK High Court ruling in 2021 that assurances were needed on First Amendment protections, respecting defense rights. Ongoing appeals emphasize procedural fairness.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence
relevant_rules: EU Charter Article 47 (Right to effective remedy)

example_name: CJEU Case C-129/14 Spasic
example_background: A Serbian national was prosecuted in Germany for drug trafficking. He received a suspended sentence there, then faced further proceedings in Bulgaria for the same acts under EU ne bis in idem rules. The defense argued that the German decision should bar further punishment, preserving presumption of innocence post-final acquittal/sentence.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU clarified that ne bis in idem applies only if the first decision prevents further prosecution entirely. Bulgaria's actions were upheld as compliant since conditions were met, reinforcing uniform application of defense rights across member states.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Poland ECtHR Cases on Presumption of Innocence (e.g., Gąsior v. Poland)
example_background: Multiple cases against Poland arose from public statements by high officials labeling suspects as guilty before conviction, particularly in corruption probes involving politicians. In Gąsior, a prosecutor publicly announced guilt in media, impacting the applicant's trial. This raised concerns over state interference with presumption of innocence and defense preparation.
process_and_outcome: ECtHR found violations in several cases, awarding damages and ordering Poland to amend practices. Led to training for prosecutors on media conduct.
source_rule: Presumption of innocence and right of defence

example_name: Salduz v. Turkey (ECtHR Grand Chamber)
example_background: The applicant, a Turkish national of Kurdish origin, was arrested in connection with criminal activities in south-eastern Turkey. He was held in police custody for several days without access to a lawyer and made a confession which was later used as evidence in his trial. The case reached the European Court of Human Rights after domestic courts convicted him based on that confession. The Court examined whether the lack of legal assistance during initial questioning undermined the fairness of the proceedings, considering the vulnerability of suspects in custody and the importance of legal advice from the outset to ensure reliable statements.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber ruled that Article 6(3)(c) of the European Convention on Human Rights provides an effective right to legal assistance from the first interrogation when custodial pressure creates vulnerability. Turkey was found to have violated the Convention, and the applicant was awarded compensation.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: Beuze v. Belgium (ECtHR Grand Chamber)
example_background: The applicant was arrested on suspicion of murder and held incommunicado for four days without access to a lawyer. He confessed during police questioning, and the confession was admitted at trial despite challenges. The case revisited the Salduz principle, examining whether compelling circumstances justified admitting the statement and whether overall fairness was preserved through other procedural protections.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber ruled there was no violation, establishing that absent compelling circumstances, statements obtained without a lawyer are inadmissible unless strict safeguards ensure fairness. Belgium's use of the statement was upheld due to corroborative evidence.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: Borgers v. Belgium
example_background: The applicant faced charges of forgery and use of forged documents. During the investigative phase, he was not allowed his lawyer's presence at the first questioning by the investigating judge, though subsequent access was granted. The national law restricted lawyer presence at initial hearings, and the applicant argued this impaired his defense preparation.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found a violation of Article 6(3)(c), holding that exclusion of a lawyer from the first interrogation by the investigating judge infringed defense rights, regardless of later access.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: John Murray v. UK
example_background: The applicant was arrested under the Prevention of Terrorism (Temporary Provisions) Act 1984 on suspicion of IRA-related offenses. He was questioned without a lawyer present due to statutory provisions allowing delay, and an inference was drawn from his silence. The silence and inferences were used at trial, prompting challenges to the fairness of the proceedings.
process_and_outcome: The Court found a violation of Article 6(1) due to lack of legal advice compromising fairness, but noted the overall trial safeguards. The UK was held accountable for the initial denial.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: Brennan v. UK
example_background: The applicant was arrested under anti-terrorism legislation and denied access to a lawyer for 48 hours. During this period, he made admissions that were admitted in evidence. The case tested the compatibility of delayed legal access with Convention rights, particularly in terrorism contexts.
process_and_outcome: The European Court found a violation of Article 6(3)(c), as the delay and lack of access led to potentially unreliable evidence without adequate counterbalancing measures.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: Hermi v. Italy
example_background: The applicant was prosecuted for drug trafficking. His lawyer was not notified of the preliminary investigation hearing where key evidence was discussed, and he learned of it only later. This prevented effective participation in the early stages of proceedings.
process_and_outcome: The Court ruled a violation of Article 6(3)(b) and (d), as the failure to involve the defense in critical investigative steps impaired rights to information and examination of witnesses.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: Simeonovi v. Bulgaria
example_background: The applicant was charged with murder and denied access to a lawyer during pre-trial detention and initial questioning. His conviction relied heavily on statements made without legal assistance, and he alleged coercion.
process_and_outcome: The European Court found violations of Articles 3 and 6, including denial of legal aid from the first interrogation, rendering the trial unfair. Bulgaria was ordered to pay damages.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: EU Directive 2012/13 on Right to Information in Criminal Proceedings Implementation - Netherlands Compliance
example_background: The Netherlands transposed EU Directive 2012/13, ensuring suspects receive a 'Letter of Rights' upon arrest, detailing access to a lawyer, interpreter, and information on charges. This was applied in routine criminal cases, with training for police and judicial authorities to facilitate immediate legal consultations.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission reviewed transposition and found no infringement, confirming effective implementation that supported defense rights from the initial stages.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.
relevant_rules: Article 47 EU Charter of Fundamental Rights

example_name: Directive 2013/48/EU on Right of Access to a Lawyer Implementation - Sweden Best Practice
example_background: Sweden implemented Directive 2013/48/EU by mandating lawyer access within three hours of arrest, including during interrogations and identification procedures. This included provisions for vulnerable suspects and exceptions only in specific pre-defined circumstances, with mandatory audio-visual recording.
process_and_outcome: Evaluations by the Council of Europe and EU Commission noted Sweden's model as exemplary, with low rates of challenges to procedural fairness in courts.
source_rule: 2.   Respect for the rights of the defence of anyone who has been charged shall be guaranteed.

example_name: Nuremberg Trials - Ex Post Facto Application Debate
example_background: Following World War II, leaders of the Nazi regime were prosecuted for war crimes and crimes against humanity at the International Military Tribunal in Nuremberg from 1945 to 1946. The charges included planning and waging aggressive war, violations of the laws or customs of war, and crimes against humanity such as mass murder and enslavement. Critics argued that some charges, particularly crimes against humanity and conspiracy to wage aggressive war, had not been explicitly defined as criminal offenses under international law prior to the war, raising questions about retroactive application of laws. The tribunal proceeded with 24 major defendants, including Hermann Göring, Rudolf Hess, and Joachim von Ribbentrop, who faced detailed evidence of their roles in the regime's actions across Europe. The proceedings involved presentation of extensive documentation, witness testimonies, and arguments over the legal basis for the charges, with defense counsel contending that the acts were not criminalized at the time of commission.
process_and_outcome: The tribunal convicted 19 defendants, with 12 receiving death sentences by hanging, three life imprisonments, four long-term imprisonments, and three acquittals. Sentences were carried out in 1946, and the judgments established precedents for international criminal law despite ongoing debates about retroactivity, which were addressed by referencing pre-existing treaty obligations and customary international law.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.
relevant_rules: London Charter of the International Military Tribunal (1945)

example_name: Lovell v. City of Griffin (1938 US Supreme Court)
example_background: Alma Lovell, a Jehovah's Witness, was convicted in 1936 under a Griffin, Georgia ordinance requiring written permission from the city manager for distributing circulars, which she did without obtaining such permission while sharing religious literature door-to-door. The ordinance, enacted after her activity began, was applied to her case. She challenged the conviction on First Amendment grounds and argued the vagueness and retroactive nature of the licensing requirement. The case reached the US Supreme Court, highlighting tensions between local regulatory authority and individual rights to free speech and religious expression.
process_and_outcome: The Supreme Court unanimously reversed the conviction in 1938, ruling the ordinance unconstitutional as it functioned as a prior restraint on free speech and press, protected under the Fourteenth Amendment. The decision invalidated the permit requirement without direct focus on retroactivity but emphasized protections against arbitrary application.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.
relevant_rules: US Constitution First and Fourteenth Amendments

example_name: Calder v. Bull (1798 US Supreme Court)
example_background: In 1795, Connecticut passed an act providing relief to parties in a probate dispute involving Dr. Benjamin Bull's will, allowing a new trial after a court had upheld the will's distribution. The original judgment was from 1793, and the challengers argued the act violated the Ex Post Fact facto Clause of the US Constitution by retroactively altering vested rights. The case involved property distribution among heirs and tested early interpretations of constitutional prohibitions on retrospective laws in civil contexts.
process_and_outcome: The Supreme Court upheld the Connecticut act in 1798, ruling 4-0 that the Ex Post Facto Clause applies only to criminal cases, not civil matters like this probate relief, thereby allowing the retrospective legislative action.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.
relevant_rules: US Constitution Article I Section 9 and 10 (Ex Post Facto Clause)

example_name: Stogner v. California (2003 US Supreme Court)
example_background: Marion Stogner was indicted in 1998 for child sexual abuse offenses allegedly committed between 1955 and 1966 against his daughters. California's statute of limitations for felony sex offenses was originally six years, which had expired decades earlier. In 1993, California enacted a new law allowing prosecution if charges were filed within one year of the victim's report, applied retroactively to revive expired cases. Stogner argued this violated ex post facto principles by extending limitation periods after the offenses and original limitation periods had lapsed.
process_and_outcome: The US Supreme Court ruled 5-4 in 2003 that the retroactive extension violated the Ex Post Facto Clause, vacating the conviction and establishing that such revivals after limitation expiration are punitive and unconstitutional.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.

example_name: EU Court of Human Rights - Scoppola v. Italy (No. 2) (2012)
example_background: Franco Scoppola was convicted in 1999 of murder and other offenses in Italy, sentenced under laws applicable at the time to life imprisonment without review. In 2006, Italy introduced a new law allowing periodic review for life sentences after 10 years for certain categories, but excluded those convicted of multiple murders like Scoppola. He argued under Article 7 ECHR that applying the stricter pre-2006 regime retroactively imposed a heavier penalty than available at commission, while a lighter penalty was later provided.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights ruled in 2012 that Italy violated Article 7 by not applying the lighter 2006 review provision to Scoppola's sentence, ordering Italy to ensure review eligibility.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 7

example_name: Weems v. United States (1910 US Supreme Court)
