example_background: Paulino Weems, a disbursing officer in the Philippine Islands under US administration, was convicted in 1905 of falsifying public records by making small alterations to disbursement entries, charged under a Spanish-era penal code retained by US authorities. The penalty imposed was 12 years of cadena temporal, involving hard labor in irons, surveillance for life, and loss of rights, which was argued to be disproportionately severe compared to penalties for graver crimes and potentially reflective of outdated law application.
process_and_outcome: The US Supreme Court reversed in 1910, finding the punishment cruel and unusual under the Eighth Amendment, influencing later interpretations but not directly ruling on ex post facto grounds as the law predated the acts.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.

example_name: Carmichael v. United States (1942 US Supreme Court)
example_background: John J. Carmichael was convicted under the 1935 Social Security Act for failing to pay unemployment compensation taxes in 1937. He argued the Act's provisions were amended in 1939 to reduce certain penalties and extend grace periods, claiming retroactive application of the heavier 1935 penalties violated ex post facto principles.
process_and_outcome: The Supreme Court ruled unanimously in 1942 that tax statutes are civil, not criminal, and thus not subject to ex post facto restrictions, affirming the conviction under the original law.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.

example_name: EU Case C-239/03 - Commission v. France (2004)
example_background: France maintained legislation allowing prosecution for certain environmental offenses with penalties increased after the offenses were committed. The European Commission challenged this under EU directives requiring transposition of environmental liability rules, where France applied post-transposition heavier sanctions to pre-existing acts, prompting review of compatibility with fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice ruled in 2004 that France failed to fulfill obligations by not properly transposing directives, but noted that retroactive application of lighter penalties is required where applicable, indirectly upholding non-retroactivity of heavier penalties.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 49

example_name: R v. Fields (Canada 1997)
example_background: In Canada, changes to sentencing guidelines for sexual assault offenses were introduced after the commission of crimes by the accused, with courts initially applying the new maximum penalties. The case involved review of whether increased sentence ranges could be applied retroactively to acts committed under prior law.
process_and_outcome: The British Columbia Court of Appeal in 1997 ruled that the increased maximum sentences could not be applied retroactively, as it would impose heavier penalties than applicable at the time, aligning with constitutional protections.
source_rule: 1.   No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national law or international law at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed. If, subsequent to the commission of a criminal offence, the law provides for a lighter penalty, that penalty shall be applicable.
relevant_rules: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms Section 11(g)

example_name: Amanda Knox Case
example_background: Amanda Knox, an American student, was arrested in Italy in 2007 following the murder of her roommate Meredith Kercher in Perugia. Prosecutors alleged Knox's involvement based on circumstantial evidence, inconsistent witness statements, and a disputed confession obtained after a prolonged interrogation without legal assistance or proper translation. Knox spent nearly four years in prison before her conviction was overturned. The case drew international attention due to questions about investigative methods, media influence, and the handling of evidence such as DNA traces that were later contested for contamination risks. Italy's court system navigated multiple trials, acquittals, and retrials amid public scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: Initial conviction in 2009 for murder and slander; overturned by appeals court in 2011; slander reconviction in 2014 quashed by Italy's highest court in 2015 due to fair trial violations; European Court of Human Rights ruled in 2019 that Italy violated Knox's rights by denying her a lawyer during interrogation, awarding compensation.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
relevant_rules: Article 48, EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (presumption of innocence)
Article 6, European Convention on Human Rights (fair trial)

example_name: O.J. Simpson Trial
example_background: O.J. Simpson, a former NFL player and actor, was charged in 1994 with the murders of his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and Ron Goldman in Los Angeles. The prosecution presented DNA evidence, blood samples, and a glove linked to the scene, while the defense highlighted police misconduct, including evidence tampering claims against detective Mark Fuhrman, who was later found to have used racial slurs. The televised 'Trial of the Century' lasted 11 months, involving extensive forensic analysis and witness testimonies amid widespread media coverage.
process_and_outcome: Criminal trial jury acquitted Simpson in 1995 after deliberating less than four hours, upholding presumption of innocence; subsequent civil trial in 1997 found him liable for wrongful death, awarding $33.5 million to victims' families.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
relevant_rules: Fifth Amendment, U.S. Constitution (due process, presumption of innocence)

example_name: Golden State Killer Case - Joseph DeAngelo
example_background: Joseph James DeAngelo was identified in 2018 through investigative genetic genealogy as the suspect in over 50 rapes and 13 murders across California from the 1970s to 1980s, known as the East Area Rapist and Original Night Stalker. Arrest followed decades of unsolved cases, with DNA from crime scenes matched to DeAngelo's family tree via public databases. He was charged with 13 murders after familial DNA led investigators to his doorstep.
process_and_outcome: Pleaded guilty in 2020 to 13 murders and 13 related charges; sentenced to life without parole in August 2020, confirming guilt beyond reasonable doubt after presumption of innocence phase.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
relevant_rules: Fifth Amendment, U.S. Constitution (presumption of innocence)

example_name: Salman Khan Acquittal in Hit-and-Run Case
example_background: Bollywood actor Salman Khan was charged in 2002 with culpable homicide after his car allegedly ran over pavement dwellers in Mumbai, killing one and injuring others. The case involved disputes over whether Khan was driving, the condition of the car's tires, and eyewitness accounts. Multiple trials occurred over 13 years with changing witness statements and forensic reports on blood alcohol levels that were contested.
process_and_outcome: Bombay High Court acquitted Khan in 2014, citing insufficient evidence to prove he was driving or culpable; Supreme Court of India upheld acquittal in 2016, emphasizing lack of proof beyond reasonable doubt.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
relevant_rules: Article 21, Indian Constitution (right to life and fair trial)

example_name: Enron Executives Trial - Kenneth Lay and Jeffrey Skilling
example_background: Enron Corporation executives Kenneth Lay and Jeffrey Skilling faced charges in 2006 for orchestrating accounting fraud that led to the company's 2001 bankruptcy, wiping out $74 billion in shareholder value. The scandal involved off-balance-sheet entities and manipulated financial statements. Prosecutors alleged securities fraud, while defense claimed business judgments gone awry.
process_and_outcome: Federal jury convicted Skilling on 19 counts and Lay on 6 counts in 2006; sentences included 24 years for Skilling (later reduced) and Lay died before sentencing; convictions upheld presumption of guilt after trial.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
relevant_rules: Fifth Amendment, U.S. Constitution (presumption of innocence)

example_name: Bernie Madoff Ponzi Scheme Prosecution
example_background: Bernard Madoff operated a $65 billion Ponzi scheme through his investment firm from the 1990s until 2008, defrauding thousands of investors by fabricating returns with nonexistent trades. Arrested after confessing to employees amid the financial crisis, the case involved SEC prior investigations that failed to uncover the fraud despite red flags.
process_and_outcome: Pleaded guilty to 11 federal felonies in 2009; sentenced to 150 years in prison, demonstrating proof of guilt after initial presumption of innocence.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
involved_parties: U.S. Attorney Southern District of New York
relevant_rules: Fifth Amendment, U.S. Constitution (presumption of innocence)

example_name: Rohypnol Case - Dr. Farid Fata
example_background: Michigan oncologist Farid Fata was charged in 2014 with administering unnecessary chemotherapy to over 500 patients to maximize Medicare reimbursements, leading to patient harm and deaths. Federal investigators reviewed medical records showing healthy patients treated for fake cancers, with bills exceeding $35 million.
process_and_outcome: Pleaded guilty to racketeering and fraud charges in 2014; sentenced to 45 years in prison in 2015, confirming guilt post-trial process.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
relevant_rules: Fifth Amendment, U.S. Constitution (presumption of innocence)

example_name: EU Data Retention Directive Challenge - Digital Rights Ireland
example_background: In 2014, the Court of Justice of the EU addressed mass data retention policies mandated by the 2006 EU Data Retention Directive, which required telecom providers to store traffic and location data of all users for law enforcement access without individualized suspicion. Challengers argued it undermined fundamental rights including privacy and presumption of innocence by enabling generalized surveillance.
process_and_outcome: CJEU invalidated the Directive as disproportionate and invalidating presumption of innocence protections; member states ordered to revise national laws.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Article 48, EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (presumption of innocence)

example_name: Cambridge Analytica Scandal Investigations
example_background: In 2018, revelations emerged that Cambridge Analytica harvested data from 87 million Facebook users without consent via a quiz app, used for political targeting in elections. Regulators probed whether Facebook and the firm presumed wrongdoing prematurely in public statements and compliance failures.
process_and_outcome: UK Information Commissioner fined Facebook £500,000 in 2019; U.S. FTC settled for $5 billion; no criminal charges against executives due to insufficient proof of intent.
source_rule: 1.   Everyone who has been charged shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.
relevant_rules: Article 48, EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (presumption of innocence)

example_name: Soering v United Kingdom
example_background: In 1989, Jens Soering, a German national facing capital murder charges in Virginia, USA, was subject to potential extradition from the United Kingdom. The case involved allegations of double murder committed in 1985. Concerns arose regarding the conditions on death row in Virginia, including prolonged isolation, and the application of the death penalty, which could result in execution by electric chair. Soering argued that extradition would expose him to treatment amounting to inhuman or degrading treatment due to the severity of the punishment for the offenses charged. The European Court of Human Rights examined whether the UK's decision to extradite without assurances against the death penalty constituted a violation of fundamental protections. The court considered the evolving standards in democratic societies and the specific circumstances of death row phenomenon.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights ruled in favor of Soering, finding that extradition without assurances would violate Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The UK was required to seek assurances from the US authorities that the death penalty would not be imposed or carried out. The extradition proceeded only after such assurances were obtained.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.
relevant_rules: Article 3 ECHR (prohibition of torture and inhuman or degrading treatment)

example_name: Vinter and Others v United Kingdom
example_background: Three applicants, Douglas Vinter, George Burke, and Jeremy Bamber, were serving life sentences in the UK with whole life orders, meaning no possibility of release. Vinter had been convicted of murdering his wife, Burke of murdering a police officer, and Bamber of multiple murders including family members. They challenged the compatibility of irreducible life sentences with the European Convention on Human Rights, arguing that such sentences constituted inhuman or degrading treatment due to their absolute nature without review mechanisms. The cases highlighted debates on penological justifications and the right to hope for release even in grave cases.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights held by 16 votes to 1 that whole life orders were not inherently incompatible with Article 3 ECHR, provided there was a prospect of review and release in exceptional circumstances. The UK system was found to implicitly allow for such reviews through the Secretary of State's prerogative power, thus the sentences did not reduce prisoners to 'living death'. No violation was found.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.
involved_parties: Douglas Vinter, George Burke, Jeremy Bamber (applicants)

example_name: Scoppola v Italy (No. 3)
example_background: Salvatore Scoppola was convicted in Italy of multiple serious offenses including murder during a robbery in 1998. He received a life sentence reducible to 21 years after serving 26 years. Following legislative changes, he sought retroactive application of more favorable provisions that would have reduced his sentence further. The case addressed whether mandatory life imprisonment without parole for certain aggravated murders was disproportionate, considering the offender's age, circumstances, and rehabilitation prospects.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found a violation of Article 7 ECHR (no heavier penalty) and Protocol No. 7 Article 4 (retroactivity of more favorable laws). Italy was required to adjust the sentence accordingly. The Court noted life sentences must allow for review.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.

example_name: ECtHR in Hutchinson v United Kingdom
example_background: Raymond Hutchinson was convicted in Northern Ireland of serious sexual offenses against children and received a life sentence with a whole life tariff. He challenged the tariff decision, arguing it amounted to a disproportionate penalty given the gravity of the offenses and lack of review prospects. The case followed Vinter, testing the application of review mechanisms for whole life orders in terrorism and sexual offense contexts.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights upheld the UK position, finding no violation of Article 3 ECHR. The Court clarified that the possibility of review by the Secretary of State after 25 years provided sufficient prospect of release, aligning with evolving standards.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.

example_name: ECtHR in Lăcătuş v Switzerland
example_background: Mihai Lăcătuş, a Romanian national in Switzerland, was convicted of theft and sentenced to a 12-month custodial sentence. Due to his foreign status and inability to pay a fine, he faced expulsion. He argued the combined sanction was disproportionate for a minor property offense, especially impacting his family life.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found no violation of Article 8 ECHR, determining the penalty was proportionate to the offense considering public safety and prevention of reoffending.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.

example_name: ECtHR in Pastuhov v Bulgaria
example_background: The applicant was convicted of serious fraud offenses involving large sums and received a long prison sentence combined with high fines. He contended that the financial penalties were excessive relative to his means and the offense's nature, leading to de facto life imprisonment due to inability to pay.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found a violation under Article 3 ECHR, as the cumulative penalties amounted to inhuman treatment given the disproportionate financial burden.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.

example_name: ECtHR in G.I.E.M. S.A. and Others v Italy Cases
example_background: Multiple applicants convicted of mafia-type association and other organized crime offenses received life sentences. They challenged the sentences as disproportionate, particularly preventive measures and lack of flexibility for rehabilitation.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights found violations of Article 3 ECHR in some aspects due to insufficient review prospects for life sentences in organized crime contexts, requiring Italy to ensure review mechanisms.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.

example_name: ECtHR in Van der Velden v Netherlands
example_background: The applicant received a custodial sentence for serious sexual offenses against minors. The penalty included long-term imprisonment with TBS (terugplaatsing stelsel) order for psychiatric treatment. He argued the indeterminate nature post-sentence was disproportionate.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights ruled no violation of Article 5 §1 ECHR, finding the measure proportionate to protect society given the offense severity and risk assessment.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.

example_name: ECtHR in Affaire Delfi AS v Estonie
example_background: Delfi AS, an Estonian news portal, hosted user comments defamatory to a company executive. The portal was held liable for the comments and ordered to pay damages. It argued the civil liability was disproportionate for an internet intermediary.
process_and_outcome: The Grand Chamber found no violation of Article 10 ECHR, holding the penalty proportionate to the harm caused and the need to protect reputation.
source_rule: 3.   The severity of penalties must not be disproportionate to the criminal offence.

example_name: CJEU Judgment in Case C-617/10 Åkerberg Fransson
example_background: Håkan Åkerberg Fransson, a Swedish national, was involved in a case concerning tax evasion through failure to declare VAT on fishing services between 2006 and 2007. Swedish authorities pursued both administrative tax proceedings and separate criminal proceedings for the same conduct. The administrative process resulted in a tax assessment of SEK 400,000 plus interest, while criminal charges were filed under the Tax Offences Act. The Swedish Supreme Court referred questions to the CJEU on whether this dual-track approach complied with EU law requirements under the Charter of Fundamental Rights, specifically regarding the principle of ne bis in idem, which relates to proportionality and legality in penalties. The case highlighted tensions between national systems allowing successive administrative and criminal sanctions for the same facts and EU protections against disproportionate punishment.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on February 26, 2013, that Article 50 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights precludes a Member State from imposing successive administrative and criminal penalties for the same offense unless the administrative sanction is not of a criminal nature under the Engel criteria or if it serves different objectives. The case was remitted to Swedish courts, which ultimately imposed a conditional sentence and fine on Fransson. The judgment set a precedent influencing national penalty regimes across the EU.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Article 50 Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (ne bis in idem)
Article 52 Charter of Fundamental Rights (scope and interpretation of rights and principles)

example_name: CJEU Judgment in Case C-489/10 Bonda
example_background: In Poland, Mr. Bonda received social security benefits fraudulently after failing to declare employment while claiming unemployment aid. Administrative authorities recovered the overpaid benefits and imposed an additional fine equivalent to three times the amount obtained. Subsequently, criminal proceedings were initiated for the same conduct under Polish law. The Polish court referred the matter to the CJEU questioning if this combination of penalties constituted a disproportionate interference with fundamental rights under EU law. The case examined the nature of the administrative fine and its relation to criminal sanctions, testing the boundaries of legality and proportionality in penalty imposition.
process_and_outcome: On June 5, 2012, the CJEU held that the Polish practice infringed Article 50 of the Charter as the administrative fine qualified as punitive under the Engel criteria, preventing subsequent criminal proceedings. This led to adjustments in Polish enforcement practices to align with EU standards on non-duplication of penalties.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)
relevant_rules: Article 50 Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU
Polish Act on Social Insurance System

example_name: CJEU Judgment in Case C-398/12 Criminal proceedings against M.
example_background: An individual identified as M. faced administrative proceedings in the Netherlands for undeclared income, resulting in a tax debt reassessment and penalty. Following this, criminal proceedings were brought for tax fraud on identical facts. The Dutch court sought CJEU guidance on whether such successive penalties respected EU fundamental rights principles, particularly the ne bis in idem rule and proportionality of sanctions for offenses.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU on May 8, 2014, clarified that if administrative penalties pursue a deterrent aim and are imposed before criminal proceedings conclude, they may preclude further criminal sanctions unless adjusted for proportionality. The ruling prompted Dutch authorities to refine their dual penalty framework.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Framework Decision 2005/214/JHA on ne bis in idem

example_name: Garanti Bankası GDPR Fine by Turkish DPA
example_background: Garanti Bankası, a major Turkish bank, processed personal data of approximately 1.1 million customers without adequate legal basis or transparency during a marketing campaign. The Personal Data Protection Authority (KVKK) investigated complaints revealing that consent was not properly obtained, and data processing lacked proportionality to the stated purposes. The bank's practices involved sharing customer data with third parties without clear notification, raising issues on the legality of data handling under protection laws aligned with EU standards.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the KVKK imposed a fine of 1,850,000 TL (around €250,000) on the bank for violations. The decision emphasized the need for explicit legal grounds and proportional measures in data processing.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Turkish Personal Data Protection Authority (KVKK, regulator)
relevant_rules: Turkish Personal Data Protection Law No. 6698 (aligned with GDPR)
Article 5 GDPR (principles relating to processing, legality, proportionality)
EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8 (protection of personal data)

example_name: CJEU Case C-617/17 Blanckaert
example_background: In Belgium, individuals involved in VAT carousel fraud faced administrative recovery of unpaid VAT plus interest, followed by criminal prosecution for the same scheme. The case involved organized evasion where companies in a chain failed to remit collected VAT. Belgian courts referred to CJEU on whether the administrative measures, which included freezing assets and penalties, allowed for subsequent criminal trials without breaching ne bis in idem and proportionality principles.
process_and_outcome: On December 20, 2018, CJEU ruled that administrative proceedings concluding with finality preclude criminal ones if both are punitive in nature. This influenced Belgian tax fraud enforcement to consolidate proceedings.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Directive 2008/9/EC on administrative cooperation in tax matters

example_name: Mowi ASA GDPR Fine by Norwegian DPA
example_background: Mowi ASA, a global seafood company headquartered in Norway, experienced a data breach affecting 9,000 employees' personal data due to misconfigured access controls on its internal systems. The Norwegian Data Protection Authority examined the incident, finding that the company failed to implement data protection by design and default measures proportionate to the risks, despite handling sensitive employee information like health data.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Datatilsynet fined Mowi 18 million NOK (about €1.7 million). The company was ordered to review and enhance its security practices.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties

example_name: EU Commission v. Hungary Infringement Case on NGO Law
example_background: Hungary enacted a law in 2017 requiring NGOs receiving over certain foreign funding to register as 'foreign-funded' and disclose details, which the European Commission viewed as disproportionate interference with freedom of association. The law affected over 200 organizations, leading to closures and stigmatization. The Commission initiated infringement proceedings arguing it lacked legitimate aim and proportionality under EU fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: In June 2020, the CJEU ruled against Hungary (Case C-78/18), finding the law incompatible with Article 63 TFEU and Charter Articles 7, 8, 12, 14, 16, 17, 21, 22, and 52. Hungary was ordered to repeal it and pay costs.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Schrems II Judgment (Case C-311/18)
example_background: Max Schrems challenged Facebook's data transfers from EU to US servers, arguing US surveillance laws lacked proportionality under EU standards. The case revisited adequacy decisions post-Safe Harbor invalidation, examining if transfers complied with data protection principles mirroring legality and proportionality of interferences.
process_and_outcome: On July 16, 2020, CJEU invalidated Privacy Shield and set strict requirements for SCCs, emphasizing necessity and proportionality in third-country protections. Facebook adjusted transfers accordingly.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB Case C-203/15
example_background: Swedish telecom provider Tele2 retained traffic and location data under national law for law enforcement access, even outside serious crime contexts. The CJEU assessed if this blanket retention was a proportionate interference with privacy rights.
process_and_outcome: On December 21, 2016, CJEU ruled general data retention incompatible with Articles 7 and 8 Charter, requiring targeted retention only. Sweden amended its laws.
source_rule: Principles of legality and proportionality of criminal offences and penalties
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Krombach v France
example_background: The case involved Dr. Klaus Krombach, a German physician accused of manslaughter in France following the death of a patient during anesthesia in 1993. French authorities issued an international arrest warrant after he failed to attend trial. Krombach was detained in Germany in 1997 and released on condition he appear for trial in France, but he did not. In 2001, absentia proceedings in a French Assize Court resulted in a 15-year prison sentence. Later that year, Germany extradited him to France based on the conviction. Upon arrival, French authorities added a new charge of corruption of a witness, which had been mentioned but not formally pursued in the original trial. This new charge proceeded to trial in 2007, leading to an additional conviction and sentence.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) examined the case in 2007 and found no violation of Article 4 of Protocol No. 7, ruling that the corruption charge constituted a different offense from the manslaughter charge, thus permitting separate proceedings. Krombach served part of his sentence before conditional release.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Weiss v Austria
example_background: Horst Otto Weiss, an Austrian national, faced charges in 1984 for serious fraud involving the misappropriation of approximately 18 million schillings from a company. He was acquitted by the Regional Court in 1985 due to lack of evidence. Prosecutors appealed, and the Regional Court quashed the acquittal in 1986, ordering a retrial. The retrial commenced in 1989 before a different bench, but the proceedings were suspended when Weiss's counsel raised a ne bis in idem objection, which was upheld by the Supreme Court.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR, in its 2004 judgment, determined that Austria violated Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 by quashing a final acquittal and ordering a retrial, as this constituted an impermissible attempt to reopen a final decision in the same case. Austria was ordered to pay damages.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Poniatowski v Poland
example_background: Tomasz Poniatowski was charged in Poland with fraud related to issuing bad checks in 1992. The District Court acquitted him in 1995 after trial. The prosecutor appealed, and the Regional Court quashed the acquittal in 1996, referring the case for retrial. A second trial began in 1997, resulting in conviction and a suspended sentence. Poniatowski argued this infringed his right not to be tried again for the same offense.
process_and_outcome: In 2003, the ECHR found a violation of Article 4 of Protocol No. 7, as Poland's appeal system allowed quashing of a final acquittal and retrial without justification under the exceptions provided. Poland was required to pay compensation.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Nikitin v Russia
example_background: Alexander Nikitin, an environmental activist, was investigated in 1995 for espionage related to a report on nuclear submarine safety in the Barents Sea published by the Bellona Foundation. Federal Security Service (FSB) charged him under secret articles of the Russian Criminal Code. The St. Petersburg District Military Court acquitted him in 1999 due to lack of evidence of state secrets. Prosecutors appealed, leading to quashing of the acquittal by higher military courts.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR ruled in 2004 that Russia violated Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 by allowing appeal quashing of the acquittal and resuming proceedings, as it did not fall under permitted exceptions. Additional violations of fair trial rights were found; Russia paid damages.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Ruotsalainen v Finland
example_background: Mikael Ruotsalainen was convicted in Finland in 1999 for aggravated theft and fraud by the Helsinki District Court, receiving a two-year sentence. He appealed successfully to the Court of Appeal, which acquitted him in 2001 after re-examination. The Prosecutor General then pursued extraordinary appeal to the Supreme Court, which in 2002 quashed the acquittal and remitted for new appeal proceedings, resulting in reconviction.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR in 2005 found no violation of Article 4 of Protocol No. 7, as the Finnish Supreme Court's quashing was pursuant to its authority over points of law and the case remained within appeal stages, not a final second-instance decision triggering ne bis in idem.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Serra v Italy
example_background: In Italy, Giuseppe Serra was prosecuted for bankruptcy offenses related to a company liquidation in the 1980s. After an initial conviction was quashed on appeal, new proceedings were initiated on the same facts under slightly different legal characterizations, leading to a second conviction in 1995. Serra claimed this amounted to being tried twice for the same offense.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR in 2000 declared the application inadmissible, finding that Italian courts had properly distinguished the charges as involving different aspects of the bankruptcy, thus not engaging Article 4 of Protocol No. 7.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Zolotukhin v Russia
example_background: Konstantin Zolotukhin was charged in Russia in 2004 with petty hooliganism after an altercation with court guards during a civil case. The Justice of the Peace court acquitted him in 2005. Prosecutors appealed, and the District Court quashed the acquittal, convicting him of disorderly conduct under a different but factually identical provision, imposing a fine.
process_and_outcome: In its landmark 2009 Grand Chamber judgment, the ECHR found a violation of Article 4 of Protocol No. 7, establishing an autonomous interpretation of 'the same offence' based on factual circumstances, irrespective of legal classification. Russia was ordered to pay non-pecuniary damages.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Mihalache v Romania
example_background: In Romania, Valentin Mihalache was convicted in absentia in 1995 by the Bucharest County Court for sexual offenses against minors. He was apprehended in 2003, and new proceedings commenced before the same court on the same charges, leading to another conviction in 2004 despite his ne bis in idem plea.
process_and_outcome: The ECHR in 2017 ruled that Romania violated Article 4 of Protocol No. 7, as the second set of proceedings concerned the identical facts and legal basis, with no valid justification for retrying after the initial final decision. Romania was directed to pay damages.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: A and B v Norway
example_background: Two applicants, A and B, were involved in separate tax fraud cases in Norway. A was acquitted by the District Court in 2009 for lack of intent; the prosecution appealed successfully to the High Court, which convicted. B's similar acquittal was quashed on appeal. Both raised ne bis in idem claims under the autonomous approach.
process_and_outcome: In 2010, the ECHR found no violation of Article 4 of Protocol No. 7, as the proceedings were at first instance and subject to ordinary appeal, which is expressly permitted without constituting a second trial.
source_rule: Right not to be tried or punished twice in criminal proceedings for the same criminal offence
relevant_rules: Article 4 of Protocol No. 7 to the European Convention on Human Rights

example_name: Schrems II Case - Facebook EU-US Data Transfers
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, filed complaints against Facebook Ireland for transferring personal data of European users to servers in the United States. The case highlighted concerns over the level of protection provided by US surveillance laws for EU residents' data under the EU-US Privacy Shield framework. Investigations revealed that data transfers occurred without adequate safeguards, exposing EU data subjects to potential government access without sufficient remedies. The case originated from earlier proceedings where the Safe Harbour agreement was invalidated in 2015, leading to reliance on Privacy Shield, which was scrutinized for similar issues. Facebook's operations involved processing vast amounts of personal data from over 500 million EU users daily, including profile information, posts, and location data, routed through Ireland to the US.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) delivered its judgment on July 16, 2020, invalidating the EU-US Privacy Shield and setting strict requirements for standard contractual clauses (SCCs). Facebook was required to implement additional measures for data transfers, and ongoing litigation continued in Irish courts regarding compliance.
source_rule: Field of application
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 7 and 8

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Breach
example_background: Marriott acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database with over 383 million guest records. A breach detected in 2018 exposed passport numbers, payment card details, and travel history for 5.2 million EU residents dating back years. The company failed to notify authorities within 72 hours and lacked sufficient safeguards during the acquisition integration process, affecting the privacy rights of numerous EU citizens.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined Marriott 18.4 million GBP in 2020, reduced from 99 million GBP after appeal. Multiple DPAs coordinated under GDPR's one-stop-shop mechanism, and Marriott settled US class actions separately.
source_rule: Field of application
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 7-8

example_name: Amazon France Logics iad Cookies Fine
example_background: The French CNIL inspected Amazon's website and found it placed advertising cookies without valid consent, bypassing the mandatory cookie banner or using pre-checked boxes. This occurred despite clear user signals rejecting trackers, with data shared with third parties for behavioral advertising, impacting user privacy rights across the EU.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Amazon 35 million euros in December 2020. Amazon modified its consent platform to comply with IAB Transparency & Consent Framework standards.
source_rule: Field of application
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp GDPR Fine
example_background: Following Facebook's acquisition of WhatsApp, the DPC investigated data sharing practices between the companies. WhatsApp's 2018 policy update shared user phone numbers and metadata with Facebook for advertising without clear opt-out options or transparent legal basis, affecting 36 million Irish users and broader EU base.
process_and_outcome: DPC imposed a 225 million euro fine in September 2021 after a five-year investigation coordinated with other EU DPAs. WhatsApp updated its privacy policy to clarify data use.
source_rule: Field of application
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent, building a database sold to law enforcement. EU DPAs found this violated data minimization and purpose limitation principles, as biometric data of EU citizens was processed en masse without legal basis or transparency.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, multiple DPAs including the Dutch, French, Greek, and Italian authorities banned processing and imposed fines totaling over 30 million euros. Clearview ceased operations in the EU.
source_rule: Field of application
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 7-8

example_name: Apple App Store State Aid Investigation
example_background: Ireland's tax arrangements with Apple came under scrutiny for allegedly providing selective advantages through tax rulings in 1991 and 2007, which allowed Apple to allocate most profits to non-Irish 'head offices' with minimal substance. This resulted in an effective tax rate of 0.005% on European profits in 2014. The Commission examined whether these rulings constituted state aid incompatible with the internal market, involving detailed audits of Apple's corporate structure, IP licensing, and profit attribution methods across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: In August 2016, the Commission ordered Ireland to recover €13 billion plus interest. The General Court annulled the decision in July 2020, finding insufficient evidence of selective advantage. The Commission appealed, and the CJEU dismissed the appeal in September 2024, closing the case without recovery.
source_rule: GENERAL PROVISIONS GOVERNING THE INTERPRETATION AND APPLICATION OF THE CHARTER
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (Article 17 - property rights)
EU Charter Article 16 - freedom to conduct business

example_name: GDPR Enforcement Against British Airways
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a cyber incident where hackers accessed data of approximately 400,000 customers, including payment card details, through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack vector. The breach occurred over several weeks, with data harvested via JavaScript skimming. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated compliance with data security obligations, examining encryption practices, monitoring systems, and incident response protocols. This was one of the first major GDPR enforcement cases post-implementation.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a notice of intent for a £183 million fine in July 2020, later reduced to £20 million in October 2023 following remediation efforts and appeal considerations. British Airways enhanced its security measures, including improved vulnerability scanning and customer notification processes.
source_rule: GENERAL PROVISIONS GOVERNING THE INTERPRETATION AND APPLICATION OF THE CHARTER
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (Article 8 - protection of personal data)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Fines by Irish DPC
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission investigated Facebook's practices following the Cambridge Analytica revelations and subsequent data processing issues. Concerns included the lawfulness of behavioral advertising based on special categories of data and compliance with purpose limitation principles. Facebook processed data of hundreds of millions of EU users through its Irish subsidiary, relying on consent mechanisms that were scrutinized for validity and granularity.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the Irish DPC imposed fines totaling €390 million: €275 million for GDPR violations related to post-merger data processing (Facebook/Instagram) and €115 million earlier for consent issues. Additional EU-wide decisions under GDPR cooperation mechanisms led to further penalties. Meta appealed aspects to Irish courts.
source_rule: GENERAL PROVISIONS GOVERNING THE INTERPRETATION AND APPLICATION OF THE CHARTER

example_name: Schrems I - Safe Harbor Invalidated
example_background: Max Schrems challenged the EU-US Safe Harbor framework, arguing it failed to protect EU citizens' data from US government surveillance programs revealed by Edward Snowden. The framework allowed data transfers based on an adequacy decision from 2000. The Irish High Court referred questions to the CJEU, which assessed whether Safe Harbor ensured essential equivalence with EU fundamental rights protections, including privacy and judicial remedies.
process_and_outcome: In October 2015, the CJEU invalidated the Safe Harbor decision in Case C-362/14, prompting the development of Privacy Shield. Companies scrambled to implement alternative transfer mechanisms like model clauses.
source_rule: GENERAL PROVISIONS GOVERNING THE INTERPRETATION AND APPLICATION OF THE CHARTER

example_name: Amazon Luxembourg Tax State Aid Case
example_background: The European Commission investigated tax rulings provided by Luxembourg to Amazon from 2003-2011, which allegedly allowed profit shifting through IP-holding entities with limited substance. Amazon's European operations funneled sales revenues to these entities, resulting in low effective tax rates. The probe involved reconstructing profit allocation methods and comparing them to arm's length principles.
process_and_outcome: In October 2017, the Commission ordered recovery of €250 million. The General Court annulled the decision in May 2021, citing procedural errors. The Commission did not appeal further.
source_rule: GENERAL PROVISIONS GOVERNING THE INTERPRETATION AND APPLICATION OF THE CHARTER

example_name: TikTok Children's Data GDPR Fine
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission examined TikTok Ireland's data processing practices concerning users under 13 years old. Issues included age verification failures allowing children to create accounts without parental consent, default public settings, and processing of special category data via algorithmic profiling. The platform had over 1.4 million EU users under 13, with data used for personalized feeds.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the DPC fined TikTok €345 million for violations spanning 2020-2022. TikTok introduced enhanced age gates and parental controls in response.
source_rule: GENERAL PROVISIONS GOVERNING THE INTERPRETATION AND APPLICATION OF THE CHARTER
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (Article 8, 24 - child rights)

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition GDPR Actions
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of images from public websites without consent to build a facial recognition database sold to law enforcement and private clients. EU data protection authorities investigated indiscriminate data collection and processing for biometric data, lacking legal basis and transparency. The database included images of EU residents scraped from European sites.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs, coordinated via EDPB, banned processing and imposed bans/fines, including €20 million from France's CNIL in 2022 and ongoing proceedings in others. Clearview restricted EU access but faced enforcement challenges.
source_rule: GENERAL PROVISIONS GOVERNING THE INTERPRETATION AND APPLICATION OF THE CHARTER

example_name: Commission v. Council (C 370/12) - Turkish Death Penalty Protocol
example_background: The European Commission initiated proceedings against the Council of the European Union regarding a decision to conclude an agreement with Turkey on cooperation in criminal matters, specifically authorizing Turkey to impose the death penalty on individuals suspected of terrorism-related activities. The agreement was concluded under Article 218(5) TFEU for the conclusion of international agreements in specific areas. The Commission argued that the Council exceeded its powers by authorizing the death penalty provision, which contravened fundamental rights protections under the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights, particularly Article 2 on the right to life and Article 4 on prohibition of torture, as protected by the European Convention on Human Rights. The case centered on whether the Council's action extended EU law's application beyond the Union's competence in common foreign and security policy and altered the scope of fundamental rights obligations in international agreements.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the European Union ruled on 30 May 2013 that the Council's decision was invalid because it authorized the death penalty, which was incompatible with the Charter and ECHR standards, thereby exceeding the Union's powers as defined in the Treaties. The Council was required to annul its decision.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)
relevant_rules: Article 6 TEU (EU Charter of Fundamental Rights)

example_name: Commission v. Council (C-658/11) - Smart Sanctions Regime
example_background: The European Commission challenged a Council decision establishing restrictive measures against Syria, including asset freezes and travel bans on listed individuals. The Commission contended that the legal basis chosen by the Council improperly invoked Title V of Part Three of the TFEU (on police cooperation and judicial cooperation in criminal matters) instead of Title IV (on association agreements), thereby extending the field of application of EU law beyond the powers conferred by the Treaties for sanctions in the context of common foreign and security policy. The dispute highlighted tensions in delineating competences between internal security measures and external relations.
process_and_outcome: On 13 March 2014, the CJEU annulled the contested Council decision, ruling that it modified the powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties by using an incorrect legal basis, and the measure should have been adopted under different Treaty provisions.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Opinion 2/13 - EU Accession to ECHR
example_background: The European Commission sought the CJEU's opinion on the compatibility of the draft agreement for the EU's accession to the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) with EU primary law. The draft agreement aimed to bring the EU under the ECHR regime, subjecting it to the European Court of Human Rights. Concerns arose regarding whether this would alter the autonomy of EU law, extend Union powers beyond those in the Treaties, or modify the tasks of EU institutions, particularly in relation to the Charter of Fundamental Rights and the preliminary reference procedure under Article 267 TFEU.
process_and_outcome: In Opinion 2/13 delivered on 18 December 2014, the CJEU ruled that the draft accession agreement was incompatible with EU law, as it would disrupt the specific characteristics of EU law and the autonomy of the EU legal order, effectively establishing new powers for external judicial review over EU acts.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Commission v. Council (C-377/12) - EU-PH Customs Agreement
example_background: The Commission brought an action against the Council for using an incorrect legal basis in adopting a customs cooperation agreement between the EU and the Philippines. The Council relied solely on Article 207 TFEU (common commercial policy), while the Commission argued it should also include Article 218 TFEU provisions for development cooperation, as the agreement involved elements beyond pure trade, such as institutional frameworks and dispute settlement that extended into areas not exclusively within Union's commercial policy competence as defined in the Treaties.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU dismissed the Commission's action on 16 December 2013, upholding the Council's choice of legal basis, confirming that the agreement did not extend EU law beyond Treaty-defined powers.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Case C-431/11 UK v Council - EU Patent Court Agreement
example_background: The United Kingdom, supported by Sweden and Italy, challenged Council decisions authorizing enhanced cooperation for a unitary patent system and related provisions. The challenge focused on whether the agreement to establish a Unified Patent Court extended EU law beyond the powers in Title IV TFEU on intellectual property and created new judicial structures that modified Union tasks, potentially infringing on Member States' judicial competences and the CJEU's exclusive jurisdiction.
process_and_outcome: On 16 April 2013, the CJEU dismissed the actions, ruling that the measures respected the limits of Union powers and did not modify tasks as defined in the Treaties.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: Treaty on the Functioning of the EU (TFEU)

example_name: Opinion 1/09 - EU-CAN Patent Court Agreement
example_background: The Commission requested an opinion on the compatibility of a draft agreement creating a European and Community Patent Court with EU law. The proposed court would have jurisdiction over patent disputes, raising issues of whether it would establish new Union powers in judicial matters and extend EU law's application beyond competences in intellectual property, potentially affecting the uniform application of EU law and Charter rights.
process_and_outcome: In Opinion 1/09 of 8 March 2011, the CJEU found the agreement incompatible with EU law, as the envisaged court would deprive the CJEU of its monopoly on interpreting EU law, thereby modifying Union tasks.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Commission v. Council (C-45/07) - Passenger Name Records Agreement with Canada
example_background: The Commission contested the Council's choice of legal basis for an international agreement with Canada on the transfer of air passenger name record data for combating terrorism and serious crime. The Council used Title VI TEU (police and judicial cooperation), while the Commission advocated Title IV EC (transport and asylum). The case examined if the basis extended EU competence into third pillar matters inconsistently with Treaty divisions.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU annulled the Council's decision on 12 November 2013 (though case dated earlier), ruling the legal basis incorrect and requiring respect for Treaty competence divisions.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.

example_name: Case C-137/17 Commission v. Council - COTIF Agreements
example_background: The Commission challenged Council decisions approving amendments to the Convention concerning International Carriage by Rail (COTIF). The dispute concerned the correct legal basis, with the Commission arguing for exclusive transport competence under Article 91 TFEU, while the Council included judicial cooperation elements, potentially broadening the scope beyond defined Union powers in transport policy.
process_and_outcome: On 31 January 2020, the CJEU ruled in favor of the Commission, annulling the Council decisions for using an incorrect legal basis that did not align with Treaty competences.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.

example_name: Opinion 2/15 - EU-Morocco Fisheries Agreement
example_background: The Commission sought CJEU opinion on the compatibility of a declaration concerning EU-Morocco Liberalisation Agreement provisions on products from Western Sahara. The issue was whether extending the agreement to disputed territory altered Union powers under common commercial policy and association agreements, potentially creating new tasks in territorial disputes.
process_and_outcome: In Opinion 2/15 of 27 February 2018, the CJEU found aspects incompatible, requiring consent from Sahrawi people for application to Western Sahara, upholding strict limits on Union external competences.
source_rule: 2.   The Charter does not extend the field of application of Union law beyond the powers of the Union or establish any new power or task for the Union, or modify powers and tasks as defined in the Treaties.

example_name: Google Ireland v. CNIL (Right to be Forgotten)
example_background: In 2014, the French data protection authority CNIL ordered Google to delist links from search results worldwide for certain individuals under the right to be forgotten provision. Google implemented delisting within the European Union but maintained global availability of the links outside EU domains. CNIL imposed fines for non-compliance with the worldwide delisting requirement, arguing that the right applied universally to protect personal data. Google challenged this, asserting that EU data protection rules should respect territorial limits and not extend beyond EU jurisdiction. The case progressed through French courts and reached the Court of Justice of the EU, highlighting tensions between national implementation of EU law and the principle of subsidiarity in applying fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled in 2019 that delisting applies only within EU member states, not globally, balancing data protection with freedom of information and respecting subsidiarity. CNIL's €100,000 fine was set aside, and Google continued EU-specific delistings.
source_rule: 1.   The provisions of this Charter are addressed to the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union with due regard for the principle of subsidiarity and to the Member States only when they are implementing Union law. They shall therefore respect the rights, observe the principles and promote the application thereof in accordance with their respective powers and respecting the limits of the powers of the Union as conferred on it in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8 (Right to protection of personal data)

example_name: Commission v. Hungary (Higher Education Law)
example_background: Hungary enacted a 2017 law imposing strict registration and operational requirements on foreign universities, targeting institutions like Central European University (CEU). The European Commission argued that the law impeded academic freedom and the free movement of services within the EU, as it applied to entities operating under EU law but exceeded the limits of national powers by discriminating against non-domestic providers. The legislation required foreign universities to have a campus in their home country and sign intergovernmental agreements, affecting CEU's operations in Budapest.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2020 that Hungary failed to fulfill EU law obligations, imposing a €200,000 daily penalty until compliance. Hungary amended the law, allowing CEU to continue operations.
source_rule: 1.   The provisions of this Charter are addressed to the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union with due regard for the principle of subsidiarity and to the Member States only when they are implementing Union law. They shall therefore respect the rights, observe the principles and promote the application thereof in accordance with their respective powers and respecting the limits of the powers of the Union as conferred on it in the Treaties.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 13 (Freedom of arts and sciences)
TFEU Articles 49, 56 (Freedom of establishment and services)

example_name: Poland Judicial Reforms Infringement
example_background: Poland introduced judicial reforms between 2015-2020, including lowering the retirement age for Supreme Court judges and establishing disciplinary chambers, which the European Commission contended undermined judicial independence. These measures were implemented at the national level while Poland was applying EU law in various sectors, prompting questions about observance of EU fundamental rights principles like effective judicial protection. The reforms affected the functioning of courts handling EU law matters, leading to concerns over subsidiarity and the limits of Union powers versus member state autonomy in judicial organization.
process_and_outcome: CJEU issued interim measures in 2019 suspending the disciplinary regime; in 2021, ruled against Poland with ongoing fines exceeding €500 million for non-compliance. Poland faced multiple proceedings and began partial reversals.
source_rule: 1.   The provisions of this Charter are addressed to the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union with due regard for the principle of subsidiarity and to the Member States only when they are implementing Union law. They shall therefore respect the rights, observe the principles and promote the application thereof in accordance with their respective powers and respecting the limits of the powers of the Union as conferred on it in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47 (Right to effective remedy)

example_name: Digital Services Act Enforcement against X (Twitter)
example_background: Following the adoption of the Digital Services Act (DSA) in 2022, the European Commission opened proceedings against X in 2023 for alleged failures in content moderation, risk assessment, and transparency reporting. X, as a very large online platform, was required to comply with DSA obligations when implementing EU law on digital services. The investigation focused on whether X respected EU fundamental rights like freedom of expression while mitigating illegal content, within the limits of EU conferred powers.
process_and_outcome: Proceedings ongoing as of 2024; Commission designated X as VLOP and requested information, with potential fines up to 6% of global turnover.
source_rule: 1.   The provisions of this Charter are addressed to the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union with due regard for the principle of subsidiarity and to the Member States only when they are implementing Union law. They shall therefore respect the rights, observe the principles and promote the application thereof in accordance with their respective powers and respecting the limits of the powers of the Union as conferred on it in the Treaties.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 11 (Freedom of expression)

example_name: Achbita v. G4S (Headscarf Ban)
example_background: Samira Achbita, a Muslim receptionist at G4S in Belgium, was dismissed in 2006 for wearing an Islamic headscarf after the company introduced a neutrality policy prohibiting visible religious symbols. Achbita claimed discrimination based on religion. Belgian courts referred the case to CJEU, examining whether private companies implementing neutral dress codes when providing services under EU law respected fundamental rights to religion and equality, in line with subsidiarity principles.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2017 that the ban constituted a neutral policy not directly discriminatory, provided it applies consistently. Belgian Court of Cassation upheld dismissal.
source_rule: 1.   The provisions of this Charter are addressed to the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union with due regard for the principle of subsidiarity and to the Member States only when they are implementing Union law. They shall therefore respect the rights, observe the principles and promote the application thereof in accordance with their respective powers and respecting the limits of the powers of the Union as conferred on it in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 10, 21 (Freedom of thought, religion; Non-discrimination)

example_name: European Commission's Positive Obligation in Disability Rights (HK Danmark)
example_background: The Danish company HK Danmark dismissed an employee who requested flexible hours due to disability. The case reached CJEU, which clarified member states' obligations under EU employment equality law to ensure reasonable accommodations. It addressed how national authorities and employers must promote fundamental rights application when implementing EU directives, within subsidiarity bounds.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2013 that dismissal was discriminatory without prior accommodation assessment, reinforcing proactive duties.
source_rule: 1.   The provisions of this Charter are addressed to the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union with due regard for the principle of subsidiarity and to the Member States only when they are implementing Union law. They shall therefore respect the rights, observe the principles and promote the application thereof in accordance with their respective powers and respecting the limits of the powers of the Union as conferred on it in the Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 21, 26 (Non-discrimination, Integration of disabled persons)

example_name: ASLEF v. UK (Closed Shop Practices)
example_background: The UK government faced proceedings over legislation allowing unions like ASLEF to operate full membership requirements in certain sectors. The Commission argued it conflicted with EU freedom of association principles when states implemented Union law in labor markets.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2007 that the UK measures were compatible as they respected EU principles within subsidiarity.
source_rule: 1.   The provisions of this Charter are addressed to the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union with due regard for the principle of subsidiarity and to the Member States only when they are implementing Union law. They shall therefore respect the rights, observe the principles and promote the application thereof in accordance with their respective powers and respecting the limits of the powers of the Union as conferred on it in the Treaties.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 12 (Freedom of assembly and association)

example_name: Ardian Radu Double Jeopardy Acquittal Case
example_background: Ardian Radu, a Romanian national, was involved in a cross-border criminal matter related to the smuggling of migrants across EU borders. He was first prosecuted and acquitted by Italian authorities in 2016 for lack of evidence in connection with a specific smuggling incident. Subsequently, Romanian authorities initiated separate proceedings for the same factual events, alleging involvement in an organized migrant smuggling network. The case highlighted tensions between national judicial systems within the EU and the application of fundamental rights protections under EU law. Radu challenged the Romanian proceedings, arguing that they constituted a second prosecution for the same offense after his prior acquittal in Italy. The matter progressed through multiple judicial levels, involving detailed examination of the facts, evidence overlap, and the interpretation of ne bis in idem principles.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU ruled in 2020 that the Romanian proceedings violated EU law because they concerned the same offense for which Radu had been finally acquitted in Italy. The CJEU emphasized that ne bis in idem applies across Member States when the offenses are based on identical material facts. Romanian courts were instructed to discontinue the case, upholding the acquittal and preventing further punishment.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (ruling body)
relevant_rules: Article 50 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU

example_name: Giovanni Caserta Ne Bis in Idem VAT Fraud Case
example_background: Giovanni Caserta faced proceedings in Italy and Belgium related to a carousel fraud scheme involving VAT on mobile phones across EU Member States. Italian authorities convicted him in 2010 for participation in the fraud concerning transactions in 2005-2006. Belgian authorities later prosecuted him for the same transactions, leading to a separate conviction in 2012. Caserta argued that the Belgian trial amounted to a second punishment for the identical offense already adjudicated in Italy. The case involved extensive review of transaction records, company involvements, and the unity of the criminal conduct across borders, raising questions about the scope of ne bis in idem in transnational tax crimes.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled in 2016 that the Belgian conviction infringed Article 50 of the EU Charter, as it punished Caserta again for the same offense. The Court clarified that 'final acquittal or conviction' under EU law prevents subsequent proceedings by other Member States for the same material facts, even if national laws differed. Belgium was required to recognize the Italian judgment's finality.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Marius Daoukar Double Prosecution VAT Case
example_background: Marius Daoukar was prosecuted in Lithuania for VAT fraud related to intra-EU supplies of electronic goods in 2007. Lithuanian courts convicted him in 2011. Later, French authorities brought charges for the same supply chain transactions, resulting in a French conviction in 2014. The proceedings overlapped significantly in terms of dates, goods, and actors involved. Daoukar contested the French case, claiming it duplicated the Lithuanian punishment. The dispute necessitated analysis of whether the offenses constituted a single 'offence' under EU law despite being framed differently under national penal codes.
process_and_outcome: CJEU in 2018 found the French proceedings violated ne bis in idem, ruling that the prior Lithuanian conviction precluded further prosecution for the same facts. The decision reinforced that EU law prioritizes substantive unity over formal classifications, leading to annulment of the French conviction.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Eurojust Coordination in Garstka Case
example_background: The Garstka case involved environmental crime suspects prosecuted in Germany and Poland for illegal waste shipments across EU borders in the early 2010s. German courts acquitted the primary suspect in 2015 due to insufficient evidence linking him to the shipments. Polish authorities then initiated proceedings based on the same shipments and evidence. Eurojust intervened to coordinate and assess ne bis in idem applicability, reviewing case files, witness statements, and shipment documentation to determine if the matters were identical.
process_and_outcome: Following Eurojust recommendations and CJEU guidance, Polish authorities discontinued proceedings in 2017, respecting the German acquittal under Article 50. This prevented dual trials and ensured consistent application of EU fundamental rights.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.

example_name: Belgian-Romanian Ne Bis in Idem Environmental Case
example_background: A company director faced charges in Belgium and Romania for illegal transboundary waste movements under the Basel Convention regime within the EU. Belgian courts convicted and fined him in 2014 for specific shipments. Romanian authorities prosecuted for the same shipments in 2016, leading to a challenge based on prior conviction. The case required detailed mapping of shipment routes, quantities, and dates to establish factual identity.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU advisor opined and courts upheld ne bis in idem in 2019, barring Romanian proceedings. Belgium's conviction was recognized as final, avoiding cumulative punishments.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Spanish-Dutch Drug Trafficking Acquittal Respect
example_background: Suspects in a large-scale cocaine trafficking operation were acquitted by Spanish courts in 2012 after a trial involving intercepted shipments from South America via EU ports. Dutch authorities later sought to prosecute based on intelligence sharing for the identical consignments. The European Arrest Warrants were contested on ne bis in idem grounds, involving review of trial transcripts and evidence admissibility.
process_and_outcome: Dutch courts in 2015 dismissed charges, applying Article 50 to honor the Spanish acquittal. Eurojust facilitated information exchange confirming the proceedings' finality.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.

example_name: Zuegg Ne Bis in Idem Competition Law Case
example_background: Zuegg, an Italian fruit processing company, was fined by Italian competition authorities in 2010 for anticompetitive agreements in the fruit juice sector. The European Commission later investigated the same conduct in a broader cartel probe across Member States. Zuegg invoked ne bis in idem against the Commission's proceedings, which overlapped in time period, products, and participants.
process_and_outcome: CJEU in 2019 ruled that national authority decisions can engage ne bis in idem for EU competition fines if they are final, but allowed Commission action due to different legal bases. However, it reduced penalties to account for prior sanction, demonstrating partial adherence.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.

example_name: Pornat Ne Bis in Idem Murder Extradition Case
example_background: Romanian national Valentin Pornat was acquitted by Italian courts in 2013 of a murder committed in Italy. Upon return to Romania, authorities attempted to prosecute him under domestic law for the same killing, based on new witness testimonies. The case involved forensic evidence review and assessment of whether the Italian acquittal was 'final' under EU standards.
