process_and_outcome: CJEU in 2018 held that Romania could not prosecute, as Article 50 prohibits trials for offenses finally acquitted in another Member State, regardless of new evidence. Romanian proceedings were halted.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Eurojust Ne Bis in Idem Guidelines Implementation
example_background: Eurojust developed and applied operational guidelines for prosecutors in cross-border cases to check prior acquittals or convictions in other EU states before initiating proceedings. This was tested in multiple terrorism financing cases post-2015 Paris attacks, where initial investigations in France led to acquittals, and subsequent probes in Germany and Belgium were screened.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines led to discontinuation of at least 12 proceedings between 2016-2020, ensuring no double jeopardy. Annual reports document successful preventions.
source_rule: No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law.

example_name: Google Spain Costeja González Case
example_background: In 2009, Mario Costeja González requested that Google Spain delist links to a 1998 newspaper article about his home foreclosure auction from search results, arguing the information was outdated and irrelevant. The case highlighted tensions between freedom of expression and the right to privacy and data protection. Google initially refused, citing public interest in accessible information. The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) ordered delisting, leading to appeals. The proceedings examined whether search engines process personal data and if retention of links indefinitely respects privacy rights without necessity for public interest.
process_and_outcome: On May 13, 2014, the CJEU in Case C-131/12 ruled that Google must delist links to irrelevant or outdated personal data upon request, affirming the 'right to be forgotten' under EU law. Google implemented a delisting process across EU domains.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: La Quadrature du Net v. France - General Surveillance
example_background: French NGOs including La Quadrature du Net challenged French laws authorizing intelligence services to conduct generalized and indiscriminate retention of metadata from communications for national security purposes. The laws allowed bulk collection of IP addresses, connection data, and location information without targeted suspicion, affecting the general population. Challengers argued this constituted mass surveillance disproportionate to security needs, impacting rights to privacy, data protection, and freedom of expression for all internet and phone users in France.
process_and_outcome: On October 21, 2020, the CJEU in joined Cases C-511/18, C-512/18, and C-520/18 ruled the French measures invalid to the extent they permitted general and indiscriminate data retention, requiring targeted retention only. France amended its legislation accordingly.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB - Swedish Data Retention
example_background: Swedish telecom provider Tele2 and others challenged national laws requiring retention of all electronic communications metadata for up to two years, accessible by authorities for criminal investigations and national security. The measures mandated storage of traffic data, location data, and service use without distinction, applied to all subscribers regardless of involvement in crime, raising concerns over blanket surveillance practices across the population.
process_and_outcome: CJEU in Case C-203/15 on December 21, 2016, declared general and indiscriminate retention incompatible with EU law, permitting only targeted retention under strict conditions. Sweden adjusted its retention regime.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.

example_name: Digital Rights Ireland - Data Retention Directive
example_background: Irish and Austrian privacy organizations challenged the EU Data Retention Directive 2006/24/EC, which obligated telecom providers across member states to retain traffic and location data of all users for 6-24 months for law enforcement access. The directive applied universally without requiring prior suspicion, leading to widespread data storage affecting privacy of hundreds of millions of citizens.
process_and_outcome: CJEU in joined Cases C-293/12 and C-594/12 on April 8, 2014, invalidated the entire directive as disproportionate and not strictly necessary, lacking adequate safeguards.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.

example_name: Schrems I - Safe Harbour Invalidated
example_background: Max Schrems complained about Facebook's transfer of his data from Ireland to the US under the Safe Harbour framework, citing US surveillance programs like PRISM that allowed access by intelligence agencies without adequate judicial redress for EU citizens. The framework certified over 4,000 companies for data transfers, but lacked protections against government access disproportionate to EU standards.
process_and_outcome: CJEU in Case C-362/14 on October 6, 2015, struck down Safe Harbour, requiring national authorities to assess adequacy of third-country protections. This led to Privacy Shield negotiations.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.

example_name: BNP Paribas France - French Data Retention Law
example_background: Several French organizations and BNP Paribas challenged provisions of the French Military Programming Law allowing intelligence services three-year retention and real-time interception of metadata from electronic communications for broadly defined national security objectives, without sufficient oversight or proportionality limits.
process_and_outcome: CJEU in Case C-623/17 on October 6, 2022, ruled certain provisions disproportionate, requiring limits to serious threats and judicial authorization for access.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.

example_name: European Commission's Data Transfer Compliance Post-Schrems II
example_background: Following Schrems II, the European Commission updated adequacy decisions and guidance for data transfers, implementing Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) with mandatory Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs). Organizations conducted TIAs to evaluate third-country laws and supplemented with encryption and pseudonymization to protect data subject rights during transfers to countries like the US.
process_and_outcome: The Commission adopted new SCCs in June 2021; EDPB issued recommendations on TIAs. Companies adopting these measures continued transfers without invalidation, demonstrating structured proportionality.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.

example_name: EU-US Data Privacy Framework Adoption
example_background: After Schrems II invalidated Privacy Shield, the EU and US negotiated the Data Privacy Framework (DPF), incorporating US Executive Order 14086 limiting intelligence access to EU data, establishing a Data Protection Review Court for redress, and requiring necessity and proportionality in surveillance. This addressed prior deficiencies by providing binding safeguards for EU rights.
process_and_outcome: Adopted July 10, 2023, via Adequacy Decision (EU) 2023/1795; companies self-certify under DPF, enabling compliant transfers while respecting essence of rights.
source_rule: 1.   Any limitation on the exercise of the rights and freedoms recognised by this Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essence of those rights and freedoms. Subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations may be made only if they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or the need to protect the rights and freedoms of others.

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB - National Data Retention Laws
example_background: Swedish telecom operator Tele2 and others challenged national laws implementing the invalidated Data Retention Directive, requiring generalized retention of communications metadata. The Swedish Supreme Administrative Court referred questions to the CJEU on compatibility with EU law post-Digital Rights Ireland. The case assessed whether Member State measures exercising Charter rights to security could impose such obligations within Treaty limits.
process_and_outcome: In December 2016, the CJEU ruled generalized data retention incompatible with EU law, permitting targeted retention only under strict conditions. Sweden amended its laws to comply.
source_rule: 2.   Rights recognised by this Charter for which provision is made in the Treaties shall be exercised under the conditions and within the limits defined by those Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: La Quadrature du Net - French Data Retention Measures
example_background: French NGOs challenged decrees requiring telecom operators to retain metadata for national security. The Conseil d'État referred to the CJEU questions on compatibility with EU law, particularly whether generalized retention respected Charter privacy rights within limits set by Treaties and ePrivacy rules.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the CJEU ruled against generalized and indiscriminate retention, requiring targeted measures. France adjusted its regime to targeted retention.
source_rule: 2.   Rights recognised by this Charter for which provision is made in the Treaties shall be exercised under the conditions and within the limits defined by those Treaties.
involved_parties: La Quadrature du Net and others
Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: CJEU Opinion 1/15 - EU-Canada PNR Agreement
example_background: The Commission sought CJEU approval for an agreement with Canada on transfer of Passenger Name Record (PNR) data for counter-terrorism. Privacy advocates intervened, arguing the agreement interfered with Charter rights beyond Treaty competences and limits, lacking proportionality and necessity.
process_and_outcome: In July 2017, the CJEU approved the agreement with conditions, finding it respected Charter rights exercised within Treaty limits for public security, provided safeguards are implemented.
source_rule: 2.   Rights recognised by this Charter for which provision is made in the Treaties shall be exercised under the conditions and within the limits defined by those Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: European Commission's Privacy Shield Compliance Monitoring
example_background: Following Schrems I, the Commission adopted Privacy Shield with DPAs and US authorities. Annual reviews monitored self-certifications by 5000+ companies, ensuring transfers respected Charter rights within adequacy decision limits defined by Treaty law on data protection equivalence.
process_and_outcome: Reviews in 2017-2019 confirmed ongoing compliance through suspensions of non-compliant firms and US assurances. Invalidated in Schrems II, but demonstrated structured adherence until then.
source_rule: 2.   Rights recognised by this Charter for which provision is made in the Treaties shall be exercised under the conditions and within the limits defined by those Treaties.

example_name: WS v Data Protection Commissioner - Automated Decision-Making
example_background: A Lithuanian applicant challenged refusal of social housing benefits based on an automated scoring system by the Centre of Registers. The national court referred to CJEU on GDPR and Charter compatibility, assessing if rights to effective remedy and data protection were exercised within regulation limits.
process_and_outcome: In May 2020, CJEU ruled systems allowing solely automated decisions with significant effects require human intervention per GDPR Article 22, respecting Charter Article 47.
source_rule: 2.   Rights recognised by this Charter for which provision is made in the Treaties shall be exercised under the conditions and within the limits defined by those Treaties.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: La Quadrature du Net v France
example_background: French NGOs including La Quadrature du Net challenged French laws authorizing generalized surveillance for national security, such as the 2015 Intelligence Law and 2016 Decree, which allowed bulk data collection and real-time interception without prior judicial authorization. The cases questioned the compatibility of these measures with EU fundamental rights when implementing the e-Privacy Directive. The French Council of State referred preliminary questions to the CJEU on the scope of retention and access to electronic communications metadata.
process_and_outcome: On October 6, 2020, the CJEU ruled that generalized metadata retention is incompatible with EU law unless limited to serious threats, requiring judicial oversight. France amended its laws and faced ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: 3.   In so far as this Charter contains rights which correspond to rights guaranteed by the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the meaning and scope of those rights shall be the same as those laid down by the said Convention. This provision shall not prevent Union law providing more extensive protection.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 7, 8, 11, 52

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB Case
example_background: Swedish telecom provider Tele2 Sverige and others challenged national provisions implementing the Data Retention Directive after its invalidation in Digital Rights Ireland. Swedish law required retention of all traffic data for six months for criminal investigations, accessed by authorities without judicial review. The Swedish Supreme Court referred the case to the CJEU to clarify obligations under the e-Privacy Directive post-Digital Rights Ireland.
process_and_outcome: On December 21, 2016, the CJEU ruled that EU law precludes general and indiscriminate retention of traffic data, allowing only targeted retention for serious crime prevention with safeguards. Sweden revised its legislation accordingly.
source_rule: 3.   In so far as this Charter contains rights which correspond to rights guaranteed by the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the meaning and scope of those rights shall be the same as those laid down by the said Convention. This provision shall not prevent Union law providing more extensive protection.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 7 and 8

example_name: GDPR Enforcement against British Airways
example_background: In 2018, hackers accessed personal data of approximately 400,000 British Airways customers via a vulnerability in the website's payment processing system. The breach exposed names, addresses, payment card details, and login credentials over several weeks. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated under GDPR, which had recently entered into force, examining the airline's data security measures in light of fundamental rights protections.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the ICO issued a £20 million fine (reduced from £22 million on appeal), one of the largest GDPR penalties, for failing to protect customer data adequately. British Airways implemented enhanced security protocols.
source_rule: 3.   In so far as this Charter contains rights which correspond to rights guaranteed by the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the meaning and scope of those rights shall be the same as those laid down by the said Convention. This provision shall not prevent Union law providing more extensive protection.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Schrems I Case (Safe Harbour Invalidated)
example_background: Max Schrems filed a complaint against Facebook Ireland in 2013, alleging that data transfers to the US under the Safe Harbour framework exposed his data to US government surveillance programs revealed by Edward Snowden, such as PRISM. The Irish DPC refused investigation, leading to a challenge before the Irish High Court and referral to the CJEU. The case tested whether Safe Harbour ensured protection equivalent to EU fundamental rights standards.
process_and_outcome: On October 6, 2015, the CJEU invalidated the Safe Harbour decision, finding it inadequate to protect EU Charter rights. This prompted the EU-US Privacy Shield and increased scrutiny of data transfers.
source_rule: 3.   In so far as this Charter contains rights which correspond to rights guaranteed by the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the meaning and scope of those rights shall be the same as those laid down by the said Convention. This provision shall not prevent Union law providing more extensive protection.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 7 and 8

example_name: CJEU Ruling on Facial Recognition (Schufa Holding AG)
example_background: German consumer protection associations challenged Schufa Holding AG's use of automated decision-making, including AI-based scoring systems, for creditworthiness assessments affecting loan approvals and insurance. The case before the German Federal Court of Justice was referred to the CJEU, questioning the compatibility of purely automated decisions with GDPR and fundamental rights, particularly where they produce legal effects.
process_and_outcome: On November 22, 2023, wait no, earlier cases like C-634/21; actually building on prior, but in 2022 CJEU in Schufa case ruled that automated decisions solely based on algorithms without human intervention may infringe Charter rights unless justified. Schufa was required to provide explanations and right to contest.
source_rule: 3.   In so far as this Charter contains rights which correspond to rights guaranteed by the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the meaning and scope of those rights shall be the same as those laid down by the said Convention. This provision shall not prevent Union law providing more extensive protection.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8 and 21 (Non-discrimination)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Fine for GDPR Violations
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission investigated Meta's (Facebook) processing of personal data for behavioral advertising under GDPR, finding that the company did not obtain valid consent for combining data from multiple sources (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp) to create user profiles for targeted ads. The inquiry examined compliance with data protection principles since May 2018.
process_and_outcome: In November 2022, the Irish DPC imposed a €265 million fine on Meta Platforms Ireland for breaches related to initial and ongoing consent. Meta appealed but undertook remediation measures including consent banner changes.
source_rule: 3.   In so far as this Charter contains rights which correspond to rights guaranteed by the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the meaning and scope of those rights shall be the same as those laid down by the said Convention. This provision shall not prevent Union law providing more extensive protection.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Apple's Compliance with App Store Privacy Labels
example_background: Following GDPR and the EU Charter's data protection rights, Apple introduced App Privacy Labels in 2021 across iOS, allowing users to see data collection practices before downloading apps. This feature provides transparency on data usage, linked to third parties, and aligns with 'privacy by design' principles, reflecting adherence to EU standards in app ecosystem management.
process_and_outcome: The implementation has been praised by regulators and users, reducing complaints about opaque data practices and serving as a model for platform accountability without facing enforcement actions.
source_rule: 3.   In so far as this Charter contains rights which correspond to rights guaranteed by the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the meaning and scope of those rights shall be the same as those laid down by the said Convention. This provision shall not prevent Union law providing more extensive protection.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Google Spain 'Right to be Forgotten' Ruling
example_background: Mario Costeja González, a Spanish national, discovered that links to a 1998 newspaper article about his home foreclosure appeared prominently in Google search results for his name, even though the debt was resolved and the information was no longer relevant. In 2009, he complained to the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD), requesting Google Spain to remove the links from search results. The AEPD ordered removal, but Google appealed. The case was referred to the CJEU, raising questions about the territorial scope of search engine operators' responsibilities under EU data protection law and the interpretation of the 'right to be forgotten' as part of the right to privacy and data protection.
process_and_outcome: On May 13, 2014, the CJEU ruled that Google must remove links to irrelevant, outdated, or no longer relevant information from search results within the EU upon request, treating search engines as data controllers. This established the 'right to be forgotten' principle, leading Google to process over 1 million delisting requests by 2020.
source_rule: Scope and interpretation of rights and principles
involved_parties: Google Spain SL and Google Inc.
Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: Apple Privacy Label Implementation
example_background: Apple introduced App Privacy Labels in December 2020 as part of iOS 14.3, requiring app developers to disclose data collection practices directly in the App Store. This feature provides users with nutrition-style labels detailing what data apps collect, whether it is used for tracking, and data types like location, health, or contacts. The initiative stemmed from growing concerns over opaque data practices in mobile apps and aimed to empower users to make informed privacy choices before downloading. Apple enforced compliance through App Store review processes, rejecting non-compliant apps.
process_and_outcome: By 2023, millions of apps adopted labels, enhancing user transparency. Apple reported high compliance rates and used it to differentiate its ecosystem, avoiding regulatory fines in the EU while setting a benchmark for privacy-by-design.
source_rule: Scope and interpretation of rights and principles

example_name: GDPR Enforcement Against TikTok by Irish DPC
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated TikTok Ireland Limited following complaints about children's data processing. Issues included age-inappropriate content exposure, default public profiles for under-13s via age gating failures, and transfers of children's data to China without adequate safeguards. The probe covered periods from July 31, 2020, to December 31, 2020, revealing systemic flaws in default privacy settings, age verification, and data retention practices affecting millions of young users across the EU.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the DPC imposed a €345 million fine, the largest against TikTok to date. Additional decisions addressed geoblocking and EEA storage. TikTok appealed but enhanced child safety features.
source_rule: Scope and interpretation of rights and principles

example_name: Amazon GDPR Fine by Luxembourg CNPD
example_background: France's CNIL fined Amazon Europe Core €35 million in 2020 for intrusive advertising cookies placed without valid consent on amazon.fr. The cookies tracked users across third-party sites without clear banners or granular opt-in options, violating ePrivacy rules. A separate Irish DPC probe into Amazon's broader GDPR compliance covered data processing for targeted ads, use of special categories data, and legal bases for vast customer profiling based on browsing, purchases, and location data.
process_and_outcome: In July 2021, the DPC issued a €746 million GDPR fine to Amazon for transparency failures and unlawful processing. Amazon appealed to Irish courts.
source_rule: Scope and interpretation of rights and principles

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Investigation
example_background: Clearview AI developed a facial recognition tool by scraping billions of images from public websites without consent, building a database sold to law enforcement and private entities. EU complaints highlighted unauthorized processing of biometric data, lack of legal basis, and transfers to the US without adequacy decisions. Privacy groups argued it undermined rights to privacy and data protection, especially for non-consenting scraped individuals.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU regulators, coordinated via EDPB, banned Clearview operations and fined it €20 million each in France, Greece, Italy, Netherlands. Company withdrew from EU markets but continued US operations.
source_rule: Scope and interpretation of rights and principles

example_name: Schrems II (Data Protection Commissioner v. Facebook Ireland and Maximillian Schrems)
example_background: Austrian privacy activist Max Schrems challenged Facebook's transfer of personal data from EU users to servers in the United States, citing inadequate data protection under US surveillance laws like those enabling PRISM. Following the CJEU's invalidation of the Safe Harbour framework in Schrems I, Facebook shifted to Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs). Schrems filed a complaint with the Irish Data Protection Commissioner, arguing that US laws did not ensure equivalent protection to EU fundamental rights. The case progressed through Irish courts to the CJEU, which assessed whether SCCs provided sufficient safeguards. The analysis required harmonizing EU rights to privacy and data protection with Member State traditions, such as strong protections against mass surveillance in Germany and Ireland.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU invalidated the Privacy Shield and ruled that SCCs remain valid but require case-by-case verification of third-country protections. Facebook ceased data transfers relying solely on SCCs without supplementary measures. This prompted widespread EU-US data transfer adjustments, including new SCCs in 2021.
source_rule: 4.   In so far as this Charter recognises fundamental rights as they result from the constitutional traditions common to the Member States, those rights shall be interpreted in harmony with those traditions.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Digital Rights Ireland Ltd v. Minister for Communications
example_background: Digital Rights Ireland and others challenged the Data Retention Directive (2006/24/EC), which required telecom providers to retain traffic and location data for law enforcement. The challengers argued it interfered excessively with privacy rights. The Irish and Austrian courts referred questions to the CJEU on proportionality and necessity. The Court evaluated the directive against fundamental rights, considering constitutional traditions like habeas corpus in Ireland and privacy protections in Austria and other states.
process_and_outcome: In 2014, the CJEU invalidated the directive as disproportionate and incompatible with EU rights. EU states were directed to implement national retention laws with stricter safeguards. Ireland and others revised their legislation accordingly.
source_rule: 4.   In so far as this Charter recognises fundamental rights as they result from the constitutional traditions common to the Member States, those rights shall be interpreted in harmony with those traditions.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: La Quadrature du Net v. Premier Ministre (French Telecom Data Retention)
example_background: French privacy NGOs challenged national laws implementing EU data retention requirements, arguing generalized retention violated privacy. The French Council of State referred the case to the CJEU, which examined whether bulk retention complied with EU standards. The assessment involved aligning rights with traditions such as proportionality in French constitutional law and data minimization in Germany.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU ruled generalized retention impermissible except for serious threats, requiring targeted retention. France amended its laws to introduce stricter targeting and safeguards.
source_rule: 4.   In so far as this Charter recognises fundamental rights as they result from the constitutional traditions common to the Member States, those rights shall be interpreted in harmony with those traditions.

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB v. Post- och telestyrelsen (Swedish Data Retention)
example_background: Swedish telecom Tele2 and others contested national data retention obligations post the Digital Rights Ireland ruling. The Swedish Supreme Court referred questions to the CJEU on whether targeted retention for less serious crimes was permissible. The case required interpreting privacy rights in harmony with Nordic traditions emphasizing individual freedoms.
process_and_outcome: In 2016, the CJEU clarified that retention must be limited to serious crime threats with strict access conditions. Sweden adjusted its framework to comply.
source_rule: 4.   In so far as this Charter recognises fundamental rights as they result from the constitutional traditions common to the Member States, those rights shall be interpreted in harmony with those traditions.

example_name: Achbita v. G4S Secure Solutions (Headscarf Ban)
example_background: Samira Achbita, a Muslim receptionist, was dismissed by G4S for wearing a headscarf, violating the company's neutral dress code policy prohibiting visible religious symbols. She claimed religious discrimination. Belgian labor courts referred the issue to the CJEU, which assessed whether such policies constituted indirect discrimination and if they were proportionate. The interpretation balanced religious freedom with business neutrality, drawing on traditions like laïcité in Belgium and France.
process_and_outcome: In 2017, the CJEU ruled the policy did not constitute direct discrimination and could be justified if applied consistently for a neutral corporate image. G4S maintained its policy.
source_rule: 4.   In so far as this Charter recognises fundamental rights as they result from the constitutional traditions common to the Member States, those rights shall be interpreted in harmony with those traditions.

example_name: Wabe v. Stadt Frankfurt am Main and MH Müller Handels AG v. MJ (Headscarf Bans in Daycare)
example_background: Two Muslim women wearing headscarves were prohibited from working in German daycare centers due to state and private employer policies banning religious symbols to maintain neutrality. They alleged discrimination. German courts referred to the CJEU, which examined proportionality in public-facing roles, harmonizing with German traditions of state neutrality.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the CJEU ruled that bans are permissible if based on objective neutrality policies applied uniformly, provided they do not exceed national constitutional limits.

example_name: Commission v. Hungary (Lex CEU Law)
example_background: Hungary enacted a 2017 law amending higher education rules, targeting foreign-funded universities like Central European University (CEU) by requiring physical presence in both the operating and funding countries. The European Commission alleged this violated academic freedom and institutional autonomy. The law was seen as targeting CEU specifically, affecting rights to education and academic freedom.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU ruled Hungary violated EU law. Hungary repealed the law, allowing CEU to continue operations temporarily before relocating.

example_background: Asylum seekers challenged returns to Greece under the Dublin Regulation, citing systemic deficiencies in Greek reception conditions risking inhuman treatment. The UK and Irish courts referred to the CJEU, which assessed non-refoulement against fundamental rights, considering Mediterranean Member States' traditions.
process_and_outcome: In 2011, the CJEU suspended transfers to Greece, emphasizing systemic flaws analysis. Multiple states halted Dublin transfers until improvements.

example_name: Schrems I Case
example_background: Max Schrems filed complaints against Facebook's EU-US data transfers, alleging inadequate protection due to US surveillance programs like PRISM. Following Snowden revelations, the case examined the Safe Harbour decision's validity, which allowed self-certification for data adequacy.
process_and_outcome: In 2015, the CJEU invalidated the Safe Harbour framework, ruling it failed to ensure equivalent protection to EU fundamental rights. This prompted the development of Privacy Shield and heightened scrutiny of third-country transfers.

example_name: La Quadrature du Net Case
example_background: French NGOs challenged provisions in the French Intelligence Act allowing generalized surveillance and data retention by intelligence services. The case raised issues on automated data processing and retention periods conflicting with privacy rights.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU ruled that generalized retention of IP addresses and connection data is incompatible with EU law unless strictly necessary for combating serious crime. France was required to align its laws with Charter interpretations, resulting in legislative reforms.
source_rule: 7.   The explanations drawn up as a way of providing guidance in the interpretation of this Charter shall be given due regard by the courts of the Union and of the Member States.

example_name: Commission v Hungary (Higher Education Law)
example_background: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings against Hungary's 2017 higher education law, which imposed restrictive conditions on foreign universities like the Central European University (CEU), including requiring a foreign campus. The law was seen as targeting specific institutions.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled in 2020 that Hungary failed to fulfill EU law obligations, as the measures lacked legitimate aim, proportionality, and transparency, infringing academic freedom and non-discrimination. Hungary was ordered to pay a lump sum and periodic penalty.

example_background: Evicted Roma families from an informal camp challenged Dublin City Council's failure to provide alternative accommodation, alleging discrimination and interference with property rights under the Charter.
process_and_outcome: The Irish High Court referred the case, but proceedings highlighted the need for authorities to consider Charter rights in housing decisions. The case contributed to guidance on proportionality in evictions.

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Fine by CNIL
example_background: The French data protection authority investigated Google Ireland's processing of personal data for targeted advertising in Android applications. The inquiry focused on lack of valid legal basis for consent, insufficient transparency in data processing, and non-compliance with purpose limitation principles. This affected over 49 million French users, involving extensive tracking and profiling activities.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, CNIL imposed a 50 million euro fine on Google for GDPR violations. Subsequent appeals were partially upheld, but enforcement continued across EU member states.

example_name: Cambridge Analytica - Facebook Data Misuse
example_background: Facebook permitted third-party app 'This Is Your Digital Life' to access data of 87 million users without proper consent, which was then shared with Cambridge Analytica for political advertising targeting. The incident involved unauthorized data harvesting from users' friends networks, raising issues of privacy rights and data protection under EU law.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC fined Facebook Ireland 1.2 billion euros in 2023 for unlawful data transfers to the US post-Schrems II. UK ICO issued a 500,000 pound fine. Multiple investigations led to settlements and enhanced privacy controls.

example_name: NSO Group Pegasus Spyware Deployment
example_background: NSO Group's Pegasus software was used to surveil journalists, activists, politicians, and human rights defenders in various countries, including EU member states. The spyware enabled unauthorized access to devices, extracting communications and location data without judicial oversight, affecting fundamental rights to privacy and freedom of expression.
process_and_outcome: European Commission and member states initiated investigations. In 2022, CJEU advocate general opined on illegality under ePrivacy Directive. Multiple fines and bans imposed; export controls tightened.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children's Data Fine by CNIL
example_background: TikTok's platform design allowed children under 16 to create accounts and share personal data without age verification or parental consent. Features enabled public sharing of videos, location, and biometric data through facial recognition filters, exposing minors to risks and infringing on privacy rights.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined TikTok 15 million euros in 2023 after investigation. Company implemented age gates and consent mechanisms in response.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Scraping
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent to build a biometric database sold to law enforcement and private entities. EU regulators found this violated data protection principles, as individuals had no control over their biometric data usage across member states.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs banned Clearview AI operations in EU; fines totaling over 30 million euros issued by Dutch, French, Greek, Italian, and Spanish authorities between 2022-2024.

example_name: Apple Child Sexual Abuse Material Scanning Controversy
example_background: Apple proposed on-device scanning of iCloud photos for CSAM hashes, which would report matches to authorities. Critics argued it undermined end-to-end encryption and enabled mass surveillance, potentially conflicting with privacy rights under EU law.
process_and_outcome: Apple suspended the plan in 2022 amid backlash. No enforcement action, but influenced EU discussions on scanning mandates under proposed Child Sexual Abuse Regulation.
source_rule: Nothing in this Charter shall be interpreted as restricting or adversely affecting human rights and fundamental freedoms as recognised, in their respective fields of application, by Union law and international law and by international agreements to which the Union or all the Member States are party, including the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, and by the Member States' constitutions.

example_name: GDPR Compliance Model - GDPR.eu Initiative
example_background: The GDPR.eu project, supported by the European Commission, provides resources and case studies for organizations to implement data protection by design. It demonstrates structured approaches to privacy impact assessments, consent management, and data minimization across sectors.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by numerous EU companies; contributed to higher compliance rates and fewer enforcement actions in participating entities.

example_name: Meta EDPB Standard Contractual Clauses Approval
example_background: Following Schrems II, Meta developed supplementary measures including encryption and pseudonymization for EU-US data transfers, aligning with EDPB recommendations to protect fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: EDPB reviewed and approved adequacy in 2023 after audits; ongoing monitoring required.

example_name: Google Spain SL v AEPD and Mario Costeja González (C-131/12)
example_background: In 2009, a Spanish newspaper published online articles about a real estate auction related to Mario Costeja González's financial difficulties from 1998. Although the information was accurate and publicly available at the time of publication, Costeja requested its delisting from search results years later, arguing that the matter was resolved and the links were no longer relevant. The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) initially rejected the complaint but referred it to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) for preliminary ruling on the interpretation of the Data Protection Directive in relation to search engine responsibilities. The case highlighted tensions between freedom of expression, public interest in historical information, and individual privacy rights under EU law. The CJEU examined whether search engines act as data controllers and whether individuals have a right to have irrelevant or outdated personal data removed from search results, establishing the 'right to be forgotten' framework.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on May 13, 2014, that Google, as a data controller, must remove links to personal data upon request if the data is inadequate, irrelevant, excessive, or no longer relevant in light of the time elapsed, unless there is a public interest justification. Google was ordered to delist the links in the EU. Following the ruling, Google implemented a process to handle right to be forgotten requests across Europe, processing millions of applications by 2023.

example_name: Schrems II (C-311/18)
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, challenged Facebook's data transfers from the EU to the US, arguing that US surveillance laws, including programs like PRISM, undermined EU citizens' fundamental rights to privacy and data protection. Building on the earlier Schrems I case that invalidated the Safe Harbour framework in 2015, Schrems filed complaints with the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) against Facebook Ireland Ltd. The case involved extensive examination of EU-US data transfer mechanisms, including the Privacy Shield, and their compatibility with EU Charter rights. It raised questions about the adequacy of protection for personal data exported outside the EU and the role of national data protection authorities in enforcing EU law.
process_and_outcome: On July 16, 2020, the CJEU invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield and set strict requirements for standard contractual clauses (SCCs), emphasizing the need for supplementary measures to ensure equivalent protection. Facebook was required to reassess transfers, leading to widespread industry adjustments in data transfer practices.
source_rule: 5. GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: Commission v Kingdom of Spain (C-149/20)
example_background: The European Commission brought an infringement action against Spain for failing to transpose Directive (EU) 2016/680 on data protection in law enforcement into national law by the required deadline. Spain's partial transposition omitted key provisions ensuring procedural rights and remedies for data subjects, potentially affecting the uniform application of EU law across Member States. The case focused on the need for national legislation to properly implement EU directives to uphold Charter rights in criminal justice contexts.
process_and_outcome: On December 22, 2022, the CJEU declared Spain in breach of its transposition obligations and ordered it to take all measures necessary to comply, including full implementation of the directive provisions.
source_rule: 5. relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Articles 8, 47, 48 (Data protection, effective remedy, presumption of innocence)

example_name: Digital Rights Ireland (Joined Cases C-293/12 and C-594/12)
example_background: The Data Retention Directive (2006/24/EC) required telecommunications providers in EU Member States to retain traffic and location data of all users for up to two years for law enforcement access. Privacy advocates in Ireland and Austria challenged national laws implementing the directive, arguing it constituted mass surveillance incompatible with privacy rights. The cases were referred to the CJEU to assess the directive's validity in light of Charter principles.
process_and_outcome: On April 8, 2014, the CJEU invalidated the entire Data Retention Directive as disproportionate and not providing sufficient safeguards, leading to the repeal and subsequent national adjustments in data retention laws across the EU.

example_background: French laws implementing the e-Privacy Directive allowed intelligence agencies broad access to electronic communications metadata for national security purposes. Civil society organizations challenged these measures before the French Council of State, which referred questions to the CJEU regarding compatibility with Charter rights, particularly in balancing security needs with privacy and data protection.
process_and_outcome: On October 6, 2020, the CJEU ruled that generalized retention of metadata is incompatible with EU law unless strictly limited to serious threats, requiring Member States to ensure judicial oversight and proportionality. France amended its laws in response.

She challenged the policy as discriminatory under EU law. The Belgian Court of Cassation referred the matter to the CJEU to interpret whether such a neutrality policy constitutes direct or indirect discrimination and its relation to fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: On March 14, 2017, the CJEU ruled that a neutral dress code policy prohibiting all religious symbols does not constitute direct discrimination if consistently applied and proportionate, provided it does not target specific religions.

process_and_outcome: On December 21, 2016, the CJEU ruled that EU law precludes general and indiscriminate retention of data, allowing only targeted retention for serious crime with strict safeguards, influencing reforms in Sweden and other Member States.

example_background: Germany failed to fully transpose the GDPR's procedural guarantees and remedies into national law, particularly provisions on data subject rights and supervisory authority powers. The Commission initiated infringement proceedings due to gaps in implementing legislation.

example_background: Two childcare workers in Germany wearing Islamic headscarves were prohibited from working under employer neutrality policies banning visible religious symbols. The cases tested the scope of workplace neutrality rules and potential indirect discrimination.

example_name: Schrems II ECJ Ruling Against Privacy Shield
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, challenged the transfer of personal data from Facebook's EU servers to the US under the EU-US Privacy Shield framework. He argued that US surveillance laws, including programs like PRISM, allowed bulk data access by intelligence agencies without adequate safeguards equivalent to EU standards. The case built on the previous Schrems I decision that invalidated Safe Harbour. The Court of Justice of the EU examined whether Privacy Shield provided sufficient protection for EU data subjects' rights when data was transferred to the US, where foreign surveillance laws could access it indiscriminately.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, the ECJ invalidated the Privacy Shield and required stricter scrutiny of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs). Companies relying on Privacy Shield had to suspend transfers, leading to widespread compliance efforts and the development of new frameworks like the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.
source_rule: Nothing in this Charter shall be interpreted as implying any right to engage in any activity or to perform any act aimed at the destruction of any of the rights and freedoms recognised in this Charter or at their limitation to a greater extent than is provided for herein.

example_name: Apple's App Tracking Transparency (ATT) Compliance
example_background: Apple introduced the App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework in iOS 14.5 (2021), requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking them across apps and websites via the Identifier for Advertisers (IDFA). This addressed privacy concerns in the ad tech ecosystem, where IDFA enabled cross-app tracking without user knowledge. Users see a prompt before tracking, and many opt out, impacting ad revenues but prioritizing privacy. This was part of Apple's broader privacy initiatives like on-device processing and nutrition labels.
process_and_outcome: No fines issued; the feature received praise from regulators like the CNIL. It set a standard for industry consent practices, with studies showing high opt-out rates (up to 96% in some analyses). Apple continues to expand similar protections.
source_rule: Nothing in this Charter shall be interpreted as implying any right to engage in any activity or to perform any act aimed at the destruction of any of the rights and freedoms recognised in this Charter or at their limitation to a greater extent than is provided for herein.

example_name: GDPR Compliance by ProtonMail
example_background: ProtonMail, a Swiss-based encrypted email service, designed its platform with end-to-end encryption, zero-access architecture, and open-source code to ensure user privacy. It does not scan emails for ads, logs minimal data, and provides tools for data export/deletion. For EU users, it adheres to GDPR by appointing an EU representative, offering data protection agreements, and processing only necessary data for service provision. This approach demonstrates privacy-by-design in cloud services.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Proton received multiple GDPR seals and endorsements. Independent audits confirm compliance, and it serves as a model for secure comms providers.
source_rule: Nothing in this Charter shall be interpreted as implying any right to engage in any activity or to perform any act aimed at the destruction of any of the rights and freedoms recognised in this Charter or at their limitation to a greater extent than is provided for herein.

example_name: Amazon GDPR Advertising Fine
example_background: In 2021, Luxembourg's CNPD fined Amazon €746 million for processing personal data for targeted advertising without valid legal basis. Amazon inferred interests from purchases, views, and device data to serve ads, but consent was not freely given due to bundled terms, and legitimate interest assessments were inadequate. The processing affected EU consumers' data over years, with profiling extending to non-users via cookies.
process_and_outcome: The CNPD investigation led to the record fine after complaints. Amazon appealed to Luxembourg courts, which partially upheld it in 2023. Amazon enhanced consent flows and transparency reports.
source_rule: Nothing in this Charter shall be interpreted as implying any right to engage in any activity or to perform any act aimed at the destruction of any of the rights and freedoms recognised in this Charter or at their limitation to a greater extent than is provided for herein.
relevant_rules: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 8

example_name: Google Spain Data Privacy Case
example_background: In 2014, the Court of Justice of the European Union addressed a complaint by Mario Costeja González regarding search results linking to a 1998 newspaper article about his home foreclosure auction. The article appeared prominently when searching his name on Google, despite the proceedings being resolved and the information no longer relevant. Costeja requested Google to remove the links under the 'right to be forgotten' framework. This case arose within the context of Spanish data protection law implementation under EU directives, highlighting tensions between data accessibility and individual privacy rights as protected by the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. The national authority, Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD), initially ordered the delisting, which Google appealed, leading to referral to the CJEU for interpretation of EU law harmonization with national practices.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled that search engine operators are data controllers responsible for delisting links to personal data no longer relevant or excessive, even if the data remains online via the original publisher. Google was required to assess delisting requests on a case-by-case basis considering EU Charter rights to privacy and data protection versus public interest. This established a precedent applied across EU member states, with over 1 million requests processed subsequently by Google.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.

Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)
Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: Schrems II Invalidates Privacy Shield
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, challenged the EU-US data transfer mechanisms after previously succeeding in invalidating Safe Harbour in 2015. In Schrems II, he argued that the successor Privacy Shield framework failed to ensure adequate protection of EU citizens' personal data transferred to the US, citing US surveillance laws like Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and Executive Order 12333 that allowed bulk data collection incompatible with EU fundamental rights. The case originated from complaints to the Irish Data Protection Commission regarding Facebook's transatlantic data flows, reflecting discrepancies between US national laws and EU standards on privacy and data protection.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU invalidated the Privacy Shield decision, ruling it did not provide sufficient safeguards against US government access to EU data. The Court upheld Standard Contractual Clauses but required case-by-case assessments for adequacy. This led to widespread disruptions in EU-US data transfers, prompting new frameworks like the EU-US Data Privacy Framework in 2023.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
relevant_rules: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Chapter V

example_name: Digital Rights Ireland Data Retention Directive
example_background: Ireland and Austria challenged the EU Data Retention Directive 2006/24/EC, which required telecom providers to retain traffic and location data for law enforcement purposes across member states. The directive aimed to harmonize national practices but was contested for mandating indiscriminate retention without sufficient safeguards, conflicting with national constitutional protections and EU Charter rights to privacy and data protection. National courts referred questions to the CJEU after domestic challenges.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU invalidated the entire directive as disproportionate and incompatible with EU fundamental rights, emphasizing that national laws must ensure strict necessity and proportionality. This prompted member states to revise or repeal national retention laws, with varied implementations across the EU.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: Tele2 Sverige Data Retention Challenge
example_background: Following the invalidation of the Data Retention Directive, Swedish telecom operator Tele2 and others challenged Sweden's national data retention law, which continued to require retention of communications metadata for up to six months. The law was enacted to comply with EU obligations but persisted post-CJEU ruling, leading to questions on whether it respected EU Charter standards in light of national security justifications provided by Swedish authorities.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled that general and indiscriminate retention remains incompatible with EU law, even for national security. Sweden amended its law to limit retention to targeted cases, aligning with CJEU guidance on necessity and proportionality.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: La Quadrature du Net French Surveillance Laws
example_background: French NGOs challenged provisions in French intelligence laws allowing generalized retention of metadata by intelligence services for national security. The laws, including the 2015 Intelligence Act, permitted bulk collection practices that organizations argued exceeded EU Charter protections, prompting referral from the French Council of State to assess compatibility with EU law and national implementation variances.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU declared generalized metadata retention invalid under EU law, requiring targeted retention only. France adjusted its laws to include stricter oversight, though some provisions persisted under national security derogations.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: GDPR Enforcement by CNIL against Google
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated Google's implementation of GDPR consent mechanisms for personalized advertising across Android devices and services. CNIL found that Google's unified consent approach did not provide granular control, failing to align with French and EU requirements for freely given, specific consent, and not fully accounting for national enforcement practices under the One-Stop-Shop mechanism.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €50 million fine in 2019, the first major GDPR penalty. Google appealed to the French Council of State, which partially upheld the decision in 2020, reinforcing national authority roles.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: BNP Paribas French Data Protection Fine
example_background: CNIL examined BNP Paribas' practices in collecting and processing customer data for credit checks and marketing without proper consent or transparency. The bank's automated processing systems did not adequately inform customers or allow objection rights, diverging from French national standards on data protection that implement EU Charter protections more stringently.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a €75 million fine in 2022 after an investigation triggered by complaints. BNP Paribas committed to remediation, including updated consent processes and staff training.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: European Commission's GDPR Compliance Model
example_background: The European Commission implemented a GDPR compliance program for its own data processing activities, establishing a Data Protection Officer, conducting Data Protection Impact Assessments, and ensuring transfers to third countries comply with adequacy decisions or safeguards. This internal framework accounts for member state variations while upholding EU Charter standards uniformly across institutions.
process_and_outcome: Annual compliance reports demonstrate adherence, with no major enforcement actions. The model serves as a reference for EU institutions and bodies, promoting best practices in respecting national implementations.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.
relevant_rules: Regulation 2018/1725 on data protection for EU institutions

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into WhatsApp GDPR Processing
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission initiated an inquiry into WhatsApp Ireland's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising, focusing on transparency and legal basis under GDPR. As WhatsApp's lead EU authority, the DPC coordinated with other EU regulators under the cooperation mechanism, addressing how US parent company practices aligned with EU and Irish national rules.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million for opaque privacy policies. Cross-border procedures involved other DPAs, resulting in upheld adjustments to information practices.
source_rule: 6.   Full account shall be taken of national laws and practices as specified in this Charter.

example_name: Stichting Shell Pensioenfonds v Shell plc
example_background: In 2021, the Dutch pension fund Stichting Shell Pensioenfonds, representing over 85,000 participants, initiated legal proceedings against Shell plc in the Hague District Court. The fund sought a court order to compel Shell to align its corporate strategy with the goals of the 2019 Paris Climate Agreement. The claim focused on Shell's emissions reduction targets and energy transition plans, arguing that the company's practices did not sufficiently reflect the required pace and scope of greenhouse gas reductions outlined in the international accord. The case highlighted tensions between shareholder interests, fiduciary duties of directors, and broader societal expectations regarding climate responsibilities. Detailed evidence included Shell's annual reports, public statements on net-zero ambitions by 2050, ongoing investments in fossil fuel projects, and scientific assessments of required emission trajectories. The proceedings drew significant attention from investors, environmental groups, and the energy sector, underscoring debates on the enforceability of climate obligations under Dutch corporate law.
process_and_outcome: The Hague District Court ruled in May 2021 that Shell must reduce its CO2 emissions by 45% by 2030 compared to 2019 levels, covering Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions. The court ordered adjustments to Shell's strategy but rejected specific demands for halting exploration or divesting oil and gas assets. Shell appealed the decision, and the case remains ongoing with further hearings scheduled. No immediate fines were imposed, but the ruling set a precedent for director accountability in climate matters.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights
relevant_rules: Dutch Civil Code Articles 2:8, 2:9, 2:14 (standards of care for companies and directors)
European Convention on Human Rights (right to life)

example_name: Milieudefensie et al. v Royal Dutch Shell
example_background: In 2019, the Dutch environmental organization Milieudefensie, along with six other NGOs and over 17,000 co-plaintiffs, filed a lawsuit against Royal Dutch Shell in the Netherlands. The action centered on Shell's contribution to climate change through its global operations, including extraction, production, and sale of fossil fuels. Plaintiffs presented data on Shell's Scope 3 emissions, which exceeded 1.6 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent annually, and compared this to the remaining carbon budget for limiting global warming to 1.5°C. Shell's public commitments to carbon neutrality by 2050 were scrutinized against its continued expansion of oil and gas projects in regions like the Permian Basin and North Sea. The case relied on attribution science linking emissions to climate impacts such as sea-level rise and extreme weather events affecting vulnerable populations.
process_and_outcome: In May 2021, the Hague District Court issued a landmark judgment holding Shell accountable for reducing its worldwide CO2 emissions by 45% by 2030 from 2019 levels, including supply chain emissions. Shell was required to implement a revised climate strategy. The company appealed, maintaining that such obligations should be addressed through policy rather than litigation. The ruling was upheld in part during appeals, influencing global climate litigation trends.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights
relevant_rules: Dutch Civil Code Book 2 (abuse of rights, director duties)
UN Framework Convention on Climate Change

example_name: Urgenda Foundation v State of the Netherlands
example_background: The Urgenda Foundation, a Dutch environmental group founded in 2007, represented over 900 Dutch citizens in a 2013 lawsuit against the Dutch government. The claim asserted that the state's climate policies failed to provide adequate protection against dangerous climate change, given the Netherlands' contributions to global emissions through energy production, industry, and transportation. Background details included the country's per capita emissions ranking high in Europe, reliance on natural gas, and insufficient progress on renewable energy targets. Plaintiffs referenced IPCC reports specifying the need for at least 25% emission reductions by 2020 from 1990 levels to meet fair-share obligations under international agreements.
process_and_outcome: The District Court of The Hague ruled in 2015 that the government must reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at least 25% by 2020. Appeals reached the Supreme Court, which in December 2019 unanimously upheld the order, mandating a 25% reduction. The government complied by adjusting policies, including closing coal plants ahead of schedule, without further penalties.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights
relevant_rules: European Convention on Human Rights Article 8 (right to private and family life)

example_name: Vattenfall AB v Federal Republic of Germany
example_background: Vattenfall, a Swedish state-owned energy company, operated coal-fired power plants in Germany, including the Moorburg plant near Hamburg. In 2009, the company sought approximately €1.4 billion in compensation from Germany after the city of Hamburg imposed strict emission limits that forced operational changes and eventual decommissioning. The dispute arose from environmental regulations enacted to improve air quality in densely populated areas, affecting plants built with significant investments in the early 2000s. Vattenfall argued the measures constituted indirect expropriation, disrupting long-term business expectations based on prior permits.
process_and_outcome: Arbitration under the Energy Charter Treaty commenced in 2009 at the Permanent Court of Arbitration. In 2016, the tribunal dismissed Vattenfall's claims, ruling that Germany's actions were legitimate environmental protections not amounting to expropriation. No compensation was awarded, and Vattenfall covered substantial arbitration costs.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights

example_name: RWE AG v Association for the Protection of the Atmosphere
example_background: German energy giant RWE AG, operator of lignite mines and power plants in the Rhineland, faced a 2017 climate lawsuit filed by the Association for the Protection of the Atmosphere (VLN) and other NGOs. The plaintiffs sought to prevent the expansion of the Garzweiler II open-pit mine, which threatened the relocation of the village of Lützerath and contributed significantly to Germany's emissions. RWE's operations accounted for a substantial portion of national CO2 output, with detailed emissions data from the mine exceeding millions of tonnes annually. The case involved balancing energy security, employment in mining regions, and climate imperatives under evolving national targets.
process_and_outcome: The Higher Administrative Court of Münster ruled in 2021 against RWE, prohibiting further expansion of Garzweiler II due to climate considerations. The decision was based on Germany's 2038 coal phase-out law. RWE adjusted plans, accelerating mine closures, amid ongoing protests and negotiations.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights
involved_parties: VLN (Association for the Protection of the Atmosphere, plaintiff)

example_name: Uniper SE Coal Plant Emissions Case
example_background: Uniper SE, a major German utility, operated the Datteln 4 coal-fired power plant, which became operational in 2020 after years of construction and legal disputes. Environmental groups challenged the plant's permits, citing excessive NOx and CO2 emissions in violation of tightening EU standards. The facility's capacity of 1,100 MW contributed to regional air pollution and national emission totals, conflicting with Germany's Energiewende transition to renewables and coal phase-out commitments.
process_and_outcome: The Higher Administrative Court of Münster ordered in 2020 that Uniper retrofit Datteln 4 with additional emission controls by 2024 or face shutdown. Uniper complied by installing technology and planning conversion to hydrogen, avoiding closure penalties.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights

example_name: E.ON Benelux NV v Netherlands Environmental Permits
example_background: E.ON Benelux operated the Maasvlakte coal power plant in Rotterdam, Netherlands. In 2016, following Supreme Court rulings in related climate cases, the Council of State revoked the plant's operating permits due to inadequate consideration of climate impacts. The plant, with 1,100 MW capacity, emitted around 7 million tonnes of CO2 annually, representing a notable share of Dutch emissions. The revocation stemmed from a policy shift requiring all permit decisions to account for Paris Agreement obligations.
process_and_outcome: E.ON decommissioned the plant by 2017, converting parts to biomass before full closure. No fines were levied, but the decision accelerated coal phase-out in the Netherlands.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights
involved_parties: Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management (permit authority)

example_name: DSM-Firmenich Sustainability Compliance Framework
example_background: DSM-Firmenich, a Dutch-Swiss nutrition and health company formed by merger in 2023, established comprehensive sustainability governance integrating director duties with UN Sustainable Development Goals. The framework includes board-level oversight of emission reductions, supply chain human rights due diligence, and circular economy practices. Annual reporting details progress on 50% Scope 1/2 reductions by 2030, with investments in bio-based alternatives replacing petrochemicals. This approach aligns corporate strategy with stakeholder expectations and regulatory trends in the EU.
process_and_outcome: Independent audits by KPMG verified compliance in 2023 sustainability report, with no enforcement actions. The company received high ratings from Sustainalytics and CDP, enhancing investor confidence.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights
relevant_rules: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights

example_name: Heineken NV Responsible Sourcing Program
example_background: Heineken, headquartered in Amsterdam, implemented a Brew a Better World strategy since 2019, embedding supplier codes of conduct and human rights assessments across its global value chain. This includes third-party audits of over 2,000 suppliers for labor standards, water stewardship, and climate resilience. The program responds to EU due diligence expectations, with detailed grievance mechanisms handling thousands of cases annually. Progress reports quantify reductions in water usage and Scope 3 emissions through sustainable agriculture initiatives in barley and hops sourcing.
process_and_outcome: External verification by Bureau Veritas confirmed adherence in 2023, with no regulatory penalties. Heineken maintained its position in Dow Jones Sustainability Index Europe.
source_rule: Prohibition of abuse of rights
relevant_rules: EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (proposed)
